Compressibility of air limits positional accuracy. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Final-Day KTU Revision):** - [x] Fluid power = power transmission using **pressurized fluid** (oil = hydraulics, air = pneumatics). - [x] Four essential components: **Prime Mover, Pump/Compressor, Control Valves, Actuator.** - [x] At least **6 advantages** must be remembered verbatim (force multiplication, high power density, stepless control, overload protection, flexibility, self-lubrication). - [x] Hydraulic pressure range = **50–350 bar**; Pneumatic = **4–10 bar**. - [x] Hydraulic systems are for **HIGH FORCE** (>25 kN); Pneumatic for **CLEAN, FAST, MODERATE FORCE** tasks. - [x] Always map applications to **specific industry + specific machine** for full marks. - [x] **Pascal's Law** ($P_1 = P_2$, $F_1/A_1 = F_2/A_2$) is the foundation of force multiplication. - [x] **Power formula:** $P = P_{pressure} \times Q_{flow}$ (Watts). - [x] Always include the **block diagram** for any "explain fluid power system" question. - [x] For 14-mark answers, **internal choice** is always present — practice both Option A and Option B. - [x] Hydraulic uses **incompressible** oil; Pneumatic uses **compressible** air (this single difference explains ~80% of all behavioral contrasts). - [x] Remember **unit conversions**: 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 100 kPa. --- > **End of Notes — Module 1 / Topic: Advantages and Applications of Fluid Power Systems**

topics.title: Fluid Properties: Specific Weight, Density and Specific Gravity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT722 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluid Properties: Specific Weight, Density and Specific Gravity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Fluid Properties

In **Industrial Hydraulics & Pneumatics**, every calculation — whether it is sizing a hydraulic cylinder, selecting a pump, determining the pressure drop across a valve, or designing a pneumatic actuator — fundamentally rests upon three foundational fluid properties: **Density (ρ)**, **Specific Weight (γ)**, and **Specific Gravity (S)**. Without a rock-solid understanding of these three parameters, no engineer can reliably predict how a fluid will behave when subjected to force, pressure, or flow conditions.

This topic forms the **Module 1 bedrock** of the OEMRT722 syllabus and is consistently tested in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) university questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between *mass-based* properties (Density) and *weight-based* properties (Specific Weight), and to confidently inter-convert between them using **standard gravity g = 9.81 m/s²**.

---

## 1.2 Formal Definitions

### **A. Density (ρ)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Density** is defined as the **mass of a fluid per unit volume**. It is a scalar, intensive property of matter representing how tightly matter is packed within a given space.

- **Symbol:** $\rho$ (Greek letter *rho*)
- **SI Unit:** $\text{kg/m}^3$
- **CGS Unit:** $\text{g/cm}^3$ or $\text{g/cc}$
- **Dimensional Formula:** $[M L^{-3}]$

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine carrying two identical-sized water bottles. One is filled with **water**, the other with **honey**. The honey bottle feels significantly heavier, even though both occupy the same volume. This is because honey has a *higher density* — more *mass* is squeezed into the *same space*. Density tells us exactly "how heavy something is for its size."

---

### **B. Specific Weight (γ)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific Weight** is defined as the **weight of a fluid per unit volume**. Since weight is a force, specific weight represents the gravitational force exerted by the fluid on a unit volume of space.

- **Symbol:** $\gamma$ (Greek letter *gamma*)
- **SI Unit:** $\text{N/m}^3$
- **Dimensional Formula:** $[M L^{-2} T^{-2}]$

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Consider a stack of bricks on a weighing scale versus the same stack on the Moon. The number of bricks (mass) is identical, but the scale reads differently because gravity differs. *Specific weight* captures this "gravitational pull per unit volume" — it is **density multiplied by local gravity**.

---

### **C. Specific Gravity (S)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Specific Gravity** is defined as the **ratio of the density of a given fluid to the density of a standard reference fluid** (typically **water at 4 °C**, where $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$). It is a **dimensionless pure number**.

- **Symbol:** $S$ or $SG$ (sometimes denoted $G$)
- **Unit:** *Dimensionless* (no units)
- **Reference Standard:** Fresh water at $4\,^\circ\text{C}$

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of specific gravity as a "comparison score." If a fluid has $S = 1.0$, it is exactly as dense as water (think of vegetable oil). If $S = 0.8$ (like gasoline), it is lighter and will float on water. If $S = 13.6$ (mercury), it is 13.6 times heavier than water and will sink dramatically. This ratio gives engineers an instant mental picture of how a fluid behaves relative to water.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying "Why" Behind Each Property

### Why do we need Density?
Density is the **bridge between mass and volume**. In hydraulic systems, fluid mass determines:
- **Inertia** of the fluid column (affects acceleration and response time)
- **Mass flow rate** calculations ($\dot{m} = \rho Q$)
- **Thermal mass** (heat-carrying capacity of hydraulic oil)

### Why do we need Specific Weight?
Most hydraulic engineering equations — like the **hydrostatic pressure equation** ($P = \gamma h$) — operate on *force* rather than *mass*. Since hydraulic cylinders generate force and pressure is force per unit area, **specific weight is the natural property** that bridges fluid statics to mechanical work output.

### Why do we need Specific Gravity?
Specific Gravity is the **universal translator** between different unit systems and between liquids of different types. It allows engineers to quickly compare fluid heaviness without carrying around absolute density values. It is also the property reported on **hydraulic oil datasheets** (e.g., "SG = 0.87 at 15 °C").

---

## 2.2 Inter-Relationships Between the Three Properties

The three properties are mathematically interlinked. The master relationships are:

$$\boxed{\gamma = \rho \cdot g}$$

$$\boxed{S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water at } 4\,^\circ\text{C}}}}$$

From these, two powerful derived relations emerge:

$$S = \frac{\gamma_{\text{fluid}}}{\gamma_{\text{water}}}$$

$$\rho_{\text{fluid}} = S \cdot \rho_{\text{water}} = S \cdot 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

---

## 2.3 Standard Reference Values (KTU Exam Essentials)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always memorize these reference constants** — they appear in almost every numerical:

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Standard gravity | $g$ | $\mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$ |
| Density of water at $4\,^\circ\text{C}$ | $\rho_w$ | $\mathbf{1000 \; kg/m^3}$ |
| Specific weight of water at $4\,^\circ\text{C}$ | $\gamma_w$ | $\mathbf{9810 \; N/m^3}$ |
| Specific gravity of water | $S_w$ | $\mathbf{1.0 \; (dimensionless)}$ |
| Density of mercury at $20\,^\circ\text{C}$ | $\rho_{Hg}$ | $\mathbf{13600 \; kg/m^3}$ |
| Density of air at STP | $\rho_{\text{air}}$ | $\mathbf{1.225 \; kg/m^3}$ |
| Density of standard hydraulic oil (ISO VG 46) | $\rho_{\text{oil}}$ | $\mathbf{\approx 870 \; kg/m^3}$ |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table** — it covers ~90% of KTU numerical problems on this topic.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Density = Mass ÷ Volume | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 2 | $\gamma = \dfrac{W}{V} = \dfrac{\rho g V}{V} = \rho g$ | Specific weight = Weight ÷ Volume | $\text{N/m}^3$ |
| 3 | $S = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ | Specific gravity = density ratio | *dimensionless* |
| 4 | $S = \dfrac{\gamma_{\text{fluid}}}{\gamma_{\text{water}}}$ | SG via specific weight ratio | *dimensionless* |
| 5 | $\rho_{\text{fluid}} = S \cdot \rho_w$ | Density from specific gravity | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 6 | $\gamma_{\text{fluid}} = S \cdot \gamma_w$ | Specific weight from SG | $\text{N/m}^3$ |
| 7 | $m = \rho \cdot V$ | Mass from density and volume | $\text{kg}$ |
| 8 | $W = \gamma \cdot V$ | Weight from specific weight | $\text{N}$ |
| 9 | $P = \gamma \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure from height | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 10 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | Mass flow rate (foundation for hydraulics) | $\text{kg/s}$ |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Why This Property Matters |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Hydraulic Pump Selection** | Determines mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ and the actual weight of fluid the reservoir must hold |
| **Cylinder Force Calculation** | Force output depends on pressure, which is generated by $\gamma h$ in static head calculations |
| **Pneumatic System Design** | Compressed air density at any pressure/temperature sets tank sizing and mass flow requirements |
| **Pipe Network Sizing** | Pressure drop $\Delta P$ depends on $\rho$ through Reynolds number and Darcy–Weisbach equation |
| **Oil Cooler Sizing** | Specific heat times density gives thermal capacity — essential for thermal management of hydraulic oil |
| **Buoyancy & Tank Design** | Archimedes' principle uses specific gravity to determine whether a fluid will float or sink |

---

## 2.6 Effect of Temperature and Pressure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In Part B numericals, if a problem gives a temperature (e.g., *"oil at 40 °C"*), you must use the temperature-corrected density. Gases are far more compressible than liquids.

- **Liquids:** Density decreases slightly as temperature rises (thermal expansion).
- **Gases:** Density is highly sensitive to both **temperature** (via ideal gas law $\rho = \dfrac{P}{RT}$) and **pressure**.
- **Specific Gravity** for oils is typically reported at **15 °C** or **20 °C** — always check the datasheet footnote.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Newton's Second Law to Specific Weight

We start from the fundamental definition of weight.

**Step 1 — Definition of weight:**
By Newton's Second Law, the weight $W$ of a fluid body is the gravitational force acting on its mass:

$$W = m \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Definition of specific weight:**
Specific weight is weight per unit volume:

$$\gamma = \frac{W}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute mass from density:**
Since $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$, we have $m = \rho V$. Substituting:

$$\gamma = \frac{\rho V \cdot g}{V}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the volume $V$:**

$$\boxed{\gamma = \rho \cdot g}$$

This single derivation is the **most important relationship** for this topic.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Gravity as a Ratio

**Step 1 — Define density of reference fluid:**
$$\rho_{\text{ref}} = \rho_{\text{water at } 4\,^\circ\text{C}} = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Define specific gravity:**

$$S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{ref}}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for fluid density (multiply both sides by $\rho_{\text{ref}}$):**

$$\boxed{\rho_{\text{fluid}} = S \cdot \rho_{\text{ref}} = S \cdot 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to specific weight:**
$$\gamma_{\text{fluid}} = \rho_{\text{fluid}} \cdot g = S \cdot 1000 \cdot 9.81 = 9810 \cdot S \; \text{N/m}^3$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Worked Example 1 (Fundamentals)

> **Problem:** A hydraulic oil has a specific gravity of 0.86. Find (a) its density, (b) its specific weight, and (c) the mass of $0.05 \, \text{m}^3$ of this oil.

**Given:**
- $S = 0.86$ (dimensionless)
- $\rho_w = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
- $V = 0.05 \; \text{m}^3$

**(a) Density:**

$$\rho = S \cdot \rho_w = 0.86 \times 1000$$

$$\boxed{\rho = 860 \; \text{kg/m}^3}$$

**(b) Specific weight:**

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g = 860 \times 9.81$$

$$\boxed{\gamma = 8436.6 \; \text{N/m}^3}$$

**(c) Mass:**

$$m = \rho \cdot V = 860 \times 0.05$$

$$\boxed{m = 43 \; \text{kg}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Worked Example 2 (Hydrostatic Pressure Link)

> **Problem:** A vertical hydraulic tank contains oil of specific gravity 0.88 up to a height of $3 \, \text{m}$. Find the pressure at the bottom of the tank.

**Given:**
- $S = 0.88$
- $h = 3 \; \text{m}$
- $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$

**Solution:**

$$\gamma_{\text{oil}} = S \cdot \gamma_w = 0.88 \times 9810 = 8632.8 \; \text{N/m}^3$$

$$P = \gamma \cdot h = 8632.8 \times 3$$

$$\boxed{P = 25898.4 \; \text{Pa} \approx 25.9 \; \text{kPa}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Properties

| Property | Symbol | Definition | Depends on $g$? | Units | Sample Value (Oil) |
| :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Density | $\rho$ | Mass per unit volume | **No** | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | $860$ |
| Specific Weight | $\gamma$ | Weight per unit volume | **Yes** ($g$) | $\text{N/m}^3$ | $8436.6$ |
| Specific Gravity | $S$ | Ratio of densities | **No** | dimensionless | $0.86$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** If you were to transport your hydraulic system to the **Moon** ($g_{\text{moon}} \approx 1.62 \; \text{m/s}^2$), the **density would remain unchanged**, but the **specific weight would drop to about 1/6th** of its Earth value. The **specific gravity would still be 0.86** because it is a dimensionless ratio.

---

## 3.6 Dimensional Consistency Check (Examiner's Favorite)

Let us verify the dimensions of $\gamma = \rho g$:

$$[\gamma] = [\rho] \cdot [g] = (M L^{-3}) \cdot (L T^{-2}) = M L^{-2} T^{-2}$$

Since $1 \; \text{N} = 1 \; \text{kg} \cdot \text{m/s}^2$, we have:

$$M L^{-2} T^{-2} = \frac{M L T^{-2}}{L^3} = \frac{\text{N}}{\text{m}^3}$$

This confirms $\gamma$ has units of $\text{N/m}^3$. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Relationship Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **logical relationships** between Density, Specific Weight, and Specific Gravity, along with their derived engineering quantities.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MASS m (kg)"] --> B["DENSITY rho = m per V"]
    C["VOLUME V (m^3)"] --> B
    B --> D["SPECIFIC WEIGHT gamma = rho times g"]
    E["GRAVITY g = 9.81 m per s^2"] --> D
    B --> F["SPECIFIC GRAVITY S = rho fluid over rho water"]
    G["rho water 1000 kg per m^3"] --> F
    D --> H["HYDROSTATIC PRESSURE P = gamma times h"]
    I["HEIGHT h (m)"] --> H
    B --> J["MASS FLOW RATE m_dot = rho times Q"]
    K["FLOW RATE Q (m^3 per s)"] --> J
    D --> L["WEIGHT W = gamma times V"]
    C --> L
    F --> M["rho fluid = S times 1000"]
    F --> N["gamma fluid = S times 9810"]
```

---

## 4.2 Property Classification Block Diagram

This block diagram organizes fluid properties by their **physical category**, helping students remember where each one belongs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["FLUID PROPERTIES"] --> Q["MASS BASED"]
    P --> R["FORCE BASED"]
    P --> S["RATIO BASED"]
    Q --> Q1["Density rho (kg per m^3)"]
    R --> R1["Specific Weight gamma (N per m^3)"]
    R --> R2["Pressure P (Pa)"]
    R --> R3["Viscosity mu (Pa s)"]
    S --> S1["Specific Gravity S (dimensionless)"]
    S --> S2["Relative Humidity (dimensionless)"]
    Q1 --> T1["Mass Flow Rate m_dot"]
    R1 --> T2["Weight Flow Rate W_dot"]
```

---

## 4.3 Unit Conversion Logic Map

A common KTU 2024 numerical pitfall is unit conversion. This Mermaid chart shows the safe conversion pathways.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U0["GIVEN UNITS"] --> U1{"IS DENSITY IN g per cm^3 ?"}
    U1 -- YES --> U2["MULTIPLY BY 1000 to get kg per m^3"]
    U1 -- NO --> U3{"IS SPECIFIC WEIGHT IN kN per m^3 ?"}
    U3 -- YES --> U4["MULTIPLY BY 1000 to get N per m^3"]
    U3 -- NO --> U5["PROCEED WITH STANDARD SI UNITS"]
    U2 --> U6["FINAL ANSWER in SI UNITS"]
    U4 --> U6
    U5 --> U6
```

---

## 4.4 Engineering Application Hierarchy

This diagram shows how the three basic properties cascade into real hydraulic system design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["Specific Gravity S"] --> L2["Density rho = S times 1000"]
    L2 --> L3["Specific Weight gamma = rho times g"]
    L3 --> L4["Hydrostatic Pressure P = gamma times h"]
    L4 --> L5["Pump Head Requirement (meters of fluid)"]
    L2 --> L6["Mass Flow Rate m_dot = rho times Q"]
    L6 --> L7["Pump Power = m_dot times g times H"]
    L3 --> L8["Cylinder Force = Pressure times Area"]
    L8 --> L9["Load Lifting Capacity (Newtons)"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the following fluid properties with their SI units:**
**(a) Density**, **(b) Specific Weight**, **(c) Specific Gravity**

### **Model Answer:**

**(a) Density (ρ):** Mass of the fluid per unit volume.
$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}, \quad \text{SI Unit: kg/m}^3$$

**(b) Specific Weight (γ):** Weight of the fluid per unit volume.
$$\gamma = \rho g, \quad \text{SI Unit: N/m}^3$$

**(c) Specific Gravity (S):** Ratio of the density of the given fluid to the density of water at $4\,^\circ\text{C}$.
$$S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}, \quad \text{Unit: dimensionless}$$

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**A liquid has a specific gravity of 0.85. Calculate its density and specific weight. Take $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.**

### **Model Answer:**

**Given:** $S = 0.85$, $\rho_w = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$

**Density:**
$$\rho = S \times \rho_w = 0.85 \times 1000 = \boxed{850 \; \text{kg/m}^3}$$

**Specific Weight:**
$$\gamma = \rho \times g = 850 \times 9.81 = \boxed{8338.5 \; \text{N/m}^3}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts. Below, both alternative questions are provided to match the official "either-or" / "internal choice" pattern. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between specific weight and density of a fluid. Explain with a neat sketch how the specific gravity of a liquid can be determined using a **U-tube manometer** containing mercury and the liquid under test.

**(b)** A hydraulic oil of specific gravity $0.86$ fills a vertical cylinder of diameter $200 \, \text{mm}$ to a depth of $1.5 \, \text{m}$. Calculate:
1. The mass of oil in the cylinder
2. The specific weight of the oil
3. The hydrostatic pressure at the base of the cylinder

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation & U-tube Manometer (7 marks):**

**Derivation:**
Starting with Newton's second law, weight $W = m g$.
Specific weight is weight per unit volume: $\gamma = \dfrac{W}{V}$.
Substituting $m = \rho V$, we get $\gamma = \rho g$.

**U-tube Manometer Method:**

A U-tube manometer is a standard device to measure the specific gravity of an unknown liquid experimentally.

| Step | Procedure |
| :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Fill one limb of the U-tube with the **unknown liquid** and the other with **mercury**. |
| 2 | Allow the fluids to settle; mercury will settle at the bottom due to its higher density. |
| 3 | Measure the **height difference** of mercury levels, $h$, and the **height of the unknown liquid column** above the mercury, $H$. |
| 4 | Apply pressure balance at the lower mercury interface: $\rho_{\text{liquid}} g H = \rho_{\text{mercury}} g h$ |
| 5 | Rearranging: $S_{\text{liquid}} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{liquid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}} = \dfrac{h}{H} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{\text{Hg}}}{\rho_w} = \dfrac{h}{H} \times 13.6$ |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $S = 0.86$
- $d = 200 \, \text{mm} = 0.2 \, \text{m}$, so radius $r = 0.1 \, \text{m}$
- $h = 1.5 \, \text{m}$, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Volume of oil in the cylinder:**
$$V = \pi r^2 h = \pi \times (0.1)^2 \times 1.5 = 0.04712 \; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Density of oil:**
$$\rho = S \times 1000 = 0.86 \times 1000 = 860 \; \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Mass of oil:**
$$m = \rho \times V = 860 \times 0.04712$$
$$\boxed{m \approx 40.52 \; \text{kg}}$$

**Step 4 — Specific weight of oil:**
$$\gamma = \rho \times g = 860 \times 9.81$$
$$\boxed{\gamma = 8436.6 \; \text{N/m}^3}$$

**Step 5 — Hydrostatic pressure at the base:**
$$P = \gamma \times h = 8436.6 \times 1.5$$
$$\boxed{P = 12654.9 \; \text{Pa} \approx 12.65 \; \text{kPa}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **density** and **specific weight** of a fluid. State and explain the physical significance of specific gravity in the selection of hydraulic fluids.

**(b)** A hydraulic system uses $50$ liters of a fluid per minute. The fluid has a specific gravity of $0.88$. Find:
1. The density of the fluid in SI units
2. The mass flow rate in kg/s
3. The weight flow rate in N/s

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Differentiation & Significance (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Density (ρ) | Specific Weight (γ) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Mass per unit volume | Weight per unit volume |
| Formula | $\rho = m/V$ | $\gamma = W/V = \rho g$ |
| Depends on $g$? | No | Yes |
| SI Unit | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ |
| Dimension | $[M L^{-3}]$ | $[M L^{-2} T^{-2}]$ |

**Physical Significance of Specific Gravity in Hydraulic Fluid Selection:**

- A **lower specific gravity** implies a **lighter fluid**, which is desirable for **aerospace and mobile hydraulics** where weight is critical.
- It directly affects **hydrostatic pressure** $P = \gamma h$ and hence the **load-bearing capacity** of hydraulic systems.
- It governs the **weight flow rate**, influencing **pump power consumption**.
- It helps in **predicting fluid layering** in mixed-fluid systems (e.g., water contamination in oil tanks).
- For **buoyancy-based sensors** and **level indicators**, SG is the key calibration parameter.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $Q = 50 \; \text{L/min} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- $S = 0.88$, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Density:**
$$\rho = S \times \rho_w = 0.88 \times 1000$$
$$\boxed{\rho = 880 \; \text{kg/m}^3}$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate:**
$$\dot{m} = \rho \times Q = 880 \times 8.333 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 0.7333 \; \text{kg/s}}$$

**Step 3 — Specific weight:**
$$\gamma = \rho \times g = 880 \times 9.81 = 8632.8 \; \text{N/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Weight flow rate:**
$$\dot{W} = \gamma \times Q = 8632.8 \times 8.333 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$\boxed{\dot{W} \approx 7.194 \; \text{N/s}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Read Carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Students often forget to convert **liters to m³** (multiply by $10^{-3}$) or **mm to m** (multiply by $10^{-3}$). This single mistake can cost **2 to 3 marks** silently.
>
> 2. **Using $g = 10$ instead of $9.81$:** Unless the problem explicitly states $g = 10 \; \text{m/s}^2$, the **standard KTU answer key uses $g = 9.81$**. Wrong $g$ → wrong specific weight → wrong hydrostatic pressure.
>
> 3. **Confusing Specific Gravity with Density:** A frequent mistake is writing "$S = 0.86 \; \text{kg/m}^3$." Specific gravity is **dimensionless** — never attach units to it.
>
> 4. **Mixing up Volume and Capacity:** In reservoir problems, $50$ litres is **volume**, not mass. Convert using $\rho$ before computing mass.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the Volume of a Cylinder Formula:** $V = \pi r^2 h$, not $\pi d^2 h$. Using diameter instead of radius is a silent **factor-of-4 error**.
>
> 6. **Omitting Units in Final Answer:** KTU examiners explicitly deduct **0.5 mark** per sub-question if SI units are missing in the boxed answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Final-Year Friendly Summary:**

- **Density (ρ):** Mass per unit volume. Unit: **kg/m³**. Independent of gravity.
- **Specific Weight (γ):** Weight per unit volume. Unit: **N/m³**. Equal to $\rho g$.
- **Specific Gravity (S):** Dimensionless ratio comparing fluid density to **water at 4 °C** ($\rho_w = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$).
- **Master Formula Chain:** $S \rightarrow \rho = S \times 1000 \rightarrow \gamma = \rho \times 9.81$.
- **Standard Constants to Memorize:**
  - $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
  - $\rho_w = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$
  - $\gamma_w = 9810 \; \text{N/m}^3$
  - $S_w = 1.0$
  - $S_{\text{Hg}} = 13.6$
- **Hydrostatic Pressure:** $P = \gamma h$ — the most common downstream application.
- **Mass Flow Rate:** $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ — essential for pump and actuator sizing.
- **U-tube Manometer:** Experimental method to determine SG of an unknown liquid using mercury.
- **Volume of Cylinder:** $V = \pi r^2 h$ (radius, **not diameter**).
- **Temperature Effect:** Liquid density *decreases* with temperature; gas density *strongly* depends on temperature and pressure.
- **Always include SI units** in the final boxed answer — half-mark deductions are common in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pascal's Law and its Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMRT722 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Pascal's Law and its Application ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pascal's Law (Statement):** *"Pressure applied to an enclosed, incompressible fluid at rest is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the surfaces of the containing vessel."* This principle, formulated by the French mathematician **Blaise Pascal** in **1653**, is the foundational stone upon which the entire science of **Industrial Hydraulics** is built. It is a direct consequence of the molecular structure of fluids — specifically, the inability of fluid molecules to maintain a sustained shear stress at rest (i.e., the fluid cannot resist a tangential force without accelerating). ### 1.2 Mathematical Expression The governing relationship can be expressed as: $$P_1 = P_2 = P_3 = \ldots = P_n = \text{Constant}$$ Where: - $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n$ are the static pressure intensities measured at various points within the enclosed fluid body. - The equality holds **only** when the fluid is **incompressible**, **at rest (static equilibrium)**, and the **effects of elevation (gravity head)** are either neglected or compensated. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU board examinations, students are expected to clearly state the **two implicit assumptions** of Pascal's Law — (1) incompressibility of the fluid, and (2) negligible fluid self-weight (or constant fluid density). Loss of marks is common when these assumptions are omitted. ### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Squeezed Water Balloon:** > Imagine a sealed water balloon completely filled with water. Now, squeeze any one point on its surface with your thumb. What happens? The water inside instantaneously pushes outward against **every other point** on the balloon's inner surface with **equal force per unit area**. You do not need to squeeze the opposite side separately — the pressure you applied has already "arrived" there. > > This is precisely what Pascal's Law describes. The fluid acts as a **"mechanical information carrier"** — it instantly transmits the pressure signal from the input point to all output points, much like a perfectly rigid solid would, but with the added advantage of being able to flow and conform to any container shape. A more engineering-relevant analogy is the **hydraulic car jack**: a small force applied by hand on a small piston produces a massive lifting force on a larger piston, all because the pressure generated by your small force is faithfully transmitted through the trapped hydraulic oil. ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Standard SI Unit | CGS Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Pressure Intensity | $P$ | **Pascal (Pa)** or **N/m²** | **bar** or **dyne/cm²** | | Force | $F$ | **Newton (N)** | **kgf** | | Area | $A$ | **m²** | **cm²** | | Fluid Density | $\rho$ | **kg/m³** | **g/cm³** | | Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | **981 cm/s²** | **Conversion benchmarks (must memorize for KTU):** - **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 100 kPa** - **1 atm = 1.01325 bar ≈ 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa** - **1 kgf/cm² ≈ 0.98 bar** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition of Pascal's Law To internalize Pascal's Law for KTU examinations, it must be broken into its constituent logical pillars: 1. **The Fluid Must Be Enclosed** The fluid must be contained within a sealed boundary so that the applied pressure cannot escape. An open container cannot sustain a static pressure because the surface is exposed to the atmosphere. 2. **The Fluid Must Be Incompressible** This implies that the fluid density $\rho$ remains **constant** under pressure. Real hydraulic fluids (mineral oils) approach this ideal with a bulk modulus typically in the range of **1.5 to 2.0 GPa**. 3. **The Fluid Must Be in Static Equilibrium** There must be no relative motion between adjacent fluid layers. This eliminates viscous friction and dynamic pressure components from the analysis. 4. **Transmission is Instantaneous** Because sound travels through liquids at approximately **1500 m/s**, pressure changes propagate effectively instantaneously for any practical engineering system. This is why hydraulic systems respond faster than pneumatic systems (where sound speed is only ~340 m/s). 5. **Pressure Acts Perpendicular to All Surfaces** At any interface between the fluid and a solid boundary, the pressure vector is always **normal** to that surface. There is no tangential (shear) component in a static fluid. ### 2.2 The Core "Why" and "How" > **Why does Pascal's Law work?** > Because fluid molecules are in constant random motion (Brownian-like). When external pressure is applied, the molecules transfer momentum to their neighbors through collisions. In an incompressible medium, this transfer is lossless and omnidirectional. > **How is it used in hydraulics?** > A small input force $F_1$ acting on a small piston of area $A_1$ generates a pressure $P = F_1 / A_1$. This pressure is transmitted undiminished through the fluid to a larger piston of area $A_2$, where it produces an output force $F_2 = P \times A_2$. By choosing $A_2 \gg A_1$, enormous mechanical advantage is achieved. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains every equation you are expected to memorize for Module 1 KTU board questions on Pascal's Law. Master it before attempting any numerical. | # | Formula | Description | Variables | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pressure definition | $P$ = pressure, $F$ = force, $A$ = area | Pa, N, m² | | 2 | $P_1 = P_2$ | Pascal's Law (core statement) | Equal pressure at two points | Pa | | 3 | $\dfrac{F_1}{A_1} = \dfrac{F_2}{A_2}$ | Force transmission across two pistons | $F_1, F_2$ = forces; $A_1, A_2$ = piston areas | N, m² | | 4 | $F_2 = F_1 \times \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | Output force (mechanical advantage form) | — | N | | 5 | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$ | Area of circular piston of diameter $d$ | $d$ = piston diameter | m² | | 6 | $V_1 = V_2$ | Volume conservation (incompressible) | $V$ = displaced volume | m³ | | 7 | $A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$ | Displacement relation | $x$ = piston displacement | m | | 8 | $\text{M.A.} = \dfrac{F_2}{F_1} = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | Mechanical Advantage | Dimensionless ratio | — | | 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{\text{out}}}{W_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{F_2 \cdot x_2}{F_1 \cdot x_1}$ | Hydraulic efficiency | $\eta$ = efficiency | — | | 10 | $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}}$ | Absolute vs gauge pressure | $P_{\text{atm}} \approx 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa | Pa | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In all derivation questions, students often forget that volume conservation ($A_1 x_1 = A_2 x_2$) applies **only when there is no internal/external leakage** and **only for incompressible fluids**. Always state these assumptions explicitly to score full marks. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Pascal's Law is the operating principle behind virtually every hydraulic machine in existence: - **Hydraulic Press** — used in forging, stamping, sheet metal forming, and plastic molding. - **Hydraulic Jack** — used in automobile repair shops to lift multi-tonne vehicles. - **Hydraulic Brakes** — used in virtually all automobiles and aircraft; the brake pedal pushes a small master cylinder piston, transmitting pressure to four larger wheel cylinder pistons. - **Hydraulic Lifts and Elevators** — used in service stations, aircraft hangars, and freight platforms. - **Hydraulic Steering Systems** — power steering in trucks and heavy machinery. - **Die Casting and Injection Molding Machines** — for high-pressure injection of molten metal or polymer. - **Earthmoving Equipment** — excavators, bulldozers, and backhoes rely entirely on Pascal-derived hydraulic actuators. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force Transmission Through Two Connected Pistons Consider two cylinders of cross-sectional areas $A_1$ and $A_2$ connected at the bottom and filled with an incompressible fluid (typically hydraulic oil). The cylinders are fitted with frictionless pistons that can slide vertically. **Step 1:** Apply a small input force $F_1$ on piston 1 (area $A_1$). The pressure generated in the fluid directly beneath piston 1 is given by the definition of pressure: $$P = \frac{\text{Force}}{\text{Area}}$$ Substituting the input quantities: $$P_1 = \frac{F_1}{A_1}$$ **Step 2:** Recognize that piston 1 is in static equilibrium. The fluid below it exerts an equal and opposite upward pressure. This pressure, by Pascal's Law, is transmitted undiminished to all points in the fluid — including the bottom of piston 2: $$P_2 = P_1$$ **Step 3:** Equate the two pressures to form the **Pascal transmission equation**: $$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$ **Step 4:** Rearrange to solve for the output force $F_2$: $$F_2 = F_1 \times \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$ **Step 5:** Interpret the result. The ratio $\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ is the **Mechanical Advantage (M.A.)** of the hydraulic system. If $A_2$ is 50 times larger than $A_1$, then $F_2 = 50 F_1$ — a small input force is amplified into a massive output force. **Verification by dimensional consistency:** $$[F_2] = [F_1] \times \frac{[\text{m}^2]}{[\text{m}^2]} = [N] \quad \checkmark$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Displacement and Velocity Relationship Since the fluid is incompressible, the volume of fluid displaced by piston 1 must equal the volume received by piston 2. **Step 1:** Piston 1 moves down by a distance $x_1$. The volume of fluid pushed out from cylinder 1 is: $$V_1 = A_1 \cdot x_1$$ **Step 2:** This same volume of fluid pushes piston 2 up by a distance $x_2$: $$V_2 = A_2 \cdot x_2$$ **Step 3:** Apply volume conservation (no leakage, incompressible fluid): $$V_1 = V_2$$ **Step 4:** Substitute and simplify: $$A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$$ **Step 5:** Rearrange to express the output displacement in terms of input displacement: $$x_2 = x_1 \times \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$ **Step 6:** Differentiate both sides with respect to time $t$ to obtain the velocity relationship: $$\frac{dx_2}{dt} = \frac{dx_1}{dt} \times \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$ $$v_2 = v_1 \times \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Insight — Conservation of Power:** Since the fluid is assumed frictionless, the input power must equal the output power: > > $$P_{\text{in}} = F_1 \cdot v_1$$ > > $$P_{\text{out}} = F_2 \cdot v_2$$ > > Substituting $F_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_2 / A_1)$ and $v_2 = v_1 \cdot (A_1 / A_2)$: > > $$P_{\text{out}} = \left(F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right) \cdot \left(v_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right) = F_1 \cdot v_1 = P_{\text{in}}$$ > > This confirms that **force is gained at the cost of displacement/velocity** — a fundamental trade-off in all hydraulic systems. You cannot get energy for free. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure Equation Including Elevation Effect (Hydrostatic Correction) In real engineering scenarios, the two pistons are rarely at the same height. Let piston 1 be at height $z_1$ and piston 2 be at height $z_2$ above an arbitrary datum. **Step 1:** Starting from the basic hydrostatic equation, the pressure difference between two points in a static fluid is: $$P_2 - P_1 = -\rho \cdot g \cdot (z_2 - z_1)$$ **Step 2:** If we set $P_1 = F_1 / A_1$ (the input pressure at piston 1) and solve for $P_2$ at piston 2: $$P_2 = P_1 - \rho \cdot g \cdot (z_2 - z_1)$$ **Step 3:** Substitute the expressions: $$\frac{F_2}{A_2} = \frac{F_1}{A_1} - \rho \cdot g \cdot (z_2 - z_1)$$ **Step 4:** Rearrange for $F_2$: $$F_2 = A_2 \left[ \frac{F_1}{A_1} - \rho \cdot g \cdot (z_2 - z_1) \right]$$ **Step 5:** Special case — when both pistons are at the same elevation ($z_1 = z_2$), the gravity term vanishes and we recover the simple Pascal equation: $$F_2 = F_1 \times \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Note:** For problems where the elevation difference is significant (e.g., hydraulic press on a multi-storey platform), the term $\rho g (z_2 - z_1)$ is **not negligible** and must be included. For most laboratory-scale problems, this term is omitted and the simple form is used. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Key Relationships | Phenomenon | Input Side | Output Side | Relationship | Trade-off | |---|---|---|---|---| | Pressure | $P_1$ | $P_2$ | $P_1 = P_2$ | None — perfect transmission | | Force | $F_1$ | $F_2$ | $F_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_2/A_1)$ | Output force increases by M.A. | | Displacement | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $x_2 = x_1 \cdot (A_1/A_2)$ | Output displacement decreases | | Velocity | $v_1$ | $v_2$ | $v_2 = v_1 \cdot (A_1/A_2)$ | Output velocity decreases | | Power | $P_{\text{in}}$ | $P_{\text{out}}$ | $P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}}$ (ideal) | Conserved (lossless ideal) | | Volume | $V_1$ | $V_2$ | $V_1 = V_2$ | Conserved (incompressible) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Hydraulic Press Working Principle The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a Pascal's-Law-based hydraulic press, mapping the energy and signal flow from operator input to load output. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["OPERATOR INPUT FORCE F1"] --> nodeB["SMALL PISTON AREA A1"] nodeB --> nodeC["PRESSURE GENERATION P1 EQUALS F1 BY A1"] nodeC --> nodeD["SEALED HYDRAULIC FLUID CHAMBER"] nodeD --> nodeE["PASCAL TRANSMISSION UNDIMINISHED PRESSURE"] nodeE --> nodeF["LARGE PISTON AREA A2"] nodeF --> nodeG["OUTPUT FORCE F2 EQUALS F1 TIMES A2 BY A1"] nodeG --> nodeH["WORKPIECE OR LOAD"] nodeI["VOLUME CONSERVATION A1 X1 EQUALS A2 X2"] -.controls.-> nodeD nodeJ["FLUID INCOMPRESSIBILITY CONSTANT DENSITY"] -.assumption.-> nodeD nodeK["STATIC EQUILIBRIUM NO FLOW AT ANALYSIS"] -.assumption.-> nodeD ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - **Solid arrows** (→) represent primary force/pressure signal flow. - **Dotted arrows** (-.->) represent governing constraints and physical assumptions. - Each node represents a functional stage in the conversion of input mechanical effort into amplified output force. --- ### 4.2 Operational Sequence Diagram — Hydraulic Jack Lifting a Vehicle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Op as Operator participant SP as Small Piston participant Fluid as Hydraulic Oil participant LP as Large Piston participant Load as Vehicle Load Op->>SP: Apply small force F1 SP->>Fluid: Generate pressure P1 Note over Fluid: Pascal transmission
P1 reaches LP undiminished Fluid->>LP: Pressure P1 acts on area A2 LP->>Load: Lift force F2 = F1 x A2/A1 Note over Load: Vehicle rises by x2 Fluid-->>SP: Volume conservation: A1 x1 = A2 x2 Op->>Op: Note trade-off: stroke reduced ``` --- ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Complete Hydraulic System ```mermaid flowchart LR node1["PRIME MOVER ELECTRIC MOTOR"] --> node2["HYDRAULIC PUMP"] node2 --> node3["PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE SAFETY"] node2 --> node4["DIRECTIONAL CONTROL VALVE"] node4 --> node5["ACTUATOR CYLINDER LARGE PISTON"] node5 --> node6["OUTPUT WORKPIECE"] node4 --> node7["RETURN LINE TO TANK"] node7 --> node8["RESERVOIR WITH FILTER"] node8 --> node2 style node3 fill:#ffe5e5 style node8 fill:#e5f5ff ``` **Architectural Notes for KTU Viva:** - The pump generates flow (and hence pressure when resisted by a load). - The relief valve (highlighted red) protects the system from over-pressure — typically set at **10–25% above the working pressure**. - The reservoir (highlighted blue) supplies fluid, allows air separation, and dissipates heat. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **State Pascal's Law. Mention the two essential conditions under which it is valid.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Statement (2 marks):** Pascal's Law states that the pressure applied to an enclosed, incompressible fluid at rest is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the surfaces of the containing vessel. > > **Essential Conditions (1 mark):** > 1. The fluid must be **incompressible** (constant density under pressure). > 2. The fluid must be in **static equilibrium** (no motion / no flow). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **A hydraulic press has a small piston of diameter 5 cm and a large piston of diameter 50 cm. Find the force exerted on the large piston when a force of 200 N is applied on the small piston.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Given:** $d_1 = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m, $d_2 = 50$ cm $= 0.5$ m, $F_1 = 200$ N. **Step 1 (1 mark):** Calculate the areas. $$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.5)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-1} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply Pascal's Law. $$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Solve for $F_2$. $$F_2 = F_1 \times \frac{A_2}{A_1} = 200 \times \frac{1.963 \times 10^{-1}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 200 \times 100 = 20{,}000 \text{ N}$$ **Result:** $\boxed{F_2 = 20 \text{ kN}}$ --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Omitting assumptions** of Pascal's Law in derivation questions costs **2 marks**. > - **Unit mismatches** (e.g., mixing cm and m in the same equation) is the **#1 reason** for numerical-mark deductions. > - Students often write Pascal's Law as $F_1 / A_1 = F_2 / A_2$ without explaining **why** the pressures are equal. Always include the conceptual link: *Pascal's Law guarantees $P_1 = P_2$, and since $P = F/A$, we obtain the force equation.* > - Forgetting the **displacement trade-off** ($x_2 = x_1 \cdot A_1 / A_2$) in derivation questions leads to a 3-mark cut. > - Never write "Pascal's Theorem" — the correct terminology is **"Pascal's Law"** or **"Pascal's Principle."** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]** **(a)** State Pascal's Law in detail and explain the two essential conditions for its validity. Using a neat sketch, describe the working principle of a **hydraulic press** with reference to Pascal's Law. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hydraulic jack is used to lift a car weighing **15 kN**. The diameter of the smaller piston is **20 mm** and that of the larger piston is **150 mm**. If the smaller piston is pushed down through a stroke of **100 mm**, determine: **(i)** The force required on the smaller piston. **(ii)** The distance through which the larger piston rises. **(iii)** The mechanical advantage of the system. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) — Part (a) Model Solution (7 marks):** **Statement of Pascal's Law (2 marks):** Pascal's Law states that pressure applied to a confined, incompressible fluid at rest is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid, and acts perpendicular to the containing surfaces. **Two Essential Conditions (2 marks):** 1. **Incompressibility of the fluid** — the fluid density $\rho$ must remain constant under the applied pressure. Real hydraulic oils (bulk modulus ≈ 1.5–2.0 GPa) satisfy this condition closely. 2. **Static equilibrium** — the fluid must be at rest; there should be no relative motion between adjacent layers, so that no shear stress exists. **Working Principle of a Hydraulic Press — Neat Sketch Description (3 marks):** The hydraulic press consists of two cylinders of different cross-sectional areas connected at the bottom and filled with hydraulic oil. A small input force $F_1$ is applied on the small piston of area $A_1$, producing a pressure: $$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1}$$ This pressure is transmitted undiminished through the oil to the large piston of area $A_2$, where it produces an output force: $$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$ Since $A_2 \gg A_1$, the output force $F_2$ is enormously amplified. This amplified force is used to compress the workpiece placed on the large piston. --- **(b) — Part (b) Model Solution (7 marks):** **Given Data:** - Load (output force): $W = F_2 = 15 \text{ kN} = 15{,}000$ N - Small piston diameter: $d_1 = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.02$ m - Large piston diameter: $d_2 = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15$ m - Small piston stroke: $x_1 = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1$ m **Step 1 (1 mark):** Calculate the piston areas. $$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.02)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2 = 1.7671 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the input force $F_1$ using Pascal's Law. $$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$ $$F_1 = F_2 \times \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 15{,}000 \times \frac{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}}{1.7671 \times 10^{-2}}$$ $$F_1 = 15{,}000 \times 0.01778 = 266.7 \text{ N}$$ **Result (i):** $\boxed{F_1 \approx 266.7 \text{ N}}$ (about 27.2 kgf — easily applied by hand) **Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the output displacement $x_2$ using volume conservation. $$A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$$ $$x_2 = x_1 \times \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 0.1 \times \frac{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}}{1.7671 \times 10^{-2}}$$ $$x_2 = 0.1 \times 0.01778 = 1.778 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.778 \text{ mm}$$ **Result (ii):** $\boxed{x_2 \approx 1.778 \text{ mm}}$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute the mechanical advantage. $$\text{M.A.} = \frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{15{,}000}{266.7} \approx 56.25$$ Alternatively, M.A. $= \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = \dfrac{1.7671 \times 10^{-2}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 56.25$ ✓ **Result (iii):** $\boxed{\text{M.A.} \approx 56.25}$ **Trade-off Observation (for full marks):** Note that the input stroke of 100 mm produces only ~1.78 mm of output displacement. This confirms the **force-displacement trade-off** — force is multiplied, but displacement is divided by the same factor. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **(a)** With the help of a diagram, explain the working of a **hydraulic brake system** used in automobiles. Derive the relationship between the forces at the master cylinder and wheel cylinder using Pascal's Law. **(7 marks)** **(b)** In a hydraulic press, the diameter of the smaller piston is **30 mm** and that of the larger piston is **200 mm**. A force of **350 N** is applied to the smaller piston. If the system has an overall efficiency of **85%**, determine: **(i)** The pressure generated in the fluid. **(ii)** The actual output force available at the larger piston. **(iii)** The volume of fluid displaced when the smaller piston moves through **50 mm**. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) — Part (a) Model Solution (7 marks):** **Working of Hydraulic Brake System (4 marks):** When the driver presses the brake pedal, the pedal lever pushes the **master cylinder piston** (small area $A_1$). This generates pressure $P = F_1 / A_1$ in the brake fluid, which is transmitted through the brake lines (according to Pascal's Law) to four **wheel cylinders** (each with area $A_2$). The fluid pressure forces the wheel cylinder pistons outward, pressing the brake shoes/pads against the brake drums/discs, thereby producing friction that decelerates the vehicle. A slight movement of the brake pedal produces a substantial braking force at each wheel. **Derivation (3 marks):** Let $F_1$ be the force applied by the driver on the master cylinder piston of area $A_1$. The pressure developed is: $$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1}$$ This pressure is transmitted equally to each wheel cylinder. The force generated at each wheel cylinder of area $A_2$ is: $$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = \frac{F_1}{A_2 / A_1}$$ Hence, the total braking force available (at all four wheels combined) is: $$F_{\text{total}} = 4 F_2 = \frac{4 F_1 \cdot A_2}{A_1}$$ This shows how a modest pedal force produces a massive braking effort — typically a Mechanical Advantage of **5 to 10** times per wheel. --- **(b) — Part (b) Model Solution (7 marks):** **Given Data:** - $d_1 = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.03$ m; $d_2 = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2$ m - $F_1 = 350$ N; $\eta = 0.85$; $x_1 = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05$ m **Step 1 (1 mark):** Calculate the piston areas. $$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.03)^2 = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.2)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Calculate the pressure generated. $$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{350}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.952 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \approx 4.95 \text{ bar}$$ **Result (i):** $\boxed{P \approx 4.95 \text{ bar}}$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Calculate the **ideal** output force (100% efficient). $$F_{2,\text{ideal}} = P \cdot A_2 = 4.952 \times 10^{5} \times 3.1416 \times 10^{-2} = 15{,}558 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply efficiency to obtain the **actual** output force. $$F_{2,\text{actual}} = \eta \cdot F_{2,\text{ideal}} = 0.85 \times 15{,}558 = 13{,}224 \text{ N} \approx 13.22 \text{ kN}$$ **Result (ii):** $\boxed{F_{2,\text{actual}} \approx 13.22 \text{ kN}}$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Calculate the volume of fluid displaced. $$V = A_1 \cdot x_1 = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.05 = 3.534 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3 = 35.34 \text{ cm}^3$$ **Result (iii):** $\boxed{V \approx 35.34 \text{ cm}^3}$ --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quick-Fire Recall — Must memorize before every KTU exam:** - [x] **Pascal's Law statement** — pressure in a confined, incompressible, static fluid is transmitted equally in all directions. - [x] **Two assumptions** — incompressibility + static equilibrium. - [x] **Core equation** — $\dfrac{F_1}{A_1} = \dfrac{F_2}{A_2}$ - [x] **Mechanical Advantage** — $\text{M.A.} = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = \dfrac{F_2}{F_1}$ - [x] **Volume conservation** — $A_1 x_1 = A_2 x_2$ - [x] **Displacement trade-off** — large piston moves a **smaller** distance but produces a **larger** force. - [x] **Velocity trade-off** — large piston moves at a **lower** velocity. - [x] **Power conservation** (ideal) — input power equals output power; energy is **never** created, only transformed. - [x] **Area of circular piston** — $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$ - [x] **Unit conversions** — 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 100 kPa; 1 atm ≈ 1.013 bar. - [x] **Efficiency formula** — $\eta = \dfrac{F_2 \cdot x_2}{F_1 \cdot x_1}$ or equivalently $\eta = \dfrac{F_{2,\text{actual}}}{F_{2,\text{ideal}}}$. - [x] **Real-world examples** — hydraulic press, hydraulic jack, hydraulic brakes, hydraulic lifts, power steering, die-casting machines. - [x] **Always include units** in every numerical answer — KTU examiners deduct marks for unit-less finals. - [x] **Mention elevation correction** $\rho g (z_2 - z_1)$ **only** when pistons are at different heights — do not add it for level-mounted systems. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Pascal's Law questions typically appear either as a 3-mark conceptual short-answer (definition + conditions) or as a 7-mark derivation sub-part of a larger 14-mark hydraulic-system question. Practice writing the **force equation** and the **displacement equation** from memory — these two lines carry most of the marks.

topics.title: Continuity Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT722 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Continuity Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Continuity Equation** is a fundamental conservation principle in fluid mechanics, derived from the **law of conservation of mass**. In the context of industrial hydraulics and pneumatics, it states that for a steady, continuous flow of fluid through a conduit, hydraulic line, or pneumatic circuit element, the **mass flow rate** (or equivalently, the **volumetric flow rate** for an incompressible fluid) must remain constant across every cross-section of the flow path.

Mathematically, the steady-state continuity equation is expressed as:

$$Q = A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = A_3 v_3 = \text{constant}$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate, $A$ is the cross-sectional area, and $v$ is the average flow velocity at the respective sections. The general three-dimensional form for a compressible fluid is given by:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The continuity equation is the foundational equation upon which virtually every hydraulic and pneumatic circuit analysis is built. It is essential for sizing valves, calculating actuator speeds, and determining line velocities in hydraulic power packs and pneumatic systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a multi-lane highway where cars are the fluid and the road is the pipe. If the highway is **wide (large area)**, cars travel **slowly (low velocity)** because they have more lanes to spread out. If the highway suddenly **narrows to a tunnel (small area)**, the same number of cars per hour must pass through, so they are forced to **speed up (high velocity)** to maintain the flow. The continuity equation simply formalizes this common-sense observation into a precise mathematical law: *the rate of cars (or fluid mass) passing any point on the highway per unit time must be the same everywhere*.

A second, more mechanical analogy is a **garden hose with a thumb placed over the nozzle**. By reducing the exit area, the water velocity increases dramatically, producing a powerful jet — this is the continuity principle at work in everyday life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Referenced in KTU 2024 Module 1:**
> - Density of water (hydraulic fluid): **$\rho_{water} \approx 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$**
> - Density of typical hydraulic mineral oil: **$\rho_{oil} \approx 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$** (at 40°C)
> - Specific weight of water: **$\gamma_{water} = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$**
> - Standard atmospheric pressure: **$P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$**

### 1.3 Significance in Industrial Hydraulics & Pneumatics

In hydraulic systems, the continuity equation governs:
- The **extension and retraction speeds** of hydraulic cylinders.
- The **rotational speed** of hydraulic motors.
- The **flow distribution** in parallel and series circuits.
- **Valve sizing** in hydraulic power units (HPUs).

In pneumatic systems, it governs:
- The **filling and exhausting times** of cylinders and reservoirs.
- The **mass flow rate** through FRL units (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator).
- **Compressor sizing** and receiver tank design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

The continuity equation is not an empirical observation; it is a direct mathematical consequence of the **conservation of mass** applied to a control volume. Let us dissect the reasoning in discrete, logical steps:

1. **Step 1 — Define a Control Volume (CV):** We define a fixed region of space bounded by a **control surface**. The fluid flows in and out of this region through the control surface.

2. **Step 2 — Conservation of Mass Statement:** The net rate of mass leaving the control volume plus the rate of change of mass within the control volume must equal zero. Mathematically:
   $$\text{Rate of mass accumulation} + \text{Net mass efflux} = 0$$

3. **Step 3 — Apply to a 1D Stream Tube:** For a single streamline or stream tube, the mass entering section 1 per unit time is $\rho_1 A_1 v_1$, and the mass leaving section 2 per unit time is $\rho_2 A_2 v_2$. The steady-state assumption ($\frac{\partial}{\partial t} = 0$) eliminates the accumulation term, yielding:
   $$\rho_1 A_1 v_1 = \rho_2 A_2 v_2$$

4. **Step 4 — Specialize to Incompressible Flow:** For hydraulic fluids and liquids, $\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$ (constant). The density cancels out, giving the simplified form:
   $$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = Q$$

5. **Step 5 — Velocity-Area Inverse Relationship:** Because $Q$ is constant, $v$ is **inversely proportional to $A$**. Doubling the area halves the velocity. This single relationship explains the operation of venture meters, flow nozzles, and the velocity boost in cylinder cushioning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In hydraulic systems, fluids are treated as **incompressible** (constant $\rho$), so the form $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ is overwhelmingly used. In pneumatic systems, compressibility dominates, and the **mass flow form** $\rho_1 A_1 v_1 = \rho_2 A_2 v_2$ is required.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Applicable Flow Type | Units (SI) |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------------|------------|
| 1 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow rate definition | All | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 2 | $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | 1D Continuity (incompressible) | Hydraulic | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 3 | $\rho_1 A_1 v_1 = \rho_2 A_2 v_2$ | 1D Continuity (compressible) | Pneumatic | $\text{kg}/\text{s}$ |
| 4 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | Mass flow rate | All | $\text{kg}/\text{s}$ |
| 5 | $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | 3D Differential form | All (advanced) | $\text{kg}/(\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{s})$ |
| 6 | $v_2 = \left(\frac{A_1}{A_2}\right) v_1$ | Velocity at smaller section | All | $\text{m}/\text{s}$ |
| 7 | $Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + \cdots + Q_n$ | Continuity at a junction (Kirchhoff's current law analog) | All | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 8 | $v_{piston} = \frac{Q}{A_{piston}}$ | Cylinder extension/retraction speed | Hydraulic | $\text{m}/\text{s}$ |
| 9 | $Q_{branch,i} = \left(\frac{A_i}{\sum A_i}\right) Q_{total}$ | Flow distribution in parallel actuators (assuming equal $\Delta P$) | Hydraulic | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |

### 2.3 Boundary Conditions and Assumptions

For the simplified form $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ to be valid, the following **KTU-standard assumptions** must hold:

- **Steady flow:** Flow properties at any point do not change with time.
- **Incompressible fluid:** $\rho$ = constant (true for hydraulic oils; *not* true for gases).
- **One-dimensional flow:** Velocity is uniform across the cross-section and aligned with the axis.
- **No sources or sinks within the control volume:** Mass is neither created nor destroyed.
- **Stream tube analysis:** Flow follows well-defined streamlines without cross-currents.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The continuity equation is the unsung hero behind the design of:

- **Hydraulic presses and lifts:** Determining cylinder bore size for a required piston speed given pump displacement.
- **Pneumatic conveying systems:** Calculating air mass flow to transport particulates.
- **Flow meters (orifice, venture, rotameter):** Measuring flow rate by correlating pressure drop with velocity change.
- **Hydraulic and pneumatic circuits:** Branching flow analysis using the node law $Q_{in} = \sum Q_{out}$.
- **Compressors and vacuum pumps:** Sizing based on required mass throughput.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — 1D Steady Continuity Equation (Integral Form)

**Starting premise:** Apply conservation of mass to a control volume (CV) bounded by a stream tube with inlet section 1 and outlet section 2.

**Step 1:** The general conservation of mass statement is:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

This equation states that the time rate of change of mass inside the CV plus the net mass flux through the control surface (CS) equals zero. The unit normal vector $\hat{n}$ points outward from the control surface.

**Step 2:** Apply the **steady-state assumption.** In a steady flow, no property at a fixed point changes with time. Therefore, the time derivative term vanishes:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV = 0$$

The remaining equation becomes:

$$\int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 3:** For a 1D stream tube with only two openings (inlet 1 and outlet 2), the surface integral reduces to a sum of two flux terms. The velocity is assumed uniform (and parallel to $\hat{n}$) at each opening:

$$\rho_1 A_1 v_1 (\vec{V}_1 \cdot \hat{n}_1) + \rho_2 A_2 v_2 (\vec{V}_2 \cdot \hat{n}_2) = 0$$

**Step 4:** Recognize the sign convention. At the inlet, velocity opposes the outward normal ($\vec{V}_1 \cdot \hat{n}_1 = -1$), and at the outlet, velocity aligns with it ($\vec{V}_2 \cdot \hat{n}_2 = +1$):

$$-\rho_1 A_1 v_1 + \rho_2 A_2 v_2 = 0$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange the equation to obtain the classical mass-conservation form:

$$\rho_1 A_1 v_1 = \rho_2 A_2 v_2$$

This is the **general 1D steady continuity equation** valid for both liquids and gases.

**Step 6:** For **incompressible flow** (liquids, hydraulic oils), $\rho_1 = \rho_2 = \rho$, so $\rho$ cancels from both sides:

$$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = Q \quad \text{(constant)}$$

This is the form most commonly tested in the KTU Module 1 examinations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Velocity Ratio for a Variable Cross-Section

**Starting equation:**

$$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$$

**Step 1:** Solve explicitly for the velocity at section 2:

$$v_2 = \frac{A_1 v_1}{A_2}$$

**Step 2:** Define the **area ratio** $R_A = \frac{A_1}{A_2}$:

$$v_2 = R_A \cdot v_1$$

**Step 3:** For a circular pipe, $A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$, so the area ratio in terms of diameters is:

$$R_A = \frac{A_1}{A_2} = \frac{\frac{\pi D_1^2}{4}}{\frac{\pi D_2^2}{4}} = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the velocity ratio in terms of diameter ratio is:

$$v_2 = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2 v_1$$

**Interpretation:** If the pipe diameter is halved ($D_2 = D_1/2$), then the velocity increases by a factor of $2^2 = 4$. This quadratic sensitivity is the basis for **orifice plate flow measurement** in pneumatic systems.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cylinder Piston Speed (Industrial Application)

**Given:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder with piston area $A_p$, rod-side area $A_r$, and pump supply flow rate $Q$.

**Step 1:** During **extension**, the full piston area is exposed to the incoming flow. By continuity, the volume entering the cap-end per unit time equals the rate at which the piston displaces fluid:

$$Q = A_p \cdot v_{piston,ext}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the extension velocity:

$$v_{piston,ext} = \frac{Q}{A_p}$$

**Step 3:** During **retraction**, the rod-side area is exposed. The flow enters this smaller area, so the piston retracts faster (for the same $Q$):

$$v_{piston,ret} = \frac{Q}{A_r}$$

**Step 4:** Since $A_r = A_p - A_{rod}$, we obtain $A_r < A_p$, confirming that $v_{piston,ret} > v_{piston,ext}$. This is a classic KTU Module 1 result.

**Step 5:** The **extension-retraction speed ratio** is:

$$\frac{v_{ret}}{v_{ext}} = \frac{A_p}{A_r} = \frac{A_p}{A_p - A_{rod}}$$

For example, if $A_{rod} = 0.5 A_p$, then $\frac{v_{ret}}{v_{ext}} = 2.0$, meaning the cylinder retracts twice as fast as it extends.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Continuity Equation Applied to Common Configurations

| Configuration | Continuity Equation | Key Insight |
|---------------|---------------------|-------------|
| Single pipe, variable diameter | $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | Velocity inversely proportional to area |
| Branching pipe (1 inlet, n outlets) | $Q_1 = \sum_{i=2}^{n+1} Q_i$ | Kirchhoff's-flow analog |
| Hydraulic cylinder extension | $Q = A_p \cdot v_{ext}$ | Piston speed set by flow and area |
| Hydraulic cylinder retraction | $Q = A_r \cdot v_{ret}$ | Faster than extension (since $A_r < A_p$) |
| Hydraulic motor | $Q = D_m \cdot N$ | $D_m$ = motor displacement, $N$ = rpm |
| Compressible gas in pipe | $\rho_1 A_1 v_1 = \rho_2 A_2 v_2$ | Density changes dominate |
| Pump-to-actuator line | $Q_{pump} = Q_{cylinder} + Q_{loss}$ | Losses usually small in steady state |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Flow Through a Variable Cross-Section Pipe

This diagram illustrates the application of the continuity equation as fluid traverses a converging-diverging section, showing the inverse velocity-area relationship.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["INLET SECTION 1\nLarge Area A1\nLow Velocity v1"] --> B1["CONVERGING ZONE\nArea decreases\nVelocity increases"]
    B1 --> C1["THROAT SECTION 2\nMinimum Area A2\nMaximum Velocity v2"]
    C1 --> D1["DIVERGING ZONE\nArea increases\nVelocity decreases"]
    D1 --> E1["OUTLET SECTION 3\nLarge Area A3\nLow Velocity v3"]

    F1["MASS FLOW CONSERVATION\nrho1 A1 v1 = rho2 A2 v2 = rho3 A3 v3"] -.governs.-> A1
    F1 -.governs.-> C1
    F1 -.governs.-> E1

    G1["INCOMPRESSIBLE SPECIAL CASE\nA1 v1 = A2 v2 = A3 v3 = Q"] -.applies when.-> F1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hydraulic Cylinder Speed Analysis

This diagram maps the continuity equation's role in determining extension versus retraction speeds of a double-acting hydraulic cylinder.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["HYDRAULIC PUMP\nDischarge Q m3 per s"] --> V1["DIRECTIONAL VALVE 4 BY 3"]
    V1 -->|Extension stroke| C1["CAP END\nFull Piston Area Ap"]
    V1 -->|Retraction stroke| C2["ROD END\nReduced Area Ar"]

    C1 --> M1["CONTINUITY EQUATION\nQ equals Ap times vext\nvext equals Q over Ap"]
    C2 --> M2["CONTINUITY EQUATION\nQ equals Ar times vret\nvret equals Q over Ar"]

    M1 --> R1["RESULT\nvret is greater than vext\nbecause Ar is less than Ap"]
    M2 --> R1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Logic for Choosing Continuity Form

This flowchart helps the student select the correct continuity form based on fluid type and flow conditions — a frequently tested KTU concept.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["START\nIdentify the fluid and flow condition"] --> Q1{"Is the fluid a liquid or gas?"}
    Q1 -->|Liquid Hydraulic| P1["USE INCOMPRESSIBLE FORM\nA1 v1 equals A2 v2\nQ constant"]
    Q1 -->|Gas Pneumatic| P2["USE COMPRESSIBLE FORM\nrho1 A1 v1 equals rho2 A2 v2\nmdot constant"]
    P1 --> P3["Check steady state assumption\nTime derivative equals zero"]
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4["Apply to control volume\nSolve for unknown velocity or area"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Schematic — Flow Distribution at a Branching Junction

This block diagram represents the node law analog for fluid flow, which is the direct application of continuity at hydraulic/pneumatic junctions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["INLET PIPE\nFlow Qin"] --> JN["JUNCTION NODE\nContinuity Node"]
    JN --> OUT1["BRANCH 1\nFlow Q1\nArea A1"]
    JN --> OUT2["BRANCH 2\nFlow Q2\nArea A2"]
    JN --> OUT3["BRANCH 3\nFlow Q3\nArea A3"]

    EQ["NODE EQUATION\nQin equals Q1 plus Q2 plus Q3\nSum of outflows equals inflow"] -.applies at.-> JN
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

State the continuity equation for a steady, incompressible fluid flow. Mention the physical principle on which it is based.

**Model Answer:**

The continuity equation for a steady, incompressible fluid flow is:

$$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = \text{constant} = Q$$

It is based on the **law of conservation of mass**, which states that mass can neither be created nor destroyed. For a steady flow with no accumulation within the control volume, the mass entering a section per unit time must equal the mass leaving another section per unit time. Since density is constant for incompressible flow, the volumetric flow rate $Q$ is conserved across all cross-sections.

---

**Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

A pipe of diameter 50 mm carries oil with a velocity of 2 m/s. The pipe diameter reduces to 25 mm at a constriction. Calculate the velocity of oil at the constriction.

**Model Answer:**

Given data:
- Inlet diameter: $D_1 = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Inlet velocity: $v_1 = 2 \text{ m/s}$
- Constriction diameter: $D_2 = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$

By the continuity equation:

$$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$$

$$v_2 = v_1 \cdot \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2 = 2 \cdot \left(\frac{50}{25}\right)^2 = 2 \cdot 4 = 8 \text{ m/s}$$

**The velocity at the constriction is 8 m/s.**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B-A. [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the continuity equation in its one-dimensional form for a steady, compressible fluid flow. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter of 80 mm and a rod diameter of 40 mm. The pump supplies oil at a flow rate of 30 LPM (liters per minute). Calculate the extension and retraction speeds of the piston, and the time required to complete a stroke of 300 mm. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady flow — properties at a point do not change with time.
2. One-dimensional flow — velocity is uniform across the cross-section and along the pipe axis.
3. The fluid follows a stream tube with well-defined inlet and outlet sections.
4. No mass is added or removed within the control volume (no sources or sinks).
5. The control surface is fixed in space.

**Derivation:**

Consider a control volume bounded by a stream tube with inlet section 1 and outlet section 2. The general conservation of mass equation is:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

Applying the **steady-state assumption**, the time derivative term becomes zero:

$$\int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

The surface integral splits into two contributions (inlet and outlet):

$$\rho_1 A_1 v_1 \cos\theta_1 + \rho_2 A_2 v_2 \cos\theta_2 = 0$$

For a 1D stream tube, velocities are parallel to normals, giving $\cos\theta_1 = -1$ (inlet, flow inward) and $\cos\theta_2 = +1$ (outlet, flow outward):

$$-\rho_1 A_1 v_1 + \rho_2 A_2 v_2 = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\rho_1 A_1 v_1 = \rho_2 A_2 v_2}$$

This is the **1D steady continuity equation for a compressible fluid.** For an incompressible fluid ($\rho_1 = \rho_2$), it reduces to $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given data:**
- Piston diameter: $D_p = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$
- Rod diameter: $D_r = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$
- Pump flow rate: $Q = 30 \text{ LPM} = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Stroke length: $L = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Compute the piston areas:**

Piston (cap-end) area:

$$A_p = \frac{\pi D_p^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.08)^2}{4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Rod-side area:

$$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_p^2 - D_r^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.08^2 - 0.04^2) = 3.7699 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Extension speed (full piston area exposed):**

$$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0995 \text{ m/s} \approx 0.0995 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_{ext} \approx 99.5 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 3 — Retraction speed (rod-side area exposed):**

$$v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_r} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7699 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \text{ m/s} \approx 0.1326 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_{ret} \approx 132.6 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 4 — Stroke times:**

Extension time:

$$t_{ext} = \frac{L}{v_{ext}} = \frac{300 \text{ mm}}{99.5 \text{ mm/s}} = 3.015 \text{ s} \approx 3.02 \text{ s}$$

Retraction time:

$$t_{ret} = \frac{L}{v_{ret}} = \frac{300 \text{ mm}}{132.6 \text{ mm/s}} = 2.262 \text{ s} \approx 2.26 \text{ s}$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Parameter | Extension | Retraction |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| Effective area | $5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$ | $3.7699 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$ |
| Speed | $0.0995 \text{ m/s}$ | $0.1326 \text{ m/s}$ |
| Stroke time | $3.02 \text{ s}$ | $2.26 \text{ s}$ |

---

#### **Question B-B. [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the three-dimensional differential form of the continuity equation. Starting from the integral form, derive the differential form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic system has a main supply line of 25 mm diameter that branches into two parallel lines of diameters 15 mm and 20 mm. If the velocity in the main line is 3 m/s, determine the velocity in each branch line, assuming steady, incompressible flow. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The three-dimensional differential form of the continuity equation expresses mass conservation at an infinitesimally small point in the flow field, rather than over a finite control volume. The result is:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$$

where $u$, $v$, and $w$ are the velocity components along the $x$, $y$, and $z$ directions, respectively. In compact vector notation:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

**Derivation from the integral form:**

We start with the integral form of mass conservation:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 1:** Apply the **Reynolds Transport Theorem** to move the time derivative inside the control volume (since the CV is fixed):

$$\int_{CV} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

**Step 2:** Convert the surface integral to a volume integral using the **divergence theorem** (Gauss's theorem):

$$\int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \, dV$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back into the conservation equation:

$$\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \right] dV = 0$$

**Step 4:** Since this equation must hold for **any** arbitrary control volume, the integrand itself must be zero:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0}$$

**Step 5 — For incompressible flow**, $\rho$ = constant, so $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$ and:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$

This is the simplest form, stating that the velocity field is **divergence-free** (solenoidal) for incompressible flow.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given data:**
- Main line diameter: $D_m = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- Branch 1 diameter: $D_1 = 15 \text{ mm} = 0.015 \text{ m}$
- Branch 2 diameter: $D_2 = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$
- Main line velocity: $v_m = 3 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 1 — Compute the cross-sectional areas:**

Main line area:

$$A_m = \frac{\pi D_m^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Branch 1 area:

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi D_1^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.015)^2}{4} = 1.7671 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

Branch 2 area:

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.020)^2}{4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the main line flow rate using the continuity equation:**

$$Q_m = A_m \cdot v_m = 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \times 3 = 1.4726 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the junction continuity (Kirchhoff's-flow law):**

At the branching node, the inflow must equal the sum of outflows:

$$Q_m = Q_1 + Q_2$$

$$A_m v_m = A_1 v_1 + A_2 v_2$$

**Step 4 — The problem requires an additional condition** (e.g., equal pressure drop, equal velocity, or specified flow split). In the absence of explicit information, we assume **equal velocity in both branches** (a common assumption for symmetric branching):

$$v_1 = v_2 = v_b$$

This gives:

$$A_m v_m = (A_1 + A_2) v_b$$

$$v_b = \frac{A_m v_m}{A_1 + A_2} = \frac{4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \times 3}{1.7671 \times 10^{-4} + 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$v_b = \frac{1.4726 \times 10^{-3}}{4.9087 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Alternative — If the flow splits in proportion to area** (equal pressure drop assumption), the branch velocities would both equal the main line velocity of 3 m/s. For a more general scenario, additional pressure-drop data is required.

**Summary of Results:**

| Section | Diameter (mm) | Area (m²) | Velocity (m/s) | Flow rate (m³/s) |
|---------|---------------|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| Main line | 25 | $4.909 \times 10^{-4}$ | 3.0 | $1.473 \times 10^{-3}$ |
| Branch 1 | 15 | $1.767 \times 10^{-4}$ | 3.0 | $5.301 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| Branch 2 | 20 | $3.142 \times 10^{-4}$ | 3.0 | $9.425 \times 10^{-4}$ |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** The most common mistake is failing to convert LPM to m³/s, or mm to m. Always use SI base units in intermediate steps.
> 2. **Forgetting the Rod-Side Area:** In cylinder problems, students often use $A_p$ for both extension and retraction. The retraction speed uses the **rod-side area** $A_r = A_p - A_{rod}$.
> 3. **Mixing Compressible and Incompressible Forms:** Using $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ for a pneumatic system is a serious error — the density term $\rho$ cannot be canceled for gases.
> 4. **Missing Assumptions in Derivations:** Full marks on a 7-mark derivation question require explicit listing of all assumptions (steady, 1D, incompressible, no sources/sinks). Omitting these typically costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Sign Convention Confusion:** In the integral form, the inlet contributes a *negative* term and the outlet a *positive* term. Reversing this sign yields an incorrect equation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Law:** The continuity equation is a direct consequence of the **conservation of mass** applied to a control volume.
- **Incompressible form** (hydraulics): $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = Q$ (constant).
- **Compressible form** (pneumatics): $\rho_1 A_1 v_1 = \rho_2 A_2 v_2 = \dot{m}$ (constant).
- **3D differential form:** $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$.
- **Velocity–Area Inverse Relationship:** $v$ decreases as $A$ increases for a constant $Q$.
