- **Diameter scaling:** For circular pipes, $v \propto D^{-2}$; halving the diameter quadruples the velocity.
- **Junction rule:** At any node, the sum of inflows equals the sum of outflows (Kirchhoff's current law analog).
- **Cylinder speeds:** $v_{ext} = Q / A_p$ and $v_{ret} = Q / A_r$, with $A_r < A_p$, so retraction is faster.
- **Motor speed:** $N = Q / D_m$, where $D_m$ is the motor displacement per revolution.
- **Standard assumptions** for Module 1 problems: steady flow, 1D flow, incompressible (for liquids), no sources/sinks, fixed control volume.
- **Unit vigilance:** Always convert LPM to m³/s, mm to m, and minutes to seconds before computation.
- **Common exam trap:** A "continuity equation" question in a hydraulic system expects the **incompressible** form; a similar question in a pneumatic context requires the **mass flow** form.
- **Derivation completeness:** Always state assumptions *before* the derivation, not after — examiners award marks for the logical setup.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conservation of Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT722 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conservation of Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Conservation of Energy** in fluid power systems is governed by the **First Law of Thermodynamics**, which states that energy can neither be created nor destroyed, but only transformed from one form to another. In a hydraulic system, the total mechanical energy (pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy) entering a control volume must equal the total mechanical energy leaving plus any energy converted into heat, sound, or other forms due to frictional and viscous losses.

Mathematically, the principle is expressed by the **steady-flow energy equation (SFEE)**:

$$h_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2} + g z_1 + q = h_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2} + g z_2 + w$$

where $h$ is specific enthalpy, $v$ is velocity, $g$ is gravitational acceleration, $z$ is elevation, $q$ is heat added, and $w$ is shaft work done per unit mass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** For incompressible hydraulic fluids (density $\rho$ = constant), the energy equation simplifies to **Bernoulli's equation** augmented with pump/turbine work and head-loss terms. This is the working equation for almost every hydraulic circuit problem in the KTU university examination.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a ball rolling down a smooth slope from rest. As it descends, it loses *height* (potential energy) but gains *speed* (kinetic energy). If you squeeze the slope at certain points, the ball loses some energy as heat from friction. The total energy you put in at the top is exactly equal to the energy that comes out at the bottom, plus all the heat lost in between.

A **hydraulic system behaves identically**:
- A **pump** acts like a person lifting that ball back up to the top — it adds energy.
- A **motor/cylinder** acts like the ball descending the slope — it extracts energy and does useful work.
- **Pipes, valves, and bends** act like the friction on the slope — they dissipate energy as heat.

So the energy "ledger" of any hydraulic circuit is simply:

$$\text{Energy In (Pump)} = \text{Energy Out (Motor/Cylinder)} + \text{Energy Lost (Friction, Heat, Sound)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants to Remember (Bold for KTU Board Exams):**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Density of standard hydraulic mineral oil: **ρ ≈ 870–900 kg/m³** (typically taken as **875 kg/m³** for ISO VG 32/46 oil)
> - Specific weight of hydraulic oil: **γ = ρg ≈ 8580 N/m³**
> - 1 bar = **10⁵ Pa = 10⁵ N/m²**

## 1.3 Why Conservation of Energy is the Backbone of Hydraulics

Every design decision in a hydraulic system — pump sizing, motor selection, pipe diameter, valve choice, reservoir capacity — flows from the energy balance. Without applying this principle, an engineer cannot:
- Predict the **power consumption** of a machine.
- Calculate the **heat generation** that the cooler must reject.
- Determine the **efficiency** of a transmission.
- Find the **force/speed** at the actuator for a given pump setting.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Energy Forms in a Hydraulic Fluid

At any point in a flowing hydraulic fluid, the specific energy (energy per unit weight of fluid) consists of three components:

| # | Energy Form | Symbol | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|-------------|--------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Pressure Energy (Flow Head) | $\frac{P}{\gamma}$ | $\frac{P}{\rho g}$ | Energy stored in fluid due to its pressure |
| 2 | Kinetic Energy (Velocity Head) | $\frac{v^2}{2g}$ | $\frac{v^2}{2g}$ | Energy due to fluid motion |
| 3 | Potential Energy (Elevation Head) | $z$ | $z$ | Energy due to height above datum |

These three are summed to give the **Total Head (H)**:

$$H = \frac{P}{\gamma} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + z$$

## 2.2 Bernoulli's Equation (Ideal Case — No Losses)

Between two points (1) and (2) along a streamline in an **ideal (inviscid, incompressible, steady) flow**:

$$\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\gamma} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

This is the classical statement of energy conservation in fluid mechanics.

## 2.3 Modified Energy Equation for Real Hydraulic Systems

In a real hydraulic circuit, we must add terms for **pump head added ($h_p$)**, **head loss ($h_L$)**, and any **useful work extracted by a motor/turbine ($h_m$)**. The complete steady-flow energy equation (S.F.E.E.) for a hydraulic circuit is:

$$\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + h_p = \frac{P_2}{\gamma} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L + h_m$$

Where:
- $h_p$ = head added by the pump (m)
- $h_L$ = total head loss due to friction, fittings, valves (m)
- $h_m$ = head extracted by the hydraulic motor/cylinder (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This single equation is the basis of every pipe-sizing, pump-selection, and system-efficiency problem in industrial hydraulics. KTU examiners routinely test this.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Sl. | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|-----|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $H = \frac{P}{\gamma} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + z$ | Total head at a point | m |
| 2 | $\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\gamma} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$ | Bernoulli's equation (no losses) | m |
| 3 | $h_f = \frac{4 f L v^2}{2 g d}$ | Darcy-Weisbach friction loss | m |
| 4 | $h_L = h_f + \sum h_{minor}$ | Total head loss = friction + minor losses | m |
| 5 | $P_{pump} = \rho g Q h_p$ | Pump power (hydraulic) | W |
| 6 | $P_{motor} = P Q_{motor} = T \omega$ | Motor hydraulic power / mechanical power | W |
| 7 | $\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{output,useful}}{P_{input}}$ | Overall efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $Q = A \cdot v$ | Continuity equation (conservation of mass) | m³/s |
| 9 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Cylinder force from pressure | N |
| 10 | $P_{hydraulic} = P \cdot Q$ | Hydraulic power transmitted | W |
| 11 | $\eta_{volumetric} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | Volumetric efficiency of pump/motor | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\eta_{mechanical} = \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{theoretical}}$ | Mechanical efficiency of pump/motor | dimensionless |
| 13 | $E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy of fluid mass | J |
| 14 | $E_{pressure} = P \cdot V$ | Pressure energy in a volume V | J |
| 15 | $\Delta P = \rho g h_L$ | Pressure drop from head loss | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder for Tables:** When writing absolute value expressions like $\vert v \vert^2$ in any table cell, **always** use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the pipe character `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table formatting.

## 2.5 Physical Interpretation of Each Term — "The Why"

- **Pressure head ($\frac{P}{\gamma}$):** Hydraulic oil at 70 bar has $\frac{P}{\gamma} = \frac{70 \times 10^5}{8580} \approx 816$ m of head. This enormous head is what enables hydraulics to generate massive forces from small flows.

- **Velocity head ($\frac{v^2}{2g}$):** Usually small (1–5 m) compared to pressure head. For oil at 2 m/s, $\frac{v^2}{2g} = \frac{4}{19.62} \approx 0.20$ m.

- **Elevation head (z):** Negligible for compact industrial machines (machine tool height < 3 m), but vital in large presses and ship hydraulics.

- **Friction head ($h_f$):** Grows with $L/d^5$ in the laminar regime, so **doubling pipe diameter reduces friction loss by 32 times** — a critical KTU design insight.

- **Pump head ($h_p$):** The energy "input" the pump must supply to overcome all downstream losses and deliver useful work.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation from Newton's Second Law

We will derive the classical energy conservation equation for an inviscid, incompressible, steady flow along a streamline.

### Step 1: Force balance on a fluid element

Consider a small fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ and length $ds$ moving along a streamline. The forces acting along the streamline are:
- Pressure force on the upstream face: $P \, dA$
- Pressure force on the downstream face: $(P + dP) \, dA$
- Gravity component along the streamline: $- \rho g \, dA \, ds \cdot \frac{dz}{ds}$

> Here, the negative sign indicates that gravity opposes the motion when the fluid moves upward.

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law

The net force on the element equals mass times acceleration along the streamline:

$$P \, dA - (P + dP) \, dA - \rho g \, dA \, ds \cdot \frac{dz}{ds} = \rho \, dA \, ds \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

### Step 3: Simplify and rearrange

Divide both sides by $\rho \, dA \, ds$:

$$- \frac{dP}{\rho \, ds} - g \frac{dz}{ds} = \frac{dv}{dt}$$

> Using the chain rule, $\frac{dv}{dt} = \frac{dv}{ds} \cdot \frac{ds}{dt} = v \frac{dv}{ds}$. Substituting:

$$- \frac{dP}{\rho} - g \, dz = v \, dv$$

> Now divide by $g$ and rearrange to isolate perfect differentials:

$$\frac{dP}{\rho g} + dz + \frac{v \, dv}{g} = 0$$

### Step 4: Recognize the exact differential form

Since $\rho$ is constant for an incompressible fluid:

$$d\left(\frac{P}{\rho g} + z + \frac{v^2}{2g}\right) = 0$$

### Step 5: Integrate between points 1 and 2

$$\int_1^2 d\left(\frac{P}{\rho g} + z + \frac{v^2}{2g}\right) = 0$$

$$\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + z_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\gamma} + z_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2g}$$

This is **Bernoulli's equation**, the cornerstone of hydraulic energy conservation. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Hydraulic Power Transmission Equation

### Step 1: Start from energy per unit time

Hydraulic power is the rate at which pressure energy is delivered by the fluid:

$$P_{hydraulic} = \text{(Force)} \times \text{(Velocity)}$$

### Step 2: Express force in terms of pressure

The force exerted by a pressurized fluid on a piston of area $A$ is:

$$F = P \cdot A$$

> Therefore, the force on a moving fluid column of area $A$ is also $F = P \cdot A$.

### Step 3: Express velocity from continuity

From the continuity equation (conservation of mass for incompressible flow):

$$Q = A \cdot v \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \frac{Q}{A}$$

### Step 4: Substitute into the power equation

$$P_{hydraulic} = F \cdot v = (P \cdot A) \cdot \left(\frac{Q}{A}\right) = P \cdot Q$$

Thus we obtain the **fundamental hydraulic power equation**:

$$P = P \cdot Q$$

where $P$ is in Pascals (Pa), $Q$ is in m³/s, and the result is in Watts (W).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trap:** Students often forget that $P \cdot Q$ gives the **hydraulic** power (the energy in the fluid), not the **shaft** power of the pump or motor. The shaft power is $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P \cdot Q}{\eta}$.

### Step 5: Express in practical engineering units

In industry, pressure is often given in **bar** and flow in **LPM (litres per minute)**. Converting:

$$P \,(\text{kW}) = \frac{P \,(\text{bar}) \times Q \,(\text{LPM})}{600}$$

Derivation of the conversion factor:

$$1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$$

$$1 \text{ LPM} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = \frac{1}{60000} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Therefore:

$$P \cdot Q = \left(10^5 \cdot P_{\text{bar}}\right) \times \left(\frac{Q_{\text{LPM}}}{60000}\right) = \frac{P_{\text{bar}} \cdot Q_{\text{LPM}}}{0.6} \text{ W} = \frac{P_{\text{bar}} \cdot Q_{\text{LPM}}}{600} \text{ kW}$$

---

## 3.3 Energy Balance Across a Pump–Motor Transmission

Consider a pump supplying oil to a hydraulic motor through a pipe. Apply the steady-flow energy equation between the pump outlet (1) and the motor inlet (2):

$$\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\gamma} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L + h_m$$

> Rearranging for the head delivered by the pump:

$$h_p = h_m + h_L + \left(\frac{P_2 - P_1}{\gamma}\right) + \left(\frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2g}\right) + (z_2 - z_1)$$

For the common industrial case where the elevation change is negligible and pipe diameters are equal at both ends (so $v_1 = v_2$):

$$h_p = h_m + h_L$$

> Multiplying by $\rho g Q$ to convert to power:

$$\rho g Q h_p = \rho g Q h_m + \rho g Q h_L$$

$$P_{pump,hydraulic} = P_{motor,hydraulic} + P_{loss}$$

This is the **energy conservation statement** for a pump–motor circuit: the hydraulic power delivered by the pump equals the hydraulic power absorbed by the motor plus the power dissipated as heat in the pipe.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A hydraulic pump delivers oil (ρ = 900 kg/m³) at a flow rate of 60 LPM against a system pressure of 120 bar. The pipe run has a total head loss of 8 m. The motor is located 2 m above the pump. Calculate: (a) the hydraulic power delivered, (b) the head added by the pump, and (c) the energy lost per minute.

### Solution:

**Given:**
- $\rho = 900$ kg/m³, $\gamma = 900 \times 9.81 = 8829$ N/m³
- $Q = 60$ LPM $= \dfrac{60 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 0.001$ m³/s
- $P = 120$ bar $= 120 \times 10^5 = 1.2 \times 10^7$ Pa
- $h_L = 8$ m
- $z_2 - z_1 = 2$ m
- Velocity heads neglected.

**Part (a): Hydraulic power**

$$P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = 1.2 \times 10^7 \times 0.001 = 12{,}000 \text{ W} = 12 \text{ kW}$$

**Part (b): Head added by the pump**

The pressure head at 120 bar:

$$\frac{P}{\gamma} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^7}{8829} = 1359.2 \text{ m}$$

Applying the energy equation (negligible velocities, $z_2 - z_1 = 2$ m):

$$h_p = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{\gamma} + (z_2 - z_1) + h_L$$

Since $P_1 = 0$ at the pump inlet (atmospheric reference):

$$h_p = 1359.2 + 2 + 8 = 1369.2 \text{ m}$$

**Part (c): Energy lost per minute**

Power lost to friction:

$$P_{loss} = \rho g Q h_L = 8829 \times 0.001 \times 8 = 70.6 \text{ W}$$

Energy lost per minute:

$$E_{loss} = P_{loss} \times 60 = 70.6 \times 60 = 4237 \text{ J/min} \approx 4.24 \text{ kJ/min}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Energy Flow in a Typical Hydraulic Circuit

| Component | Energy In | Energy Out | Energy Converted/Form |
|-----------|-----------|------------|-----------------------|
| Electric Motor | Electrical (kW) | Mechanical (shaft) | Electrical → Mechanical |
| Pump | Mechanical (shaft) | Hydraulic (P·Q) | Mechanical → Pressure/Kinetic |
| Pipe (Straight) | Hydraulic (Pressure) | Hydraulic (Pressure) | Pressure → Heat (friction) |
| Control Valve | Hydraulic (High P) | Hydraulic (Low P) | Pressure → Heat (throttling) |
| Actuator (Cylinder) | Hydraulic (P·Q) | Mechanical (F·v) | Pressure → Useful Work |
| Hydraulic Motor | Hydraulic (P·Q) | Mechanical (T·ω) | Pressure → Rotational Work |
| Tank/Reservoir | Hydraulic + Heat | Thermal dissipation | Hydraulic → Heat + Air-cooled |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Flow Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The following diagram shows the flow of energy through a typical open-loop hydraulic transmission, applying the conservation of energy at every node.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electrical Supply Mains] --> B[Electric Motor AC 3 phase]
    B --> C[Mechanical Shaft Power kW]
    C --> D[Hydraulic Pump Fixed Displacement]
    D --> E[Hydraulic Power P x Q]
    E --> F[Pressure Relief Valve Throttling]
    E --> G[Control Valve Directional DCV]
    F --> H[Heat Loss to Reservoir]
    G --> I[Hydraulic Actuator Cylinder Motor]
    I --> J[Mechanical Useful Work F x v or T x omega]
    I --> K[Pipe Friction Loss]
    K --> H
    D --> L[Volumetric Loss Internal Leakage]
    L --> H
    H --> M[Heat Exchanger Cooler]
    M --> N[Rejected to Atmosphere]

    style A fill:#ffe4b5
    style B fill:#87ceeb
    style C fill:#87ceeb
    style D fill:#90ee90
    style E fill:#ffff99
    style F fill:#ffb6c1
    style G fill:#ffb6c1
    style H fill:#ff6347
    style I fill:#dda0dd
    style J fill:#dda0dd
    style K fill:#ffb6c1
    style L fill:#ffb6c1
    style M fill:#ff6347
    style N fill:#d3d3d3
```

### Reading the Diagram:
- **Yellow node (E)** represents the hydraulic power form (P·Q).
- **Pink nodes (F, G, K, L)** are the energy dissipation/control points.
- **Red nodes (H, M, N)** are the final heat sink stages.
- The conservation law requires: **Input at A = Useful Output at J + All Heat at N**.

---

## 4.2 Energy Transformation Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1 Electrical Energy at Motor Terminals] -->|Step 1 eta EM| S2[Stage 2 Mechanical Shaft Energy of Motor]
    S2 -->|Step 2 eta V x eta M of Pump| S3[Stage 3 Hydraulic Energy in Fluid]
    S3 -->|Step 3 minus pipe losses| S4[Stage 4 Hydraulic Energy at Actuator Inlet]
    S4 -->|Step 4 eta M x eta V of Motor or Cylinder| S5[Stage 5 Mechanical Output Energy at Load]

    S1 -.Heat Loss EM.-> H1[Stage H1 Heat to Ambient]
    S2 -.Heat Loss Pump.-> H2[Stage H2 Heat to Reservoir]
    S3 -.Pipe Friction.-> H3[Stage H3 Heat to Pipe Wall]
    S4 -.Throttling.-> H4[Stage H4 Heat at Valve]
    S5 -.Load Friction.-> H5[Stage H5 Heat at Load]

    style S1 fill:#fff8dc
    style S2 fill:#e0ffff
    style S3 fill:#f0e68c
    style S4 fill:#f0e68c
    style S5 fill:#98fb98
    style H1 fill:#ffa07a
    style H2 fill:#ffa07a
    style H3 fill:#ffa07a
    style H4 fill:#ffa07a
    style H5 fill:#ffa07a
```

### Interpretation:
- Each arrow represents a transformation step with an associated efficiency.
- The horizontal flow shows useful energy progression; the downward arrows show the *entropy tax* — energy that escapes as heat.
- The overall efficiency is the product of all stage efficiencies:

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{EM} \cdot \eta_{pump,vol} \cdot \eta_{pump,mech} \cdot (1 - f_{pipe}) \cdot \eta_{act,vol} \cdot \eta_{act,mech}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This cascaded structure is exactly what examiners use to set 14-mark problems. You are often asked to compute the overall efficiency given the individual stage efficiencies and the load demand.

---

## 4.3 Conservation of Energy — Closed-Loop Hydrostatic Transmission

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Variable Displacement Pump] -->|High Pressure Line| M1[Reversible Hydraulic Motor]
    M1 -->|Low Pressure Return Line| P1
    P1 -->|Case Drain| T1[Reservoir Tank]
    M1 -->|Case Drain| T1
    T1 -->|Charge Pump| P1
    M1 -->|Output Shaft| LOAD[Mechanical Load]

    S[Energy Source Prime Mover] --> P1
    M1 --> OUT[Output Useful Power to Load]

    style P1 fill:#90ee90
    style M1 fill:#dda0dd
    style T1 fill:#add8e6
    style S fill:#ffe4b5
    style LOAD fill:#ffb6c1
    style OUT fill:#98fb98
```

In this closed-loop system, **energy is conserved** as follows:

$$P_{prime\,mover} = P_{load} + P_{case\,drain} + P_{charge\,pump} + P_{heat\,rejected}$$

> Note: The same reversible machine acts as a pump (when receiving power) and a motor (when delivering power) depending on the direction of energy flow. This is the fundamental principle behind hydrostatic transmissions in machine tools, construction equipment, and ship steering systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**State the principle of conservation of energy as applied to a hydraulic system and write the steady-flow energy equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The principle of conservation of energy, as applied to a hydraulic system, states that the energy supplied to the fluid by a pump must equal the energy delivered to the actuators plus all the energy lost due to friction, throttling, leakage, and other irreversibilities. For a steady, incompressible flow between section 1 (inlet) and section 2 (outlet), the **Steady-Flow Energy Equation (S.F.E.E.)** is:

$$\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + h_p = \frac{P_2}{\gamma} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L + h_m$$

where $h_p$ is the head added by the pump, $h_L$ is the head lost to friction, and $h_m$ is the head extracted by the motor/actuator. **(3 marks: 1 mark for statement + 2 marks for S.F.E.E.)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Distinguish between the three forms of mechanical energy present in a flowing hydraulic fluid.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three forms of mechanical energy per unit weight of fluid (i.e., the three "heads") are:

| Sl. | Form | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|-----|------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | Pressure Head | $\frac{P}{\gamma}$ | Energy stored in the fluid by virtue of its pressure |
| 2 | Velocity (Kinetic) Head | $\frac{v^2}{2g}$ | Energy due to the motion of the fluid |
| 3 | Elevation (Potential) Head | $z$ | Energy due to the height of the fluid above a reference datum |

The sum $\frac{P}{\gamma} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + z$ gives the **total head** at a point, which remains constant along a streamline in an ideal flow. **(3 marks: 1 mark per form)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** A hydraulic system uses a pump that delivers oil (specific weight 8800 N/m³) at a flow rate of 50 LPM. The system pressure is 100 bar. The pipe between the pump and the actuator has a frictional head loss of 5 m, and the actuator is located 3 m above the pump level. Neglecting the velocity heads, determine:
1. The hydraulic power transmitted.
2. The total head that the pump must supply.
3. The useful hydraulic power available at the actuator inlet.

**(b) [7 marks]** If the pump has a mechanical efficiency of 90% and a volumetric efficiency of 95%, while the actuator (cylinder) has a mechanical efficiency of 85% and a volumetric efficiency of 98%, calculate the overall efficiency of the system and the electrical input power required if the electric motor driving the pump has an efficiency of 92%.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Given:**
- $\gamma = 8800$ N/m³
- $Q = 50$ LPM $= \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s
- $P = 100$ bar $= 10^7$ Pa
- $h_L = 5$ m, $z_2 - z_1 = 3$ m
- $\eta_{pump,m} = 0.90$, $\eta_{pump,v} = 0.95$
- $\eta_{act,m} = 0.85$, $\eta_{act,v} = 0.98$
- $\eta_{motor,E} = 0.92$

#### Part (a) Solution

**1. Hydraulic power transmitted:**

$$P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q = 10^7 \times 8.333 \times 10^{-4} = 8333.3 \text{ W} \approx 8.33 \text{ kW}$$

**2. Total head that the pump must supply:**

Pressure head:

$$\frac{P}{\gamma} = \frac{10^7}{8800} = 1136.4 \text{ m}$$

Applying the S.F.E.E. with neglected velocity heads:

$$h_p = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{\gamma} + (z_2 - z_1) + h_L = 1136.4 + 3 + 5 = 1144.4 \text{ m}$$

**3. Useful hydraulic power at actuator inlet:**

The pressure at the actuator is reduced by the friction and elevation losses:

$$P_{act} = P - \rho g h_L = 10^7 - 8800 \times 5 = 10^7 - 44{,}000 = 9.956 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$P_{hyd,act} = P_{act} \cdot Q = 9.956 \times 10^6 \times 8.333 \times 10^{-4} = 8296.7 \text{ W} \approx 8.30 \text{ kW}$$

#### Part (b) Solution

**Overall efficiency:**

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{EM} \cdot \eta_{pump,m} \cdot \eta_{pump,v} \cdot \eta_{act,m} \cdot \eta_{act,v}$$

$$\eta_{overall} = 0.92 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.85 \times 0.98$$

Computing step by step:

$$0.92 \times 0.90 = 0.828$$

$$0.828 \times 0.95 = 0.7866$$

$$0.7866 \times 0.85 = 0.6686$$

$$0.6686 \times 0.98 = 0.6552$$

$$\eta_{overall} \approx 0.655 \text{ or } 65.5\%$$

**Electrical input power required:**

$$P_{electrical} = \frac{P_{hyd,act}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{8296.7}{0.6552} \approx 12{,}663 \text{ W} \approx 12.66 \text{ kW}$$

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Starting from Newton's second law applied to a fluid element along a streamline, derive Bernoulli's equation for the flow of an incompressible, inviscid fluid. State all assumptions clearly.

**(b) [7 marks]** A hydraulic press has a pump delivering 80 LPM of oil (ρ = 880 kg/m³) at 140 bar. The oil is supplied through a 20 mm diameter pipe of length 6 m (friction factor f = 0.025). The cylinder bore is 100 mm, and the load requires 50 kN to operate. Calculate:
1. The pressure available at the cylinder.
2. The cylinder force actually produced.
3. The efficiency of transmission.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady flow.
2. Incompressible fluid (ρ = constant).
3. Inviscid (frictionless) flow.
4. Flow along a streamline.
5. No shaft work (no pump or turbine) between the two points.

**Derivation:**

Consider a fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ and length $ds$ on a streamline inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. The pressure force on the upstream face is $P \, dA$, and on the downstream face is $(P + dP) \, dA$. The weight component along the streamline is $-\rho g \, dA \, ds \cdot \sin\theta = -\rho g \, dA \, dz$ (since $dz = ds \sin\theta$).

Applying Newton's second law along the streamline:

$$P \, dA - (P + dP) \, dA - \rho g \, dA \, dz = \rho \, dA \, ds \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

Simplify (the $P \, dA$ terms cancel):

$$-dP - \rho g \, dz = \rho \, ds \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

Use the chain rule $\frac{dv}{dt} = v \frac{dv}{ds}$:

$$-dP - \rho g \, dz = \rho v \, dv$$

Divide through by $\rho$:

$$-\frac{dP}{\rho} - g \, dz = v \, dv$$

For an incompressible fluid ($\rho$ = constant), divide by $g$ and rearrange:

$$\frac{dP}{\rho g} + dz + \frac{v \, dv}{g} = 0$$

This is an exact differential:

$$d\left(\frac{P}{\rho g} + z + \frac{v^2}{2g}\right) = 0$$

Integrating between points 1 and 2 along the streamline:

$$\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + z_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\gamma} + z_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} = \text{constant}$$

This is **Bernoulli's equation** for steady, incompressible, inviscid flow. **(7 marks: 1 mark for assumptions, 4 marks for step-by-step derivation, 2 marks for final form and statement.)**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $Q = 80$ LPM $= \frac{80 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 1.333 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s
- $\rho = 880$ kg/m³, $\gamma = 880 \times 9.81 = 8633$ N/m³
- $P_1 = 140$ bar $= 1.4 \times 10^7$ Pa
- Pipe: $d = 20$ mm $= 0.02$ m, $L = 6$ m, $f = 0.025$
- Cylinder bore $D = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m
- Load force $F_{load} = 50$ kN

**Step 1: Velocity in the pipe**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A_{pipe}} = \frac{1.333 \times 10^{-3}}{\frac{\pi}{4}(0.02)^2} = \frac{1.333 \times 10^{-3}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.244 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Friction head loss using Darcy-Weisbach**

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L v^2}{2 g d} = \frac{4 \times 0.025 \times 6 \times (4.244)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.02}$$

Numerator: $4 \times 0.025 \times 6 = 0.6$; $(4.244)^2 = 18.01$; product $= 10.81$

Denominator: $2 \times 9.81 \times 0.02 = 0.3924$

$$h_f = \frac{10.81}{0.3924} = 27.55 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Pressure drop due to friction**

$$\Delta P = \rho g h_f = 880 \times 9.81 \times 27.55 = 237{,}851 \text{ Pa} \approx 2.38 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 4: Pressure available at the cylinder**

$$P_2 = P_1 - \Delta P = 1.4 \times 10^7 - 2.378 \times 10^5 = 1.3762 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \approx 137.62 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 5: Cylinder force produced**

$$F = P_2 \cdot A_{cyl} = 1.3762 \times 10^7 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1)^2 = 1.3762 \times 10^7 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$F = 108{,}090 \text{ N} \approx 108.1 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Transmission efficiency**

The force available to do useful work is 108.1 kN, but only 50 kN is required. The ratio of useful to available gives transmission efficiency:

$$\eta_{transmission} = \frac{F_{load}}{F_{available}} = \frac{50}{108.1} = 0.4625 \text{ or } 46.25\%$$

> **Alternative interpretation:** If the efficiency is defined as the ratio of useful power output to input hydraulic power, then:

$$P_{input} = P_1 \cdot Q = 1.4 \times 10^7 \times 1.333 \times 10^{-3} = 18{,}662 \text{ W} = 18.66 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_{output,useful} = F_{load} \cdot v_{cyl}$$

Cylinder velocity:

$$v_{cyl} = \frac{Q}{A_{cyl}} = \frac{1.333 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1697 \text{ m/s}$$

$$P_{output,useful} = 50{,}000 \times 0.1697 = 8487 \text{ W} = 8.49 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta_{useful} = \frac{8.49}{18.66} = 0.4549 \text{ or } 45.5\%$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** The most common mistake is mixing up LPM with m³/s, or bar with Pa. Always convert to SI units first, then carry through the calculation. **One wrong unit = 2 marks lost.**
> 2. **Forgetting the elevation head:** Many students apply Bernoulli's equation without including the $z$ term even when the problem explicitly states an elevation difference. **Cost: 1–2 marks.**
> 3. **Ignoring friction loss in long-pipe problems:** Industrial hydraulic lines of 5–10 m are not frictionless. Use the Darcy-Weisbach equation. **Cost: 3 marks.**
> 4. **Confusing hydraulic power with shaft power:** $P \cdot Q$ is hydraulic power, not what the electric motor must supply. You must divide by all relevant efficiencies. **Cost: 2–3 marks.**
> 5. **Writing Bernoulli's equation without stating assumptions:** KTU examiners specifically ask for assumptions before the derivation. **Cost: 1 mark.**
> 6. **Sign convention errors:** Pressure drops in the direction of flow; energy added by a pump is *positive*; energy extracted by a motor is *positive* in the head-extracted sense. **Cost: 1–2 marks.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision — Conservation of Energy in Hydraulics**

- [x] **Core Principle:** Energy in = Energy out + Energy lost (heat, sound, leakage).
- [x] **Bernoulli's Equation:** $\frac{P_1}{\gamma} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\gamma} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$ — valid for ideal, steady, incompressible, inviscid flow.
- [x] **S.F.E.E. (Real Systems):** Add $h_p$ (pump head) and $h_L$ (losses) and $h_m$ (motor head) to Bernoulli's equation.
- [x] **Three Heads:** Pressure head ($\frac{P}{\gamma}$), Velocity head ($\frac{v^2}{2g}$), Elevation head ($z$).
- [x] **Hydraulic Power:** $P_{hyd} = P \cdot Q$ (with $P$ in Pa, $Q$ in m³/s, result in W).
- [x] **Practical Power Formula:** $P\,(\text{kW}) = \dfrac{P\,(\text{bar}) \times Q\,(\text{LPM})}{600}$.
- [x] **Friction Loss (Darcy-Weisbach):** $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L v^2}{2 g d}$ — dominant in long pipes.
- [x] **Continuity Equation:** $Q = A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ — conservation of mass for incompressible flow.
- [x] **Cylinder Force:** $F = P \cdot A = P \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$.
- [x] **Overall Efficiency:** $\eta_{overall} = \prod \eta_i$ = product of all stage efficiencies (motor, pump, actuator, etc.).
- [x] **Volumetric Efficiency:** $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ — accounts for internal leakage.
- [x] **Mechanical Efficiency:** $\eta_m = \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{theoretical}}$ — accounts for friction.
- [x] **Standard Values:** $\gamma_{oil} \approx 8580$ N/m³, $\rho_{oil} \approx 875$ kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s².
- [x] **Conversion Factors:** 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa, 1 LPM = $\frac{1}{60000}$ m³/s, 1 kW = 1000 W.
- [x] **Assumptions of Bernoulli's Equation:** Steady, incompressible, inviscid, along a streamline, no shaft work.
- [x] **Real Hydraulic Systems Always Have Losses:** Pipe friction, valve throttling, pump leakage, motor friction.
- [x] **Energy Conservation Across Components:**
  - Pump: Mechanical → Hydraulic
  - Motor: Hydraulic → Mechanical
  - Valve: Hydraulic → Heat
  - Pipe: Hydraulic → Heat (friction)
- [x] **Bernoulli ≠ Conservation of Energy with Losses:** The full S.F.E.E. is the working equation for real systems.
- [x] **Hydraulic Press / Machine Tool Example:** Conservation of energy gives $F_1 v_1 = F_2 v_2$ (Pascal's law) only for ideal systems; real systems have $\eta < 1$.
- [x] **Heat Generation Rate:** $P_{heat} = P_{in} - P_{out,useful}$ — must be removed by the cooler.
- [x] **Dimensionless Check:** All "head" terms must be in metres; all "power" terms must be in Watts or kW.

---

**End of Module 1 — Conservation of Energy Notes**

topics.title: Frictional Losses in Hydraulic Pipelines - Laminar and Turbulent Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT722 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Frictional Losses in Hydraulic Pipelines - Laminar and Turbulent Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Frictional Losses in Pipelines

In the context of industrial hydraulics, **frictional losses** refer to the irreversible conversion of mechanical energy (pressure energy) into thermal energy due to viscous resistance offered by the fluid against the inner wall of the pipe during flow. This energy loss manifests as a **drop in pressure** (or equivalent head) along the direction of flow, even in a straight, constant-diameter pipe with no fittings, valves, or sudden area changes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Frictional loss is the head loss that occurs due to the viscous shear stresses developed between adjacent fluid layers and between the fluid and the pipe wall, quantified by the Darcy–Weisbach equation, and is fundamentally dependent on the flow regime (laminar or turbulent) characterized by the Reynolds Number.*

Mathematically, the pressure drop due to friction in a horizontal pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$ carrying a fluid of density $\rho$ at mean velocity $v$ is given by:

$$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

where $f$ is the **Darcy friction factor** (a dimensionless quantity), and the term $\frac{\rho v^2}{2}$ is the dynamic pressure of the flow.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Simple Intuitive View

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Crowd in a Corridor**
> Imagine a large crowd of people trying to walk through a long, narrow corridor. If they walk slowly and in an organized, single-file manner (laminar flow), they barely brush against the walls and the energy required is small. But if the same crowd starts rushing, pushing, and swirling chaotically (turbulent flow), the friction against the walls intensifies dramatically and people lose energy bumping into walls and each other. The pipe acts as the corridor; the fluid acts as the crowd; the friction against the pipe wall is the crowd's friction against the corridor walls. The faster and more chaotic the flow, the greater the energy loss.

**Key Physical Constants to Remember:**
- **Acceleration due to gravity** ($g$): **$9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$**
- **Density of water at $4^\circ \text{C}$** ($\rho_w$): **$1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$**
- **Dynamic viscosity of water at $20^\circ \text{C}$** ($\mu_w$): **$1.002 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$**
- **Kinematic viscosity of water at $20^\circ \text{C}$** ($\nu_w$): **$1.004 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$**

## 1.3 Classification of Flow Regimes — Reynolds Number

Osborne Reynolds (1883) demonstrated that the nature of fluid flow in a pipe depends on a dimensionless ratio now called the **Reynolds Number ($Re$)**:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}$$

| Flow Regime | Reynolds Number Range | Physical Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar Flow** | $Re < 2300$ | Smooth, layered, parallel streamlines; no mixing between layers |
| **Transitional Flow** | $2300 \le Re \le 4000$ | Unstable; fluctuates between laminar and turbulent |
| **Turbulent Flow** | $Re > 4000$ | Chaotic, with eddies and cross-currents; vigorous mixing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:**
> The number **2300** is the *critical Reynolds number* for pipe flow. Students must explicitly state $Re_{cr} = 2300$ for flow in circular pipes (for non-circular ducts, it is different and based on hydraulic diameter).

## 1.4 Why Frictional Loss Matters in Industrial Hydraulics

- Determines the **pump power requirement** in hydraulic power packs.
- Governs the **heat generation** in hydraulic circuits (energy is converted to heat).
- Influences the **selection of pipe diameter** to minimize losses.
- Critical for **long pipelines** in process plants, oil & gas, water supply networks, and hydraulic presses.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Reynolds Number — Theoretical Foundation

The Reynolds number physically represents the ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces** in the flow:

$$Re = \frac{\text{Inertial Forces}}{\text{Viscous Forces}} = \frac{\rho v^2 D^2}{\mu v D} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$$

- **Low $Re$ (laminar):** Viscous forces dominate → flow is orderly.
- **High $Re$ (turbulent):** Inertial forces dominate → flow is chaotic.

## 2.2 Shear Stress Distribution in a Pipe

For fully developed laminar flow in a circular pipe, a force balance on a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $dx$ gives the classic parabolic velocity distribution (Hagen–Poiseuille flow):

$$\tau = -\mu \frac{dv}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P}{2 L} \cdot r$$

The shear stress varies **linearly** from zero at the pipe centerline ($r=0$) to a maximum $\tau_w$ at the pipe wall ($r = D/2$).

## 2.3 Friction Factor — The Heart of the Problem

The friction factor is the bridge between geometry, fluid properties, and flow regime. KTU tests love these relationships:

| Flow Regime | Darcy Friction Factor $f$ | Fanning Friction Factor $f_{Fan}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar** ($Re < 2300$) | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | $f_{Fan} = \dfrac{16}{Re}$ |
| **Turbulent** ($Re > 4000$) | Read from **Moody Chart** | $f_{Fan} = f / 4$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** KTU examiners frequently use the Fanning friction factor in problems, and students mistakenly apply the Darcy formula. Always check: $f_{Darcy} = 4 \times f_{Fanning}$. Also, some textbooks (e.g., in chemical engineering) define $f$ based on $4 \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$ instead of $\frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{v D}{\nu}$ | Flow regime identification | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\Delta P_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Darcy–Weisbach pressure drop | Pa |
| 3 | $h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | Head loss due to friction | m |
| 4 | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ (Laminar only) | Friction factor in laminar regime | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P D^4}{128 \mu L}$ | Hagen–Poiseuille volumetric flow | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 6 | $v_{avg} = \dfrac{\Delta P \, D^2}{32 \mu L}$ | Mean velocity in laminar pipe flow | m/s |
| 7 | $v_{max} = 2 \, v_{avg}$ (Laminar) | Centreline-max velocity relation | m/s |
| 8 | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \, D}{4 L}$ | Wall shear stress (for any regime) | Pa |
| 9 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 10 | Moody / Colebrook: $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}}\right)$ | Turbulent friction factor (implicit) | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $D_h = \dfrac{4A}{P_{wet}}$ | Hydraulic diameter (non-circular ducts) | m |
| 12 | $\Delta P = 0.5 \, f \, L \, \rho \, v^2 / D$ | Compact form for repeated use | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> $\varepsilon$ in the Colebrook equation is the **absolute roughness** of the pipe (in metres), a property of the pipe material (e.g., commercial steel $\varepsilon \approx 0.045 \, \text{mm}$).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pump and compressor selection:** Knowing the exact frictional loss dictates the required pump head.
- **Hydraulic circuit design:** The OHT (oil hydraulic trainer) and pneumatic trainer labs in OEMRT722 require students to compute line losses to predict actuator response time.
- **Fire-fighting networks, district cooling, and oil pipelines:** Engineers use these equations to design pipe diameters that balance cost vs. pumping energy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Number (Conceptual)

Consider a fluid element of characteristic length $L_c$ moving with velocity $v$. The inertial force scales as $F_I \sim \rho v^2 L_c^2$. The viscous (frictional) force scales as $F_V \sim \mu \dfrac{v}{L_c} L_c^2 = \mu v L_c$. Their ratio gives the dimensionless Reynolds number:

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{F_I}{F_V} \\[4pt]
   &= \frac{\rho v^2 L_c^2}{\mu v L_c} \\[4pt]
   &= \frac{\rho v L_c}{\mu}
\end{aligned}$$

For pipe flow, the characteristic length is the pipe diameter $D$, hence $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Hagen–Poiseuille Equation (Laminar Flow)

This derivation is a **KTU board favourite** for 7-mark sub-questions. We derive the velocity profile and the pressure-drop relation by applying Newton's second law to a cylindrical fluid element.

**Step 1: Control Volume Setup**

Consider a horizontal pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$. Fully developed, steady, incompressible laminar flow. Apply a force balance on a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ ($r \le R$) and length $dx$.

The forces acting on the element in the flow ($x$) direction:
- Pressure force on left face: $P \cdot \pi r^2$
- Pressure force on right face: $(P + dP) \cdot \pi r^2$
- Shear force on the curved surface: $\tau \cdot (2\pi r \, dx)$

**Step 2: Force Balance (Steady State ⇒ $\Sigma F_x = 0$)**

$$\begin{aligned}
P \cdot \pi r^2 - (P + dP) \cdot \pi r^2 - \tau \cdot 2\pi r \, dx &= 0 \\[4pt]
-dP \cdot \pi r^2 &= \tau \cdot 2\pi r \, dx \\[4pt]
\tau &= -\frac{r}{2} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}
\end{aligned}$$

Since pressure drops in the flow direction, $dP/dx$ is negative, and $\tau$ is positive (acts in the negative $x$ direction on the fluid).

**Step 3: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity**

For laminar flow, shear stress is proportional to the velocity gradient:

$$\tau = -\mu \frac{dv}{dr}$$

**Step 4: Equate and Integrate**

$$\begin{aligned}
-\mu \frac{dv}{dr} &= -\frac{r}{2} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx} \\[4pt]
\frac{dv}{dr} &= \frac{1}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx} \cdot r \\[4pt]
\int_{v}^{0} dv &= \frac{1}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx} \int_{r}^{R} r \, dr
\end{aligned}$$

(We use boundary conditions: at $r = R$, $v = 0$ (no-slip), and at $r = r$, $v = v(r)$.)

$$\begin{aligned}
-v(r) &= \frac{1}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx} \left[\frac{R^2 - r^2}{2}\right] \\[4pt]
v(r) &= -\frac{1}{4\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx} \left(R^2 - r^2\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\frac{dP}{dx} = -\frac{\Delta P}{L}$ (negative), define $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ as the pressure drop over length $L$. Then:

$$v(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} \left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

This is the **parabolic velocity profile** (max at center, zero at wall).

**Step 5: Maximum and Mean Velocities**

Maximum velocity (at $r = 0$):

$$v_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{4\mu L} = \frac{\Delta P \, D^2}{16 \mu L}$$

Mean (average) velocity, obtained by integrating across the cross-section:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_{avg} &= \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_{0}^{R} v(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_{0}^{R} \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L} \left(R^2 - r^2\right) 2\pi r \, dr \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L R^2} \int_{0}^{R} \left(R^2 r - r^3\right) dr \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L R^2} \left[\frac{R^2 \cdot R^2}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4}\right] \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L R^2} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{8\mu L} = \frac{\Delta P \, D^2}{32\mu L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Establish the Fundamental Laminar Relation**

Comparing the two:

$$v_{max} = 2 \, v_{avg} \quad \text{(Laminar flow only)}$$

**Step 7: Derive the Volumetric Flow Rate (Hagen–Poiseuille Equation)**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= v_{avg} \cdot A = v_{avg} \cdot \pi R^2 \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{\pi R^2 \cdot \Delta P \, R^2}{8\mu L} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{\pi \, \Delta P \, R^4}{8\mu L} = \frac{\pi \, \Delta P \, D^4}{128 \, \mu L}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** — directly testable in 7-mark questions.

## 3.3 Derivation of Darcy Friction Factor for Laminar Flow

Starting from the Hagen–Poiseuille equation:

$$\Delta P = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$$

Express $Q$ in terms of velocity: $Q = v \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$. Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= \frac{128 \mu L}{\pi D^4} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2 v}{4} \\[4pt]
         &= \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^2}
\end{aligned}$$

Now compare this to the Darcy–Weisbach form $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} &= \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^2} \\[4pt]
f \cdot \frac{\rho v}{2} &= \frac{32 \mu}{D} \\[4pt]
f &= \frac{64 \, \mu}{\rho v D} = \frac{64}{Re}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Result:** $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ is **valid only for laminar flow**. For turbulent flow, $f$ is obtained from the Moody chart or the Colebrook equation.

## 3.4 The Colebrook Equation (Turbulent Flow)

For turbulent flow, the friction factor is given implicitly by the Colebrook–White equation (valid for the entire turbulent range):

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left( \frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right)$$

This is implicit in $f$, so it requires iterative (Newton–Raphson) or trial-and-error solution. KTU problems often use the explicit Swamee–Jain approximation:

$$f = \frac{0.25}{\left[ \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}} \right) \right]^2}$$

for the range $10^{-6} \le \varepsilon/D \le 10^{-2}$ and $5000 \le Re \le 10^8$.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example for Reference (Not Exam Question — see Section 5 for actual KTU pattern):**
> Oil with $\mu = 0.1 \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $\rho = 900 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ flows through a horizontal steel pipe of $D = 50 \, \text{mm}$, $L = 100 \, \text{m}$, at $v = 0.5 \, \text{m/s}$.
>
> (a) Calculate the Reynolds number and identify the flow regime.
> (b) Determine the friction factor.
> (c) Compute the pressure drop.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{900 \times 0.5 \times 0.05}{0.1} = 225
\end{aligned}$$

Since $Re = 225 < 2300$, the flow is **laminar**.

$$\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{64}{Re} = \frac{64}{225} \approx 0.2844
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_f &= f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} \\[4pt]
           &= 0.2844 \times \frac{100}{0.05} \times \frac{900 \times (0.5)^2}{2} \\[4pt]
           &= 0.2844 \times 2000 \times 112.5 \\[4pt]
           &= 63{,}990 \, \text{Pa} \approx 64 \, \text{kPa}
\end{aligned}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Flow Regime Decision Tree

This diagram maps the logic a KTU student should follow when given a pipe-flow problem to determine friction loss.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given Pipe Flow Problem] --> B[Compute Re = rho v D / mu]
    B --> C{Re less than 2300?}
    C -- YES --> D[LAMINAR FLOW]
    D --> E[f = 64 over Re]
    E --> F[Use Hagen Poiseuille: Q = pi dP D4 over 128 mu L]
    C -- NO --> G{Re between 2300 and 4000?}
    G -- YES --> H[TRANSITIONAL FLOW]
    H --> I[Use either laminar or turbulent design value as per KTU]
    G -- NO --> J[TURBULENT FLOW]
    J --> K{Relative Roughness epsilon over D known?}
    K -- YES --> L[Use Colebrook equation iteratively]
    K -- NO --> M[Use Blasius for smooth pipes or Swamee Jain]
    L --> N[Compute f from Moody Chart]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Apply Darcy Weisbach: dP = f L rho v2 over 2D]
    F --> P[Compute Pressure Drop or Head Loss]
    O --> P
    I --> P
    P --> Q[End: Report Result in Pa or metres of fluid]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Pipeline Loss Analysis

This diagram shows the interaction between physical components, fluid properties, and the analytical framework used in industrial hydraulics.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        A1[Pipe Geometry L, D, epsilon]
        A2[Fluid Properties rho, mu]
        A3[Flow Conditions Q or v]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[COMPUTATIONAL LAYER]
        B1[Reynolds Number Re Calculator]
        B2[Friction Factor Module f]
        B3[Pressure Drop Engine dP]
    end

    subgraph REGIMES[FLOW REGIMES]
        C1[LAMINAR: f = 64 over Re]
        C2[TRANSITIONAL: Design Safety Factor]
        C3[TURBULENT: Moody Chart and Colebrook]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT METRICS]
        D1[Head Loss hf in metres]
        D2[Pressure Drop dP in Pa]
        D3[Pump Power P = rho g Q hf]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B1 -->|Classify| C1
    B1 -->|Classify| C2
    B1 -->|Classify| C3
    C1 --> B2
    C2 --> B2
    C3 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    A2 --> B3
    A3 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> D1
    B3 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D2 --> D3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Velocity Profile Comparison

This diagram visually contrasts the velocity distribution in laminar and turbulent pipe flow (using raw text labels to comply with Mermaid safety rules).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pipe Cross Section] --> B[Laminar Flow]
    A --> C[Turbulent Flow]
    B --> B1[Parabolic Profile: v max at center]
    B --> B2[v max equals 2 v avg]
    C --> C1[Flatter Profile due to eddies]
    C --> C2[v max roughly 1.2 v avg]
    B1 --> D1[Use Hagen Poiseuille Equations]
    C1 --> D2[Use Darcy Weisbach with Moodys Chart]
```
