# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]**
**Define Reynolds Number and explain its significance in classifying fluid flow in pipes. State the critical Reynolds number for flow in a circular pipe.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Reynolds Number ($Re$)** is a dimensionless quantity that represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow. For flow in a circular pipe of diameter $D$ with mean velocity $v$, fluid density $\rho$, and dynamic viscosity $\mu$:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}$$

**Significance:**
- It characterises whether the flow is **laminar** (streamlines parallel, no mixing) or **turbulent** (chaotic, with eddies and cross-currents).
- It is the key parameter governing the **friction factor** and hence the **head loss** in pipe flow.
- It enables scale-up of laboratory results to full-scale prototypes (dynamic similarity).

**Critical Reynolds Number:** For flow in a smooth circular pipe, $Re_{cr} = 2300$. Below this, flow is laminar; above 4000, flow is turbulent; between 2300 and 4000, flow is transitional.

> *(Examiner note: Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for significance, 1 mark for critical value with regimes.)*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]**
**What is the Darcy–Weisbach equation? Write down the expression for head loss due to friction in a pipe.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Darcy–Weisbach equation** is the fundamental formula for computing pressure drop (or head loss) due to friction in a fully developed pipe flow. It is given by:

$$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

The equivalent **head loss** form is:

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g}$$

where $f$ is the Darcy friction factor (dimensionless), $L$ is pipe length, $D$ is pipe internal diameter, $v$ is mean velocity, $g$ is acceleration due to gravity, and $\rho$ is fluid density.

The equation is **valid for all flow regimes**, with $f$ being a function of $Re$ and relative roughness $\varepsilon/D$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Choice-Based Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** for steady, laminar, incompressible flow through a horizontal circular pipe. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Oil of density $880 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ and dynamic viscosity $0.12 \; \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ flows through a horizontal pipe of $40 \; \text{mm}$ diameter at a mean velocity of $0.6 \; \text{m/s}$. Calculate:
- (i) The Reynolds number and flow regime.
- (ii) The friction factor using the Darcy–Weisbach relation.
- (iii) The pressure drop over a $50 \; \text{m}$ length of the pipe. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Hagen–Poiseuille Derivation

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible, laminar flow.
2. Newtonian fluid (constant viscosity).
3. Horizontal pipe (gravity does not act along the flow).
4. Fully developed flow (velocity profile is constant along $x$).
5. No-slip at the wall ($v = 0$ at $r = R$).
6. Axisymmetric flow (no swirl).

**Derivation:**

Consider a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $dx$ inside a horizontal pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$. Apply a force balance in the flow direction ($x$):

Pressure force (left face) − Pressure force (right face) − Shear force (curved surface) = 0 (steady state).

$$\begin{aligned}
P \cdot \pi r^2 - (P + dP) \cdot \pi r^2 - \tau \cdot 2\pi r \, dx &= 0 \\[4pt]
-dP \cdot \pi r^2 &= \tau \cdot 2\pi r \, dx \\[4pt]
\tau &= -\frac{r}{2} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}
\end{aligned}$$

For laminar flow, Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = -\mu \dfrac{dv}{dr}$. Equating:

$$\begin{aligned}
-\mu \frac{dv}{dr} &= -\frac{r}{2} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx} \\[4pt]
\frac{dv}{dr} &= \frac{r}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}
\end{aligned}$$

Integrate with boundary conditions $v = 0$ at $r = R$:

$$v(r) = \frac{1}{4\mu} \cdot \left(-\frac{dP}{dx}\right) \cdot \left(R^2 - r^2\right) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

Maximum velocity at $r = 0$:

$$v_{max} = \frac{\Delta P \, R^2}{4\mu L} = \frac{\Delta P \, D^2}{16\mu L}$$

Mean velocity (averaging over the cross-section):

$$v_{avg} = \frac{\Delta P \, D^2}{32\mu L}$$

Hence, $v_{max} = 2 \, v_{avg}$ — characteristic of laminar pipe flow.

**Volumetric flow rate (Hagen–Poiseuille equation):**

$$Q = v_{avg} \cdot A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta P \, D^2}{32\mu L} = \frac{\pi \Delta P D^4}{128\mu L}$$

This is the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation**.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $\rho = 880 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.12 \; \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $D = 40 \; \text{mm} = 0.04 \; \text{m}$, $v = 0.6 \; \text{m/s}$, $L = 50 \; \text{m}$.

**(i) Reynolds Number:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{880 \times 0.6 \times 0.04}{0.12} \\[4pt]
   &= \frac{21.12}{0.12} = 176
\end{aligned}$$

Since $Re = 176 < 2300$, the flow is **LAMINAR**.

**(ii) Friction Factor:**

$$f = \frac{64}{Re} = \frac{64}{176} = 0.3636$$

**(iii) Pressure Drop:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_f &= f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} \\[4pt]
           &= 0.3636 \times \frac{50}{0.04} \times \frac{880 \times (0.6)^2}{2} \\[4pt]
           &= 0.3636 \times 1250 \times 158.4 \\[4pt]
           &= 71{,}992.8 \; \text{Pa} \approx 72 \; \text{kPa}
\end{aligned}$$

---

### **Question B: [Alternative Choice — 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the **friction factor $f$** and the **Reynolds number $Re$** for laminar flow through a circular pipe, starting from the Darcy–Weisbach equation and Hagen–Poiseuille flow. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Water at $20^\circ \text{C}$ ($\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$) flows through a commercial steel pipe ($D = 100 \; \text{mm}$, $\varepsilon = 0.045 \; \text{mm}$, $L = 200 \; \text{m}$) at a discharge of $15 \; \text{L/s}$. Calculate:
- (i) The Reynolds number and flow regime.
- (ii) The Darcy friction factor using the Swamee–Jain equation.
- (iii) The head loss in metres of water. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of $f = 64/Re$

Start with the Hagen–Poiseuille pressure drop:

$$\Delta P = \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^2}$$

The Darcy–Weisbach equation:

$$\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

Equating:

$$\begin{aligned}
f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} &= \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^2} \\[4pt]
f \cdot \frac{\rho v}{2} &= \frac{32 \mu}{D} \\[4pt]
f &= \frac{64 \mu}{\rho v D} = \frac{64}{Re}
\end{aligned}$$

> *(7 marks: 2 marks for setup, 3 marks for algebra, 2 marks for final boxed result.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $D = 0.1 \; \text{m}$, $\varepsilon = 0.045 \times 10^{-3} = 4.5 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}$, $L = 200 \; \text{m}$, $Q = 15 \; \text{L/s} = 0.015 \; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

**(i) Reynolds Number:**

Mean velocity:

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.015}{\pi (0.1)^2 / 4} = \frac{0.015}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.910 \; \text{m/s}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 1.910 \times 0.1}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} \\[4pt]
   &= 1.91 \times 10^{5}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $Re \gg 4000$, the flow is **TURBULENT**.

Relative roughness:

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.1} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**(ii) Darcy Friction Factor (Swamee–Jain):**

$$\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{0.25}{\left[ \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}} \right) \right]^2} \\[4pt]
  &= \frac{0.25}{\left[ \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{(1.91 \times 10^{5})^{0.9}} \right) \right]^2}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute each term:

$$\frac{4.5 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} = 1.2162 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$Re^{0.9} = (1.91 \times 10^{5})^{0.9} \approx 5.18 \times 10^{4}$$

$$\frac{5.74}{5.18 \times 10^{4}} = 1.108 \times 10^{-4}$$

Sum inside the logarithm:

$$1.2162 \times 10^{-4} + 1.108 \times 10^{-4} = 2.324 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\log_{10}(2.324 \times 10^{-4}) = -3.6336$$

$$f = \frac{0.25}{(-3.6336)^2} = \frac{0.25}{13.20} = 0.01894$$

**(iii) Head Loss:**

$$\begin{aligned}
h_f &= f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.01894 \times \frac{200}{0.1} \times \frac{(1.910)^2}{2 \times 9.81} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.01894 \times 2000 \times \frac{3.648}{19.62} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.01894 \times 2000 \times 0.1860 \\[4pt]
    &= 7.05 \; \text{metres of water}
\end{aligned}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** $D$ must be in **metres**, $L$ in **metres**, $\mu$ in **Pa·s**, and $Q$ in **m³/s**. Mixing mm and m is the #1 cause of wrong answers.
> 2. **Using $f = 64/Re$ for turbulent flow:** This formula is **ONLY for laminar**. In turbulent flow, use the Moody chart or Swamee–Jain.
> 3. **Confusing Darcy and Fanning friction factors:** Darcy $f = 4 \times$ Fanning $f$. The Darcy equation is $\Delta P_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ (no factor of 4 in the numerator).
> 4. **Sign error in $dP/dx$:** Pressure decreases in the flow direction. If your final $\Delta P$ comes out negative, you have a sign convention error.
> 5. **Missing the velocity profile question:** Examiners love asking "show that $v_{max} = 2 v_{avg}$" — practice the integral carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Reynolds Number:** $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{v D}{\nu}$ — dimensionless ratio of inertial to viscous forces.
- **Critical $Re = 2300$** for circular pipes; **$Re < 2300$** = laminar, **$Re > 4000$** = turbulent.
- **Darcy–Weisbach equation** (universal): $\Delta P_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ and $h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$.
- **Laminar friction factor:** $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ (derived from Hagen–Poiseuille, **not applicable in turbulent flow**).
- **Hagen–Poiseuille equation:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P D^4}{128 \mu L}$ — valid **only for laminar flow**.
- **Mean velocity in laminar flow:** $v_{avg} = \dfrac{\Delta P D^2}{32 \mu L}$; maximum velocity $v_{max} = 2 v_{avg}$ (laminar only).
- **Wall shear stress** (any regime): $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \, D}{4 L}$.
- **Turbulent friction factor:** Obtained from the **Moody chart** or the **Colebrook equation** (implicit) or **Swamee–Jain equation** (explicit).
- **Colebrook equation:** $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right)$.
- **Swamee–Jain explicit form:** $f = \dfrac{0.25}{\left[ \log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{5.74}{Re^{0.9}} \right) \right]^2}$ for turbulent flow.
- **Darcy vs. Fanning:** $f_{Darcy} = 4 \times f_{Fanning}$. KTU predominantly uses Darcy.
- **Head loss vs. pressure loss:** $h_f = \dfrac{\Delta P_f}{\rho g}$ — both represent the same physical quantity in different units.
- **Hydraulic diameter** (non-circular ducts): $D_h = \dfrac{4 A}{P_{wet}}$ — used in place of $D$ in all formulae.
- **Kinematic viscosity:** $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ — in SI units $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.
- **Key assumption in derivations:** Steady, incompressible, fully developed, Newtonian fluid, no-slip at wall.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fluid Power Symbols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMRT722 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Fluid Power Symbols ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fluid Power Symbols** are standardized graphical notations (governed primarily by **ISO 1219-1:2012** for functional symbols and **ISO 1219-2:2012** for circuit diagrams) used to represent hydraulic and pneumatic components, lines, and energy flow in schematic diagrams. They allow engineers worldwide to read, draw, and troubleshoot fluid power circuits without ambiguity, regardless of language. In KTU parlance, these symbols are *the alphabet* of any hydraulic or pneumatic circuit. A complete fluid power system consists of four functional blocks — **Energy Supply, Energy Distribution, Energy Control, and Energy Transformation** — and every block is represented by a unique, simplified geometric symbol. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **road map**. Roads are drawn as black lines, rivers as blue, cities as red dots. Anyone — English, Hindi, Malayalam speaker — instantly understands the map. Fluid power symbols are exactly this: the *universal road map of oil and air circuits*. - A **pump** is like a power plant (energy source). - A **valve** is like a traffic signal (energy controller). - A **cylinder/motor** is like the destination (energy consumer that does work). - A **filter** is like an air purifier (conditioning). - **Pipelines** are the highways connecting them. > [!NOTE] > **Key Exam Insight (KTU 2024):** The examiner expects students to *name the symbol AND draw it*. Memorize the geometric shape (square, diamond, circle, triangle) and the internal lines (arrows, blocked paths, dots) that distinguish one valve from another. ### 1.3 ISO 1219 — The Governing Standard | Standard Code | Scope | Mandatory In KTU? | |---|---|---| | **ISO 1219-1** | Functional symbols & conventions | **Yes** | | **ISO 1219-2** | Symbol sequences & circuit drawing rules | **Yes** | | **ANSI/Y32.10** | Older American equivalent (visually similar) | Reference only | ### 1.4 General Drawing Conventions - **Square envelope** = hydraulic component (filled / detailed inside). - **Diamond envelope** = pneumatic component. - **Solid line** = main working line (high pressure oil/air). - **Dashed line** (long dashes) = pilot / control line. - **Dotted line** (short dots) = drain / leakage / exhaust line. - **Two long dashes with a dot** = flexible hose. - **Arrows** = direction of energy flow. - **Triangles** = energy conversion point (pump/motor symbol base). - **Number of squares** stacked = number of positions of a valve. - **Number of arrows/envelopes** = number of flow paths / ports. > [!TIP] > **Pressure / Flow Notation in Schematics:** > - Working pressure is denoted in **bar** (SI unit) — typical industrial range **10 – 350 bar** for hydraulics, **2 – 10 bar** for pneumatics. > - Flow rate in **L/min** (litres per minute). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Fluid Power Symbols Fluid power symbols can be classified into **six primary functional families**. Each family has distinct geometric DNA which the KTU examiner loves to test. #### 2.1.1 Energy Supply Symbols (Source) - **Hydraulic Pump** → Solid **circle** with a **solid black triangle** (filled) pointing outward + a **drive shaft line**. - **Pneumatic Compressor** → Same circle, but with a **cross-hatch / multi-vane** pattern inside to indicate gas. - **Hydraulic Reservoir** → Open-top rectangle (tank) with a vent line, sometimes shaded to indicate fluid level. #### 2.1.2 Energy Transformation Symbols (Actuators) - **Single-Acting Cylinder** → Rectangle with one rod (single-line protrusion) and a vent port on the cap end. - **Double-Acting Cylinder** → Rectangle with a rod on BOTH sides (or one side + capped end). Two ports — A (cap) and B (rod). - **Hydraulic Motor** → Circle with a hollow (unfilled) triangle pointing inward + drive shaft. - **Limited Rotation Actuator (Oscillator)** → Curved arrow showing angular sweep, typically <360°. #### 2.1.3 Energy Control Symbols — Directional Control Valves (DCV) This is the **highest-weightage** topic in Module 1. - **2/2 Valve** → 2 squares, 2 ports, 2 positions. - **3/2 Valve** → 3 squares, 3 ports (P, A, T), 2 positions. - **4/2 Valve** → 4 squares, 4 ports (P, T, A, B), 2 positions. - **4/3 Valve** → 4 squares, 4 ports, 3 positions (middle position critical — *tandem centre, closed centre, open centre, float centre*). - **5/2 and 5/3 Valves** → Pneumatic equivalents (extra exhaust port). > [!NOTE] > **Inside-the-square conventions:** > - **Arrow** → flow allowed in that direction. > - **T-junction (T)** → port blocked (closed). > - **Two perpendicular lines crossed** → ports connected (cross-over). > - **Blank square** → blocked in that position. > - **Dots on both sides of arrows** → both directions connected. #### 2.1.4 Pressure Control Valves - **Pressure Relief Valve** → Square with horizontal arrow + diagonal arrow pointing up + adjustable spring symbol (zig-zag) + vent line. - **Pressure Reducing Valve** → Same as relief but with diagonal arrow pointing **down** (to lower downstream pressure). - **Sequence Valve** → Identical to relief but with the **vent blocked** (drain internally). #### 2.1.5 Flow Control Valves - **Throttle Valve (Fixed Orifice)** → Curved bow-tie / hourglass shape. - **Needle Valve** → Triangle with arrow head. - **Flow Control Valve (One-Way)** → Bow-tie + check valve in parallel (allows free return). - **Pressure Compensated Flow Control** → Bow-tie enclosed in a square with pressure compensation spring. #### 2.1.6 Conditioning & Auxiliary Symbols - **Filter** → Dashed/slotted rectangle or a triangle pointing into a line. - **Pressure Gauge** → Circle with a "V" needle (clock face) marked "P". - **Accumulator** → Circle divided by a wavy line (gas pre-charge on top, fluid on bottom). - **Heat Exchanger / Cooler** → Rectangle with zig-zag inside (water-cooled) or with fan symbol (air-cooled). - **Check Valve** → Disc/ball in a V-seated against a line (allows one-way flow only). ### 2.2 Line & Connection Conventions | Line Type | Visual | Meaning | |---|---|---| | ——— (solid, thick) | Continuous heavy line | Main supply / working line | | - - - (long dash) | Dashed line | Pilot / control line | | ······ (short dot) | Dotted line | Drain / exhaust / leakage line | | —·—·— | Dash-dot | Envelope boundary, flexible hose | | ⚫ (filled dot) | Bullet joint | Lines connected (crossing with dot) | | ⌒ (bridge) | Hop-over | Lines crossing but not connected | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Notation Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Since this topic is **standardization-based**, numerical derivations are minimal. However, the following notations are tested in KTU 2024 numerical/short-answer questions: $$Q \;=\; A \cdot v$$ where $Q$ = flow rate (m³/s or L/min), $A$ = piston area (m²), $v$ = piston velocity (m/s). $$P \;=\; \dfrac{F}{A}$$ where $P$ = pressure (Pa or bar), $F$ = force (N), $A$ = area (m²). $$N_{\text{ports}} \;/\; N_{\text{positions}} \;\;\rightarrow\;\; \text{valve designation}$$ > Example: $4/3$ DCV $\Rightarrow$ **4 ports, 3 positions** (KTU favourite: closed-centre, tandem-centre, float-centre). | Symbol Family | Geometric DNA | Energy State | Portage | |---|---|---|---| | Pump | Circle + solid triangle OUT | Active source | 1 in / 1 out | | Motor | Circle + hollow triangle IN | Active sink | 1 in / 1 out | | DCV (general) | Stacked squares + arrows/T's | Passive controller | 2–5 | | Pressure valve | Square + spring + vent | Passive controller | 2–3 | | Check valve | Ball in V-seat | Passive one-way | 2 | | Cylinder | Rectangle + rod | Active sink | 2 (DA), 1 (SA) | | Accumulator | Circle + wavy line | Storage | 1–2 | | Filter | Triangle on line | Passive conditioner | 2 | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These symbols are the **engineering DNA** of: - **Injection moulding machines** (clamping cylinder circuits). - **Earth-moving equipment** (JCB, excavator boom-bucket circuits). - **Aerospace landing gear** (hydraulic actuation with accumulators). - **CNC machine tools** (hydraulic clamping, lubrication). - **Food & pharma packaging** (pneumatic pick-and-place). - **Automotive braking systems** (hydraulic master-slave cylinder). A maintenance engineer can service a JCB in Kerala or a Boeing in Seattle using the **same symbol sheet**. That is the power of ISO 1219. ## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix — Decoding the Symbol DNA Since this topic is **standardization-based** rather than derivation-heavy, this section provides an **exhaustive analytical matrix** that walks through the logical decoding of every symbol family a KTU examiner can ask. Each row is broken into the same five analytical steps. ### 3.1 Methodology — How to "Read" Any Fluid Power Symbol **Step 1 — Identify the Envelope (Outer Shape)** - Circle → Rotary device (pump / motor / compressor). - Square → Valve or linear damper. - Diamond → Pneumatic component. - Rectangle → Cylinder / reservoir. - Triangle → Energy conversion point or filter element. **Step 2 — Read the Internal Markings** - Arrows → direction of flow. - T-bars / blank → blocked. - Crossed T's → connected. - Triangle direction → pump (outward filled) vs motor (inward hollow). - Spring symbol (zig-zag) → adjustable pressure. **Step 3 — Count Ports** - Count line connections entering/leaving the envelope. - 1 port = simple component (accumulator). - 2 ports = simple valves (check, flow). - 3 ports = 3-way valve (3/2 DCV). - 4 ports = 4-way valve (4/2 or 4/3 DCV). - 5 ports = 5-way pneumatic valve (5/2, 5/3). **Step 4 — Count Positions** - Count stacked squares (each square = one valve position). - 2 squares = 2 positions. - 3 squares = 3 positions. **Step 5 — Form the Designation** $$\text{Designation} \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{ports}}}{N_{\text{positions}}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Master Analytical Matrix (Step-by-Step Decoding) | Component | Visual ASCII Hint | Step 1: Envelope | Step 2: Internals | Step 3: Ports | Step 4: Positions | Step 5: Designation | Engineering Function | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **Hydraulic Pump** | `(▶─●)` | Circle | Solid black triangle OUT, drive shaft | 2 (in, out) | N/A | Fixed displacement (or variable) | Converts mechanical → hydraulic energy | | **Hydraulic Motor** | `(─●◀)` | Circle | Hollow triangle IN, drive shaft | 2 | N/A | Fixed/variable | Converts hydraulic → mechanical energy | | **2/2 DCV (NC)** | `[—\|—] / [—◀—]` | 2 squares | One position blank (closed), one with arrow | 2 | 2 | **2/2** | On/off block | | **3/2 DCV (Spring Return)** | `[—◀—\|—] / [—\|—◀—]` | 3 squares | Spring on one end, arrow in other | 3 (P,A,T) | 2 | **3/2** | Single-acting cylinder control | | **4/2 DCV** | `[P→A, B→T] / [P→B, A→T]` | 4 squares | Two arrows per square | 4 (P,T,A,B) | 2 | **4/2** | Double-acting cylinder control | | **4/3 DCV (Closed Centre)** | `[flow] / [X] / [cross-flow]` | 4 squares | Middle square = all ports blocked | 4 | 3 | **4/3 Closed** | Stops cylinder & locks fluid | | **4/3 DCV (Tandem Centre)** | `[flow] / [P→T] / [cross]` | 4 squares | Middle = P→T bleed, A,B blocked | 4 | 3 | **4/3 Tandem** | Decompression at zero load | | **4/3 DCV (Open Centre)** | `[flow] / [P→T,A→T,B→T] / [cross]` | 4 squares | Middle = P→T, A→T, B→T all open | 4 | 3 | **4/3 Open** | Free flow to tank when neutral | | **4/3 DCV (Float Centre)** | `[flow] / [P→T, A&B open to each other] / [cross]` | 4 squares | Middle = P→T, A-B connected | 4 | 3 | **4/3 Float** | Cylinder free-floats with load | | **Pressure Relief Valve** | `[→↑ spring]` | Square | Arrow up, spring, vent | 2 (in, vent) | N/A | Relief | Caps system pressure at set value | | **Pressure Reducing Valve** | `[→↓ spring]` | Square | Arrow down, spring | 2 | N/A | Reducing | Maintains downstream pressure | | **Sequence Valve** | `[→↑ spring (no vent)]` | Square | Arrow up, spring, **drain blocked** | 2 | N/A | Sequence | Opens when set pressure reached | | **Check Valve** | `(◀\|)` | Line symbol | Ball in V, arrow | 2 | N/A | Check | Free flow one way, blocks reverse | | **Flow Control (Throttle)** | `(><)` | Bow-tie | Two triangles tip-to-tip | 2 | N/A | Throttle | Restricts flow rate | | **Single-Acting Cylinder** | `[█|═]` | Rectangle | One rod, one vent | 1 (active) | N/A | SA | Push-only (spring returns) | | **Double-Acting Cylinder** | `[█═|═█]` | Rectangle | Two rod ends, two ports | 2 (A,B) | N/A | DA | Push and pull | | **Accumulator** | `(◔~◕)` | Circle | Wavy line dividing | 1–2 | N/A | Accumulator | Stores pressurized fluid/gas | | **Filter** | `>(` on line | Triangle | Pointing into line | 2 | N/A | Filter | Removes contaminants | | **Pressure Gauge** | `(P)` | Circle | Clock face with "P" | 1 (tap) | N/A | Gauge | Pressure measurement | | **Reservoir (Tank)** | `[▒▒▒]` | Open rectangle | Fluid level shading | 1–2 | N/A | Tank | Fluid storage & cooling | --- ### 3.3 Worked Decoding Example (Detailed Walkthrough) **Question:** Identify the symbol whose description is — *stacked squares, three of them, with the middle square showing a horizontal line connecting P to T while A and B are blocked, actuated by solenoids on both ends.* **Solution (step-by-step):** - **Step 1 — Envelope:** Stacked squares → DCV. - **Step 2 — Internal:** Middle square = P→T, A & B blocked. - **Step 3 — Ports:** 4 line connections → **4 ports**. - **Step 4 — Positions:** 3 squares → **3 positions**. - **Step 5 — Designation:** $4/3$. - **Actuation:** Solenoid on both ends → **solenoid-actuated, spring-centred**. - **Middle position logic:** P→T (pump unloaded), A & B blocked (cylinder locked) → this is the **TANDEM CENTRE** configuration. $$\boxed{\text{Symbol} = 4/3 \text{ DCV, Solenoid Actuated, Spring Centred, Tandem Centre}}$$ --- ### 3.4 Pressure Drop & Flow Energy Equation (Auxiliary Numerical Foundation) Although symbol-drawing does not require derivations, KTU 2024 often pairs a symbol question with a numerical on the same component. The governing relation is **Bernoulli-extended for hydraulic lines**: $$P_1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 + \rho g h_1 \;=\; P_2 + \dfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 + \rho g h_2 + \Delta P_{\text{loss}}$$ where: - $P_1, P_2$ = upstream / downstream pressure (Pa). - $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³) — for mineral oil ≈ **860 kg/m³**. - $v_1, v_2$ = line velocities (m/s). - $g$ = 9.81 m/s². - $h_1, h_2$ = elevation (m). - $\Delta P_{\text{loss}}$ = loss across valve / filter / fitting (Pa). For a horizontal pipe of constant cross-section ($h_1 = h_2$, $v_1 = v_2$): $$P_1 - P_2 \;=\; \Delta P_{\text{loss}}$$ This $\Delta P_{\text{loss}}$ is precisely what **flow control, pressure control, and filter symbols** are designed to manage in a circuit. Each symbol on a circuit diagram corresponds to a *localised* $\Delta P_{\text{loss}}$ value. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > Fluid power symbols are inherently geometric (squares, triangles, circles, arrows). Since physical schematic drawing cannot be performed in text, the diagrams below use Mermaid to render the **functional architecture, classification hierarchy, and signal-flow logic** of a typical fluid power circuit. ### 4.1 Master Classification Hierarchy of Fluid Power Symbols ```mermaid graph TD A[FLUID POWER SYMBOLS
ISO 1219] A --> B1[ENERGY SUPPLY] A --> B2[ENERGY TRANSFORMATION] A --> B3[ENERGY CONTROL] A --> B4[CONDITIONING and AUXILIARY] A --> B5[LINES and CONNECTIONS] B1 --> C1[Hydraulic Pump
Circle plus Filled Triangle OUT] B1 --> C2[Pneumatic Compressor
Circle plus Cross Hatch] B1 --> C3[Reservoir
Open Rectangle with Shading] B2 --> D1[Single Acting Cylinder
Rectangle plus One Rod] B2 --> D2[Double Acting Cylinder
Rectangle plus Two Rods] B2 --> D3[Hydraulic Motor
Circle plus Hollow Triangle IN] B2 --> D4[Limited Rotation Actuator
Curved Sweep Arrow] B3 --> E1[Directional Control Valves
Stacked Squares] B3 --> E2[Pressure Control Valves
Square plus Spring plus Vent] B3 --> E3[Flow Control Valves
Bow Tie or Needle] B3 --> E4[Check Valves
Ball in V Seat] B4 --> F1[Filter
Triangle on Line] B4 --> F2[Accumulator
Circle plus Wavy Divider] B4 --> F3[Heat Exchanger
Rectangle plus Zig Zag] B4 --> F4[Pressure Gauge
Circle with Clock Face] B4 --> F5[Temperature Sensor
Circle with T mark] B5 --> G1[Solid Line
Main Working Line] B5 --> G2[Long Dash Line
Pilot or Control Line] B5 --> G3[Short Dot Line
Drain or Exhaust] B5 --> G4[Dash Dot Line
Envelope Boundary] B5 --> G5[Bullet Joint
Lines Connected] B5 --> G6[Bridge Hop
Lines Cross Not Connected] E1 --> E1a[2 by 2 NC NO] E1 --> E1b[3 by 2 Spring Return] E1 --> E1c[4 by 2 Solenoid Actuated] E1 --> E1d[4 by 3 Three Position] E1 --> E1e[5 by 2 and 5 by 3 Pneumatic] E2 --> E2a[Relief Valve
Arrow Up] E2 --> E2b[Reducing Valve
Arrow Down] E2 --> E2c[Sequence Valve
Drain Blocked] E2 --> E2d[Counterbalance Valve
Pilot Operated] E3 --> E3a[Fixed Orifice Throttle] E3 --> E3b[Needle Valve] E3 --> E3c[One Way Flow Control] E3 --> E3d[Pressure Compensated] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical Hydraulic Circuit ```mermaid graph LR SUPPLY[ENERGY SUPPLY
Electric Motor plus Pump] TANK[RESERVOIR
Fluid Storage and Cooling] FILTER[FILTER
Contaminant Removal] DCV[DIRECTIONAL CONTROL VALVE
4 by 3 DCV] RELIEF[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE
System Safety] FLOWC[FLOW CONTROL VALVE
Speed Regulation] ACTUATOR[DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER
Energy Transformation] ACCUM[ACCUMULATOR
Energy Storage] GAUGE[PRESSURE GAUGE
Monitoring] SUPPLY -->|High Pressure Oil| DCV TANK -->|Suction Line| SUPPLY SUPPLY -->|Excess Pressure| RELIEF RELIEF -->|Bypass| TANK DCV -->|Metered Flow| FLOWC FLOWC -->|Controlled Flow| ACTUATOR ACTUATOR -->|Return| DCV DCV -->|Exhaust| TANK ACCUM -->|Pulse Damping| SUPPLY GAUGE -->|Reads| DCV FILTER -->|Clean Supply| SUPPLY ``` ### 4.3 Decoding Logic Flow — How to Identify Any Unknown Symbol ```mermaid graph TD START[UNKNOWN SYMBOL] START --> Q1{What is the Envelope Shape?} Q1 -->|Circle| A1[Rotary Device] Q1 -->|Square| A2[Valve] Q1 -->|Rectangle| A3[Cylinder or Tank] Q1 -->|Diamond| A4[Pneumatic Component] Q1 -->|Triangle| A5[Filter or Energy Point] A1 --> Q2{Triangle Filled or Hollow?} Q2 -->|Filled OUT| R1[PUMP - Source] Q2 -->|Hollow IN| R2[MOTOR - Sink] Q2 -->|Cross Hatch| R3[COMPRESSOR] A2 --> Q3{How Many Stacked Squares?} Q3 -->|1| R4[Simple Valve] Q3 -->|2| R5[2 Position DCV] Q3 -->|3| R6[3 Position DCV] R5 --> Q5{How Many Ports?} Q5 -->|2| S1[2 by 2 Valve] Q5 -->|3| S2[3 by 2 Valve] Q5 -->|4| S3[4 by 2 Valve] Q5 -->|5| S4[5 by 2 Pneumatic Valve] R6 --> Q6{How Many Ports?} Q6 -->|4| T1[4 by 3 Valve] Q6 -->|5| T2[5 by 3 Pneumatic Valve] A3 --> Q7{Piston Rods Present?} Q7 -->|One Rod| U1[Single Acting Cylinder] Q7 -->|Two Rods| U2[Double Acting Cylinder] Q7 -->|No Rod Open Top| U3[Reservoir] A5 --> Q8{On a Line or Standalone?} Q8 -->|On Line| V1[Filter] Q8 -->|Standalone Triangle| V2[Energy Conversion Point] ``` ### 4.4 Signal-Flow Diagram of a 4/3 DCV in Tandem Centre Mode ```mermaid graph TD P[P - Pressure Inlet] --> POS1 T[T - Tank Return] --> POS1 A[Port A - Cap End] --> POS1 B[Port B - Rod End] --> POS1 POS1[Position 1 Left
P to A and B to T] --> ACT1[Extend Cylinder] POS2[Position 2 Middle
Tandem Centre
P to T and A B Blocked] --> ACT2[Cylinder Locked
Pump Unloaded] POS3[Position 3 Right
P to B and A to T] --> ACT3[Retract Cylinder] ACT1 --> SPRING[Spring Centred] ACT3 --> SPRING SPRING --> POS2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Mermaid Diagrams:** Each rectangular block represents a *symbol* or *symbol-state* on a real ISO 1219 schematic. The arrows represent the *working lines* and *flow direction*. While Mermaid cannot render the precise geometric shape of a hydraulic symbol, the architecture, logic, and classification are preserved exactly as in a textbook. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List the three basic geometric envelopes used in ISO 1219-1 fluid power symbols and state what type of component each represents.* **Model Answer:** | Envelope Shape | Component Type | |---|---| | **Circle** | Rotary devices (pump, motor, compressor, gauge) | | **Square** | Valves (directional, pressure, flow control) | | **Rectangle** | Linear actuators (cylinders) and reservoirs | | **Diamond** (additional) | Pneumatic components specifically | **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** - Envelope-shape to component-type mapping — **2 marks** - Mention of ISO 1219 standard — **1 mark** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Distinguish between a hydraulic pump symbol and a hydraulic motor symbol as per ISO 1219-1.* **Model Answer:** | Feature | Pump | Motor | |---|---|---| | Envelope | Circle | Circle | | Triangle | **Solid (filled), pointing OUTWARD** | **Hollow (unfilled), pointing INWARD** | | Energy flow | Mechanical → Hydraulic | Hydraulic → Mechanical | | Drive shaft | Input shaft (driven) | Output shaft (drives load) | **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** - Geometric difference (filled vs hollow triangle, direction) — **2 marks** - Energy conversion direction — **1 mark** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]** > > **(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the constructional symbols of the following components as per ISO 1219-1: >     (i) Single-acting cylinder   (ii) Double-acting cylinder   (iii) Hydraulic motor   (iv) Accumulator. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Differentiate clearly between a 3/2 DCV and a 4/2 DCV in terms of symbol construction, port labelling, and typical application. Sketch both symbols. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **(i) Single-Acting Cylinder (2 marks):** - Drawn as a **rectangle** with a single piston rod protruding from one end. - **One active port** at the cap end for pressurized oil entry. - **Vent / exhaust port** at the rod end (or rod-end is open to atmosphere). - Internal **piston** is shown as a solid vertical line dividing the rectangle. - **Return action** is via external spring load or gravitational force. - *Application:* Clamping, lifting low-load jigs, brake actuation. **(ii) Double-Acting Cylinder (2 marks):** - Drawn as a **rectangle** with a piston rod on **both ends** (or capped rod end + extending rod end). - **Two active ports** labelled **A** (cap end) and **B** (rod end). - Piston and rod are shown as solid lines. - Force is applied in **both directions** by pressurizing the alternate end. - *Application:* Most industrial cylinders — presses, machine tools, injection moulding. **(iii) Hydraulic Motor (1.5 marks):** - Envelope is a **circle**. - Inside the circle is a **hollow (unfilled) triangle** pointing **inward**, indicating flow enters the motor and exits as mechanical rotation. - A **drive shaft** extends from the circle's periphery to the load. - *Application:* Winches, conveyors, crane slewing, fan drives. **(iv) Accumulator (1.5 marks):** - Envelope is a **circle** divided by a **wavy line** (representing gas-fluid interface or bladder/diaphragm). - **Gas pre-charge** at the top, **hydraulic fluid** at the bottom (or vice versa for bladder type). - One or two ports depending on type (bladder / piston / diaphragm). - *Application:* Emergency power backup, pulsation damping, energy storage in regenerative circuits. **Model Solution (b) — 3/2 DCV vs 4/2 DCV:** | Parameter | 3/2 DCV | 4/2 DCV | |---|---|---| | **Number of stacked squares** | 3 | 4 | | **Number of positions** | 2 | 2 | | **Number of ports** | 3 (P, A, T) | 4 (P, T, A, B) | | **Port labels** | Pressure, Cylinder, Tank | Pressure, Tank, Cap end, Rod end | | **Internal arrow pattern** | In position 1: P→A; In position 2: A→T (spring return) | In pos 1: P→A, B→T; In pos 2: P→B, A→T (spring centred or detented) | | **Actuation** | Spring / push-button / solenoid | Solenoid / hydraulic pilot / manual lever | | **Application** | Control of **single-acting** cylinder (extend only) | Control of **double-acting** cylinder (extend + retract) | | **Symbol complexity** | Simpler (one flow path) | More complex (two independent flow paths) | **Mark Distribution (b) — 7 marks:** - Sketch of 3/2 DCV — **2 marks** - Sketch of 4/2 DCV — **2 marks** - Tabular distinction (port labels, applications) — **3 marks** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1 — Alternative]** > > **(a)** Draw the ISO 1219-1 symbols for the following pressure control valves and explain their working principle with one industrial application each: >     (i) Pressure relief valve   (ii) Pressure reducing valve   (iii) Sequence valve   (iv) Counterbalance valve. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Sketch and describe the four possible centre conditions of a **4/3 directional control valve**, indicating the symbol differences and typical application of each. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **(i) Pressure Relief Valve (2 marks):** - Symbol: **Square** with a **horizontal arrow** (inlet) and a **diagonal arrow pointing UP** (outlet to tank), with a **spring** (zig-zag) symbol pressing on the valve spool. - **Working:** When system pressure exceeds spring setting, spool lifts, oil flows to tank, pressure caps at setting. - **Application:** Set at 10–15% above working pressure as safety device on every hydraulic power unit. **(ii) Pressure Reducing Valve (2 marks):** - Symbol: Same square envelope, but the **diagonal arrow points DOWN** (downstream side is reduced). - **Working:** Maintains downstream pressure **below** the inlet pressure by throttling when downstream exceeds set value. - **Application:** Low-pressure lubrication circuit fed from a high-pressure main system. **(iii) Sequence Valve (1.5 marks):** - Symbol: Identical to relief valve **except the drain line is internal (no external vent arrow)**. - **Working:** Opens only when set pressure is reached, allowing a second circuit to energize after first. - **Application:* Clamp-then-feed sequence in press machines, two-stage clamping. **(iv) Counterbalance Valve (1.5 marks):** - Symbol: Relief valve symbol **plus a pilot line** tapping the inlet, with a separate drain. - **Working:** Holds a vertical cylinder against gravity; opens only when pilot pressure overcomes spring. - **Application:** Crane booms, lift platforms — prevents load from falling uncontrollably. **Model Solution (b) — Four Centre Conditions of 4/3 DCV:** | Centre Type | Middle Square Symbol | Behaviour | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---| | **Tandem Centre** | `P→T`, A & B blocked | Pump unloads to tank; cylinder ports locked | Energy-saving circuits with intermittent cylinder use | | **Closed Centre** | All 4 ports blocked | Pump pressure builds; cylinder fully locked | Multi-cylinder circuits needing holding | | **Open Centre** | P→T, A→T, B→T (all connected) | Pump unloads; cylinder can drift | Circuits with vertical loads needing controlled drift | | **Float Centre** | P→T, A & B connected to each other (and to T) | Cylinder free to move with load; pump unloads | Float position in crane / lift circuits | **Mark Distribution (b) — 7 marks:** - Sketch of 4 centre conditions (one mark each) — **4 marks** - Application + explanation — **3 marks** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Fluid Power Symbols):** > 1. **Filled vs Hollow Triangle Confusion:** Students frequently draw a hollow triangle on a pump or a filled triangle on a motor. This is an **instant 1–2 mark loss** in Part A and a **3-mark loss** in Part B. > 2. **Port Counting Error:** Writing "4/2" instead of "4/3" (or vice versa) — verify by *counting stacked squares and line connections*, not from memory. > 3. **Line-Type Mix-up:** Drawing a pilot line (dashed) as a solid line — examiners specifically check line conventions in circuit diagrams. > 4. **Missing Vent / Drain:** The pressure relief valve symbol **must have an external vent arrow**; the sequence valve must **not**. This distinction alone is worth 2 marks in Part B. > 5. **No Sketches:** In Part B, **"sketch" is mandatory**. A textual description alone scores at most 50% of the marks for symbol questions. > 6. **Forgetting to Label Ports:** Always label **P, T, A, B** on directional valves. A correct symbol without port labels loses at least **1 mark**. > 7. **Middle-Position Logic Blunder:** For 4/3 valves, students often describe the middle position incorrectly (e.g., "all ports open" instead of "tandem" or "float" specifically). Be precise. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Revision — Fluid Power Symbols (Module 1)** - [x] **Standard:** ISO 1219-1 (symbols) + ISO 1219-2 (circuit rules). - [x] **Envelopes:** Circle (rotary), Square (valve), Rectangle (cylinder/tank), Diamond (pneumatic). - [x] **Pump vs Motor:** Filled triangle OUT (pump), Hollow triangle IN (motor). - [x] **DCV Designation Formula:** $N_{\text{ports}} / N_{\text{positions}}$. - [x] **3/2 DCV** → 3 ports, 2 positions, 1 inlet + 1 outlet + 1 exhaust, used for **single-acting** cylinders. - [x] **4/2 DCV** → 4 ports (P, T, A, B), 2 positions, used for **double-acting** cylinders. - [x] **4/3 DCV Centres:** Tandem, Closed, Open, Float — know the middle square of each. - [x] **5/2 and 5/3** → Pneumatic only (separate exhaust ports). - [x] **Pressure Relief:** Arrow up, external vent, system safety device. - [x] **Pressure Reducing:** Arrow down, maintains lower downstream pressure. - [x] **Sequence Valve:** Like relief, but drain is internal (no vent). - [x] **Check Valve:** One-way flow, ball in V-seat. - [x] **Flow Control:** Bow-tie shape; one-way FCV has a parallel check valve. - [x] **Lines:** Solid = main, Long dash = pilot, Short dot = drain/exhaust, Dash-dot = envelope/hose. - [x] **Accumulator:** Circle with wavy divider (gas-fluid interface). - [x] **Filter:** Triangle on a line. - [x] **Gauge:** Circle with clock face marked "P". - [x] **Single-Acting Cylinder:** One rod, one active port, spring/gravity return. - [x] **Double-Acting Cylinder:** Rod both ends, ports A (cap) and B (rod). - [x] **Always label ports** as P, T, A, B in any valve sketch. - [x] **Bernoulli's extended equation** governs $\Delta P_{\text{loss}}$ across filter/valve symbols: $P_1 - P_2 = \Delta P_{\text{loss}}$ for horizontal constant-area line. - [x] **Real-world applications:** Injection moulding, JCB, CNC clamping, aerospace landing gear, food packaging.

topics.title: Gear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT722 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Gear Pumps & Motors

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gear Pump (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **gear pump** is a *positive displacement (PD) rotary hydraulic machine* that uses the **meshing action of gears** to move fluid by *displacement* from the low-pressure inlet port to the high-pressure outlet port. It belongs to the family of *fixed-displacement* hydraulic pumps (unless designed as an external-mesh variable unit).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gear Motor:** A gear motor is the *reverse operating* counterpart of a gear pump — a **hydraulic motor** that converts *hydraulic pressure energy* into *rotary mechanical energy* (torque × angular velocity) using the same meshing-gear principle. Theoretically, any gear pump can operate as a gear motor (and vice-versa) if its internal clearances permit bi-directional rotation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In Module-2 of OEMRT722, "Gear" refers to **Hydraulic Gear Pumps & Gear Motors** — *not* mechanical power-transmission gears. They are classified as **positive displacement (PD) rotary actuators / pumps**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

**Analogy 1 — The Two Spoons in a Honey Jar:** Imagine dipping two interlocked spoons into thick honey. As the spoons rotate, honey trapped *between the teeth and the jar wall* is carried from the bottom (inlet) to the top (outlet) without ever flowing back. The "teeth" do the pumping.

**Analogy 2 — The Metro Turnstile:** Picture the rotating turnstile at a metro station. People (fluid) can only pass through the *enclosed compartments* formed between the arms (gear teeth) and the wall (housing). Each compartment carries exactly one "person-worth" of fluid per rotation — this is *positive displacement*.

**Analogy 3 — The Reciprocity Rule:** A ceiling fan running on electricity (motor) and a hand-cranked fan that pushes air (pump) share the same blade geometry. Similarly, a gear motor *driven by oil* produces shaft rotation, while a gear pump *driven by a shaft* produces oil flow.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following *standard industrial metrics* are used to characterize gear pumps/motors:

- **Volumetric Displacement ($V_D$):** typically **1 cm³/rev to 200 cm³/rev** for industrial gear pumps.
- **Operating Pressure:** typically up to **175 bar (≈ 17.5 MPa)** for external gear, and up to **250 bar (≈ 25 MPa)** for internal gear units.
- **Rotational Speed:** typically **500 rpm to 4000 rpm**.
- **Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_v$):** **85 % – 98 %** at rated conditions.
- **Overall Efficiency ($\eta_o$):** **75 % – 90 %** at rated conditions.
- **Standard Reference Fluids:** Mineral oils ISO VG 32, VG 46, VG 68 (kinematic viscosity $\approx$ **10 cSt – 100 cSt** at 40 °C).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always quote efficiency ranges and pressure limits with units. Examiners reward dimensional discipline heavily in OEMRT722 papers.

## 1.4 Classification of Gear Units (Roadmap for Module-2)

Two principal configurations are studied in Module-2:

| # | Type | Pressure Class | Typical Application |
|---|------|----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **External Gear Pump / Motor** | Low–Medium (≤ 175 bar) | Machine tools, mobile hydraulics, lube systems |
| 2 | **Internal Gear Pump / Motor** | Medium–High (≤ 250 bar) | Chemical dosing, high-viscosity fluids, truck lifts |

A third configuration — the **Lobe Pump** — is occasionally referenced as a *gear-pump variant* and uses two non-meshing lobed rotors.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — External Gear Pump (Step-by-Step Logic)

The external gear pump consists of **two identical spur gears** — a *drive gear* and an *idler gear* — housed inside a tight-tolerance casing. The gears mesh at the centre of the pump body. The principle operates in three discrete phases per revolution:

**Phase 1 — Inlet (Suction) Stroke:**
- As the gears unmesh on the *inlet side*, the volume enclosed between adjacent teeth and the housing *expands*.
- This creates a **local low-pressure zone** (partial vacuum, typically **0.2 bar to 0.8 bar absolute**).
- Atmospheric pressure on the reservoir pushes oil through the inlet port into the tooth spaces.

**Phase 2 — Conveyance (Transport) Stroke:**
- Oil is trapped in the *tooth-space pockets* formed by the gear flanks, the housing bore, and the side plates.
- This trapped volume is carried around the periphery of the gears *without* escaping, because the gears mesh tightly on the outlet side and the side plates (bearing blocks) seal the axial faces.

**Phase 3 — Outlet (Discharge) Stroke:**
- As the gears re-mesh on the *outlet side*, the trapped volume is *squeezed out* and pushed through the outlet port into the hydraulic system.
- Tooth-to-tooth contact on the outlet side acts as a near-perfect seal — this is why the gear pump is a **positive displacement device**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
> - Why does oil enter? → Because unmeshing teeth *create volume* (pressure must drop below atmospheric, per Boyle's law).
> - Why does oil not leak back? → Because meshing teeth *destroy volume* on the outlet side, maintaining a pressure differential.
> - Why is it called "positive displacement"? → Because each rotation delivers a *fixed, predictable* volume of fluid — independent of outlet pressure (within mechanical limits).

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Internal Gear Pump

The internal gear pump uses **two gears: an internal (ring) gear with teeth on the inside, and a smaller external (rotor) gear** that rotates eccentrically inside it. A **crescent-shaped partition** fills the space between the two gears' teeth at the meshing point.

- Fluid is conveyed from inlet to outlet *between the rotor teeth, the ring-gear teeth, and the crescent partition*.
- Internal gear pumps handle **higher viscosities** (up to **5000 cSt**) and **abrasive fluids** better than external gear units.
- Used in **bitumen handling, resin transfer, and truck tail-lift hydraulics**.

## 2.3 Gear Motor — The Reciprocal Operation

When pressurized oil is fed into the outlet port of a gear assembly:
1. Pressure acts on the *unmeshing side* of the gears (now functioning as the high-pressure side).
2. The resultant **pressure × area** force on each tooth creates a **net tangential torque** on the drive shaft.
3. The drive shaft rotates, delivering mechanical work to the load.

The same volumetric displacement $V_D$ governs the relationship between shaft rotation and oil consumption.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master every row below** — these formulas appear in nearly every Part-B (14-mark) question on gear pumps/motors in OEMRT722.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Theoretical Volumetric Displacement (External Gear) | $V_D = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot m^2 \cdot b \cdot z$ | m³/rev | $m$ = module, $b$ = face width, $z$ = number of teeth |
| 2 | Simplified Displacement Approximation | $V_D \approx 2 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot b \cdot m$ | m³/rev | $D$ = pitch-circle diameter |
| 3 | Volumetric Displacement (Internal Gear) | $V_D = \pi \cdot b \cdot (R_e^2 - R_i^2) - V_{crescent}$ | m³/rev | $R_e$ = ring pitch radius, $R_i$ = rotor pitch radius |
| 4 | Theoretical Flow Rate | $Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N$ | m³/s | $N$ = rotational speed in rev/s |
| 5 | Actual Flow Rate | $Q_{act} = Q_{th} \cdot \eta_v = V_D \cdot N \cdot \eta_v$ | m³/s | $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency |
| 6 | Theoretical Torque (Motor) | $T_{th} = \dfrac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2 \pi}$ | N·m | $\Delta P$ = pressure differential |
| 7 | Actual Torque | $T_{act} = \dfrac{V_D \cdot \Delta P \cdot \eta_m}{2 \pi}$ | N·m | $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency |
| 8 | Theoretical Hydraulic Power | $P_{th} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{act} = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot N \cdot T_{act}}{\eta_m}$ | W | Power balance identity |
| 9 | Shaft Power Output (Motor) | $P_{out} = 2 \pi \cdot N \cdot T_{act}$ | W | For motor side |
| 10 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_{loss}}{V_D \cdot N}$ | — | Loss due to internal leakage |
| 11 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{T_{act}}{T_{th}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{loss}}{T_{th}}$ | — | Loss due to friction |
| 12 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | — | Overall performance metric |
| 13 | Slip / Leakage Flow (Empirical) | $Q_{slip} = K_s \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu}$ | m³/s | $K_s$ = slip coefficient, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity |
| 14 | Pump Input Shaft Torque | $T_{in} = \dfrac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2 \pi \cdot \eta_m}$ | N·m | Required to drive the pump |
| 15 | Pressure Rise in Pump | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 \pi \cdot T_{in} \cdot \eta_m}{V_D}$ | Pa | Inverse of torque equation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Board-Exam Tip:** The displacement $V_D$ of an external gear pump with **$z$ teeth, module $m$, and face width $b$** is rigorously derived as:
> $$V_D = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot m^2 \cdot b \cdot z$$
> Do **not** confuse this with the simpler pump displacement $V_D = \pi \cdot d \cdot b \cdot z \cdot m / 2$. Use the first formula unless explicitly stated otherwise.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile Hydraulics:** Excavators, backhoe loaders, forklifts — external gear pumps dominate due to compactness and cost.
- **Lubrication Systems:** Gear pumps are *the* standard lube-oil pump in engines and gearboxes.
- **Chemical Industry:** Internal gear pumps move high-viscosity polymers, resins, and inks.
- **Aerospace Ground Support:** Gear motors drive actuators on hydraulic test rigs.
- **Agricultural Machinery:** Tractor PTO and hydraulic remotes use gear pumps/motors extensively.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Volumetric Displacement for an External Gear Pump

### 3.1.1 Geometric Setup

Consider **two identical external spur gears** of:
- Module: $m$ (in metres)
- Number of teeth: $z$ (per gear)
- Face width: $b$ (in metres)
- Pitch-circle diameter: $d = m \cdot z$

For one rotation of a gear, the *volume swept by one tooth space* equals the volume of the space between two adjacent teeth up to the pitch circle, projected along the face width.

The *theoretical volume trapped between two adjacent teeth of one gear, per revolution*, is approximately:

$$V_{tooth} = \text{(Tooth space area)} \times \text{(face width)}$$

For a standard full-depth involute tooth, the tooth-space area equals:

$$A_{tooth} = \frac{\pi \cdot m^2}{2}$$

> *Reasoning:* A standard full-depth involute tooth has a pitch-line tooth thickness equal to half the circular pitch $p_c = \pi m$. The space between two adjacent teeth, integrated from root to pitch circle, occupies exactly half the pitch-cylinder cross-sectional area per tooth.

### 3.1.2 Total Displacement Per Revolution

Since the pump has **two gears** and only one gear (the drive gear) is doing the *conveying* on the inlet side while the idler mirrors it, the total theoretical displacement per revolution of the drive gear is:

$$V_D = 2 \cdot z \cdot A_{tooth} \cdot b$$

Substituting $A_{tooth} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot m^2}{2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_D &= 2 \cdot z \cdot \left(\frac{\pi \cdot m^2}{2}\right) \cdot b \\[6pt]
V_D &= \pi \cdot m^2 \cdot z \cdot b
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Step Logic:** The factor **2** appears because *both gears* contribute conveying volume. The factor $z$ accounts for all tooth spaces per gear. The factor $\dfrac{\pi m^2}{2}$ is the area of *one tooth space*.

However, because the meshing region *discharges oil simultaneously*, we must subtract the volume re-filled by the meshing teeth. For symmetric involute gears, this correction reduces the displacement to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_D &= 2 \cdot \pi \cdot m^2 \cdot z \cdot b - \text{(meshing correction)} \\[4pt]
V_D &\approx 2 \cdot \pi \cdot m^2 \cdot z \cdot b \cdot (1 - \epsilon)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\epsilon$ is a small mesh-overlap factor ($\epsilon \approx 0.05$ for standard gears).

For **engineering-grade analysis at KTU level**, we use the simplified form:

$$\boxed{V_D = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot m^2 \cdot b \cdot z \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{rev}]}$$

### 3.1.3 Final Form in Terms of Pitch Diameter

Since $d = m \cdot z$, we can rewrite $V_D$ as:

$$V_D = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot m \cdot b \cdot d$$

This form is convenient when $d$ and $m$ are known but $z$ is not.

## 3.2 Derivation of Theoretical and Actual Flow Rate

The theoretical flow rate (no leakage) is:

$$Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N$$

where $N$ is in *revolutions per second*. To convert rpm to rev/s: $N_{rev/s} = N_{rpm} / 60$.

The **actual flow rate** accounts for internal leakage $Q_{slip}$:

$$Q_{act} = Q_{th} - Q_{slip}$$

By the definition of volumetric efficiency:

$$\eta_v = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = \eta_v \cdot V_D \cdot N}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Theoretical Torque (Gear Motor Side)

Consider a gear motor with displacement $V_D$ running at pressure differential $\Delta P$. In one complete revolution, the fluid does work equal to pressure × volume:

$$W = \Delta P \cdot V_D$$

The torque times angular displacement per revolution equals this work:

$$T_{th} \cdot 2\pi = \Delta P \cdot V_D$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{T_{th} = \frac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi} \quad [\text{N·m}]}$$

Including mechanical efficiency $\eta_m$, the actual output torque is:

$$T_{act} = \eta_m \cdot T_{th} = \frac{\eta_m \cdot V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi}$$

## 3.4 Power Balance Derivation

**Input Hydraulic Power to Motor:**

$$P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{act} = \Delta P \cdot \eta_v \cdot V_D \cdot N$$

**Mechanical Shaft Output Power:**

$$P_{shaft} = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot T_{act} = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot \frac{\eta_m \cdot V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi} = \eta_m \cdot V_D \cdot N \cdot \Delta P$$

**Equating** (the hydraulic input equals the shaft output divided by overall efficiency):

$$P_{shaft} = \eta_o \cdot P_{hyd} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m \cdot P_{hyd}$$

This confirms the overall efficiency identity.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board-Style)

> **Problem:** An external gear pump has **module $m = 4$ mm**, **number of teeth $z = 12$**, and **face width $b = 25$ mm**. The pump runs at **$N = 1450$ rpm** and delivers oil at a pressure of **$\Delta P = 100$ bar**. Given $\eta_v = 0.95$ and $\eta_m = 0.90$, compute:
> (a) Volumetric displacement $V_D$
> (b) Actual flow rate $Q_{act}$
> (c) Theoretical torque $T_{th}$ and actual torque $T_{act}$
> (d) Shaft power input required

### Solution

**Given (in SI units):**
- $m = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $z = 12$
- $b = 25 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $N = 1450 / 60 = 24.167$ rev/s
- $\Delta P = 100 \times 10^{5}$ Pa = $10^{7}$ Pa
- $\eta_v = 0.95$, $\eta_m = 0.90$

**(a) Displacement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_D &= 2 \cdot \pi \cdot m^2 \cdot b \cdot z \\[4pt]
&= 2 \cdot \pi \cdot (4 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot (25 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 12 \\[4pt]
&= 2 \cdot \pi \cdot (1.6 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot (3 \times 10^{-1}) \\[4pt]
&= 2 \cdot \pi \cdot (4.8 \times 10^{-6}) \\[4pt]
&= 3.0159 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3/\text{rev} \\[4pt]
&\approx 30.16 \ \text{cm}^3/\text{rev}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Actual Flow Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{act} &= \eta_v \cdot V_D \cdot N \\[4pt]
&= 0.95 \cdot (3.0159 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 24.167 \\[4pt]
&= 6.926 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s} \\[4pt]
&\approx 41.56 \ \text{L}/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Theoretical and Actual Torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{th} &= \frac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2 \pi} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(3.0159 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot (10^{7})}{2 \pi} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{301.59}{6.2832} \\[4pt]
&= 48.0 \ \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{act} &= \eta_m \cdot T_{th} = 0.90 \cdot 48.0 = 43.2 \ \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(d) Shaft Input Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= \frac{2 \pi \cdot N \cdot T_{act}}{\eta_m} = \frac{2 \pi \cdot N \cdot T_{th}}{\eta_o} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{V_D \cdot N \cdot \Delta P}{\eta_o} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{(3.0159 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 24.167 \cdot 10^{7}}{0.95 \cdot 0.90} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{7289.8}{0.855} \\[4pt]
&\approx 8526 \ \text{W} \approx 8.53 \ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Parameters

| Parameter | Effect on $Q_{act}$ | Effect on $\Delta P$ | Effect on $\eta_o$ |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| ↑ Rotational speed $N$ | ↑ Linear increase | Marginal increase | ↓ (due to higher friction & cavitation risk) |
| ↑ Pressure $\Delta P$ | ↓ (due to ↑ leakage) | Direct (set by load) | ↓ (mechanical losses rise) |
| ↑ Oil viscosity $\mu$ | ↓ (↑ leakage losses at high $\mu$) | ↑ at fixed $Q$ | Optimal at ~20–40 cSt |
| ↑ Wear (clearance ↑) | ↓ (leakage ↑) | ↓ at fixed $T$ | ↓ significantly |
| ↑ Temperature | ↓ viscosity, ↑ leakage | Variable | ↓ at extremes |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 External Gear Pump — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Drive Shaft Coupling] --> B[Drive Gear]
    B <--> C[Idler Gear]
    C --> D[Side Plate and Bearing Block Left]
    B --> E[Side Plate and Bearing Block Right]
    F[Inlet Port Low Pressure] --> G[Inlet Chamber Tooth Space Expansion]
    G --> H[Conveyance Around Gear Periphery]
    H --> I[Outlet Chamber Tooth Space Compression]
    I --> J[Outlet Port High Pressure]
    J --> K[Hydraulic System Load]
    L[Housing with Precision Bore] --- B
    L --- C
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **drive gear** is coupled to the external prime-mover (electric motor, IC engine).
- The **idler gear** is driven by the drive gear through meshing.
- Oil enters through the **inlet port** into the *unmeshing zone* (left side) where volume expansion occurs.
- The tooth spaces carry oil around the housing periphery.
- Oil is discharged through the **outlet port** at the *meshing zone* (right side).
- **Side plates** (bearing blocks) seal the axial faces of the gears and support the shafts.

## 4.2 Internal Gear Pump — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PUMP [Internal Gear Pump Casing]
        R1[Internal Ring Gear Stationary Outer]
        R2[External Rotor Gear Rotating Inner]
        CR[Crescent Shaped Partition Fixed]
        SH[Drive Shaft Eccentric Mounted]
        IN[Inlet Port]
        OUT[Outlet Port]
    end
    SH --> R2
    R2 -. rotates eccentric inside .-> R1
    R2 -. teeth sweep past .-> CR
    IN --> R2
    R2 --> OUT
```

**Key architectural notes:**
- The **ring gear** has teeth on its *inner* circumference.
- The **rotor gear** is smaller and is mounted on an *eccentric shaft* — its centre is offset from the ring-gear centre.
- The **crescent partition** is a stationary filler block that separates the inlet and outlet zones and prevents back-flow.
- Fluid is conveyed *between rotor teeth, ring-gear teeth, and the crescent* in crescent-shaped pockets.

## 4.3 Gear Motor — Reciprocal Operating Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Hydraulic Pump External Source] --> V[Direction Control Valve]
    V --> M1[Gear Motor Inlet Port]
    M1 --> M2[Tooth Engagement Zone Pressure Force]
    M2 --> M3[Shaft Torque Generated]
    M3 --> L[Mechanical Load Coupling]
    L --> FB[Feedback Speed Sensor Optional]
    FB --> V
```

**Operating logic:**
- Pressurized oil enters through what was originally the outlet port (now functioning as the motor's high-pressure inlet).
- Pressure acts on the *unmeshing side* of the gears, generating a *tangential force* on each tooth.
- The resultant moment produces **shaft torque**, which is delivered to the mechanical load.

## 4.4 Internal Clearances & Leakage Paths (Critical Engineering Detail)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLEARANCES [Internal Leakage Paths in a Gear Pump]
        CL1[Tip Clearance Gear Tooth Tip to Housing Bore]
        CL2[Side Clearance Between Gear Face and Side Plate]
        CL3[Meshing Zone Leakage Tooth to Tooth at Contact]
    end
    CL1 --> LK[Total Leakage Flow Qslip]
    CL2 --> LK
    CL3 --> LK
    LK --> ETA[Reduced Volumetric Efficiency]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manufacturing Note (Board-Exam Favourite):** Industrial gear pumps are manufactured to clearances of **5 µm to 20 µm** to balance leakage (which must be minimized) against *thermal expansion* (which must be accommodated). Excessive clearance → poor volumetric efficiency. Insufficient clearance → seizure under thermal load.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART-A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between an external gear pump and an internal gear pump with respect to construction and application.**

**Model Answer:**
An **external gear pump** uses two *identical spur gears* meshing externally in a tight housing; it operates at moderate pressure (≤ 175 bar) and is widely used in *machine tools and mobile hydraulics*. An **internal gear pump** uses *one external rotor gear meshing inside an internal ring gear*, separated by a *crescent partition*; it operates at higher pressure (≤ 250 bar) and is preferred for *high-viscosity fluids and chemical transfer*. The internal gear design offers better *self-priming*, *quieter operation*, and tolerance to *abrasive media*. (3 marks: 1 for construction difference, 1 for pressure capability, 1 for application distinction)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is a gear pump classified as a "positive displacement" pump? State any two reasons.**

**Model Answer:**
A gear pump is classified as a *positive displacement (PD)* pump because:

1. **Fixed Volume Delivery:** Each rotation delivers a theoretically fixed volume of fluid ($V_D = 2\pi m^2 b z$), independent of the discharge pressure (within mechanical limits).
2. **Sealed Conveyance:** Fluid is trapped in *enclosed tooth spaces* between the gear flanks, housing bore, and side plates — preventing back-flow and ensuring the entire displaced volume is forced out through the outlet port.

A key consequence is that *the flow is directly proportional to rotational speed* ($Q = V_D \cdot N$), making gear pumps ideal for *metering applications*.

---

## 5.2 PART-B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **construction and working of an external gear pump**. Highlight the role of the *idler gear*, *side plates*, and *housing clearances*. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An external gear pump has the following specifications: *module $m = 5$ mm, number of teeth $z = 14$, face width $b = 30$ mm, speed $N = 1500$ rpm, pressure $\Delta P = 120$ bar, volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.94$, mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.88$.* Calculate: (i) displacement $V_D$, (ii) actual delivery $Q_{act}$, (iii) input torque $T_{in}$, and (iv) input power $P_{in}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction:** The external gear pump consists of two *identical spur gears* — a **drive gear** keyed to the input shaft and an **idler gear** driven by the drive gear. Both gears are supported by **sintered bronze or steel-backed bearing blocks** (side plates) that seal the axial faces of the gears. The **housing** has a precision-bored cylindrical chamber with inlet and outlet ports on diametrically opposite sides.

**Working Principle:**
1. *Inlet side:* As gears unmesh, volume expands → low pressure → oil is drawn in from the reservoir.
2. *Conveyance:* Oil trapped between adjacent teeth and the housing is carried around the periphery.
3. *Outlet side:* As gears re-mesh, volume contracts → oil is forced out through the discharge port.

**Roles of key components:**
- **Idler gear:** Provides the *second sealing surface* and balances side loads on the drive gear.
- **Side plates:** Provide *axial sealing* to prevent fluid from escaping between gear faces and housing; also serve as *journal bearings*.
- **Housing clearances:** Must be tight (**5–20 µm** tip clearance) to minimize leakage yet allow for thermal expansion.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $m = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $z = 14$, $b = 30 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $N = 1500/60 = 25$ rev/s, $\Delta P = 120 \times 10^5$ Pa, $\eta_v = 0.94$, $\eta_m = 0.88$.

**(i) Displacement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_D &= 2 \pi m^2 b z \\
&= 2 \pi \cdot (5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot (30 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 14 \\
&= 2 \pi \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot (4.2 \times 10^{-1}) \\
&= 6.597 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{m}^3/\text{rev} \\
&\approx 65.97 \ \text{cm}^3/\text{rev}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Actual Delivery:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{act} &= \eta_v \cdot V_D \cdot N \\
&= 0.94 \cdot (6.597 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 25 \\
&= 1.550 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s} \\
&\approx 93.0 \ \text{L}/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Input Torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{in} &= \frac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2 \pi \cdot \eta_m} \\
&= \frac{(6.597 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot (120 \times 10^5)}{2 \pi \cdot 0.88} \\
&= \frac{791.64}{5.529} \\
&\approx 143.2 \ \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Input Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= 2 \pi \cdot N \cdot T_{in} \\
&= 2 \pi \cdot 25 \cdot 143.2 \\
&\approx 22{,}490 \ \text{W} \approx 22.49 \ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of an internal gear pump** with a labelled block diagram. Compare its merits and demerits against the external gear pump. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gear motor receives oil at a flow rate of *60 L/min* at a pressure of *150 bar*. The motor has a *volumetric displacement of 80 cm³/rev* and runs at *mechanical efficiency 0.90, volumetric efficiency 0.95*. Calculate: (i) the theoretical speed of rotation, (ii) the actual speed of rotation, (iii) the output torque, and (iv) the output power. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working of Internal Gear Pump:**
The internal gear pump consists of an **outer ring gear** (with internal teeth, stationary) and a smaller **rotor gear** (with external teeth, mounted on an eccentric shaft). A **crescent-shaped partition** separates the inlet and outlet zones. As the rotor turns, fluid is trapped between the rotor teeth, the ring-gear teeth, and the crescent. On the *inlet side*, teeth unmesh from the crescent → volume expansion → fluid enters. On the *outlet side*, teeth re-engage the crescent → volume contraction → fluid is discharged.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | External Gear Pump | Internal Gear Pump |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Pressure rating | ≤ 175 bar | ≤ 250 bar |
| Noise level | Higher | Lower (smoother meshing) |
| Viscosity handling | Up to ~500 cSt | Up to ~5000 cSt |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Self-priming ability | Good | Excellent |
| Fluid cleanliness sensitivity | Higher | Lower |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $Q_{in} = 60$ L/min = $60 \times 10^{-3}/60 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s, $\Delta P = 150 \times 10^5$ Pa, $V_D = 80 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/rev, $\eta_m = 0.90$, $\eta_v = 0.95$.

**(i) Theoretical Speed:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{th} &= \frac{Q_{in}}{V_D} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{80 \times 10^{-6}} = 12.5 \ \text{rev/s} = 750 \ \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Actual Speed** (motor speed = theoretical speed × $\eta_v$, since some oil leaks back without contributing to rotation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{act} &= \eta_v \cdot N_{th} = 0.95 \cdot 750 = 712.5 \ \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Output Torque:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{out} &= \eta_m \cdot \frac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2 \pi} \\
&= 0.90 \cdot \frac{(80 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (150 \times 10^5)}{2 \pi} \\
&= 0.90 \cdot \frac{1200}{6.2832} \\
&= 0.90 \cdot 190.99 \\
&\approx 171.9 \ \text{N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Output Power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{out} &= 2 \pi \cdot N_{act} \cdot T_{out} \\
&= 2 \pi \cdot \left(\frac{712.5}{60}\right) \cdot 171.9 \\
&= 2 \pi \cdot 11.875 \cdot 171.9 \\
&\approx 12{,}824 \ \text{W} \approx 12.82 \ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion in Flow Rate:** Students often forget to convert L/min to m³/s. Always use **SI units** in KTU papers. (–1 mark penalty)
> 2. **Mixing Up $N_{th}$ and $N_{act}$:** For motors, $N_{act} = \eta_v \cdot N_{th}$ (NOT the other way around). The leakage reduces actual speed.
> 3. **Torque Sign Convention:** Some students report output torque as *input* torque and lose the $\eta_m$ factor. For motors, *output* torque uses $\eta_m$ directly: $T_{out} = \eta_m \cdot T_{th}$.
> 4. **Forgetting the "2" in $V_D$:** The displacement formula has a factor of 2 for two-gear contribution. Forgetting it gives $V_D$ that is *half* the correct value. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Side-Plate Leakage:** When asked "why does a gear pump lose efficiency at high pressure?", students often mention only *tip clearance* and forget the *side-clearance* contribution. Both must be listed for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final consolidated checklist before your KTU exam — bookmark this section!**

- ✅ **Definition:** Gear pump = positive-displacement rotary pump using meshing gears to convey fluid from inlet to outlet.
- ✅ **Two main types:** External gear (two external spur gears) and Internal gear (rotor inside ring gear + crescent).
- ✅ **Master displacement formula:** $\mathbf{V_D = 2 \pi m^2 b z}$ — remember the factor of **2** for both gears.
- ✅ **Flow rate:** $Q_{act} = \eta_v \cdot V_D \cdot N$ — convert $N$ from rpm to rev/s before calculation.
- ✅ **Torque (motor):** $T_{out} = \eta_m \cdot \dfrac{V_D \cdot \Delta P}{2\pi}$.
- ✅ **Power balance:** $P_{out} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m \cdot P_{in} = \eta_o \cdot P_{hyd}$.
- ✅ **Efficiency order:** $\eta_v > \eta_m > \eta_o$ (always). Typical values: $\eta_v \approx 0.95$, $\eta_m \approx 0.90$, $\eta_o \approx 0.85$.
- ✅ **Pressure limits:** External ≤ **175 bar**, Internal ≤ **250 bar**.
- ✅ **Three leakage paths:** (1) tip clearance, (2) side-plate clearance, (3) meshing zone — all three together cause $Q_{slip}$.
- ✅ **Reciprocity:** Any gear pump can run as a gear motor if clearances allow bi-directional rotation.
- ✅ **Why "positive displacement"?** Because each rotation delivers a *fixed* theoretical volume regardless of pressure (until mechanical limits are hit).
- ✅ **Common board traps:**
  - Forgetting $\eta_m$ when computing input torque for pumps.
  - Mixing theoretical and actual values.
  - Skipping unit conversions (bar → Pa, L/min → m³/s, rpm → rev/s).
  - Omitting the factor of 2 in $V_D$.

> **End of Module-2 Gear Notes — OEMRT722**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 (2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Aligned)*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
