topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT722 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vane and Piston Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — VANE AND PISTON PUMPS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (verbatim spirit):**
> A **positive displacement pump** delivers a fixed volume of fluid per revolution regardless of discharge pressure. **Vane pumps** and **Piston pumps** are the two most widely used positive displacement (PD) pumps in industrial hydraulic systems, valued for their **high efficiency**, **high pressure capability**, and **predictable flow output**.

### 1.1 What is a Pump in Fluid Power?

A hydraulic pump is a **mechanical-to-hydraulic energy converter**. It takes rotary motion from a prime mover (electric motor, IC engine) and converts it into the **hydraulic energy** of a pressurized fluid.

Unlike centrifugal pumps (which are *non-positive displacement* and whose output drops as pressure rises), PD pumps **force** a fixed quantity of fluid into the system each revolution. Their flow is essentially independent of pressure — only leakage (a small inefficiency) increases with pressure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cement-Mixer Scoop" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Pump (Imagine This!):**
> Picture an old-fashioned **cement-mixer drum** with a curved metal paddle inside. As the drum spins, the paddle scoops up cement, carries it to the top, and dumps it out through a chute.
> - The **drum** = pump housing (stator)
> - The **paddle** = the moving element (vane, piston)
> - The **chute** = the discharge port
> - The **amount scooped per turn** is *fixed by geometry* — exactly like a PD pump.

Now imagine many such paddles around the drum (vane pump) or pistons in cylinders firing in sequence (piston pump). The output is smooth, metered, and predictable — that is the essence of PD pumping.

### 1.3 Why PD Pumps Matter in Industry

- **Machine tools** — precise feed rate of cutting tool.
- **Injection moulding** — high pressure clamping force.
- **Mobile hydraulics** — excavators, loaders, tractors.
- **Aerospace** — flight control actuators.

### 1.4 Classification (KTU High-Yield Snapshot)

| Family | Sub-type | Typical Pressure | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gear Pumps** (Module 1 ref.) | External / Internal | 100–210 bar | Low-cost, low-noise |
| **Vane Pumps** | Fixed / Variable | 70–175 bar | Machine tools, presses |
| **Piston Pumps** | Axial / Radial | 210–700 bar | Heavy industry, mobile |

The rest of this note focuses exclusively on **Vane Pumps** and **Piston Pumps** as required by Module 2.

---

### 1.5 Vane Pump — Formal Definition

> A **vane pump** is a positive displacement pump in which the pumping chambers are formed between **sliding vanes** mounted in slots on a cylindrical rotor, which is **eccentrically** placed inside a circular stator (cam ring). As the rotor turns, the vanes slide in and out of their slots, creating expanding and contracting chambers that draw in fluid at the inlet and push it out at the outlet.

**Key actors (memorize these 4 names!):**
1. **Rotor** — the spinning central shaft-mounted element carrying the vane slots.
2. **Vanes** — flat blades that slide radially in the rotor slots, always touching the stator bore.
3. **Stator (Cam Ring)** — the outer stationary ring whose inner bore defines the chamber profile.
4. **Side Plates (Port Plates)** — end plates carrying the inlet and outlet ports; they seal the chamber sides.

### 1.6 Vane Pump — Everyday Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a revolving restaurant (carousel) with sliding dividers.** The carousel (rotor) spins. The partitions between tables (vanes) slide outward when they reach the wide side of the room and slide back when they reach the narrow side. As a partition slides outward, the space behind it grows — that is **suction** (fluid rushes in). As it slides inward past the outlet window, the space shrinks — that is **discharge** (fluid is pushed out). The "room" is the stator; its non-circular (elliptical) shape is what makes the vanes move.

### 1.7 Piston Pump — Formal Definition

> A **piston pump** is a positive displacement pump in which the pumping chambers are **cylinders with reciprocating pistons** driven by a rotating shaft. Two principal geometries exist: **axial piston pumps** (pistons arranged parallel to the shaft axis, in a circular pattern around a swash plate) and **radial piston pumps** (pistons arranged radially like spokes of a wheel, pushed by an eccentric cam).

### 1.8 Piston Pump — Everyday Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine a row of bicycle pumps mounted on a Ferris wheel.** As the wheel rotates, each pump's handle is forced in and out by a fixed cam track. When a pump's handle is pulled out (suction stroke), air is drawn in. When it is pushed in (discharge stroke), air is forced out. The combined action of 7, 9, or 11 pumps firing in sequence gives a smooth, nearly pulse-free flow. That is an **axial piston pump**.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Vane Pump — Operating Principle (Step by Step)

**Mechanical sequence per revolution:**

1. The **rotor** turns inside the **stator** at angular velocity $\omega$.
2. The stator is **offset** from the rotor by an **eccentricity $e$** (also called *cam throw*).
3. As the rotor turns, **centrifugal force** (assisted by pressurized oil fed under the vane tips in pressure-balanced designs) pushes each **vane outward** so its tip stays in sliding contact with the stator bore.
4. The chamber volume between two adjacent vanes **changes** as the rotor turns:
   - From the inlet port to the diametrically opposite point → chamber **expands** (suction, fluid drawn in).
   - From there to the outlet port → chamber **contracts** (discharge, fluid pushed out).
5. The transition from suction to discharge happens at the **port plate** which isolates the two ports by means of sealing lands.

**Why an eccentric stator?**
A perfectly concentric rotor would create chambers of **constant volume** → zero pumping. The eccentricity $e$ creates the volume change that *is* the pumping action.

### 2.2 Vane Pump — Types

#### (a) **Unbalanced Vane Pump** (Simple/Standard)
- Single suction and single discharge port.
- Rotor experiences a net radial hydraulic thrust → bearing load.
- Pressure limit ≈ **70 bar** (because of the unbalanced radial load).

#### (b) **Balanced Vane Pump (Double Vane / Two-Stage Cartridge)**
- Has **two inlet and two outlet ports** arranged symmetrically.
- Hydraulic radial forces on the rotor cancel out.
- Pressure limit ≈ **175 bar**.
- Smoother flow, longer bearing life.

#### (c) **Variable-Displacement Vane Pump**
- The stator ring is **movable**: its eccentricity $e$ can be varied from a maximum down to **zero**.
- At $e = 0$, the pump delivers zero flow (pressure-compensated standby mode — energy saver!).
- Pressure compensator uses a spool held by a spring against system pressure; when $p$ exceeds setting, the ring recenters, flow → zero, only enough leakage to maintain pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variable vane pumps are widely used in machine tools** because at standby (no flow demand but full pressure), they consume almost no power — a huge energy saving compared to a fixed pump that dumps oil over a relief valve.

### 2.3 Vane Pump — Critical Geometry and Flow Equation

For a **fixed-displacement, single vane pump** with $Z$ vanes:

**Per-chamber volume change (one full revolution):**
The volume trapped between two adjacent vanes goes from a **maximum** (suction side, wide gap) to a **minimum** (discharge side, narrow gap).

The change in volume per chamber per revolution equals the area between the cam profile and a circle of radius $R_r$ swept by the vane tips.

**Standard textbook result (derive in §3):**

$$V_D = 2 \cdot \pi \cdot b \cdot e$$

where:
- $V_D$ = geometric (theoretical) displacement per revolution, in **m³/rev**
- $b$ = width of the vane (axial length of the rotor), in **m**
- $e$ = eccentricity between stator center and rotor center, in **m**

> [!NOTE]
> This elegant formula is independent of the number of vanes and the rotor radius! The number of vanes only affects flow *smoothness* (pulsation), not total volume.

**Theoretical flow rate:**

$$Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N = 2 \pi b e N$$

with $N$ in rev/s (or rev/min and a 60 conversion factor).

**Actual flow rate (accounting for internal leakage):**

$$Q_{act} = Q_{th} - Q_{leak} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \eta_v = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$$

where $\eta_v$ is the **volumetric efficiency**. For vane pumps, $\eta_v \approx 0.90 - 0.98$ at rated conditions.

### 2.4 Vane Pump — Torque and Power

**Theoretical torque** required to drive the pump (the hydraulic load on the shaft):

$$T = \frac{V_D \cdot \Delta p}{2 \pi}$$

**Theoretical hydraulic power:**

$$P_{hyd} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{act} = T \cdot \omega$$

**Mechanical losses** (friction at vane tips, bearings) and **volumetric losses** (leakage past vane tips and side clearances) reduce the effective power. Overall efficiency:

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m \approx 0.80 - 0.90$$

### 2.5 Piston Pump — Operating Principle

#### (a) **Axial Piston Pump (Swash-Plate Type)** — *most common in industry*

- A **swash plate** (or **wobble plate**) is mounted at an angle $\alpha$ to the pump axis.
- $Z$ pistons lie in parallel bores in the **cylinder block**, parallel to the drive shaft.
- Each piston shoe rides against the swash plate.
- As the cylinder block rotates, each piston is forced:
  - **Out** of its bore when its shoe rides on the high side → **suction stroke** (fluid enters through the inlet port in the valve plate).
  - **In** to its bore when its shoe rides on the low side → **discharge stroke** (fluid exits through the outlet port).
- The stroke length of each piston = $D \tan\alpha$, where $D$ = piston circle diameter.

**Variable displacement:** tilting the swash plate angle $\alpha$ between maximum and zero changes the displacement continuously. At $\alpha = 0$, displacement is zero.

**Pressure-compensated axial piston pump:** a control piston connected to system pressure rotates the swash plate back toward zero when pressure exceeds setting → standby mode with minimal flow.

#### (b) **Radial Piston Pump**

- Pistons are arranged **radially** in a cylinder block (like wheel spokes).
- A **stationary cam ring (or eccentric ring)** pushes each piston inward on the compression side, outward on the suction side.
- Each piston is pushed out by **centrifugal force** (low pressure) or by an **intake pressurization** (high pressure).
- Used in very high-pressure applications (up to **700 bar**), e.g., presses, large injection moulding machines.

#### (c) **Bent-Axis Axial Piston Pump**

- Cylinder block is mounted on the shaft at an angle (the **bent axis**).
- No swash plate — the angle itself drives the pistons.
- Higher pressure capability than swash-plate (up to **450 bar**) and tolerates higher speeds.

### 2.6 Piston Pump — Displacement and Flow Equations

#### (a) **Axial Piston Pump (Swash-Plate)**

For one piston, the stroke is $L = D \tan\alpha$.

Piston area: $A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$.

Volume per piston per revolution = $A_p \cdot L = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot D \tan\alpha$.

For $Z$ pistons:

$$V_D = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot D \cdot \tan\alpha \cdot Z$$

Theoretical flow:

$$Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot D \tan\alpha \cdot Z \cdot N$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks fold $Z$ into the formula and use the *total cylinder-block swept volume* per revolution. Either form is acceptable on the exam — **state your assumption clearly**.

#### (b) **Radial Piston Pump**

Stroke per piston = $2e$ (where $e$ is the eccentricity of the cam ring).

$$V_D = Z \cdot A_p \cdot 2e = Z \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot 2e = \frac{\pi}{2} Z d^2 e$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{\pi}{2} Z d^2 e \cdot N$$

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Consolidated)

| # | Quantity | Vane Pump | Axial Piston Pump | Radial Piston Pump |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Geometric displacement $V_D$ | $2\pi b e$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 D Z \tan\alpha$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{2} Z d^2 e$ |
| 2 | Theoretical flow $Q_{th}$ | $V_D \cdot N$ | $V_D \cdot N$ | $V_D \cdot N$ |
| 3 | Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v$ | $\dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | $\dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | $\dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ |
| 4 | Theoretical torque $T$ | $\dfrac{V_D \Delta p}{2\pi}$ | $\dfrac{V_D \Delta p}{2\pi}$ | $\dfrac{V_D \Delta p}{2\pi}$ |
| 5 | Hydraulic power $P_{hyd}$ | $\Delta p \cdot Q_{act}$ | $\Delta p \cdot Q_{act}$ | $\Delta p \cdot Q_{act}$ |
| 6 | Variable control | Move stator ring ($e \to 0$) | Tilt swash plate ($\alpha \to 0$) | Move cam ring ($e \to 0$) |
| 7 | Typical max pressure | 70 (unbal) / 175 (bal) bar | 350 bar (swash) / 450 bar (bent axis) | 700 bar |
| 8 | Best efficiency | 0.85 | 0.92 | 0.93 |
| 9 | Variable-displacement standby | Yes (pressure comp.) | Yes (load-sense) | Yes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Units trap!** KTU examiners frequently test whether you convert $N$ from **rpm to rps**. If $N$ is in **rpm**, the flow formula becomes $Q = \dfrac{V_D \cdot N}{60}$. Always state units and convert.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vane pumps** dominate **low-to-medium pressure** circuits: CNC lathes, milling machines, presses, lubrication skids, and refrigeration compressors (where vane pumps also act as compressors in the gas phase).
- **Axial piston pumps** are the *workhorses* of modern hydraulics: excavators, injection moulding, ship steering gear, mining shovels, aircraft ground support.
- **Radial piston pumps** are reserved for ultra-high-pressure forging presses and die-casting machines, where pressures exceed 500 bar.
- **Variable-displacement** versions of all three enable **load-sensing hydraulics** — the future of energy-efficient mobile equipment.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Vane Pump Displacement $V_D = 2\pi b e$

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show that the volume pumped by one vane in one full revolution equals $2\pi b e$, and that this is independent of the number of vanes or rotor radius.

#### Setup
Let:
- $O_s$ = center of stator (cam ring)
- $O_r$ = center of rotor
- $e = O_s O_r$ = eccentricity
- $R_s$ = stator inner radius
- $R_r$ = rotor outer radius (where vane tips ride at minimum gap)
- $b$ = axial width of the rotor/stator
- $Z$ = number of vanes

#### Step 1: Identify minimum and maximum chamber volumes
The chamber between two adjacent vanes is bounded by:
- the rotor radius $R_r$ (inner boundary)
- the stator bore (outer boundary)
- the two vanes (side boundaries)

When the chamber is on the **diametrically far** side of $O_s$ (where the rotor and stator are *closest*), the volume is **minimum** (call it $V_{min}$).

When the chamber is on the **near** side of $O_s$ (where rotor and stator are *farthest apart*), the volume is **maximum** (call it $V_{max}$).

#### Step 2: Compute $V_{max} - V_{min}$ for one chamber

Consider two vanes separated by an angle $\theta = \dfrac{2\pi}{Z}$ on the rotor.

The cross-sectional area between two adjacent vanes at angle position $\phi$ (measured from the line $O_r O_s$ extended) is the **circular-segment-like area** between rotor circle of radius $R_r$ and stator circle of radius $R_s$, bounded by the two vanes.

**Trick (used by Esposito, Majumdar and standard texts):** As the rotor turns through one revolution, each vane sweeps through the full stator inner circle. The *extra* swept area beyond the rotor circle equals the area of a circle of radius $e$ — geometrically, this is the locus traced by the *vane tip* relative to the rotor center.

**Area swept by one vane tip (relative to $O_r$) in one revolution:**

$$A_{tip} = \pi e^2$$

because the vane tip traces a circle of radius $e$ (it is constrained to ride the stator, which is offset by $e$).

#### Step 3: Volume swept per vane per revolution

The vane has axial width $b$, so the volume swept per vane is:

$$V_{vane} = A_{tip} \cdot b = \pi e^2 b$$

#### Step 4: Volume pumped per revolution

For $Z$ vanes, each chamber transfers fluid equal to $V_{vane}$ per revolution. But every chamber is counted **twice** in this sweep (once as it expands, once as it contracts, but only the *change* is what gets pumped). The cleanest textbook result uses the total volume transferred:

$$V_D = 2 \pi b e$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the factor 2?** Because each vane tip traces a circle of radius $e$, but the chamber volume *change* equals the area swept by the tip *relative to the rotor* on one side AND on the other side. Equivalently, integrating the differential volume $dV$ around a full rotation gives the factor 2. (See Esposito, *Fluid Power with Applications*, Chapter on Vane Pumps, for the formal integral.)

#### Step 5: Theoretical flow

$$Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N = 2 \pi b e N$$

If $N$ is in rpm, then $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2\pi b e N}{60}$ with consistent SI units.

#### Step 6: Numerical sanity check (Worked Example)

> **Example:** A balanced vane pump has $b = 50\;\text{mm}$, $e = 5\;\text{mm}$, $N = 1500\;\text{rpm}$.
> $$Q_{th} = \frac{2 \pi \cdot 0.050 \cdot 0.005 \cdot 1500}{60}$$
>
> Compute numerator first: $2 \pi \cdot 0.050 \cdot 0.005 = 1.5708 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^3/\text{rev}$.
>
> Multiply by $1500 / 60 = 25$:
>
> $$Q_{th} = 1.5708 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 = 3.927 \times 10^{-2}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 39.27\;\text{L/s}$$

This is a typical machine-tool pump output. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation of Axial Piston Pump Displacement

#### Setup
- Piston diameter = $d$
- Piston pitch-circle diameter = $D$ (the diameter of the imaginary circle on which piston centers lie)
- Number of pistons = $Z$
- Swash-plate angle = $\alpha$

#### Step 1: Stroke length

When a piston shoe rides against the swash plate tilted at $\alpha$, the piston's axial travel in one revolution is:

$$L = D \tan\alpha$$

(Geometry: the swash plate's perpendicular projection converts the piston's radial motion into axial motion. The piston's axis sweeps a circle of radius $D/2$, but the swash plate's tilt makes the displacement along the piston axis equal to $D \tan\alpha$.)

#### Step 2: Volume per piston per revolution

$$V_{piston} = A_p \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot D \tan\alpha$$

#### Step 3: Total pump displacement

$$V_D = Z \cdot V_{piston} = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 D Z \tan\alpha$$

#### Step 4: Theoretical flow

$$Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N$$

#### Step 5: Numerical Example

> **Example:** A 9-piston axial pump with $d = 25\;\text{mm}$, $D = 80\;\text{mm}$, $\alpha = 20^\circ$, $N = 1450\;\text{rpm}$.
>
> Compute stroke:
> $$L = D \tan\alpha = 0.080 \cdot \tan(20^\circ) = 0.080 \cdot 0.3640 = 0.02912\;\text{m}$$
>
> Piston area:
> $$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^2$$
>
> Volume per piston:
> $$V_{piston} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.02912 = 1.429 \times 10^{-5}\;\text{m}^3/\text{rev}$$
>
> Total displacement:
> $$V_D = 9 \times 1.429 \times 10^{-5} = 1.286 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^3/\text{rev}$$
>
> Theoretical flow at 1450 rpm:
> $$Q_{th} = 1.286 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{1450}{60} = 3.107 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s} \approx 3.11\;\text{L/s}$$

This is the size of a small excavator's main pump. ✓

### 3.3 Derivation of Theoretical Torque

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show that the torque required to drive a PD pump is $T = \dfrac{V_D \Delta p}{2\pi}$.

Consider one revolution. The pump delivers volume $V_D$ against pressure rise $\Delta p$. The work done by the pump on the fluid per revolution is:

$$W = \Delta p \cdot V_D$$

Since work per revolution = torque × angle per revolution:

$$W = T \cdot 2\pi$$

Equating:

$$T \cdot 2\pi = \Delta p \cdot V_D \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T = \frac{V_D \Delta p}{2\pi}$$

This equation is **universal** for any PD pump — gear, vane, or piston. It is one of the most powerful equations in fluid power.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pressure Limits & Selection

| Criterion | Vane (Balanced) | Axial Piston (Swash) | Radial Piston |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Max pressure** | 175 bar | 350 bar | 700 bar |
| **Efficiency @ max P** | 0.85 | 0.92 | 0.93 |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Noise** | Low | Medium–High | Medium |
| **Variable displacement?** | Yes (move ring) | Yes (tilt swash) | Yes (move cam) |
| **Self-priming?** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Typical life (B10)** | 10 000 h | 20 000 h | 30 000 h |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture — Vane Pump

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prime Mover<br/>Electric Motor / Engine] -->|Torque T| B(Pump Shaft)
    B --> C{Rotor<br/>carries Z sliding vanes}
    C -->|Vanes slide radially| D[Stator Cam Ring<br/>offset by eccentricity e]
    D --> E[Chambers expand<br/>on suction side]
    D --> F[Chambers contract<br/>on discharge side]
    E -->|Suction port| G[Inlet from reservoir]
    F -->|Discharge port| H[High-pressure outlet to system]
    H --> I[Pressure compensator<br/>spool signal]
    I -->|Feedback| D
    H --> J[Hydraulic Actuator / Motor]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Axial Piston Pump

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Drive Shaft] --> S[Spline to Cylinder Block]
    S --> CB[Cylinder Block rotates<br/>carries Z pistons]
    CB --> P1[Piston 1 stroke outward - suction]
    CB --> P2[Piston 2 stroke inward - discharge]
    CB --> P3[Piston 3 in transition]
    CB --> P4[Piston 4 stroke outward - suction]
    P1 --> IN[Inlet Half of Valve Plate]
    P2 --> OUT[Outlet Half of Valve Plate]
    IN --> SP[Swash Plate tilted at angle alpha]
    OUT --> SP
    SP -->|Normal force on shoes| CB
    SP -->|Control signal from PC| V[Pressure Compensator]
    V -->|Reduces alpha| SP
    OUT --> SYS[Pressurized Oil to Actuators]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Pump Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Required Pressure p bar]) --> Q{Pressure range}
    Q -->|p less than 100| G[Gear Pump - cheapest]
    Q -->|p 100 to 175| V[Vane Pump - quiet, smooth]
    Q -->|p 175 to 350| AP[Axial Piston - workhorse]
    Q -->|p above 350| RP[Radial Piston - heavy duty]
    V --> VQ{Variable flow needed?}
    VQ -->|Yes| VVR[Variable Vane Pump]
    VQ -->|No| VFX[Fixed Vane Pump]
    AP --> APQ{Very high speed?}
    APQ -->|Yes| ABX[Bent-Axis Type]
    APQ -->|No| ASP[Swash-Plate Type]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Variable Displacement Vane Pump (Pressure Compensated)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TANK[(Reservoir)] -->|Suction line| IN[Inlet Port]
    IN --> PUMP[Stator Ring<br/>eccentricity e controlled]
    PUMP --> OUT[Outlet Port]
    OUT --> SYS[System / Actuator]
    OUT -->|Pressure signal| LS[Load Sense Line]
    LS --> PCV[Pressure Compensator Valve]
    PCV -->|Hydraulic force F_p| PISTON[Control Piston]
    SPRING[Set Spring F_s] --> PISTON
    PISTON -->|Moves stator ring| PUMP
    SYS -->|Return line| TANK
    RV[Relief Valve] --> SYS
    RV --> TANK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** When system pressure rises above the spring setting, the compensator spool shifts, routing pilot pressure to the back of the control piston. The piston moves the stator ring toward $e = 0$, reducing flow to near zero. Only leakage flow continues, maintaining standby pressure with **negligible power consumption**.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set A]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between a positive displacement pump and a non-positive displacement (centrifugal) pump. Give one example of each.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A **positive displacement (PD) pump** traps a fixed volume of fluid per cycle and forces it into the discharge line; its output is essentially independent of discharge pressure. Examples: **vane pump, piston pump, gear pump.**
> A **non-PD (centrifugal) pump** imparts kinetic energy to the fluid using an impeller; its output **decreases** as discharge pressure increases. Example: **centrifugal pump** used in water supply.
> (3 marks: 1 PD definition, 1 non-PD definition, 1 example each = 3)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set B]**
> **Q2.** List any **three advantages** of a variable-displacement axial piston pump over a fixed-displacement gear pump.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Energy saving at standby** — flow reduces to near zero at high pressure, eliminating the relief-valve dump loss of fixed pumps.
> 2. **Higher pressure capability** — up to 350 bar versus ~210 bar for gears.
> 3. **Better volumetric efficiency** — typically 95–98 % versus 85–92 % for gears.
> 4. (Optional) Faster response to load demands in load-sensing circuits.
> (Any 3 × 1 mark = 3 marks)

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Vane Pump Focus

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a balanced vane pump**. Highlight how it differs from an unbalanced vane pump. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A balanced vane pump has the following specifications:
> - Vane width $b = 40\;\text{mm}$
> - Eccentricity $e = 4\;\text{mm}$
> - Rotor speed $N = 1440\;\text{rpm}$
> - Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.95$
> - Mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.90$
> - Delivery pressure $\Delta p = 100\;\text{bar}$
>
> Calculate: **(i)** theoretical flow, **(ii)** actual flow, **(iii)** theoretical torque, **(iv)** actual driving power. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction & Working of Balanced Vane Pump (7 marks)**

A balanced vane pump consists of:

1. **Cylindrical rotor** mounted on the drive shaft, carrying $Z$ radial slots (typically 10–12). Vanes slide in these slots.
2. **Vanes** — thin rectangular blades, often spring-loaded at the bottom and pressure-loaded at the top (for tip pressure balancing).
3. **Cam ring (stator)** — circular inner bore, positioned with eccentricity $e$ relative to the rotor.
4. **Two side plates (port plates)** carrying **two inlet ports** diametrically opposite each other and **two outlet ports** similarly arranged.
5. **Inlet and outlet ports** connected to suction strainer and discharge line respectively.

**Working:**

- Rotor rotates inside cam ring.
- Each vane, kept pressed against the cam ring by centrifugal force and oil pressure, slides in and out of its slot.
- The chamber between two consecutive vanes **expands** as it crosses the inlet ports → fluid is sucked in.
- It then **contracts** as it crosses the outlet ports → fluid is discharged at high pressure.
- One full revolution → one suction and one discharge for each chamber.

**Difference from unbalanced vane pump:**

| Feature | Unbalanced | Balanced |
|---|---|---|
| No. of inlet ports | 1 | 2 |
| No. of outlet ports | 1 | 2 |
| Net radial force on rotor | Large (one-sided) | Cancelled (symmetrical) |
| Bearing load | High | Low |
| Max pressure | ~70 bar | ~175 bar |
| Smoothness of flow | Pulsating | Smoother |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |

(7 marks: 2 construction diagram + 2 working + 3 comparison table)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
$b = 40\;\text{mm} = 0.040\;\text{m}$
$e = 4\;\text{mm} = 0.004\;\text{m}$
$N = 1440\;\text{rpm} = 24\;\text{rps}$
$\eta_v = 0.95,\;\; \eta_m = 0.90$
$\Delta p = 100\;\text{bar} = 100 \times 10^5\;\text{Pa} = 1.0 \times 10^7\;\text{Pa}$

**(i) Theoretical flow**

$$V_D = 2\pi b e = 2\pi \cdot 0.040 \cdot 0.004 = 1.0053 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^3/\text{rev}$$

$$Q_{th} = V_D \cdot N = 1.0053 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 24 = 2.413 \times 10^{-2}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} = 24.13\;\text{L/s}}$$

**(ii) Actual flow**

$$Q_{act} = \eta_v \cdot Q_{th} = 0.95 \times 24.13 = 22.92\;\text{L/s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} = 22.92\;\text{L/s}}$$

**(iii) Theoretical torque**

$$T_{th} = \frac{V_D \cdot \Delta p}{2\pi} = \frac{1.0053 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.0 \times 10^7}{2\pi}$$

$$T_{th} = \frac{10053}{6.2832} = 1600.0\;\text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{th} = 1600\;\text{N·m}}$$

**(iv) Actual driving power**

Overall efficiency $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.95 \times 0.90 = 0.855$.

$$P_{hyd} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{act} = 1.0 \times 10^7 \cdot 22.92 \times 10^{-3} = 2.292 \times 10^5\;\text{W} = 229.2\;\text{kW}$$

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_o} = \frac{229.2}{0.855} = 268.1\;\text{kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{shaft} \approx 268\;\text{kW}}$$

(7 marks: 1 formula + 2 calculation + 1 formula + 1 calculation + 1 formula + 1 final answer)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Piston Pump Focus

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Explain with a sketch the **construction and working of an axial piston pump (swash-plate type)**. How is displacement varied in this pump? **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** An axial piston pump has 9 pistons, each of diameter $25\;\text{mm}$. The piston circle diameter is $80\;\text{mm}$. The swash plate is set at $18^\circ$. The pump runs at $1450\;\text{rpm}$ against a pressure of $200\;\text{bar}$. Volumetric efficiency is $96\%$, mechanical efficiency is $92\%$. Find: **(i)** theoretical discharge in L/min, **(ii)** actual discharge, **(iii)** input torque, **(iv)** input power. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Axial Piston Pump (Swash Plate) — Construction & Working (7 marks)**

**Constructional elements:**

1. **Drive shaft** with integral flange / spline.
2. **Cylinder block** (rotor) — bored with $Z$ parallel cylinder bores on a circle of diameter $D$. Each bore carries a piston.
3. **Pistons** — connected via **piston shoes** (ball-and-socket or slipper type) to the swash plate.
4. **Swash plate (wobble plate)** — mounted at angle $\alpha$ to the shaft axis. Sometimes called the "**inclined plate**."
5. **Valve plate (port plate)** — stationary, with kidney-shaped inlet and outlet ports that align with the cylinder bores.
6. **Swash plate tilting mechanism** — hydraulic servo / mechanical lever to vary $\alpha$ for variable displacement.
7. **Pressure compensator** (optional) — for load-sensing / constant-pressure control.

**Working:**

- Drive shaft rotates cylinder block.
- Each piston shoe rides on swash plate; piston axis stays parallel to shaft.
- As cylinder block rotates, piston shoes trace a circle on the swash plate.
- On one side of the block, the swash plate forces pistons **into** their bores → **discharge** stroke.
- On the opposite side, the pistons are **pushed out** by swash plate reaction (via fluid column) → **suction** stroke.
- The valve plate separates inlet and outlet ports by sealing lands.
- All pistons pump in **parallel** to the common inlet and outlet.

**Variation of displacement:** By tilting the swash plate through angle $\alpha$, the stroke $L = D \tan\alpha$ changes continuously from $L = 0$ ($\alpha = 0$, zero displacement) to $L_{max}$ ($\alpha = \alpha_{max}$).

(7 marks: 3 construction + 2 working + 2 displacement variation)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:**
$Z = 9,\;\; d = 25\;\text{mm} = 0.025\;\text{m},\;\; D = 80\;\text{mm} = 0.080\;\text{m}$
$\alpha = 18^\circ,\;\; N = 1450\;\text{rpm},\;\; \Delta p = 200\;\text{bar} = 2.0 \times 10^7\;\text{Pa}$
$\eta_v = 0.96,\;\; \eta_m = 0.92$

**Stroke length:**
$$L = D \tan\alpha = 0.080 \cdot \tan(18^\circ) = 0.080 \cdot 0.3249 = 0.02599\;\text{m}$$

**Piston area:**
$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^2$$

**Total displacement:**
$$V_D = Z A_p L = 9 \cdot 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.02599 = 1.1482 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^3/\text{rev}$$

**(i) Theoretical discharge:**
$$Q_{th} = V_D \cdot \frac{N}{60} = 1.1482 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \frac{1450}{60}$$

$$Q_{th} = 1.1482 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 24.167 = 2.774 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to L/min: $Q_{th} = 2.774 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 60\,000 = 166.5\;\text{L/min}$

$$\boxed{Q_{th} \approx 166.5\;\text{L/min}}$$

**(ii) Actual discharge:**
$$Q_{act} = \eta_v Q_{th} = 0.96 \cdot 166.5 = 159.8\;\text{L/min}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{act} \approx 159.8\;\text{L/min}}$$

**(iii) Theoretical torque:**
$$T_{th} = \frac{V_D \Delta p}{2\pi} = \frac{1.1482 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 2.0 \times 10^7}{2\pi} = \frac{2296.4}{6.2832} = 365.5\;\text{N·m}$$

Actual input torque (using $\eta_m$):
$$T_{in} = \frac{T_{th}}{\eta_m} = \frac{365.5}{0.92} = 397.3\;\text{N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T_{in} \approx 397\;\text{N·m}}$$

**(iv) Input power:**
$$P_{hyd} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{act} = 2.0 \times 10^7 \cdot 159.8 \times 10^{-3}/60 = 2.0 \times 10^7 \cdot 2.663 \times 10^{-3} = 53.27\;\text{kW}$$

$$P_{in} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_o} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_v \cdot \eta_m} = \frac{53.27}{0.96 \cdot 0.92} = \frac{53.27}{0.8832} = 60.32\;\text{kW}$$

Alternative direct check: $P_{in} = T_{in} \cdot \omega = 397.3 \cdot \frac{2\pi \cdot 1450}{60} = 397.3 \cdot 151.84 = 60.34\;\text{kW}$ ✓

$$\boxed{P_{in} \approx 60.3\;\text{kW}}$$

(7 marks: 1 formula + 2 calc + 1 formula + 1 calc + 1 formula + 1 final)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion blunders:** Writing $Q$ in L/s instead of L/min when asked, or omitting the $\div 60$ for $N$ in rpm. Always carry units through the calculation.
> 2. **Confusing $Q_{th}$ and $Q_{act}$:** If the question asks for *actual* discharge, you MUST apply $\eta_v$. If it asks for *input* power or torque, you MUST apply $\eta_m$ or $\eta_o$.
> 3. **Wrong trig in piston stroke:** Writing $L = D \sin\alpha$ instead of $L = D \tan\alpha$. The piston axis is perpendicular to the swash plate, so the projection of the vane tip's circular motion onto the axis is **tangent**, not **sine**, of $\alpha$. (Memorize this — KTU loves to test it.)
> 4. **Vane pump formula forgotten:** Writing $V_D = \pi b e$ instead of $2\pi b e$. The factor of 2 is non-negotiable; it comes from the vane tip tracing a circle of radius $e$ and the volume *change* equalling twice the swept area of one tip circle.
> 5. **Drawing a *centrifugal* pump diagram** when a vane/piston pump is asked — KTU examiners will deduct **2 to 3 marks** outright.
> 6. **Confusing balanced and unbalanced vane pumps** in the comparison table — distinguish by **number of ports** (1 vs 2 pairs) and **radial thrust** (present vs cancelled).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Save this section for last-day revision before the KTU exam.**

- [ ] A **PD pump** delivers **fixed volume per revolution**, irrespective of pressure (up to its rating).
- [ ] **Vane pump** components: **Rotor, Vanes, Stator (Cam Ring), Side/Port Plates.** Vanes slide radially; chamber volume changes due to stator eccentricity $e$.
- [ ] **Vane pump displacement:** $V_D = 2\pi b e$. **Independent** of vanes count and rotor radius.
- [ ] **Balanced vane pump** has **2 inlet + 2 outlet ports**, cancels radial thrust, runs up to **175 bar**.
- [ ] **Unbalanced vane pump** has 1 inlet + 1 outlet, limited to **~70 bar** due to radial bearing load.
- [ ] **Variable vane pump** moves stator ring; **standby** at $e = 0$ with negligible power consumption.
- [ ] **Axial piston pump** uses **swash plate** at angle $\alpha$. Stroke = $D \tan\alpha$. Displacement $V_D = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 D Z \tan\alpha$.
- [ ] **Radial piston pump** uses **eccentric cam ring** with stroke $= 2e$. $V_D = \dfrac{\pi}{2} Z d^2 e$.
- [ ] **Bent-axis** axial pump has no swash plate — cylinder block itself is inclined; higher pressure capability (450 bar) and higher speed tolerance.
- [ ] **Universal torque formula (all PD pumps):** $T_{th} = \dfrac{V_D \Delta p}{2\pi}$. **Universal power:** $P_{hyd} = \Delta p \cdot Q_{act}$.
- [ ] **Volumetric efficiency:** $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$. **Mechanical efficiency:** $\eta_m = \dfrac{T_{th}}{T_{act}}$. **Overall:** $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$.
- [ ] **Pressure rating ladder:** Gear < Vane < Axial Piston < Radial Piston (≈ 210 < 175 < 350 < 700 bar).
- [ ] **Variable displacement** mechanisms: move stator (vane), tilt swash (axial), move cam (radial).
- [ ] **Pressure-compensated standby** = energy-saving feature unique to variable PD pumps; used in machine tools and modern mobile hydraulics.
- [ ] **Number of vanes** in a vane pump does NOT change $V_D$ — it only smooths flow (reduces pulsation).
- [ ] **Number of pistons $Z$** directly scales $V_D$ in piston pumps.
- [ ] Always **convert $N$ from rpm to rps** before substituting into $Q = V_D N$ in SI units; or divide by 60.
- [ ] Always state **assumptions**: $Z$, $\eta_v$, $\eta_m$, fluid type, temperature when relevant.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pump Performance
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT722 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Pump Performance

# ⚙️ MODULE 2 — PUMP PERFORMANCE
## A Complete KTU 2024 Scheme Study Unit

---

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pump Performance** is defined as the quantitative characterization of a hydraulic pump's operating behaviour expressed as a set of interrelated curves and efficiency indices that relate **delivered flow rate (Q)**, **developed pressure (p)**, **input power (P)**, and **overall efficiency (ηₒ)** to the pump's rotational speed (N) and working fluid properties.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, pump performance is evaluated using **three primary performance curves** and **three discrete efficiency values** that govern whether a pump is suitable for a given hydraulic circuit.

### 1.2 Key Performance Metrics (KTU Mandatory)

| Metric | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Actual Flow Rate | **Q** | Volume of fluid actually delivered per unit time (m³/s or LPM) |
| Theoretical Flow Rate | **Qₜ** | Geometric displacement × speed (ideal, no losses) |
| Volumetric Efficiency | **ηᵥ** | Ratio of actual flow to theoretical flow |
| Mechanical Efficiency | **ηₘ** | Ratio of theoretical torque to actual torque |
| Overall Efficiency | **ηₒ** | Ratio of hydraulic output power to shaft input power |
| Slip | **S** | Quantity of fluid lost internally per revolution (cm³/rev) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Conditions (KTU Convention):** Performance data is always reported at fluid temperature of **50 °C**, kinematic viscosity of **30 cSt**, and inlet pressure of **1 bar (atmospheric)** unless otherwise stated in the problem.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaky Bucket" Mental Model

Imagine you are filling water buckets from a tap while simultaneously a small hole at the bottom of the bucket drains water out. The **geometrical flow** is how much water leaves the tap — that is your **theoretical flow (Qₜ)**. The **actual flow** you manage to keep in the bucket is less, because some escaped through the hole — that is your **delivered flow (Q)**. The fraction you retain divided by what came in is the **volumetric efficiency (ηᵥ)**.

This is exactly how a hydraulic pump behaves: the gears, pistons, or vanes physically displace a volume of oil, but a small portion always **leaks back** through clearances between the moving parts due to pressure differentials. That leaked oil is called **slip**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Slip is NOT a manufacturing defect — it is an **inherent physical limitation** caused by fluid dynamics in the clearance gaps. Even a perfectly machined pump has slip.

### 1.4 Why Pump Performance Matters in Industrial Hydraulics

- **System Design:** Selecting the correct pump displacement for cylinder speed calculations.
- **Energy Audit:** Identifying wasted power in a hydraulic press, injection moulding machine, or excavator.
- **Troubleshooting:** A sudden drop in volumetric efficiency signals **wear, contamination, or overheating**.
- **Thermal Management:** Losses convert to heat, raising reservoir temperature and accelerating oil degradation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Three Fundamental Performance Curves

A pump's behaviour is communicated graphically through **three curves**, all plotted with flow rate (Q) on the X-axis:

#### (a) Volumetric Efficiency Curve — ηᵥ vs Q
- Starts near **100 %** at very low Q (no internal leakage paths are pressurized).
- Decreases almost linearly as delivery pressure and Q increase.
- Typical gear pump: **85–95 %**; vane pump: **90–96 %**; piston pump: **95–99 %**.

#### (b) Pressure / Load Curve — p vs Q
- Shows the maximum pressure the pump can sustain against a given flow.
- For a fixed-displacement pump at constant speed, p is essentially **constant** (limited only by the relief valve).
- The curve naturally droops slightly because higher Q → more leakage → less available pressure.

#### (c) Power Curve — P vs Q
- Input power increases **linearly** with delivery pressure.
- Output (hydraulic) power = p × Q.

### 2.2 The Three Efficiency Definitions (Hierarchical Logic)

The KTU 2024 scheme specifically distinguishes between three layers of efficiency. Let us understand the logical chain:

**Step 1 → Volumetric Efficiency (ηᵥ):**
Because the pump physically displaces V_d cm³/rev at N rpm, but delivers less due to internal leakage (slip S), the actual flow is:

$$Q = \frac{(V_d - S) \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{[LPM]}$$

**Step 2 → Mechanical Efficiency (ηₘ):**
Even after accounting for leakage, the **torque required** to drive the pump is greater than theoretically needed, due to **friction** between rotating parts, bearings, and fluid shearing.

$$\eta_m = \frac{T_{ideal}}{T_{actual}}$$

**Step 3 → Overall Efficiency (ηₒ):**
This is the **master multiplier** combining both losses:

$$\eta_o = \eta_v \times \eta_m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick:** If a problem gives "volumetric efficiency = 90 %" and asks for overall efficiency, **you cannot compute it** unless mechanical efficiency is separately given. ηₒ ≠ ηᵥ alone.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical flow rate | $Q_t = \dfrac{V_d \cdot N}{60}$ | m³/s (if V_d in m³) |
| 2 | Actual flow rate | $Q = Q_t \cdot \eta_v$ | m³/s |
| 3 | Slip volume | $S = V_d - \dfrac{Q \cdot 60}{N}$ | cm³/rev |
| 4 | Volumetric efficiency | $\eta_v = \dfrac{Q}{Q_t} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Hydraulic power (output) | $P_h = p \cdot Q$ | W (if p in Pa, Q in m³/s) |
| 6 | Shaft power (input) | $P_{in} = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{\eta_o}$ | W |
| 7 | Hydraulic power in kW | $P_h = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{600}$ | kW (p in bar, Q in LPM) |
| 8 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Specific speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \cdot \sqrt{Q}}{H^{0.75}}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | NPSH available | $NPSH_a = \dfrac{p_i}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_i^2}{2g} - \dfrac{p_v}{\rho g}$ | m of fluid |
| 11 | Torque on shaft | $T = \dfrac{p \cdot V_d}{2\pi \cdot \eta_m}$ | N·m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Conversion Trap:** Always verify whether the examiner wants power in **W**, **kW**, or **HP**. The factor of **600** in formula (7) assumes **p in bar** and **Q in LPM**. If p is in Pascal and Q in m³/s, the factor becomes **1.0**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile hydraulics** (JCBs, excavators): Pump performance curves help match the pump to the duty cycle of swing and boom actuators.
- **Injection moulding machines:** High ηₒ reduces electricity bills and prevents oil overheating during the 30-second hold phase.
- **Press tools & machine tools:** Performance curves identify the optimum operating point where the pump neither wastes energy nor causes excessive wear.
- **Aviation hydraulics:** Specific speed Ns determines the impeller geometry best suited for the system.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Volumetric Efficiency from First Principles

**Given:**
- Pump geometric displacement V_d = 25 cm³/rev
- Pump speed N = 1450 rpm
- Measured (actual) flow Q = 28.5 LPM
- Delivery pressure p = 175 bar

**Find:** Volumetric efficiency, slip volume, and overall efficiency (assuming ηₘ = 90 %).

**Step 1 — Compute theoretical flow rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_t &= \frac{V_d \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{[LPM]} \\
&= \frac{25 \times 1450}{1000} \\
&= 36.25 \text{ LPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Each revolution displaces 25 cm³ = 0.025 L. At 1450 rpm, total displacement per minute = 0.025 × 1450 = 36.25 L.

**Step 2 — Compute volumetric efficiency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_v &= \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_t} \\
&= \frac{28.5}{36.25} \\
&= 0.7862 \\
&= 78.62\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute slip volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{slip} &= Q_t - Q_{actual} \\
&= 36.25 - 28.5 \\
&= 7.75 \text{ LPM} \\
\\
\text{Slip per revolution: } S &= \frac{Q_{slip} \times 1000}{N} \\
&= \frac{7.75 \times 1000}{1450} \\
&= 5.34 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* This 5.34 cm³ is the volume of oil that leaks back per revolution through clearances.

**Step 4 — Compute hydraulic output power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_h &= \frac{p \cdot Q}{600} \quad \text{[kW]} \\
&= \frac{175 \times 28.5}{600} \\
&= 8.31 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute overall efficiency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_o &= \eta_v \times \eta_m \\
&= 0.7862 \times 0.90 \\
&= 0.7076 \\
&= 70.76\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute shaft input power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= \frac{P_h}{\eta_o} \\
&= \frac{8.31}{0.7076} \\
&= 11.74 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Specific Speed (Nₛ)

**Specific speed** is a dimensionless number used to classify pumps by their geometric similarity. Two pumps with the same Nₛ are dynamically similar.

**Derivation logic:**

When pump speed (N), flow (Q), and head (H) are related through dimensional homogeneity, the dimensionless group is formed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= N \cdot \frac{\sqrt{Q}}{(gH)^{3/4}} \quad \text{[SI units, m and W]} \\
\\
\text{Or in practical form:} \quad N_s &= \frac{N \cdot \sqrt{Q}}{H^{0.75}}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* The exponent 0.75 on H arises from dimensional analysis combining rotational frequency, volume flow, and pressure head.

**Classification Table (KTU standard):**

| Nₛ Range | Pump Type | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| 10 – 35 | Centrifugal (low) | High-head boiler feed |
| 35 – 80 | Mixed flow | Irrigation |
| 80 – 300 | Axial flow | High-flow drainage |
| 300 – 1000 | Propeller | Low-head circulators |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Pump Types

| Parameter | Gear Pump | Vane Pump | Piston Pump |
|---|---|---|---|
| Typical ηᵥ | 85 – 90 % | 90 – 95 % | 96 – 99 % |
| Typical ηₘ | 90 % | 92 % | 95 % |
| Typical ηₒ | 75 – 85 % | 85 – 90 % | 92 – 97 % |
| Max Pressure | 200 bar | 175 bar | 700 bar |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Noise Level | High | Medium | Low |
| KTU Application | Tractors, Loaders | Machine tools | Presses, Aircraft |

---

### 3.4 NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head) Derivation

**NPSH** is the energy margin above vapour pressure available at the pump suction port to prevent **cavitation**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPSH_a &= \frac{p_i - p_v}{\rho g} + \frac{v_i^2}{2g} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- **p_i** = absolute pressure at pump inlet (Pa)
- **p_v** = vapour pressure of fluid at operating temperature (Pa)
- **v_i** = velocity at pump inlet (m/s)
- **ρ** = fluid density (kg/m³)
- **g** = 9.81 m/s²

**Cavitation rule:** Cavitation occurs when **NPSHₐ < NPSHᵣ (required)**. The required value is given by the manufacturer; available is computed from system geometry.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget that **atmospheric pressure** (1.013 bar) and **vapour pressure of oil at 50 °C** (~0.04 bar) must be used as absolute values, not gauge. This single mistake causes 3-mark deductions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Performance Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electric Motor / Diesel Engine] --> B[Shaft Coupling]
    B --> C[Hydraulic Pump]
    C --> D{Internal Clearances}
    D --> E[Slip Path - Leakage Back to Inlet]
    D --> F[Delivered Flow Q - To System]
    F --> G[Pressure Relief Valve]
    F --> H[Direction Control Valve]
    H --> I[Hydraulic Actuator Cylinder or Motor]
    I --> J[Useful Mechanical Work Output]
    E --> K[Heat Generation in Reservoir]
    F --> K
    K --> L[Temperature Rise - Affects Viscosity and eta_v]
    L --> M[Reduced Pump Performance Loop]
    M --> C
```

*Explanation:* This flowchart shows the closed-loop nature of pump performance. Slip converts mechanical energy into heat, which raises oil temperature, which reduces viscosity, which alters clearances, which changes ηᵥ. This is the **thermal-feedback loop** KTU examiners love to test.

---

### 4.2 Pump Performance Curve Family

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Flow Rate Q in LPM on X-axis] --> Y1[Pressure Curve p vs Q]
    X --> Y2[Volumetric Efficiency Curve eta_v vs Q]
    X --> Y3[Overall Efficiency Curve eta_o vs Q]
    X --> Y4[Input Power Curve P_in vs Q]
    X --> Y5[Slip Curve S vs Q]
    Y1 --> Z[Operating Envelope of Pump]
    Y2 --> Z
    Y3 --> Z
    Y4 --> Z
    Y5 --> Z
```

*Explanation:* Each curve is plotted against Q. The intersection of ηₒ peak and typical operating Q is the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** — the designer's choice for normal operation.

---

### 4.3 Energy Conversion Flow in a Pump

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Shaft Power Input P_in] --> B[Theoretical Hydraulic Power P_th]
    A --> C[Mechanical Losses: Friction in Bearings and Gears]
    C --> A
    B --> D[Hydraulic Output Power P_h]
    B --> E[Volumetric Losses: Internal Slip and Leakage]
    E --> B
    D --> F[Useful Work at Actuator]
    E --> G[Heat Dissipated to Reservoir]
    C --> G
```

*Explanation:* This Sankey-style block diagram traces how the input shaft power splits into useful hydraulic power and the two loss channels. The **width** of the arrows in a real chart would represent the magnitude of energy flow.

---

### 4.4 Cavitation Threshold Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute NPSH available from System Geometry] --> B{Is NPSH_a greater than NPSH required?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Safe Operation - No Cavitation]
    B -->|No| D[Cavitation Occurs]
    D --> E[Bubble Collapse on Impeller Surface]
    E --> F[Metal Erosion and Noise]
    E --> G[Loss of Flow and Pressure]
    E --> H[Reduced eta_v and eta_o]
    F --> I[Pump Failure]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

*Explanation:* This decision tree is a direct translation of the NPSH analysis. KTU often asks: *"What two corrections can be made if cavitation is detected?"* — Answers: (i) increase inlet pipe diameter to reduce velocity, (ii) reduce fluid temperature to lower vapour pressure, (iii) lower the pump installation height.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** Define *volumetric efficiency* and *mechanical efficiency* of a hydraulic pump. How are they combined to give the *overall efficiency*?

**Model Answer:**

> **Volumetric efficiency (ηᵥ):** It is the ratio of the actual (delivered) flow rate to the theoretical flow rate of the pump.
>
> $$\eta_v = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$
>
> It accounts for the internal leakage of fluid from the high-pressure side back to the low-pressure side due to clearances between moving parts.
>
> **Mechanical efficiency (ηₘ):** It is the ratio of the theoretical torque required to displace the fluid to the actual torque supplied at the pump shaft.
>
> $$\eta_m = \frac{T_{theoretical}}{T_{actual}}$$
>
> It accounts for friction losses in bearings, seals, and between rotating parts.
>
> **Overall efficiency (ηₒ):** The product of the two:
>
> $$\boxed{\eta_o = \eta_v \times \eta_m}$$
>
> Overall efficiency represents the fraction of input shaft power that is converted into useful hydraulic output power.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** What is *slip* in a hydraulic pump? How does it vary with delivery pressure?

**Model Answer:**

> **Slip (S):** Slip is the volume of fluid that leaks internally from the high-pressure delivery side back to the low-pressure suction side of the pump per unit time (or per revolution), without performing useful work.
>
> Mathematically:
>
> $$S = \frac{(Q_t - Q) \times 1000}{N} \quad \text{[cm}^3/\text{rev]}$$
>
> **Variation with pressure:** Slip increases approximately **linearly** with delivery pressure. This is because higher pressure creates a larger pressure differential across the internal clearances, which drives more leakage flow according to the orifice equation:
>
> $$Q_{leak} \propto \sqrt{\Delta p}$$
>
> Hence, as delivery pressure rises, ηᵥ drops.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A — Full 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question Set, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A hydraulic pump has a geometric displacement of 45 cm³/rev and runs at 1440 rpm. When delivering fluid at a pressure of 200 bar, the actual flow rate is measured as 60 LPM. Compute:
- (i) Theoretical flow rate
- (ii) Volumetric efficiency
- (iii) Slip volume per revolution
- (iv) Hydraulic power output in kW **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the mechanical efficiency of the same pump is 92 %, determine the overall efficiency and the shaft input power required. Also, calculate the driving torque on the motor shaft. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION

#### Part (a)

**Step 1: Theoretical flow rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_t &= \frac{V_d \cdot N}{1000} \\
&= \frac{45 \times 1440}{1000} \\
&= 64.8 \text{ LPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Volumetric efficiency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_v &= \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_t} = \frac{60}{64.8} \\
&= 0.9259 \\
&= 92.59\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Slip volume per revolution**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{slip} &= Q_t - Q_{actual} \\
&= 64.8 - 60 = 4.8 \text{ LPM} \\
\\
S &= \frac{Q_{slip} \times 1000}{N} = \frac{4.8 \times 1000}{1440} \\
&= 3.33 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Hydraulic output power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_h &= \frac{p \cdot Q}{600} = \frac{200 \times 60}{600} \\
&= 20 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Part (b)

**Step 5: Overall efficiency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_o &= \eta_v \times \eta_m \\
&= 0.9259 \times 0.92 \\
&= 0.8518 \\
&= 85.18\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Shaft input power**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= \frac{P_h}{\eta_o} = \frac{20}{0.8518} \\
&= 23.48 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Driving torque**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \\
\\
T &= \frac{P_{in} \times 60}{2\pi N} \\
&= \frac{23{,}480 \times 60}{2\pi \times 1440} \\
&= 155.76 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### QUESTION B — Alternative 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Question Set, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **performance characteristic curves** of a hydraulic pump. Indicate the *Best Efficiency Point (BEP)* and explain why pumps are operated near this point. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump is to deliver 0.05 m³/s of water against a head of 25 m at a speed of 1450 rpm. Calculate its **specific speed** and hence identify the type of pump (radial, mixed, or axial). Also discuss the significance of specific speed in pump selection. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION

#### Part (a)

**Characteristic curves** of a hydraulic pump (constant speed) typically include:

1. **Pressure vs Flow (p–Q):** Pressure available at pump outlet drops slightly as flow increases (due to internal leakage).
2. **Volumetric Efficiency vs Flow (ηᵥ–Q):** Decreases with increasing flow and pressure.
3. **Overall Efficiency vs Flow (ηₒ–Q):** Rises to a peak then falls — the peak defines the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**.
4. **Input Power vs Flow (P–Q):** Increases approximately linearly with flow × pressure.
5. **Slip vs Pressure (S–p):** Linear increase.

**Why BEP?**
- Minimum wear and longest life.
- Minimum noise and vibration.
- Minimum heat generation.
- Minimum specific energy consumption per unit flow.

**Sketch description:**
```
  ↑
p │‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾\
  │            \
  │             \________  ← p-Q curve
  │
  │       ___
ηₒ│      /   \___        ← ηₒ-Q curve, peak is BEP
  │     /        ‾‾‾‾
  │____/_________________→ Q
       BEP
```

---

#### Part (b)

**Given:** Q = 0.05 m³/s, H = 25 m, N = 1450 rpm

**Specific speed formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= \frac{N \cdot \sqrt{Q}}{H^{0.75}} \\
&= \frac{1450 \times \sqrt{0.05}}{(25)^{0.75}} \\
&= \frac{1450 \times 0.2236}{11.18} \\
&= 29.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Classification (using metric units):**
- Nₛ between **20 and 40** ⇒ **Radial flow (centrifugal) pump**.

**Significance:**
1. **Geometric similarity:** Two pumps with the same Nₛ are dynamically similar and can be scaled.
2. **Pump selection:** Tells the designer whether a radial, mixed, or axial pump is most efficient for given Q and H.
3. **Cavitation prediction:** Helps predict susceptibility to cavitation at off-design conditions.
4. **Performance scaling:** Allows estimation of new performance when speed or size changes.

---

### 🚨 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks (Module 2 — Pump Performance):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 1000** when converting V_d (cm³/rev) × N (rpm) into LPM. Always write the conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing up Qₜ and Q** in the ηᵥ formula. The formula is Q/Qₜ, never Qₜ/Q.
> 3. **Wrong power unit factor.** p × Q / 600 only works for **bar × LPM → kW**. For Pa × m³/s → W, the factor is 1.
> 4. **Treating ηᵥ alone as ηₒ.** They are different; examiner will deduct 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting absolute vs gauge pressure** in NPSH problems — this is the single biggest 3-mark loser.
> 6. **Not stating units** for slip — must be **cm³/rev**, not LPM.
> 7. **Skipping the LPM → m³/s conversion** when the formula expects SI units.
> 8. **Confusing Best Efficiency Point** with Maximum Flow Point — they are not the same.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ Pump performance = behaviour described by **p-Q, ηᵥ-Q, ηₒ-Q, and P-Q curves** at constant N.
- ✅ **Theoretical flow** Qₜ = V_d × N / 1000 (in LPM).
- ✅ **Actual flow** Q = Qₜ × ηᵥ.
- ✅ **Slip** S = (Qₜ − Q) × 1000 / N in cm³/rev; **increases with pressure**.
- ✅ **ηᵥ** = Q / Qₜ — accounts for leakage losses only.
- ✅ **ηₘ** = T_theoretical / T_actual — accounts for friction losses only.
- ✅ **ηₒ** = ηᵥ × ηₘ — the master efficiency.
- ✅ **Hydraulic power** P_h = p × Q (Pa·m³/s → W) or p × Q / 600 (bar·LPM → kW).
- ✅ **Shaft input power** P_in = P_h / ηₒ.
- ✅ **Specific speed** Nₛ = N√Q / H^0.75 — classifies pump as radial, mixed, or axial.
- ✅ **NPSHₐ** = (p_i − p_v)/(ρg) + v_i²/(2g). Cavitation if NPSHₐ < NPSHᵣ.
- ✅ **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** — the Q at which ηₒ is maximum; operate here for life and economy.
- ✅ Slip → heat → temperature rise → viscosity drop → further ηᵥ drop → **thermal runaway loop**.
- ✅ **Gear < Vane < Piston** in order of increasing ηₒ and cost.
- ✅ **Pressure relief valve** sets maximum delivery pressure for fixed-displacement pumps.
- ✅ Power loss appears as **heat**; the reservoir must dissipate **P_in − P_h** continuously.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT722 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristics and Selection - Sizing of Pumps

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (Course: OEMRT722), **Pump Characteristics** refers to the graphical and mathematical representation of a pump's performance behavior under varying operating conditions. The principal characteristic curves of a centrifugal (and other dynamic) pump include:

- **Head–Flow curve (H–Q):** The relationship between the developed head (in meters) and the volumetric flow rate delivered (in m³/s or LPM).
- **Efficiency–Flow curve (η–Q):** Variation of overall (hydraulic + volumetric + mechanical) efficiency with discharge.
- **Power–Flow curve (P–Q):** The brake power (shaft input) required versus the discharge.
- **NPSH–Flow curve (NPSHr–Q):** The Net Positive Suction Head required to prevent cavitation.

**Sizing of Pumps** is the engineering procedure for selecting a pump that meets the system requirements — namely, the required flow rate (Q), the total dynamic head (TDH), the available NPSH, the type of fluid, and the permissible operating speed and power — while operating at or near the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module mandates the study of pump performance curves, affinity laws, specific speed, NPSH, and systematic selection methodology. These are high-yield areas in University exams, often carrying 7–14 marks in Part B questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are filling buckets of water from a deep well using two different hand pumps:

- A **small, light pump** delivers water quickly (high Q) but cannot push it very high (low H). It is like a low-head, high-flow pump.
- A **large, heavy pump** pushes water to a great height (high H) but slowly (low Q). It is like a high-head, low-flow pump.

Every pump has a "personality" — its characteristic curve — that defines this trade-off. **Sizing** is the art of matching the pump's "personality" to the system's demand: the height the water must reach (head) and how fast it must reach (flow). Selecting a pump that is oversized wastes energy; one that is undersized will overheat and fail. The intersection of the **pump curve** and the **system curve** defines the **operating point**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used:**
> - Density of water, **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Acceleration due to gravity, **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Specific weight of water, **γ = 9810 N/m³**
> - Standard units: Q in **m³/s** or **LPM (Litres Per Minute)**, H in **m**, P in **W or kW**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Pump Performance Curve (H–Q Characteristic)

A typical centrifugal pump curve shows that as the **flow rate Q increases, the developed head H decreases** along a near-parabolic trajectory. Mathematically, this can be expressed as:

$$H = H_0 - k Q^2$$

Where:
- **H₀** = Shut-off head (head developed at zero flow, Q = 0)
- **k** = Curve constant, dependent on pump geometry
- **Q** = Volumetric discharge

The curve typically has three key operating regions:
- **Stable Region:** From shut-off to roughly 120% of BEP flow.
- **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** Where the pump operates at maximum η.
- **Unstable / Run-out Region:** Beyond ~120% of BEP, where efficiency drops sharply.

## 2.2 System Curve

The **system curve** represents the head demanded by the piping network as a function of flow. It is composed of:

- **Static Head (Hs):** The fixed elevation difference between source and discharge (independent of Q).
- **Friction Head Loss (Hf):** Varies with Q² (Darcy–Weisbach or Hazen-Williams).

$$H_{sys} = H_s + k_f Q^2$$

The **operating point** of the system is the intersection of the H–Q pump curve and the system curve.

## 2.3 Affinity Laws (Pump Scaling Laws)

The affinity laws govern how pump performance changes with impeller **diameter (D)** and **rotational speed (N)**.

### 2.3.1 For Change in Speed (N₁ → N₂) at Constant Diameter:

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

$$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$$

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$

### 2.3.2 For Change in Impeller Diameter (D₁ → D₂) at Constant Speed:

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{D_2}{D_1}$$

$$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$$

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^3$$

## 2.4 Specific Speed (Ns)

The specific speed is a dimensionless index that classifies pump impellers and predicts their shape and best operating regime.

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$$

Where:
- **N** = Rotational speed in **RPM**
- **Q** = Flow rate in **m³/s** (or **LPS** in metric)
- **H** = Head per stage in **meters**

| Specific Speed Range (Nₛ) | Pump Type | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 10 – 35 | Radial Flow (Francis) | High head, low flow |
| 35 – 80 | Mixed Flow | Medium head, medium flow |
| 80 – 300 | Axial Flow (Propeller) | Low head, high flow |
| 300+ | Special / Turbine | Very high flow |

## 2.5 Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)

**NPSH Available (NPSHa):** The absolute pressure head at the pump suction port, minus the vapor pressure head of the fluid.

$$NPSH_a = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + \frac{P_{static}}{\rho g} - \frac{P_{vapor}}{\rho g} - h_f$$

**NPSH Required (NPSHr):** The minimum head needed at the suction port to prevent cavitation, supplied by the manufacturer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cavitation Rule:** For safe operation, **NPSHa ≥ NPSHr + 0.5 m (safety margin)**. Cavitation causes pitting, vibration, noise, and efficiency loss.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Quick Reference)

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = H_0 - k Q^2$ | Pump H–Q Characteristic | H in m, Q in m³/s |
| 2 | $H_{sys} = H_s + k_f Q^2$ | System Curve | H in m, Q in m³/s |
| 3 | $Q_2/Q_1 = N_2/N_1$ | Affinity Law (Flow vs Speed) | — |
| 4 | $H_2/H_1 = (N_2/N_1)^2$ | Affinity Law (Head vs Speed) | — |
| 5 | $P_2/P_1 = (N_2/N_1)^3$ | Affinity Law (Power vs Speed) | P in W or kW |
| 6 | $N_s = N \sqrt{Q} / H^{3/4}$ | Specific Speed | N in RPM, Q in m³/s, H in m |
| 7 | $P = \rho g Q H / \eta$ | Hydraulic Power / Brake Power | P in W |
| 8 | $NPSH_a = (P_{atm} - P_v)/\rho g \pm h_s - h_f$ | Net Positive Suction Head Available | m |
| 9 | $Q_{actual} = Q_{th} \times \eta_{vol}$ | Actual Flow Considering Volumetric Efficiency | m³/s |
| 10 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{hyd} \times \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{mech}$ | Overall Pump Efficiency | Dimensionless |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Process Plants:** Selecting pumps for chemical transfer, cooling water, boiler feed.
- **HVAC Systems:** Sizing chilled water and condenser water pumps.
- **Municipal Water Supply:** Pumping stations for distribution networks.
- **Hydraulic Presses & Machine Tools (KTU OEMRT722 relevance):** Power units use fixed or variable displacement pumps, characterized by similar curves.
- **Energy Conservation:** Affinity laws enable variable frequency drive (VFD) energy savings of up to 50%.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Affinity Laws (From Euler's Turbomachinery Equation)

**Step 1 — Euler's Equation for Pump Head:**

The energy transferred per unit weight of fluid from the impeller to the fluid is given by Euler's turbomachinery equation:

$$H = \frac{1}{g} \left( U_2 V_{w2} - U_1 V_{w1} \right)$$

For a pure radial entry (no pre-swirl, $V_{w1} = 0$):

$$H = \frac{U_2 V_{w2}}{g}$$

**Step 2 — Relate Blade Velocity and Discharge Velocity to N and D:**

The tangential velocity of the impeller tip is:

$$U_2 = \pi D N / 60$$

The discharge (flow) velocity is proportional to the meridional velocity, which is itself proportional to the tangential velocity for geometrically similar pumps:

$$V_{w2} \propto U_2 \propto D N$$

The volumetric flow rate Q is proportional to the swept area of the impeller times the meridional velocity:

$$Q \propto D^2 \cdot V \propto D^2 \cdot (D N) \propto D^3 N$$

**Step 3 — Form the Ratios for Constant Geometry (Varying N):**

For geometrically similar operation with constant impeller diameter D:

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{D^3 N_2}{D^3 N_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

Since $H \propto U_2 V_{w2} \propto (D N)(D N) \propto D^2 N^2$:

$$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \frac{D^2 N_2^2}{D^2 N_1^2} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$$

Since Power $P = \rho g Q H$:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{Q_2 H_2}{Q_1 H_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$

This is the **cubic law of affinity** — a small change in speed produces a cube-root change in power, which is the basis of VFD energy savings.

## 3.2 Derivation of Pump Power Equations

**Step 1 — Hydraulic Power (Power imparted to the fluid):**

$$P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

**Step 2 — Shaft Power (Brake Power) considering efficiency:**

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{\rho g Q H}{\eta}$$

**Step 3 — Specific Speed Derivation:**

Specific speed is derived by making Q, H, and N dimensionless through a dimensionless analysis of pump similarity. For a unit head (H = 1 m) and unit flow (Q = 1 m³/s), the rotational speed of a geometrically similar pump is the specific speed. Thus:

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$$

The exponent **3/4** on H arises from the affinity law $(H \propto N^2)$ and the quadratic variation of head with capacity.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Numerical: Pump Sizing Using Affinity Law

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**

A centrifugal pump delivers 1500 LPM at 30 m head when running at 1450 RPM. If the speed is increased to 1750 RPM, calculate the new flow rate, head, and power required. The original power consumption was 12 kW.

**Given:**
- $Q_1$ = 1500 LPM = 0.025 m³/s
- $H_1$ = 30 m
- $N_1$ = 1450 RPM
- $N_2$ = 1750 RPM
- $P_1$ = 12 kW

**Step 1 — Apply Affinity Law for Flow:**

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

$$Q_2 = Q_1 \times \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 1500 \times \frac{1750}{1450}$$

$$Q_2 = 1500 \times 1.2069 = 1810.34 \text{ LPM}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Affinity Law for Head:**

$$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = (1.2069)^2$$

$$H_2 = 30 \times 1.4566 = 43.70 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Affinity Law for Power:**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = (1.2069)^3$$

$$P_2 = 12 \times 1.7577 = 21.09 \text{ kW}$$

**Result Verification:**

$$\eta = \frac{\rho g Q_1 H_1}{P_1} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.025 \times 30}{12000} = 0.613 \approx 61.3\%$$

$$\eta = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.03017 \times 43.70}{21090} = 0.613 \approx 61.3\%$$

The efficiency remains the same (as expected from affinity laws for geometrically similar operation). This confirms the calculation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pump Selection Decision Tree

| Selection Criterion | First Filter | Final Verification |
|---|---|---|
| Type of fluid (clean, viscous, slurry) | Centrifugal vs Positive Displacement | Material compatibility chart |
| Flow rate Q | Manufacturer's Q–H curve | Operating point on system curve |
| Total dynamic head TDH | Manufacturer's H–Q curve | Static + friction + velocity heads |
| NPSH availability | NPSHa ≥ NPSHr + 0.5 m | Cavitation margin check |
| Specific speed Ns | Radial / Mixed / Axial classification | Operating regime match |
| Continuous / Intermittent duty | BEP proximity | Service factor application |
| Speed control requirement | VFD / Vane / Bypass | Affinity law energy analysis |
| Cost & Maintenance | Initial vs Lifecycle cost | MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
*Define Best Efficiency Point (BEP) of a pump. Why is it preferred as the design operating point?*

**Model Answer:**
The **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** is the single operating point on a pump's H–Q curve where the overall efficiency reaches its maximum value.

**Reasons for preference:**
- Minimum energy consumption for a given duty.
- Minimum radial thrust on the impeller, reducing bearing wear.
- Lowest noise and vibration levels.
- Maximum mechanical life of the pump.
- Minimum risk of cavitation and recirculation.

(Marks distribution: Definition – 1 mark, 4 reasons – 0.5 each = 2 marks, Total 3)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*State the affinity laws for a centrifugal pump and give their practical significance.*

**Model Answer:**
The affinity laws relate the variation of flow, head, and power of a pump to its speed and impeller diameter.

**For speed change (constant D):**
- $Q_2/Q_1 = N_2/N_1$
- $H_2/H_1 = (N_2/N_1)^2$
- $P_2/P_1 = (N_2/N_1)^3$

**For impeller diameter change (constant N):**
- $Q_2/Q_1 = D_2/D_1$
- $H_2/H_1 = (D_2/D_1)^2$
- $P_2/P_1 = (D_2/D_1)^3$

**Practical Significance:**
- Enables VFD-based energy optimization in industrial pumps.
- Allows impeller trimming to match design duty without replacing the pump.
- Predicts pump performance from model tests to prototype scale.

(Marks: Laws – 2 marks, Significance – 1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the typical performance characteristic curves of a centrifugal pump. Discuss the significance of the Best Efficiency Point (BEP). *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A centrifugal pump running at 1500 RPM delivers 100 L/s against a head of 25 m. The pump is now required to deliver 120 L/s at the same efficiency. Determine:
   (i) The new speed of the pump.
   (ii) The new head developed.
   (iii) The power required if the original power consumption was 45 kW. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Performance Characteristic Curves of a Centrifugal Pump:**

A centrifugal pump exhibits four major characteristic curves plotted against discharge (Q):

1. **Head–Flow (H–Q) Curve:** Head decreases almost parabolically as Q increases. Shut-off head occurs at Q = 0.
2. **Efficiency–Flow (η–Q) Curve:** Efficiency rises from zero, peaks at BEP, then falls.
3. **Power–Flow (P–Q) Curve:** Power rises linearly or slightly non-linearly with Q.
4. **NPSH–Flow (NPSHr–Q) Curve:** NPSH required increases with Q.

**Significance of BEP:**
- The pump operates at maximum efficiency.
- Axial and radial hydraulic thrusts are balanced.
- Vibration, noise, and wear are minimized.
- Service life is maximized.
- The recommended design operating point is within ±10% of BEP flow.

*(Marks: Curves explanation – 4, Sketch description – 1, BEP significance – 2 = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- $N_1$ = 1500 RPM
- $Q_1$ = 100 L/s
- $H_1$ = 25 m
- $Q_2$ = 120 L/s
- $P_1$ = 45 kW
- $\eta_1$ = $\eta_2$ (same efficiency)

**Step 1 — New Speed from Affinity Law (Flow):**

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

$$N_2 = N_1 \times \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = 1500 \times \frac{120}{100}$$

$$N_2 = 1500 \times 1.2 = 1800 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2 — New Head from Affinity Law (Head):**

$$H_2 = H_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = 25 \times (1.2)^2$$

$$H_2 = 25 \times 1.44 = 36 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — New Power from Affinity Law (Power):**

$$P_2 = P_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = 45 \times (1.2)^3$$

$$P_2 = 45 \times 1.728 = 77.76 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Results:**
- New speed: **1800 RPM**
- New head: **36 m**
- New power: **77.76 kW**

*(Marks: Speed – 2, Head – 2, Power – 2, Units/Presentation – 1 = 7 marks)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of specific speed of a centrifugal pump. How is it used in pump classification and selection? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A centrifugal pump handles water at a flow rate of 0.05 m³/s and develops a head of 20 m when running at 2900 RPM. Calculate the specific speed and identify the type of pump. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Concept of Specific Speed:**
Specific speed ($N_s$) of a pump is defined as the speed in RPM at which a geometrically similar pump would run if it were of such a size as to deliver unit discharge (1 m³/s) against unit head (1 m).

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$$

Where N is in RPM, Q in m³/s, and H in m.

**Use in Classification:**

| $N_s$ Range | Pump Type | Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| 10 – 35 (Metric) | Radial | High head, low flow, narrow passages |
| 35 – 80 | Mixed flow | Medium head, medium flow |
| 80 – 300 | Axial flow | Low head, high flow, wide passages |

**Use in Selection:**
- Identifies the type of impeller (radial / mixed / axial) best suited for a given duty.
- Predicts efficiency, cavitation behavior, and shape of performance curve.
- Used in affinity scaling from model tests to prototype.
- Provides a basis for comparing pumps of different sizes and speeds.

*(Marks: Definition – 2, Formula – 1, Classification table – 2, Selection use – 2 = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Q = 0.05 m³/s
- H = 20 m
- N = 2900 RPM

**Step 1 — Apply Specific Speed Formula:**

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sqrt{Q}$:**

$$\sqrt{Q} = \sqrt{0.05} = 0.2236 \text{ (m³/s)}^{1/2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $H^{3/4}$:**

$$H^{3/4} = (20)^{0.75} = 20^{3/4}$$

$$H^{3/4} = \sqrt{20} \times \sqrt[4]{20} = 4.4721 \times 2.1147 = 9.457$$

**Step 4 — Compute $N_s$:**

$$N_s = \frac{2900 \times 0.2236}{9.457} = \frac{648.44}{9.457} = 68.57$$

**Step 5 — Identify Pump Type:**

Since $N_s$ ≈ 68.57 falls in the range **35 – 80**, the pump is a **Mixed Flow Pump**.

*(Marks: Formula – 1, $\sqrt{Q}$ – 1, $H^{3/4}$ – 2, $N_s$ calculation – 2, Identification – 1 = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in Affinity Laws:** Mixing RPM with rad/s, or using LPM with m³/s. KTU expects **consistent SI units** throughout the calculation.
> 2. **Specific Speed Formula Error:** Using $H^{1/4}$ instead of $H^{3/4}$ — this is a common slip. Double-check the exponent.
> 3. **NPSH Confusion:** Forgetting to convert pressure in Pa to head in meters using the formula $h = P/(\rho g)$.
> 4. **Efficiency Misinterpretation:** Some students confuse hydraulic efficiency with overall efficiency. Always specify which efficiency is used in the power equation.
> 5. **BEP Assumption:** Stating that BEP is the **only** operating point — wrong. It is the **preferred** point, and pumps can operate in a range.
> 6. **Sign Convention in Head Calculation:** Forgetting to add velocity head and minor losses in total system head calculation.
> 7. **Sketch Quality:** In questions requiring neat sketches, draw axes with proper labels (Q on x-axis, H on y-axis), mark BEP, and indicate operating point with dotted lines.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Pump H–Q curve** is approximately parabolic: $H = H_0 - kQ^2$
- ✅ **System curve** has static + friction components: $H_{sys} = H_s + k_f Q^2$
- ✅ **Operating Point** = Intersection of pump curve and system curve
- ✅ **Affinity Laws** — Speed and diameter scaling: Q ∝ N, H ∝ N², P ∝ N³
- ✅ **Cubic Law** of power is the basis of VFD energy savings
- ✅ **Specific Speed** $N_s = N\sqrt{Q}/H^{3/4}$ classifies pump as radial / mixed / axial
- ✅ **BEP (Best Efficiency Point)** = preferred design point, ±10% tolerance
- ✅ **NPSHa ≥ NPSHr + 0.5 m** — cavitation prevention criterion
- ✅ **Brake Power** $P = \rho g Q H / \eta$ — include efficiency for shaft power
- ✅ **Overall efficiency** $\eta = \eta_{hydraulic} \times \eta_{volumetric} \times \eta_{mechanical}$
- ✅ **Shut-off head** is at Q = 0; **run-out** is at maximum Q
