- **Step 2:** Directional valve 1 shifts. Clamp cylinder extends.
- **Step 3:** Clamp cylinder hits load. Pressure rises.
- **Step 4:** Sequence valve opens, energising the drill feed directional valve.
- **Step 5:** Drill cylinder extends, performs drilling.
- **Step 6:** After drilling, drill retracts. Clamp retracts (sequence may reverse or be controlled by limit switches).
- **Step 7:** When no actuator is active, unloading valve opens → pump idles at low pressure.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a sequence valve. How does it differ from a relief valve?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **sequence valve** is a pressure-controlled valve used to direct fluid flow to a secondary actuator only after a preset pressure is reached at its inlet. It is normally closed and opens when the pilot pressure exceeds the spring setting.

**Difference from relief valve:**

| Aspect | Sequence Valve | Relief Valve |
|---|---|---|
| Function | Directs flow to a **second actuator** | Vents flow to **tank** |
| Purpose | Enforce sequence | Limit max pressure |
| Connection | Between two actuators | Across pump and tank |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for at least two differences, 1 mark for clarity)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is an unloading valve? Why is it preferred over a relief valve during dwell periods?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **unloading valve** is a pressure-controlled, normally closed valve that connects the pump outlet directly to the reservoir when system pressure reaches a preset level, allowing the pump to idle at near-zero pressure.

**Why preferred over relief valve:**
- A relief valve dissipates **100 % of pump power** as heat during continuous bypass.
- An unloading valve routes flow back to tank at **2–5 bar**, reducing power loss to **≈ 2–3 %**.
- This prevents excessive **heat generation**, **oil degradation**, and **energy waste**.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the comparison with numerical or logical justification)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Format

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a pilot-operated sequence valve**. State two industrial applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A pilot-operated sequence valve consists of:
- A **main spool** held against its seat by a comparatively light spring.
- A **pilot poppet** with an adjustable spring (sets the cracking pressure).
- An **inlet port (P)**, an **outlet port (A)** to the secondary circuit, and an **external drain port (Y)**.
- A **pilot line** connecting the inlet pressure to the pilot chamber.
- An **adjustment screw** for setting the spring preload.

**Working (3 marks):**
1. Inlet pressure $P_{\text{inlet}}$ enters the valve body and acts on the pilot poppet.
2. When $P_{\text{inlet}} < P_{\text{set}}$, the pilot poppet remains closed; the main spool is held closed by its light spring. No flow passes to the secondary circuit.
3. When $P_{\text{inlet}} \geq P_{\text{set}}$, the pilot poppet lifts slightly, allowing a small bleed flow through the pilot orifice. This bleed creates a pressure differential across the main spool (due to the throttle effect).
4. The pressure differential, combined with the inlet pressure acting on the spool end area, shifts the main spool against its spring.
5. Full flow from inlet (P) now passes to outlet (A), supplying the secondary actuator.
6. The sequence valve thus **enforces sequencing** between primary and secondary actuators.

**Applications (1 mark):**
- Clamp-then-drill circuits in machining centres.
- Stamping-then-ejection cycle in press tools.

---

**(b)** A fixed displacement pump delivers **30 L/min** at a maximum system pressure of **160 bar**. During a dwell of **15 seconds** in each **45-second cycle**, an unloading valve (set at 4 bar) routes the pump output to the tank. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
- (i) Power dissipated during dwell with the unloading valve.
- (ii) Power dissipated if a relief valve were used instead.
- (iii) Annual energy saved if the machine operates 8 hours/day, 250 days/year.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$Q_p = 30$ L/min, $P_{\text{sys}} = 160$ bar, $P_{\text{unload}} = 4$ bar, $t_d = 15$ s, $T_{\text{cycle}} = 45$ s.

**(i) Power dissipated with unloading valve:**

$$P_{\text{unload}} = \dfrac{Q_p \cdot P_{\text{unload}}}{600}$$

$$P_{\text{unload}} = \dfrac{30 \times 4}{600} = \dfrac{120}{600}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{unload}} = 0.200 \text{ kW}}$$

**(ii) Power dissipated with relief valve:**

$$P_{\text{relief}} = \dfrac{Q_p \cdot P_{\text{sys}}}{600}$$

$$P_{\text{relief}} = \dfrac{30 \times 160}{600} = \dfrac{4800}{600}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{relief}} = 8.000 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Annual energy saved:**

Power saved per dwell cycle:

$$\Delta P = 8.000 - 0.200 = 7.800 \text{ kW}$$

Energy saved per cycle:

$$E_{\text{cycle}} = 7.800 \times \dfrac{15}{3600} = 0.0325 \text{ kWh}$$

Number of cycles per day:

$$N_{\text{day}} = \dfrac{8 \times 3600}{45} = 640 \text{ cycles/day}$$

Energy saved per day:

$$E_{\text{day}} = 640 \times 0.0325 = 20.80 \text{ kWh/day}$$

Annual energy saved:

$$E_{\text{year}} = 20.80 \times 250$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{year}} = 5200 \text{ kWh/year}}$$

Monetary saving @ ₹9/kWh = **₹46,800/year**.

*(Award 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii) including unit conversion and final answer)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with a circuit diagram the working of a **two-cylinder sequencing circuit** using a sequence valve. Mention the role of pilot line and drain connection. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A pump supplies oil through a filter and a relief valve to a sequencing manifold. Two branch lines emerge:
- Branch 1 (Primary): Flow goes to Cylinder A through a 4/3 directional control valve (DCV-A).
- Branch 2 (Secondary): Flow passes through the sequence valve before reaching Cylinder B through DCV-B.

**Working Sequence (3 marks):**
1. DCV-A is shifted. Flow enters Cylinder A's blank end. Cylinder A extends against load.
2. Pilot line from the inlet of Cylinder A connects to the pilot port of the sequence valve.
3. While Cylinder A is extending, its pressure is moderate. Sequence valve remains closed; Cylinder B is stationary.
4. When Cylinder A reaches end of stroke, pressure in its line rises sharply (cylinder dead-ends).
5. This pressure, transmitted via the pilot line, overcomes the sequence valve's spring setting.
6. Sequence valve opens, allowing flow to DCV-B.
7. DCV-B is shifted (either manually or via pressure-actuated mechanism). Cylinder B extends.
8. Sequence: **A first → B second** — enforced by valve.

**Role of Pilot Line (1 mark):**
The pilot line is the **signal channel** that informs the sequence valve about the upstream pressure. It is the sensing element that converts pressure into mechanical spool motion.

**Role of Drain Connection (1 mark):**
The drain (or vent) line prevents **back-pressure** in the spring chamber, ensuring that the sequence valve responds only to inlet pressure and not to downstream pressure variations. **External drain** is preferred when downstream pressure can exceed 30 bar; otherwise, internal drain is acceptable.

---

**(b)** A hydraulic press requires clamping pressure of **100 bar** in Cylinder 1 (bore 100 mm) before the pressing cylinder (bore 150 mm) can advance. If the pressing load is **80 kN**, determine: **(7 marks)**
- (i) Sequence valve setting pressure (with 10 bar margin).
- (ii) Pressure in the pressing cylinder.
- (iii) Whether pressing cylinder can complete its stroke under this setting.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Sequence valve setting pressure:**

Pressure needed in Cylinder 1 for clamping = 100 bar (given).

Adding margin of 10 bar:

$$P_{\text{set,seq}} = 100 + 10$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{set,seq}} = 110 \text{ bar}}$$

**(ii) Pressure in the pressing cylinder:**

Piston area of pressing cylinder:

$$A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (150)^2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 22500$$

$$A_p = 17671.46 \text{ mm}^2$$

Pressure in pressing cylinder to overcome 80 kN load:

$$P_{\text{press}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{load}}}{A_p}$$

$$P_{\text{press}} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{17671.46}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{press}} \approx 4.53 \text{ bar}}$$

**(iii) Will the pressing cylinder complete its stroke?**

Yes. The sequence valve is set at 110 bar. Once the clamping pressure reaches this threshold, the valve opens and the full pump pressure (assumed to be at least 110 bar) is supplied to the pressing cylinder. The pressing cylinder only needs **4.53 bar** to move its load — far less than 110 bar. Therefore, the cylinder will easily complete its stroke.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Note:** A higher sequence valve setting does **not** restrict downstream actuator movement, as long as the pump can deliver at that setting. It only **delays** the start of the secondary actuator until the upstream pressure is confirmed.

*(Award 2 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 3 marks for (iii) with full reasoning)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** The most common error is mixing L/min with m³/s or bar with Pa. Always convert to consistent SI units before final calculation. The $Q \cdot P / 600$ shortcut is valid **only** when Q is in LPM and P is in bar (giving kW).
>
> 2. **Confusing Symbols:** Students often write $P_{\text{set}}$ when they mean $P_{\text{cracking}}$ — these are identical numerically but $P_{\text{cracking}}$ is the **measured** pressure at which the valve first opens, while $P_{\text{set}}$ is the **intended** spring preload. Examiner may deduct 0.5 marks for imprecise terminology.
>
> 3. **Missing Margin:** Always add the safety margin (10–15 %) when computing sequence valve settings. Real valves have **hysteresis** of up to 10 %.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Drain Port:** When drawing the sequence valve, students frequently omit the **Y (drain) port**. This is a 1-mark deduction in KTU diagrams.
>
> 5. **Heat Calculation Trap:** When asked to compute "heat to be removed by the cooler," remember that **all** energy lost through relief/unloading valves eventually becomes heat. Cooler capacity = power dissipated (in kW) × 3412 BTU/hr.
>
> 6. **Directional Valve vs. Sequence Valve:** Many students mistakenly label the sequence valve as a "directional valve" in diagrams. The sequence valve is a **pressure-control valve** that incidentally redirects flow.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know Concepts for KTU Board Exam:**

- **Sequence Valve = Pressure-actuated 2-way valve** that opens when inlet pressure exceeds set value.
- **Sequence Valve vs. Relief Valve:** Sequence redirects flow to a second actuator; relief vents to tank.
- **Pilot-operated sequence valves** have lower hysteresis (3–5 %) vs. direct-acting (10–15 %).
- **External drain** is mandatory on pilot-operated sequence valves when downstream pressure can exceed spring chamber pressure tolerance.
- **Unloading Valve Purpose:** Reduce pump power consumption during system idle/dwell.
- **Power formula (consistent units):** $P = \dfrac{Q_p \cdot P_p}{600}$ when Q in LPM, P in bar → kW.
- **Typical unloading pressure:** 2–5 bar (vs. system pressure of 100–200 bar).
- **Unloading saves:** ≈ 95–98 % of the energy otherwise wasted as heat in a relief valve bypass.
- **Pilot ratio** = Pilot area / Spool area; typical range 0.4–0.7.
- **Sequence valve setting** must include a **10–15 % margin** over calculated load pressure.
- **Time-delay sequencing** uses an accumulator + orifice; delay $t_d = \dfrac{V_a}{Q_p} \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} - 1\right)$.
- **Internal leakage** is a key failure mode — sequence valves must have leak-free poppets for accurate setting.
- **Industrial applications:** Clamp-drill cycles, mould-close-inject cycles, stamping-ejection, accumulator charging.
- **Always** identify whether the circuit uses a **direct-acting** or **pilot-operated** valve before computing flow forces.
- **Drain port** must be piped **separately to tank**, not connected internally, in pilot-operated valves used in high-pressure circuits.
- **Energy audit numerical** (power saved per year) is a frequently-asked 7-mark problem in KTU Part B.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Control Valves and its Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT722 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flow Control Valves and its Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Flow Control Valve (FCV):** A flow control valve is a pressure-independent or pressure-dependent hydraulic/pneumatic component used to regulate the rate of fluid flow to and from an actuator, thereby controlling the **velocity** (and consequently the **speed**) of the actuator's piston or rotary motion. It accomplishes this by creating a variable restriction in the flow path, generating a controlled **pressure drop** ($\Delta P$) across an adjustable orifice.

In hydraulic systems, the volumetric flow rate $Q$ is governed by the classical orifice equation derived from Bernoulli's principle:

$$Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate (m³/s or LPM)
- $C_d$ = Coefficient of discharge (typically **0.6 to 0.9** for sharp-edged orifices)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of the orifice (m²)
- $\Delta P$ = Pressure drop across the valve (Pa or bar)
- $\rho$ = Density of the fluid (kg/m³) — for mineral oil, $\rho \approx 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at **40 °C**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Garden Tap Analogy:** Imagine a garden tap connected to a hose. The tap is, in essence, a *manual* flow control valve. As you rotate the handle, you change the cross-sectional area through which water can pass. A small opening → thin water jet (low flow, slow stream). A large opening → thick, fast water jet (high flow). 
>
> Now scale this idea up to industrial hydraulics. The "tap" becomes a precisely engineered needle, spool, or poppet mechanism. The "water" is incompressible hydraulic oil (viscosity ~ **30 cSt to 68 cSt** at 40 °C), and the "hose" is feeding a hydraulic cylinder that lifts tonnes of steel. 
>
> **Key Insight:** Flow control valves do NOT control pressure directly. Pressure is a *byproduct*. By restricting flow, they indirectly affect pressure, but their *primary* job is to meter the volume of fluid delivered per unit time, which directly translates into actuator speed:
>
> $$\text{Cylinder Velocity } (v) = \frac{Q}{A_p}$$
>
> Where $A_p$ = piston effective area. So *less flow* = *slower motion* of the ram.

## 1.3 Classification Hierarchy (At a Glance)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Classification Matters:** In KTU examinations, the classification tree of flow control valves is one of the most frequently tested topics. Understanding the distinction between **pressure-compensated** (also called *pressure-independent* or *automatic*) and **non-pressure-compensated** (also called *manual* or *fixed-restriction*) types is critical because it determines whether the actuator speed remains **constant** under varying load conditions.

| Category | Sub-Type | Pressure Compensation? | Speed Stability Under Load |
|----------|----------|------------------------|-----------------------------|
| Non-Compensated | Needle Valve | ❌ No | ❌ Poor (varies with load) |
| Non-Compensated | Gate (Sliding) Valve | ❌ No | ❌ Poor |
| Non-Compensated | Globe Valve | ❌ No | ❌ Poor |
| Compensated | Pressure Compensated FCV | ✅ Yes | ✅ Good (within limits) |
| Compensated | Pressure & Temperature Compensated FCV | ✅ Yes | ✅ Excellent |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle: The Why and How

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Engineering Trade-off:** Every flow control valve is fundamentally a **deliberate energy dissipator**. It converts useful hydraulic energy (pressure × flow) into heat via throttling. This is a thermodynamic reality — you cannot restrict flow without losing energy. The art of good valve design is to do this *predictably* and *reliably*.

### 2.1.1 Non-Pressure-Compensated (Manual) Flow Control Valves

These valves use a **fixed, manually adjustable** orifice. The flow rate is therefore a function of both the orifice area AND the pressure drop.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Operator rotates the adjustment knob → moves a tapered needle or sliding spool.
2. The variable orifice area $A$ changes.
3. With system pressure $P_s$ and downstream pressure $P_1$ (load pressure), a pressure drop $\Delta P = P_s - P_1$ develops.
4. As load on the actuator increases, $P_1$ rises, and $\Delta P$ falls, so $Q$ decreases → **cylinder slows down**.
5. Conversely, as load decreases, $Q$ increases → **cylinder speeds up**.

This makes them suitable only for **constant-load** applications or for **meter-out circuits** where back-pressure helps stabilize motion.

### 2.1.2 Pressure-Compensated Flow Control Valves

These valves incorporate an internal **pressure compensator** (essentially a spool held in equilibrium by a light spring, with end faces exposed to upstream and downstream pressures).

**Logic Steps:**
1. Fluid enters the valve and passes through a **fixed (non-adjustable)** restrictor — typically a sharp-edged orifice called the *pressure drop point* or *compensating land*.
2. A pressure differential $\Delta P$ is created across this fixed restrictor.
3. The pressure compensator spool automatically shifts to **maintain a constant $\Delta P$** across the fixed restrictor, regardless of supply or load pressure fluctuations.
4. Since $A$ (fixed) and $\Delta P$ (held constant) are both steady, $Q$ remains **virtually constant** → **actuator speed remains stable** under varying loads.

### 2.1.3 Pressure & Temperature Compensated Flow Control Valves

Add a **thermal compensator** (usually a bimetallic strip or a polymer element with high thermal expansion coefficient) that adjusts the orifice area to counteract viscosity changes in the fluid as temperature rises or falls.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat-Sheet Box:** Memorize these equations — they cover ~80% of KTU numerical problems on flow control valves.

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Use | Units |
|---|---------|--------------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Orifice flow equation (Bernoulli-derived) | m³/s |
| 2 | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A_p}$ | Cylinder piston velocity | m/s |
| 3 | $\omega = \dfrac{Q}{D_m \cdot 2\pi}$ | Hydraulic motor angular velocity | rad/s |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D_m}{2\pi} \cdot \eta_m$ | Hydraulic motor torque (with mech. efficiency $\eta_m$) | N·m |
| 5 | $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q$ | Hydraulic power dissipated (heat) | W |
| 6 | $P_{input} = P_s \cdot Q_{pump}$ | Pump input hydraulic power | W |
| 7 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | Volumetric efficiency (accounts for leakage) | dimensionless (%) |
| 8 | $H_f = \dfrac{f \cdot L \cdot v^2}{2 g \cdot D}$ | Frictional head loss (Darcy-Weisbach) | m |
| 9 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Kinematic viscosity relationship | m²/s |
| 10 | $C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{SG}{\Delta P}}$ | Valve flow coefficient (US standard) | GPM/psi½ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions & Validity:**
> - The orifice equation (Formula 1) assumes **steady, incompressible, inviscid** flow — valid for Reynolds number $Re > 10^4$ (turbulent regime).
> - $C_d$ accounts for vena contracta effects and viscous losses at the orifice.
> - For laminar flow ($Re < 2300$), use the **Hagen-Poiseuille** equation instead: $Q = \dfrac{\pi \cdot r^4 \cdot \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flow control valves are the **"speed governors"** of fluid power systems. Their applications span:

- **Injection molding machines** — controlling platen closing/opening speed to avoid mold damage.
- **Heavy earth-moving equipment** (JCBs, excavators) — controlled lowering of booms.
- **Aircraft hydraulic systems** — precise flap and landing gear actuation.
- **Machine tools** — feed rate control on lathe slides and milling tables.
- **Die-casting machines** — synchronized multi-cylinder motion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Bernoulli's Equation to the Orifice Flow Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Derive It?** KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions often include 14-mark problems where you must derive the fundamental orifice equation AND apply it to a cylinder/motor speed problem. Memorizing the formula is insufficient — you must demonstrate the *line of reasoning*.

### Step 1: Start with Bernoulli's Equation (Incompressible, Steady, Inviscid Flow)

For a fluid stream flowing between two points (1 = upstream of orifice, 2 = vena contracta / downstream):

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2
$$

**Text Row 1 Explanation:** This is the energy conservation equation for an ideal fluid. Each term represents energy per unit weight of fluid: pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head.

### Step 2: Apply Geometric & Boundary Simplifications

Assume the flow is **horizontal** (so $z_1 = z_2$) and that the upstream velocity $v_1 \approx 0$ because the pipe cross-section is much larger than the orifice:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g}
$$

**Text Row 2 Explanation:** We eliminate the elevation terms (typical assumption for compact valves) and drop $v_1$ because $A_{pipe} \gg A_{orifice}$, so by continuity, $v_1 \ll v_2$.

### Step 3: Solve for the Theoretical (Ideal) Velocity at the Vena Contracta

$$
v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}
$$

**Text Row 3 Explanation:** Multiply through by $2g$ and take the square root. $P_1 - P_2 = \Delta P$ is the pressure drop driving the flow.

### Step 4: Convert Ideal Velocity to Actual Volumetric Flow Rate

The actual flow area is the **contracted area** $A_c = C_c \cdot A$, where $C_c$ is the **coefficient of contraction** (typically 0.6 to 0.7 for sharp-edged orifices). Multiplying velocity by area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{ideal} &= A \cdot v_2 = A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}} \\
Q_{actual} &= C_c \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Text Row 4 Explanation:** The fluid jet necks down after passing the orifice (vena contracta). We account for this by multiplying by $C_c$.

### Step 5: Introduce the Discharge Coefficient

Empirically, $C_c$ alone doesn't capture viscous and turbulence losses, so we lump them into a single $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ (where $C_v$ is the velocity coefficient):

$$
\boxed{Q = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}}
$$

**Text Row 5 Explanation:** The final boxed result is the **orifice flow equation** — a cornerstone of fluid power. For hydraulic valves, $C_d$ is typically **0.6 to 0.9** depending on geometry.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Cylinder Speed Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** This 7-mark style problem is highly representative of what appears in ESE Module examinations.

**Problem:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter of **80 mm** and a rod diameter of **45 mm**. A pressure-compensated flow control valve supplies oil at a constant **30 LPM** to the blank (cap) end during extension. Calculate the extension velocity of the piston in m/s and m/min.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert Flow to SI Units:**

$$
Q = 30 \text{ LPM} = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{60} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Text Row 1 Explanation:** 1 litre = 10⁻³ m³, and 1 minute = 60 seconds.

**Step 2 — Calculate Piston Effective Area (Cap End):**

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.080)^2
$$

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0064 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Text Row 2 Explanation:** For extension, the full piston area is exposed to the cap-end pressure.

**Step 3 — Calculate Extension Velocity:**

$$
v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
v_{ext} = 0.0995 \text{ m/s} \approx 0.1 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Text Row 3 Explanation:** Apply the velocity-flow relationship derived earlier.

**Step 4 — Convert to m/min for reporting:**

$$
v_{ext} = 0.0995 \times 60 = 5.97 \text{ m/min} \approx 6 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Text Row 4 Explanation:** Multiply by 60 to get m/min. **Final Answer: $v_{ext} \approx 0.1$ m/s or 6 m/min.**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Type?

| Application Scenario | Recommended Valve Type | Reason |
|----------------------|------------------------|--------|
| Constant load (e.g., horizontal slide) | Needle / Gate valve | Simple, cheap, sufficient |
| Variable load (e.g., vertical lift) | Pressure-compensated FCV | Maintains constant speed |
| Outdoor mobile equipment (temp swings) | Pressure & Temp. Compensated FCV | Compensates for viscosity changes |
| Meter-in (controlled extension) | FCV in supply line | Controls speed into actuator |
| Meter-out (controlled retraction under load) | FCV in return line | Prevents cavitation, controls back-pressure |
| Counterbalance + speed control | Counterbalance valve + FCV | For overrunning loads |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Flow Control Valve Functional Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol, the following is a **functional architecture flow** rather than a physical drawing. It maps the signal and fluid flow interactions that govern how each type of flow control valve operates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydraulic Power Supply: Pump, Reservoir, Filter] --> B[Pressure Relief Valve Sets Max System Pressure]
    B --> C[Directional Control Valve DCV: 4 by 3 Solenoid]
    C --> D1[Flow Control Valve Type Decision]
    D1 --> E1[Type A: Needle Valve Manual Non Compensated]
    D1 --> E2[Type B: Pressure Compensated FCV]
    D1 --> E3[Type C: Pressure and Temperature Compensated FCV]
    D1 --> E4[Type D: Deceleration FCV with Check Valve]
    E1 --> F1[Fixed Orifice Area A]
    E2 --> F2[Fixed Orifice A and Constant delta P held by Compensator Spool]
    E3 --> F3[Fixed Orifice A Constant delta P and Thermal Element Modifies A]
    E4 --> F4[Bidirectional Free Flow via Check Valve and Throttled Reverse Flow]
    F1 --> G1[Actuator Speed Varies with Load]
    F2 --> G2[Actuator Speed Approximately Constant]
    F3 --> G3[Actuator Speed Constant Across Pressure and Temperature Changes]
    F4 --> G4[Asymmetric Speed: Fast One Direction, Slow Other]
    G1 --> H[Hydraulic Cylinder or Motor]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Load: Resists or Assists Motion]
    I --> J[Return Line via Tank Port to Reservoir]
    J --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram: Pressure Compensator Spool Equilibrium

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Diagram:** The pressure compensator is the *brain* of a pressure-compensated FCV. This state diagram illustrates how it dynamically shifts to maintain constant $\Delta P$.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> EquilibriumState
    EquilibriumState --> LoadIncreaseDetected: Upstream Pressure Rises
    LoadIncreaseDetected --> SpoolShiftsRight: Compensator Spool Moves to Restrict Flow
    SpoolShiftsRight --> NewEquilibrium: delta P Restored to Setpoint
    NewEquilibrium --> EquilibriumState: Steady Operation
    EquilibriumState --> LoadDecreaseDetected: Downstream Pressure Rises Relative
    LoadDecreaseDetected --> SpoolShiftsLeft: Compensator Spool Opens More
    SpoolShiftsLeft --> NewEquilibrium2: delta P Restored to Setpoint
    NewEquilibrium2 --> EquilibriumState
    note right of EquilibriumState: Spring force balances pressure differential forces
    note right of SpoolShiftsRight: Spool movement adjusts variable land to maintain fixed delta P
```

## 4.3 ISO 1219 Graphical Symbols Reference (KTU Exam Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU Board Examiners *expect* you to draw standard **ISO 1219** symbols in circuit diagrams. The following Mermaid block summarizes the canonical symbol for each valve type.

```mermaid
graph LR
    sym1[Needle Valve Symbol: Bow Tie with Adjustable Arrow on Diagonal Line] --> desc1[Adjustable Restriction in One Direction, Free Flow in Reverse via Internal Check]
    sym2[Pressure Compensated FCV Symbol: Fixed Restriction Followed by Compensator Symbol: Variable Restriction with Pressure Arrow Above] --> desc2[Fixed Restriction Plus Compensator for Constant delta P]
    sym3[Temperature Compensated FCV: Same as PCFCV Plus Thermistor or Thermal Element Symbol] --> desc3[Adds Temperature Stability]
    sym4[Deceleration FCV: Adjustable Restriction Bypassed by Check Valve] --> desc4[Speed Control in One Direction Only]
    sym5[Two Way Valve: Two Port Symbol With Arrow at Junction] --> desc5[Simple In Line Throttling]
    sym6[Three Way Valve: Three Port Symbol With Branching Path] --> desc6[Diverts or Mixes Flow Paths]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate clearly between a non-pressure-compensated flow control valve and a pressure-compensated flow control valve. State one practical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Non-Pressure-Compensated FCV | Pressure-Compensated FCV |
|--------|------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Restriction type | Manually adjustable, fixed once set | Fixed restriction + automatic compensator |
| $\Delta P$ across restriction | Varies with load | Maintained constant by compensator |
| Speed under varying load | **Not constant** (varies inversely with load) | **Approximately constant** |
| Cost & complexity | Low | Higher |
| Example application | Constant load horizontal slide in a machine tool | Vertical lift of a load on a hydraulic press |

**Applications (1 mark each):**
- **Non-PC FCV:** Feed rate control on a lathe carriage (constant chip load).
- **PC FCV:** Speed control of crane hoist with varying lifted load.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**What is the function of a check valve bypass in a flow control valve assembly? Sketch its ISO 1219 symbol and explain its operation in a meter-out circuit.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A check valve placed in parallel with the adjustable throttling element provides a **free-flow path in the reverse direction** while restricting flow in the forward direction. 

**ISO 1219 Symbol (described):** A ball-on-seat symbol with an arrow indicating free flow direction, connected in parallel with a bow-tie adjustable restriction.

**Operation in a meter-out circuit (2 marks):**
1. During extension, oil from the pump enters the cap end. Return oil from the rod end must pass through the FCV (meter-out), since the check valve is closed (flow opposes its cracking direction).
2. The throttling element controls the rod-end discharge rate, thereby controlling retraction speed on the next cycle and preventing **runaway** under overrunning loads.
3. On free reverse flow (e.g., during gravity-assisted descent), the check valve opens, allowing unrestricted return.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Pattern with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Penalty (-1 mark):** Examiners *hate* mixed units. If $D$ is given in mm, convert to **m** before computing area in m². LPM must be converted to **m³/s**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Coefficient of Discharge (-1 mark):** If the problem provides $C_d$ or asks for "actual flow," do NOT skip it. State the value and its physical meaning.
> 3. **Confusing Meter-In and Meter-Out (-2 marks):** State explicitly *which* side of the actuator the FCV is installed on, and justify *why* (load direction, cavitation prevention, etc.).
> 4. **ISO Symbol Mistakes (-1 mark):** Drawing the check valve arrow in the wrong direction is a common error. Always cross-verify with the circuit's intended flow path.

---

### Question A: 14 Marks [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **pressure-compensated flow control valve**. State its advantages over a simple needle valve.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):** A pressure-compensated FCV consists of:
1. An **adjustable needle** (1) for setting the desired flow.
2. A **fixed orifice** (2) — the throttling element with a fixed area.
3. A **compensator spool** (3) — a small spool held against a light spring (4), with one end exposed to upstream pressure $P_1$ and the other end exposed to downstream pressure $P_2$.
4. **Inlet port (A)**, **outlet port (B)**, and a **drain/vent** for the spring chamber.

**Working (3 marks):** 
- As load changes, $P_2$ varies. The compensator spool experiences a net hydraulic force $F_{net} = (P_1 - P_2) \cdot A_{spool}$.
- When load increases, $P_2$ drops, $F_{net}$ increases, pushing the spool to **restrict** the bypass passage, thereby **increasing** the effective restriction on the main flow path. This raises the pressure drop across the fixed orifice back to the design $\Delta P$.
- When load decreases, the spool shifts the other way, opening the bypass, lowering restriction, and again restoring $\Delta P$.
- Since $A$ and $\Delta P$ are both held constant, $Q$ remains constant → constant actuator speed.

**Advantages over needle valve (1 mark):**
- Maintains **constant flow** despite load variations → constant actuator speed.
- Better for **vertical lifts** and **variable load** applications.
- Improved **process consistency** and **safety**.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A hydraulic cylinder with a bore of **100 mm** and rod diameter of **60 mm** is extended by a flow control valve that delivers **40 LPM** of oil. The system operates at **100 bar** supply pressure, and the load pressure during extension is **80 bar**. Calculate:
1. The extension velocity of the piston in m/s.
2. The retraction velocity of the piston in m/s (assuming same flow rate on the rod end during retraction).
3. The hydraulic power dissipated in the flow control valve.
4. The thrust force developed by the cylinder during extension.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 100$ mm $= 0.1$ m, $d = 60$ mm $= 0.06$ m
- $Q = 40$ LPM $= \dfrac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s
- $P_s = 100$ bar $= 10 \times 10^6$ Pa, $P_{load} = 80$ bar $= 8 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $\Delta P_{FCV} = 100 - 80 = 20$ bar $= 2 \times 10^6$ Pa

**Step 1: Piston effective area (cap end / extension):**

$$
A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Piston effective area (rod end / retraction):**

$$
A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.1^2 - 0.06^2) = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Extension velocity:**

$$
v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_{cap}} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0849 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4: Retraction velocity:**

$$
v_{ret} = \frac{Q}{A_{rod}} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5: Hydraulic power dissipated in the FCV:**

$$
P_{FCV} = \Delta P \cdot Q = 2 \times 10^6 \cdot 6.667 \times 10^{-4} = 1333.4 \text{ W} \approx 1.33 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6: Thrust force during extension:**

$$
F_{thrust} = P_{load} \cdot A_{cap} = 8 \times 10^6 \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 62832 \text{ N} \approx 62.83 \text{ kN}
$$

**Final Answer Box:**
- $v_{ext} \approx 0.085$ m/s
- $v_{ret} \approx 0.133$ m/s
- $P_{FCV} \approx 1.33$ kW (this energy becomes heat!)
- $F_{thrust} \approx 62.83$ kN

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Classify flow control valves in detail. Compare **meter-in** and **meter-out** flow control circuits with neat sketches. Under what conditions is meter-out preferred?

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Flow Control Valves (3 marks):**

```
FLOW CONTROL VALVES
├── 1. Non-Pressure-Compensated (Manual)
│   ├── Needle Valve
│   ├── Gate Valve (Sliding Spool)
│   └── Globe Valve
├── 2. Pressure-Compensated (Automatic)
│   ├── Pressure-Compensated FCV
│   ├── Pressure & Temperature Compensated FCV
│   └── 2-Way / 3-Way FCV
└── 3. Special Function
    ├── Deceleration Valve
    ├── Quick-Exhaust + Throttle Combo
    └── Time-Delay FCV
```

**Meter-In vs Meter-Out Comparison (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Meter-In | Meter-Out |
|--------|----------|-----------|
| Valve location | Supply line (to actuator) | Return line (from actuator) |
| Controls | Speed of extension/forward motion | Speed of return/retraction |
| Load direction effect | Affected by positive load | Stable with negative (overrunning) load |
| Cavitation risk | Higher (vacuum at cap end) | Lower |
| Back-pressure | None on return | High (acts as counterbalance) |

**When Meter-Out is Preferred (1 mark):**
- **Overrunning loads** (gravity-driven descent) — prevents runaway.
- **Vertical loads** being lowered — back-pressure prevents free fall.
- **High-speed reciprocating machines** — controls deceleration.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A pressure-compensated flow control valve is set to deliver **25 LPM** at a constant pressure drop of **12 bar** across its fixed restrictor. The coefficient of discharge of the fixed restrictor is **0.65**. The hydraulic oil has a density of **870 kg/m³**. Calculate:
1. The effective orifice area of the fixed restrictor in mm².
2. If the system supply pressure is **150 bar** and the load pressure fluctuates between **80 bar** and **130 bar**, comment on whether the flow rate will vary.
3. The expected actuator speed if this flow is fed to a hydraulic motor with displacement **50 cc/rev** (cm³/rev).

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q = 25$ LPM $= 4.167 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s
- $\Delta P = 12$ bar $= 1.2 \times 10^6$ Pa
- $C_d = 0.65$, $\rho = 870$ kg/m³
- $P_s = 150$ bar, $P_{load}$ varies from 80 to 130 bar
- $D_m = 50$ cc/rev $= 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/rev

**Step 1: Solve the orifice equation for area:**

$$
A = \frac{Q}{C_d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}}
$$

$$
\sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.2 \times 10^6}{870}} = \sqrt{2758.6} = 52.52 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
A = \frac{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}{0.65 \times 52.52} = \frac{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}{34.14} = 1.221 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

Convert to mm²:

$$
A = 1.221 \times 10^{-5} \times 10^6 = 12.21 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2: Check load pressure variation:**

- When $P_{load} = 80$ bar: $\Delta P = 150 - 80 = 70$ bar
- When $P_{load} = 130$ bar: $\Delta P = 150 - 130 = 20$ bar

Since the fixed restrictor has $\Delta P = 12$ bar design, and actual $\Delta P$ across the restrictor is held at 12 bar **by the compensator** (which adjusts the variable bypass), the **flow rate remains constant at 25 LPM**. The compensator spool dynamically responds to maintain the 12 bar drop.

**Conclusion:** The flow rate does **not vary** with load changes — this is the whole purpose of pressure compensation. The compensator spool is the "self-correcting" element.

**Step 3: Actuator (motor) speed:**

$$
N = \frac{Q}{D_m} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{min}}{50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}} = 500 \text{ rev/min (rpm)}
$$

**Final Answer Box:**
- $A \approx 12.21$ mm²
- Flow rate stays **constant at 25 LPM** (pressure-compensated)
- $N = 500$ rpm

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This Before Your Exam:**

- ☐ Flow control valves regulate **velocity**, NOT pressure. Pressure is a *consequence* of restriction.
- ☐ The fundamental equation is $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ — derived from Bernoulli + vena contracta correction.
- ☐ **Non-pressure-compensated** = manual, cheap, but speed varies with load. Suitable for **constant-load** applications.
- ☐ **Pressure-compensated** = automatic, holds $\Delta P$ constant across a fixed restrictor → constant flow under varying load. Uses a spring-loaded compensator spool.
- ☐ **Pressure & temperature compensated** = adds a thermal element to counteract viscosity changes in oil as it heats up during operation.
- ☐ **Meter-in** = FCV on the supply line → controls speed into actuator. Risk: cavitation at cap end if too restrictive.
- ☐ **Meter-out** = FCV on the return line → controls speed out of actuator. Preferred for **overrunning/negative loads** (gravity descent) to prevent runaway.
- ☐ **Check valve bypass** = allows free flow in reverse direction; restricts only one way. Common in deceleration circuits.
- ☐ Flow control valves **dissipate energy as heat**: $P_{loss} = \Delta P \times Q$. This is unavoidable throttling loss.
- ☐ **ISO 1219 symbols** must be used in circuit diagrams — bow-tie for adjustable restriction, ball-on-seat for check valve.
- ☐ For double-acting cylinders, retraction speed > extension speed (for the same flow) because $A_{rod} < A_{cap}$.
- ☐ Hydraulic motor speed $N = Q / D_m$ (rev/s), torque $T = \Delta P \cdot D_m \cdot \eta_m / 2\pi$.
- ☐ Always check units: **LPM → m³/s** (divide by 60,000), **bar → Pa** (multiply by 10⁵), **mm → m** (multiply by 10⁻³).
- ☐ Typical oil density: **870 kg/m³** at 40 °C. Typical $C_d$: **0.6–0.9**.
- ☐ Flow control valves are essential for **cycle time control** in industrial automation — they ensure repeatable, safe operation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proportional Valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT722 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Proportional Valves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proportional Valve:** A **proportional valve** is an electrohydraulic (or electropneumatic) control valve in which the output hydraulic parameter — **flow rate ($Q$)**, **pressure ($P$)**, or **directional flow path** — varies in **continuous proportion** to the magnitude of an input **electrical reference signal** (current in **mA** or voltage in **V**). The spool position is actuated by a **proportional solenoid** that produces a force directly proportional to the energising current, enabling stepless, infinitely variable control of actuator velocity, force, and position.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, proportional valves are positioned between **conventional (on/off) directional valves** and **high-response servo valves**. They offer far greater control resolution than bang-bang valves, yet are more rugged and less expensive than servo valves, making them the **workhorse of modern industrial hydraulic automation**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a *dimmer switch* for hydraulic power.**

Imagine the lighting in your room:

- An **ordinary switch** is either fully **ON** or fully **OFF** — no middle state. This is what a conventional solenoid-operated DCV does to a hydraulic cylinder: full flow or no flow, full pressure or zero.
- A **dimmer (rheostat) switch** lets you smoothly slide the brightness from 0 % to 100 %. The light intensity is **proportional** to how far you rotate the knob.

A **proportional valve** is the hydraulic equivalent of that dimmer knob:

| Lighting Analogy | Hydraulic Counterpart |
| :--- | :--- |
| Light switch | 4/3 manually-operated DCV |
| Dimmer knob | Proportional solenoid current ($I$) |
| Bulb brightness | Flow rate $Q$ to actuator |
| Mains voltage stability | Supply pressure $P_s$ |
| Feedback from your eye | LVDT position sensor (closed-loop) |

So instead of "all or nothing," the proportional valve gives **infinitely variable control** of cylinder speed or motor RPM — infinitely variable between 0 % and 100 % of the maximum.

---

## 1.3 Key Technical Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three canonical KTU characteristics to remember:**
> 1. **Proportionality:** Spool displacement $x \propto I$ (current).
> 2. **Force generation:** Magnetic force $F_m$ of proportional solenoid is a function of current and armature geometry (not constant like a binary solenoid).
> 3. **Continuous control:** Output $Q$ or $P$ can be set to any intermediate value, not just discrete steps.

**Standard reference signals** used in industrial proportional hydraulics:
- **Voltage:** $0$–$10$ **V DC**
- **Current:** $4$–$20$ **mA** (industry-standard for noise immunity)

---

## 1.4 Position in the KTU Module-3 Hierarchy

```
Module 3 — Hydraulic Control Valves
├── 3.1 Conventional DCV / Pressure / Flow Valves (review)
├── 3.2 Servo Valves (high-response, closed-loop, mechanical feedback)
└── 3.3 PROPORTIONAL VALVES  ← (this topic)
      ├── 3.3.1 Proportional Directional Control Valve (PDCV)
      ├── 3.3.2 Proportional Pressure Control Valve (PPCV)
      ├── 3.3.3 Proportional Flow Control Valve (PFCV)
      └── 3.3.4 Open-loop vs Closed-loop configurations
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Proportional Valves

Proportional valves are classified by the **hydraulic parameter they modulate**:

| Type | Modulated Parameter | Typical Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **PDCV** — Proportional Directional Control Valve | Flow direction + magnitude | Speed & direction control of cylinder / motor |
| **PPCV** — Proportional Pressure Control Valve | System pressure | Force / clamping / tension control |
| **PFCV** — Proportional Flow Control Valve | Flow rate $Q$ | Precise speed control independent of load |

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Proportional Valve — Component Breakdown

A proportional valve is essentially a **conventional DCV spool** driven by a **proportional solenoid** instead of a binary solenoid. Its critical sub-components are:

1. **Proportional Solenoid** — produces a force $F_m$ proportional to coil current $I$.
2. **Armature / Plunger** — converts magnetic force into linear spool motion.
3. **Centring Spring(s)** — return spool to neutral when de-energised.
4. **Metering Notches (Control Edges)** — precision-machined openings on the spool land that convert spool displacement $x$ into a variable orifice area $A$.
5. **Spool Position Sensor (LVDT)** — optional, used in **closed-loop** versions.
6. **Electronic Amplifier Card** — converts low-power reference signal into solenoid drive current.

---

## 2.3 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "proportional"?** Because the magnetic force generated by the proportional solenoid is *not constant* — it increases **linearly** (within the working range) with the applied current.

**Operational Logic Chain:**

- **Step 1 — Reference Input:** Operator (or PLC) sends an electrical reference, e.g. $V_{ref} = +6$ **V** for half-stroke extension.
- **Step 2 — Amplification:** The on-board amplifier converts this into a proportional solenoid current $I \approx 600$ **mA** (assuming $0$–$10$ V $\to$ $0$–$1000$ mA mapping).
- **Step 3 — Magnetic Force Generation:** The proportional solenoid produces an axial force:
$$F_m \approx k_1 \cdot I - k_2 \cdot x^2$$
  (first term is current-driven, second term is armature-position dependent).
- **Step 4 — Spool Displacement:** Force balance against the spring + hydraulic flow forces moves the spool to a new equilibrium position $x$.
- **Step 5 — Variable Orifice:** The metering notches expose a **variable area** $A(x)$ to the pressure flow.
- **Step 6 — Flow Output:** Flow to actuator is determined by the standard orifice equation:
$$Q = C_d \, A(x) \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}$$
- **Step 7 (Optional) — Feedback:** An LVDT measures actual spool position $x_{actual}$ and closes the loop, eliminating hysteresis.

---

## 2.4 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop — KTU Critical Distinction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently tests this distinction.**

| Feature | Open-Loop Proportional Valve | Closed-Loop Proportional Valve |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Position feedback | **None** | **LVDT** on spool |
| Hysteresis | High (typically $5$–$10$ %) | Very low ($< 1$ %) |
| Repeatability | Moderate | Excellent |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (electronics + sensor) |
| Typical use | Mobile machinery, simple automation | Machine tools, precision presses |
| Bandwidth | Lower | Higher |

The closed-loop version uses an **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** integrated into the spool body to sense actual spool position, comparing it with the command signal in a **PID controller** on the amplifier card.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examinable. Memorise units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $F_m \approx k_1 I - k_2 x^2$ | Magnetic force of proportional solenoid | $F_m$ in **N**, $I$ in **A**, $x$ in **m** |
| 2 | $Q = C_d A(x) \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ | Flow through metering edge | $Q$ in **m³/s**, $C_d \approx 0.6$–$0.7$ |
| 3 | $A(x) = \pi \, d_s \, x \cdot \sin\alpha$ | Area of a triangular notch (typical spool geometry) | $d_s$ = spool dia., $\alpha$ = land chamfer angle |
| 4 | $P_{ctrl} = k_v (x_{ref} - x_{act})$ | Closed-loop position error signal | PID controller |
| 5 | $Q_{max} = C_d A_{max} \sqrt{2(P_s - P_L) / \rho}$ | Maximum flow at rated pressure drop | $P_s$ = supply, $P_L$ = load |
| 6 | $\eta_v = Q_{act} / Q_{th}$ | Volumetric efficiency (with pressure compensator) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\tau_{spool} = \sqrt{m / k_h}$ | Spool natural time constant | $m$ = spool mass, $k_h$ = hydraulic stiff. |
| 8 | $H \% = \Delta I_{hys} / I_{max} \times 100$ | Hysteresis percentage | Open-loop: $5$–$10$ %; Closed-loop: $<1$ % |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Proportional valves are the **backbone of modern hydraulic automation**:

- **Injection moulding machines:** closed-loop pressure + flow profiling during plastification, holding, and cooling.
- **Mobile hydraulics (excavators, tractors):** load-independent flow distribution via LS (Load-Sensing) proportional valves.
- **Press brakes:** force-controlled bending with PPCV + position-controlled ram with PDCV.
- **Ship steering systems:** proportional DCV with joystick input for variable rudder speed.
- **Steel mills:** proportional pressure + flow for rolling-mill stand loading.

They replaced bulky servo systems in **~70 %** of industrial applications where ultra-high dynamic response ($> 50$ **Hz**) is **not** required.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force Balance on the Proportional Spool

We consider a single-ended proportional solenoid driving a 4-way, 3-position proportional DCV spool. We derive the equilibrium spool displacement $x$ as a function of current $I$.

**Step 1 — Identify all forces acting on the spool.**

There are **four** dominant axial forces:

1. **Magnetic force** $F_m$ (proportional solenoid, drives the spool).
2. **Centring spring force** $F_s = K_s \cdot (x_0 + x)$ — opposing motion.
3. **Hydraulic flow force** $F_h$ on the spool lands (jet reaction).
4. **Steady-state friction & damping** (neglected in first-order model).

**Step 2 — Write the static force equilibrium** (steady-state, spool at rest).

$$\sum F_x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_m = F_s + F_h$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the constitutive expressions.**

The proportional solenoid magnetic force is empirically modelled as:

$$F_m = k_1 \, I - k_2 \, x^2$$

where:
- $k_1$ = current-force coefficient [**N/A**],
- $k_2$ = position-decay coefficient [**N/m²**] (accounts for armature end-stroke demagnetisation).

The centring spring force:

$$F_s = K_s (x_0 + x)$$

where $K_s$ = spring stiffness [**N/m**] and $x_0$ = spring preload deflection [**m**].

**Step 4 — Substituting and solving for $x$.**

$$k_1 I - k_2 x^2 = K_s (x_0 + x) + F_h$$

Rearranging into a standard quadratic in $x$:

$$-k_2 x^2 - K_s x + (k_1 I - K_s x_0 - F_h) = 0$$

$$\boxed{k_2 x^2 + K_s x + (K_s x_0 + F_h - k_1 I) = 0}$$

**Step 5 — Solve the quadratic** (taking the physically meaningful positive root):

$$x = \frac{-K_s + \sqrt{K_s^2 - 4 k_2 (K_s x_0 + F_h - k_1 I)}}{2 k_2}$$

**Interpretation:** For low currents, $k_1 I$ is small and the square-root term is small — the spool barely moves (dead-zone). For higher currents, $x$ grows, demonstrating the **proportional** behaviour. The negative $k_2 x^2$ term introduces a *non-linearity* that limits the working stroke, hence the need for closed-loop LVDT feedback in precision applications.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Flow Through the Metering Notch

**Step 1 — Geometry of a typical metering land.**

A typical proportional spool has triangular (or rectangular) metering notches cut into the spool lands. For a **rectangular notch** of circumferential width $w$ and spool diameter $d_s$, the exposed area at spool displacement $x$ is:

$$A(x) = w \cdot x \quad \text{(rectangular notch)}$$

For a **triangular (chamfered) notch** with land angle $\alpha$:

$$A(x) = \pi \, d_s \, x \, \tan\alpha$$

**Step 2 — Apply the standard orifice flow equation.**

For an incompressible fluid (typical hydraulic oil), the volumetric flow rate is:

$$Q = C_d \, A(x) \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

where:
- $C_d$ = discharge coefficient ($\approx 0.62$ for sharp-edged orifices),
- $\Delta P = P_s - P_L$ = pressure drop across the metering edge [**Pa**],
- $\rho$ = fluid density ($\approx 870$ **kg/m³** for mineral oil).

**Step 3 — Combine geometry and orifice equation.**

$$Q = C_d \cdot w \cdot x \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

Since $x \propto I$ (from force balance), we get:

$$Q = C_d \cdot w \cdot \left( \frac{k_1 I - K_s x_0 - F_h}{K_s} \right) \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

This is the **fundamental proportional-flow relation** that KTU expects you to know.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hysteresis Calculation

**Step 1 — Define hysteresis.**

Hysteresis is the difference in spool position (or current) for the same output when approaching a setpoint from increasing vs. decreasing input. It arises from Coulomb friction and magnetic hysteresis of the solenoid armature.

**Step 2 — Empirical formula.**

$$H \, [\%] = \frac{I_{hys}}{I_{max}} \times 100$$

where $I_{hys}$ is the current difference at mid-stroke between the up-going and down-going characteristic curves.

**Step 3 — Numerical example.**

For an open-loop proportional valve with $I_{max} = 800$ **mA** and $I_{hys} = 40$ **mA**:

$$H = \frac{40}{800} \times 100 = 5 \, \%$$

Closed-loop versions typically reduce this to $H < 1 \, \%$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Proportional vs Servo vs Conventional

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-yield KTU comparison. Memorise the row contents.**

| Parameter | Conventional DCV | Proportional Valve | Servo Valve |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Control signal | Manual / solenoid (binary) | Variable current $0$–$800$ mA | Variable current $\pm 40$ mA |
| Output stages | $2$ or $3$ discrete | Infinitely variable | Infinitely variable |
| Hysteresis | N/A | $5$–$10$ % (open), $<1$ % (closed) | $< 0.5$ % |
| Bandwidth | N/A | $10$–$30$ **Hz** | $50$–$200$ **Hz** |
| Cost (relative) | $1\times$ | $5$–$10\times$ | $20$–$50\times$ |
| Filtering required | No | Yes (on amplifier card) | Yes (mandatory) |
| Contamination tolerance | High | Moderate | Low ($< 5 \,\mu$m filtration) |
| Typical application | Loaders, simple presses | Injection moulding, mobile hydraulics | Flight simulators, machine tools |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Closed-Loop Proportional Valve System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Operator Panel or PLC]
    nodeB[Reference Signal Vref 0 to 10 V]
    nodeC[Electronic Amplifier with PID]
    nodeD[Proportional Solenoid]
    nodeE[Spool with Metering Notches]
    nodeF[LVDT Position Sensor on Spool]
    nodeG[Hydraulic Actuator Cylinder or Motor]
    nodeH[Load and Pipeline]
    nodeI[Feedback Signal Vfb to Amplifier]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC -- Solenoid Current I --> nodeD
    nodeD -- Magnetic Force Fm --> nodeE
    nodeE -- Variable Orifice Area A --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeC
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **outer loop** (nodeA → nodeB → nodeC → nodeD → nodeE → nodeG → nodeH) is the **forward power path**.
- The **inner loop** (nodeE → nodeF → nodeI → nodeC) is the **feedback path** with LVDT spool-position sensing.
- Without nodeF and nodeI, the system is **open-loop** — cheaper but with higher hysteresis.

---

## 4.2 Spool with Metering Notches — Functional Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph spoolBody[Proportional Spool Assembly]
        nodeS1[Supply Port P]
        nodeS2[Spool Land with Triangular Notch]
        nodeS3[Centring Spring Left]
        nodeS4[Proportional Solenoid Right Side]
        nodeS5[Tank Port T]
        nodeS6[Work Port A]
        nodeS7[Work Port B]
    end
    nodeS1 --> nodeS2
    nodeS2 --> nodeS6
    nodeS2 --> nodeS7
    nodeS6 --> nodeS5
    nodeS3 -. Preloads .-> nodeS2
    nodeS4 -. Pushes right at current I .-> nodeS2
```

**Key insight:** When the solenoid energises with current $I$, the spool shifts right by $x$, exposing the triangular notch to port A — flow $Q_A = C_d A(x)\sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho}$ enters the actuator. The opposite side vents to tank through port B.

---

## 4.3 Pressure-Flow Characteristic Curve — Conceptual Plot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeQ1[Spool Stroke x at 0 percent] --> nodeQ2[Spool Stroke x at 25 percent]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ3[Spool Stroke x at 50 percent]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeQ4[Spool Stroke x at 75 percent]
    nodeQ4 --> nodeQ5[Spool Stroke x at 100 percent]
    nodeQ1 -- Q near 0 L per min --> nodeA[Q x Characteristic Curve monotonically increasing]
    nodeQ2 -- Q rises --> nodeA
    nodeQ3 -- Q mid range --> nodeA
    nodeQ4 -- Q high --> nodeA
    nodeQ5 -- Q at rated max --> nodeA
    nodeA -. With pressure compensator .-> nodeB[Flat Isobaric Curve]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pressure compensation matters:** Without a compensator, $Q$ varies with load ($\Delta P$ in the orifice equation). Adding a **pressure compensator** (two-way valve holding $\Delta P$ constant across the metering edge) makes $Q$ depend *only* on $x$, giving a flat, predictable characteristic.

---

## 4.4 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop — Side-by-Side Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph openLoop[Open Loop Proportional System]
        o1[Command Signal] --> o2[Amplifier]
        o2 --> o3[Proportional Solenoid]
        o3 --> o4[Spool drives Actuator]
        o4 --> o5[No Feedback]
    end
    subgraph closedLoop[Closed Loop Proportional System]
        c1[Command Signal] --> c2[Amplifier with PID]
        c2 --> c3[Proportional Solenoid]
        c3 --> c4[Spool drives Actuator]
        c4 --> c5[LVDT senses spool]
        c5 --> c6[Feedback to Amplifier]
        c6 --> c2
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Define a proportional valve. How does it differ from a conventional DCV?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A proportional valve is an electrohydraulic control valve in which the output flow or pressure varies continuously in proportion to an input electrical signal (typically $0$–$10$ **V** or $4$–$20$ **mA**). The spool is driven by a **proportional solenoid** that generates a force proportional to coil current.

**Difference from conventional DCV:**

| Aspect | Conventional DCV | Proportional Valve |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Input | Manual lever / binary solenoid | Variable current / voltage |
| Output | Two discrete states (ON/OFF) | Infinitely variable |
| Control resolution | Low | High (continuous) |

**(1 mark each for: definition, identification of input difference, identification of output difference.)**

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is the role of an LVDT in a closed-loop proportional valve?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is a non-contact position sensor mounted coaxially with the proportional spool. Its role is:

1. **(1 mark)** To continuously measure the **actual spool position** $x_{act}$ in real time.
2. **(1 mark)** To feed back a voltage proportional to $x_{act}$ to the **PID controller** on the amplifier card.
3. **(1 mark)** This closed-loop feedback **eliminates hysteresis**, improves **repeatability**, and allows precise tracking of the reference signal — reducing hysteresis from $5$–$10$ % (open-loop) to less than $1$ %.

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, with Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Proportional Directional Valve Analysis

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module-3 Internal Choice Option A]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **proportional directional control valve**. Compare its performance with a **servo valve**.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A proportional DCV has a spool of diameter $d_s = 25$ **mm** with rectangular metering notches of width $w = 6$ **mm**. At a solenoid current of $I = 500$ **mA**, the spool displacement is $x = 2.5$ **mm**. The supply pressure is $P_s = 200$ **bar** and the load pressure is $P_L = 80$ **bar**. Fluid density $\rho = 870$ **kg/m³**, discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.62$. Calculate the flow rate to the actuator.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Spool:** Precision-ground cylindrical spool with metering notches (rectangular or triangular) cut on each land.
- **Proportional Solenoid:** Replace the binary solenoid of a conventional DCV. Force output is proportional to coil current.
- **Centring Springs:** Two opposed springs preload the spool to its centred (neutral) position.
- **LVDT (in closed-loop version):** Mounted inside the solenoid body for spool-position feedback.
- **Amplifier Card:** External PCB providing current-regulated drive to the solenoid.

**Working (2 marks):**
- On receiving an electrical reference signal, the amplifier supplies a regulated current to the solenoid.
- The solenoid generates a magnetic force $F_m = k_1 I - k_2 x^2$.
- This force overcomes the centring spring and shifts the spool by displacement $x$.
- The metering notches expose a variable orifice area $A(x)$, giving flow rate proportional to current.
- In a closed-loop version, LVDT feedback compensates for hysteresis and disturbances.

**Comparison with Servo Valve (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Proportional Valve | Servo Valve |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bandwidth | $10$–$30$ **Hz** | $50$–$200$ **Hz** |
| Hysteresis | $5$–$10$ % (open), $<1$ % (closed) | $<0.5$ % |
| Cost | Moderate | Very high |
| Filtration | $25 \,\mu$m nominal | $<5 \,\mu$m absolute |

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Numerical

**Given:**
- $d_s = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m
- $w = 6$ mm $= 0.006$ m
- $I = 500$ mA $= 0.5$ A
- $x = 2.5$ mm $= 0.0025$ m
- $P_s = 200$ bar $= 200 \times 10^5$ **Pa**
- $P_L = 80$ bar $= 80 \times 10^5$ **Pa**
- $\rho = 870$ kg/m³
- $C_d = 0.62$

**Step 1 — Orifice area at $x = 2.5$ mm (rectangular notch):**

$$A = w \cdot x = 0.006 \times 0.0025 = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Pressure drop across the metering edge:**

$$\Delta P = P_s - P_L = (200 - 80) \times 10^5 = 120 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.2 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Apply orifice equation:**

$$Q = C_d \, A \, \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the inner term:**

$$\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho} = \frac{2 \times 1.2 \times 10^7}{870} = 27586.2 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 5 — Square root:**

$$\sqrt{27586.2} = 166.09 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 — Final multiplication:**

$$Q = 0.62 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 166.09 = 1.544 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to L/min:**

$$Q = 1.544 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 \times 1000 = 92.65 \text{ L/min}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 92.65 \text{ L/min}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Proportional Pressure & Flow Control

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module-3 Internal Choice Option B]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Explain the construction and working of a **proportional pressure relief valve** with a neat sketch. How is it used for **pressure profiling** in an industrial press?

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
Derive the **force-balance equation** of a single-stage proportional solenoid valve spool and show how spool displacement $x$ is related to the input current $I$. State two assumptions made.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (3 marks):**
- A conventional direct-operated relief valve with the **adjustment screw replaced** by a **proportional solenoid** acting on the pilot stage.
- The pilot poppet / cone is spring-biased and modulated by solenoid force $F_m \propto I$.
- **Damping orifice / nozzle** in the pilot circuit to suppress oscillations.
- **Pressure transducer** feedback for closed-loop pressure control (optional).

**Working (2 marks):**
- Coil current $I$ generates axial force on the pilot armature.
- This force sets the cracking pressure $P_{crack}$ of the relief valve continuously.
- As $I$ rises, $P_{crack}$ rises proportionally; the valve opens when system pressure exceeds $P_{crack}$.
- Excess flow dumps to tank, regulating system pressure to the value commanded by $I$.

**Pressure Profiling in a Press (2 marks):**
- In a hydraulic press, the **pressing force** $F = P \cdot A_{ram}$ is controlled by modulating $P$.
- A **pressure-time profile** is programmed: low pressure for fast approach, high pressure for forming, hold for dwell, then release.
- The PPCV tracks this profile in real time, allowing different force curves for different materials (e.g. soft aluminium vs. hard steel).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Derivation

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Steady-state operation (spool acceleration negligible).
2. Negligible Coulomb friction and viscous damping.

**Force Balance (3 marks):**

Four forces act on the spool:
1. Magnetic force $F_m = k_1 I - k_2 x^2$
2. Centring spring force $F_s = K_s (x_0 + x)$
3. Hydraulic flow force on spool land $F_h$
4. Weight (neglected horizontally)

Equilibrium:

$$F_m = F_s + F_h$$

$$k_1 I - k_2 x^2 = K_s (x_0 + x) + F_h$$

**Solving for $x$ (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{x = \frac{-K_s + \sqrt{K_s^2 - 4 k_2 (K_s x_0 + F_h - k_1 I)}}{2 k_2}}$$

This shows $x$ is a **monotonically increasing function of $I$** within the working range, validating the proportional behaviour.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in Proportional Valve questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in numericals** — KTU examiners deduct $1$–$2$ marks if Pa is not converted from bar, or m³/s is not converted to L/min in the final answer.
> 2. **Skipping the negative sign in $k_2 x^2$** — The position-dependent decay term is *subtractive*; many students write $F_m = k_1 I + k_2 x^2$, which is physically wrong.
> 3. **Confusing proportional with servo valves** — Examiners specifically test the *boundary conditions* ($5$–$10$ % hysteresis vs $< 0.5$ %, $10$–$30$ Hz vs $50$–$200$ Hz bandwidth). Writing "same as servo valve" loses 2 full marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the role of pressure compensator** — When asked about flow regulation, students must mention the **two-way pressure compensator** that holds $\Delta P$ constant; without it, $Q$ is load-dependent.
> 5. **Forgetting to draw/sketch in part-(a) type questions** — KTU mandates a *neat labelled sketch*; absence costs at least **1 mark** out of 7.
> 6. **No mention of LVDT in closed-loop answers** — Always state that closed-loop versions use an LVDT for spool-position feedback.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the night before the exam:**

- [ ] **Definition:** Proportional valve = electrohydraulic valve with output continuously proportional to input electrical signal.
- [ ] **Core equation:** $Q = C_d \, A(x) \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$.
- [ ] **Magnetic force:** $F_m = k_1 I - k_2 x^2$ (note the minus sign on $x^2$).
- [ ] **Three types:** PDCV (direction + flow), PPCV (pressure), PFCV (flow only).
- [ ] **Reference signal standards:** $0$–$10$ V DC, or $4$–$20$ mA industrial.
- [ ] **Open-loop hysteresis:** $5$–$10$ %; **Closed-loop hysteresis:** $< 1$ % (via LVDT feedback).
- [ ] **Bandwidth:** Proportional valve $10$–$30$ Hz vs Servo valve $50$–$200$ Hz.
- [ ] **Pressure compensator:** A two-way valve that holds $\Delta P$ constant across the metering edge to make $Q$ independent of load.
- [ ] **Cost hierarchy:** Conventional $< $ Proportional $< $ Servo.
- [ ] **Filtration requirement:** Proportional valves need $\leq 25 \,\mu$m filtration; servo valves require $\leq 5 \,\mu$m.
- [ ] **Applications to remember:** Injection moulding, mobile hydraulics (LS systems), press brakes, ship steering, steel mills.
- [ ] **Always include:** a labelled sketch in any descriptive answer; mention LVDT for closed-loop; convert units properly in numericals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Servo Valves: Mechanical Type and Electrohydraulic Servo Valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT722 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Servo Valves: Mechanical Type and Electrohydraulic Servo Valves

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Servo Valve?

A **Servo Valve** is a high-precision, closed-loop hydraulic (or pneumatic) control element that regulates fluid flow, pressure, or direction in direct response to a low-power input command signal. Unlike conventional directional control valves (DCVs) which have discrete ON/OFF positions, a servo valve provides **infinitely variable, proportional, and bi-directional control** of hydraulic parameters. The output (fluid power) is many times larger than the input (electrical or mechanical signal power), making it a true **power amplification device**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3 Highlight):**
> A servo valve is defined as a *transducer in which a hydraulic output (flow or pressure) is made to vary in a predetermined relationship to an electrical (or mechanical) input signal*. It forms the heart of any Electro-Hydraulic Servo System (EHSS).

### 1.2 Classification of Servo Valves (As per KTU Module 3)

For the scope of this KTU module, servo valves are classified into two broad families:

| **Family** | **Input Energy** | **Application Domain** |
|------------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Mechanical Servo Valves** | Purely mechanical (lever, cam, pedal, push-pull rod) | Aircraft flight controls (legacy), heavy machinery linkages, machine tool slide positioning |
| **Electrohydraulic Servo Valves (EHSV)** | Electrical current (mA) from a controller | CNC machines, aerospace actuators, robotics, military steering, injection molding |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Faucet"

Imagine a normal household tap. You turn the handle *fully* to get full flow, or *fully* off to get zero flow — there is no in-between precision. Now imagine a **smart faucet** where you press a tiny touchpad by **0.5 mm** and the water flow changes by exactly **1 liter/minute**, where pressing by **2 mm** gives **4 liters/minute**, and the relationship is linear and repeatable to within 0.01 mm. The tiny finger movement (input) is amplified into a large, controlled water flow (output).

- Your finger = the **input signal** (electrical/mechanical)
- The touchpad = the **torque motor or mechanical linkage** (first stage)
- The water flow adjustment = the **main spool movement** (power stage)
- The smart faucet = the **servo valve**

> [!NOTE]
> The defining feature of a servo valve is **closed-loop feedback**. A sensor measures the actual output (e.g., cylinder position) and compares it with the commanded position. Any error (called the **null error** or **offset**) generates a corrective signal to the servo valve until the error is driven to zero.

### 1.4 Why Servo Valves Matter in Industry

- **Bandwidth:** Servo valves can respond to input signals at frequencies up to **100–500 Hz**, whereas a proportional valve is limited to about **10–30 Hz**.
- **Hysteresis:** Servo valves typically exhibit hysteresis of less than **3%**, compared to **5–10%** for proportional valves.
- **Resolution:** A high-grade servo valve can resolve input signals as small as **±0.1%** of full scale.

These metrics make EHSVs indispensable in applications requiring **dynamic response, precision, and repeatability**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanical Servo Valves

A **mechanical servo valve** is a spool-type valve in which the main spool is positioned by a purely mechanical input — typically a lever, cam, bell-crank, or push-pull linkage. The valve still functions as a *servo* (closed-loop) device when it is placed inside a feedback loop where a mechanical linkage (e.g., a connecting rod from the output cylinder) restores the spool to a balanced position.

#### 2.1.1 Operating Principle — Lever-Operated Spool

1. The operator (or upstream controller) applies a force $F_i$ at the input lever.
2. The lever pivots about a fulcrum, producing a small linear displacement $x_s$ at the spool end.
3. The spool shifts by $x_s$, opening the metering windows to the actuator ports.
4. The actuator produces an output force $F_o$ proportional to spool displacement.
5. A mechanical feedback linkage (often a lever attached to the actuator rod) opposes the input force.
6. **Equilibrium** is reached when the input force equals the feedback force — the spool recenters and the actuator holds its position.

The spool acts as a **floating summing junction**: input force on one side, feedback force on the other.

#### 2.1.2 Advantages and Limitations

- **Advantages:** No electrical power required; inherently fail-safe; immune to electromagnetic interference (EMI); rugged.
- **Limitations:** Bulky mechanical linkages; limited bandwidth (typically **< 10 Hz**); difficult to integrate with digital controllers; prone to friction and backlash.

### 2.2 Electrohydraulic Servo Valves (EHSV)

The **Electrohydraulic Servo Valve** is the workhorse of modern precision hydraulics. It converts a low-power electrical current (typically $\pm 10\,\text{mA}$ to $\pm 40\,\text{mA}$) into a precisely metered hydraulic flow. A KTU examiner expects you to know the **two-stage, flapper-nozzle type** in detail, as it is the industry standard (e.g., Moog G761, Parker T6).

#### 2.2.1 The Two-Stage Flapper-Nozzle EHSV — Block Architecture

A two-stage EHSV consists of two cascaded power-amplification stages:

**Stage 1 — Electrical-to-Mechanical Pilot Stage (Torque Motor + Flapper-Nozzle):**
- A **torque motor** (an electromagnetic actuator) receives the input current $I$.
- It produces a small angular torque $\tau$ on an armature.
- The armature is rigidly connected to a **flapper** (a thin flexible arm).
- The flapper sits between two **nozzles** that discharge pressurized pilot oil to a drain.
- Deflection of the flapper changes the **nozzle back-pressure**, which becomes the hydraulic control signal.

**Stage 2 — Hydraulic Power Stage (Spool + Feedback Wire):**
- The differential nozzle pressure acts on the ends of a second-stage **main spool**.
- The spool shifts, opening the main flow path from pump (P) to actuator (A/B) and to tank (T).
- A thin **feedback wire (mechanical spring)** is bent by spool motion and produces a restoring torque on the armature.
- When the restoring torque equals the motor torque, the flapper recenters and the spool holds its new position.

This internal feedback loop is what makes the valve *self-balancing* and gives it its characteristic **proportional, linear flow-vs-current relationship**.

#### 2.2.2 The Three-Stage EHSV (Brief Note for KTU)

For very high flow rates (above **~400 L/min**), a single two-stage valve cannot produce enough pilot force. A **three-stage** design is used:
- **Stage 1:** Torque motor + flapper-nozzle (electrical-to-pilot).
- **Stage 2:** Pilot-operated hydraulic amplifier (boosts pilot pressure to ~70 bar).
- **Stage 3:** Main spool, driven by the boosted pilot pressure (handles full system flow).

This is commonly seen in steel-mill hydraulic presses and naval deck machinery.

#### 2.2.3 Jet-Pipe Servo Valve (Alternative Pilot Stage)

In a **jet-pipe** valve, instead of a flapper between two nozzles, a small jet of oil is aimed at one of two receiver ports. The flapper is replaced by a deflectable **jet pipe**. Jet-pipe valves are more **contamination-tolerant** but have slightly lower bandwidth than flapper-nozzle designs.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are essential for KTU Numerical Answer Questions (NAQs) and 14-mark derivations.

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Significance** |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|-----------|------------------|
| $Q_L$ | Load flow (through servo valve) | $Q_L = C_d A \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Square-root flow-pressure relationship |
| $K_q$ | Flow gain of valve | $K_q = \dfrac{\partial Q_L}{\partial x_v}$ at constant $\Delta P$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | How much flow per mm of spool shift |
| $K_c$ | Pressure gain of valve | $K_c = -\dfrac{\partial Q_L}{\partial P_L}$ at constant $x_v$ | $\text{m}^3/(\text{s}\cdot\text{Pa})$ | Sensitivity of flow to load pressure |
| $x_v$ | Spool displacement from null | $x_v = K_{sv} \cdot I$ (linear region) | $\text{m}$ | Linearized input-output relation |
| $K_{sv}$ | Servo valve gain | $K_{sv} = \dfrac{Q_{rated}}{I_{rated}}$ | $\text{m}^3/(\text{s}\cdot\text{A})$ | Rated flow per rated current |
| $f_{-3\text{dB}}$ | Valve bandwidth | $f_{-3\text{dB}} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau_v}$ | $\text{Hz}$ | Frequency at which response drops to **-3 dB** (70.7%) |
| $\omega_n$ | Natural frequency of spool | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K_{mech}}{m_{spool}}}$ | $\text{rad}/\text{s}$ | Spool-mass vs spring-stiffness resonance |
| $F_{fric}$ | Friction force on spool | $F_{fric} = \mu \, F_{normal}$ | $\text{N}$ | Coulomb friction (negligible in good servos) |
