**(iii) Reseat pressure ($P_r$):** The pressure at which the valve fully closes again after having been open. It is always lower than the cracking pressure, and the difference $P_c - P_r$ is called **blowdown** or **reseat margin**, which prevents valve chatter.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Distinguish between a pressure relief valve and a pressure reducing valve in three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Pressure Relief Valve | Pressure Reducing Valve |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Connected in **parallel** with the pump; between pressure line and tank. | Connected in **series** with the downstream (secondary) circuit. |
| 2 | Pressure-sensing port is on the **inlet (upstream)** side. | Pressure-sensing port is on the **outlet (downstream)** side. |
| 3 | Limits the **maximum** system pressure; normally closed, opens only on overpressure. | Maintains a **reduced constant** pressure downstream; normally open, throttles when downstream pressure rises. |
| 4 | Vents excess flow to **tank** when open. | Restricts flow to **downstream circuit** to create pressure drop. |
| 5 | Used as a **safety** device. | Used as a **regulating** device for multi-pressure circuits. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a pilot-operated pressure relief valve**. Discuss why it is preferred over a direct-operated valve in high-flow systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pressure relief valve has a poppet seat diameter of 20 mm and is held closed by a spring of stiffness 12 N/mm with a pre-compression of 10 mm. The valve is required to pass a full flow of 40 L/min at a system pressure 8% above the cracking pressure. Take oil density as 870 kg/m³ and discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.65$. Calculate: **(i)** the cracking pressure, **(ii)** the full-flow pressure, **(iii)** the diameter of the orifice through which the oil is vented to tank at full flow. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Construction and Working of Pilot-Operated PRV** (7 marks)

A pilot-operated (compound) pressure relief valve consists of two stages:

**Main Stage:**
- A large main spool/poppet held against its seat by a very light spring.
- A small **drain orifice** is drilled through the main spool.
- The chamber above the main spool is connected via this drain orifice to the tank.

**Pilot Stage:**
- A small direct-operated relief valve mounted on top of the main stage.
- Has its own adjustment screw and is set to the desired cracking pressure $P_c$.
- The pilot is fed by an internal passage from the system pressure.

**Working Sequence:**

1. **System pressure $P_{sys} < P_c$:** Pilot valve is closed. No flow passes through the drain orifice. Pressure in the chamber above the main spool equals system pressure. Net hydraulic force on main spool keeps it firmly closed. Pump flow goes to actuators.

2. **System pressure $P_{sys} \geq P_c$:** Pilot valve begins to open. A small pilot flow (typically 1–2% of main flow) escapes through the pilot to tank.

3. **Pressure Drop Across Drain Orifice:** As pilot flow passes through the drain orifice, a pressure drop $\Delta P_o = P_{sys} - P_{chamber}$ develops. The chamber pressure falls.

4. **Main Stage Opens:** When chamber pressure falls sufficiently, the hydraulic force on the main spool (from system pressure on the seat area) exceeds the chamber pressure force plus spring force. The main spool lifts, and full pump flow is dumped to tank.

5. **Steady State:** The pilot modulates its opening to maintain just enough drain flow to keep the main spool at the position needed to pass excess flow. The system stabilizes at $P_{sys} \approx P_c$.

**Why Preferred in High-Flow Systems:**
- The pilot handles only a tiny flow, so it can use a stiff, well-controlled spring with low override.
- The main stage opens with very little pressure rise above $P_c$ (override < 1–2%).
- A direct-operated valve would need a massive, stiff spring to overcome the seat hydraulic force at 200 L/min — ergonomically and dimensionally impractical.
- Pilot-operated valves also enable **remote adjustment** and **unloading** by venting the chamber pressure.

**ISO Symbol:** Square box with arrow to tank + small pilot sub-symbol on side, dashed connection from main to pilot.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution** (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $d_s = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$
- $k = 12 \text{ N/mm} = 12{,}000 \text{ N/m}$
- $x_0 = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.010 \text{ m}$
- $Q = 40 \text{ L/min} = 40 \times 10^{-3} / 60 = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $\rho = 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C_d = 0.65$
- Pressure override: $P_f = 1.08 \, P_c$

**Step 1 — Seat area:**

$$A_s = \frac{\pi d_s^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.020)^2}{4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Cracking pressure:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \frac{k \, x_0}{A_s} \\
&= \frac{12{,}000 \times 0.010}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= \frac{120}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= 381{,}972 \text{ Pa} \\
&\approx 3.82 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Full-flow pressure:**

$$P_f = 1.08 \times P_c = 1.08 \times 3.82 \approx 4.13 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the orifice equation to find the vent diameter:**

At full flow, the vent passes the full $Q = 40 \text{ L/min}$ to tank, with the full-flow pressure as the upstream pressure (downstream is tank ≈ 0 gauge):

$$Q = C_d \, A_v \sqrt{\frac{2 P_f}{\rho}}$$

Solving for $A_v$:

$$A_v = \frac{Q}{C_d} \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{2 P_f}}$$

Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_v &= \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{0.65} \sqrt{\frac{870}{2 \times 413{,}520}} \\
&= 1.0257 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{\frac{870}{827{,}040}} \\
&= 1.0257 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{1.0521 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= 1.0257 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.03244 \\
&= 3.327 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Vent diameter:**

$$d_v = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_v}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 3.327 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi}}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
d_v &= \sqrt{4.236 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= 6.51 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \\
&= 6.51 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answers (boxed style for KTU script):**
- (i) $P_c = 3.82 \text{ bar}$
- (ii) $P_f = 4.13 \text{ bar}$
- (iii) $d_v = 6.51 \text{ mm}$

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a pressure reducing valve**. How does it differ from a pressure relief valve in terms of circuit connection and sensing port? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of an **unloading valve with an accumulator circuit**. A hydraulic system uses an accumulator pre-charged to 80 bar and an unloading valve set to open when system pressure reaches 150 bar and close at 120 bar. Calculate: **(i)** the pump flow required if the actuator consumes 30 L/min during the 5-second work stroke, and the pump must refill the accumulator between strokes, **(ii)** the average power saved by unloading compared to a system that maintains 150 bar continuously during the 5-second idle period. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Pressure Reducing Valve** (7 marks)

A pressure reducing valve maintains a **constant lower pressure** in a secondary circuit regardless of higher primary pressure. It is fundamentally a normally-open valve that progressively closes as downstream pressure approaches the set value.

**Construction:**
- A spool or poppet is held **open** by a spring on one end.
- A sensing passage connects the **outlet (downstream)** port to the spring-end chamber.
- An optional relief (drain) port vents leakage.

**Working:**

1. **Initial State (system just energized):** Downstream pressure is zero. Spool is pushed fully open by the spring. Full flow from primary circuit passes through to the secondary circuit.

2. **Downstream Pressure Rises:** As the secondary circuit fills, downstream pressure $P_2$ acts on the spring-end of the spool (via sensing passage), opposing the spring force.

3. **Throttling Begins:** When $P_2 \times A_2 \geq F_{spring}$, the spool starts moving against the spring, partially closing the valve. A pressure drop $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ develops across the valve.

4. **Regulation Equilibrium:** The spool reaches an equilibrium position where the spring force balances the hydraulic force from downstream pressure. Downstream pressure stabilizes at $P_{set,reduce} = F_{spring} / A_2$.

5. **If Primary Pressure Drops:** The spool opens more to maintain $P_2$. If primary pressure equals or falls below $P_{set,reduce}$, the valve is fully open and behaves as a simple passage.

**Differences from Pressure Relief Valve:**

| Aspect | Pressure Relief Valve | Pressure Reducing Valve |
|---|---|---|
| Circuit connection | **Parallel** to pump (between pressure line and tank) | **Series** with downstream circuit |
| Sensing port | **Inlet (upstream)** — senses system pressure | **Outlet (downstream)** — senses secondary pressure |
| Normal state | **Closed** | **Open** |
| Action | **Opens** when pressure exceeds set | **Closes** when downstream pressure reaches set |
| Purpose | **Limits maximum** pressure (safety) | **Maintains constant** lower pressure (regulation) |

**Typical Application:** In a machine tool, the main hydraulic supply is at 200 bar for heavy cutting, but the work-holding clamp requires only 50 bar. A pressure reducing valve provides this 50 bar clamping pressure from the 200 bar main supply.

---

**(b) Unloading Valve with Accumulator — Numerical** (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Accumulator pre-charge: $P_{pre} = 80 \text{ bar}$
- Upper switch pressure (unloading opens): $P_H = 150 \text{ bar}$
- Lower switch pressure (unloading closes): $P_L = 120 \text{ bar}$
- Actuator consumption: $Q_{act} = 30 \text{ L/min}$
- Work stroke duration: $t_{work} = 5 \text{ s}$
- Pump must refill accumulator between strokes (steady state).

**Step 1 — Volume of fluid delivered by accumulator during work stroke:**

The accumulator releases fluid as pressure drops from $P_H$ to $P_L$. For an ideal gas (pre-charge) using Boyle's law approximation, the usable volume is:

$$\Delta V_{acc} = V_{acc} \left[ \left(\frac{P_{pre}}{P_L}\right)^{0.715} - \left(\frac{P_{pre}}{P_H}\right)^{0.715} \right]$$

For a typical industrial accumulator with pre-charge ratio, the **usable volume** is often approximated as:

$$V_{usable} \approx V_{acc} \times \frac{P_{pre}}{P_L} \left( 1 - \frac{P_L}{P_H} \right)$$

But since we are not given $V_{acc}$, we take a different approach.

**Step 1 (Alternative approach) — Fluid used in work stroke:**

$$V_{used} = Q_{act} \times t_{work} = 30 \text{ L/min} \times \frac{5}{60} \text{ min}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{used} &= 30 \times 0.0833 \\
&= 2.5 \text{ L}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the volume the accumulator must supply (and therefore the pump must replenish) in each cycle.

**Step 2 — Recharge time:**

If the pump delivers $Q_{pump}$ and during recharge the actuator is idle (no consumption), the entire pump flow goes to recharging the accumulator until pressure rises from $P_L$ to $P_H$. However, **without the accumulator volume** we cannot directly solve for recharge time, so we treat this differently.

**Step 3 — Determine required pump flow:**

For continuous operation with a cycle time $T_{cycle} = t_{work} + t_{recharge}$, the pump must supply an average flow equal to actuator consumption:

$$Q_{pump,avg} = Q_{act} = 30 \text{ L/min}$$

**Therefore, the pump must be sized to deliver at least 30 L/min.** If we assume the pump charges the accumulator from $P_L$ to $P_H$ during the recharge period and the pump charges at 30 L/min directly, the system is balanced.

**Step 4 — Power saved by unloading:**

When the system is unloaded, the pump discharges to tank at near-zero pressure. Without unloading, the pump would maintain 150 bar continuously, including during the 5-second idle period, doing **no useful work** but generating heat.

**Power without unloading (continuous 150 bar):**

$$P_{no\,unload} = \Delta P_{relief} \times Q_{pump} = 150 \text{ bar} \times 30 \text{ L/min}$$

Converting units: $150 \text{ bar} = 15 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$, $30 \text{ L/min} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{no\,unload} &= 15 \times 10^6 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \\
&= 7500 \text{ W} \\
&= 7.5 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Power with unloading (pump at ~0 bar during idle):**

The pump consumes only the power needed to overcome line losses and overcome the unloading valve's low cracking pressure (say 2 bar, often ignored):

$$P_{unload} \approx 0 \text{ bar} \times 30 \text{ L/min} \approx 0 \text{ kW (ideal)}$$

**Power saved during the 5-second idle period:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{saved} &= P_{no\,unload} \times t_{idle} \\
&= 7.5 \text{ kW} \times 5 \text{ s} \\
&= 37.5 \text{ kJ}
\end{aligned}$$

**Average power saved (per 5-second idle window):**

$$P_{saved,avg} = \frac{E_{saved}}{t_{idle}} = 7.5 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Realization:** In a real factory cycle of (say) 10 seconds (5 s work + 5 s idle), unloading saves 7.5 kW continuously when idle, which is enormous. Over an 8-hour shift, this is $7.5 \times 8 = 60 \text{ kWh}$ per day. At ₹8/kWh industrial tariff, that's ₹480/day, or **₹1.75 lakh/year per machine**. Unloading valves pay for themselves in weeks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Pressure is often given in bar but the formula uses Pa. Forgetting to multiply by $10^5$ leads to answers off by 5 orders of magnitude. **Always show unit conversion explicitly.**
> 2. **Confusing Cracking with Full-Flow Pressure:** Many students assume these are the same. They are not. Always state both and compute the override.
> 3. **Wrong Sensing Port in Reducing Valve:** Students often draw the sensing arrow from the **inlet** for a reducing valve. The correct source is the **outlet** — this is what makes it reduce downstream pressure.
> 4. **Force Balance Direction Sign Error:** In the spool force balance, ensure the spring force opposes hydraulic force, not aids it. Draw a free-body diagram before writing the equation.
> 5. **Forgetting Orifice Coefficient:** When applying the orifice flow equation $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2\Delta P/\rho}$, omitting $C_d$ (or assuming $C_d = 1$) is a common mistake. Use the given value.
> 6. **Sketch Quality:** KTU awards 1–2 marks for a **labelled sketch** of the valve construction. A generic rectangle without internal details scores zero. Always show the spring, spool/poppet, ports, and sensing passage.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pressure Regulation (Module 4)**

- **Pressure control valves** are classified by **function** (relief, reducing, sequence, unloading, counterbalance), not by physical appearance.
- **Cracking pressure $P_c$** is the opening threshold; **full-flow pressure $P_f$** is reached at rated flow; **reseat pressure $P_r$** is the closing threshold on falling pressure.
- **Pressure override** $\Delta P = P_f - P_c$ — keep it small for stable regulation.
- **Blowdown** $= P_c - P_r$ — must be positive to prevent valve **chatter**.
- **Direct-operated PRV:** $P_c = k x_0 / A_s$. Simple, robust, but high override.
- **Pilot-operated PRV:** Two-stage; very low override (< 1%); preferred for high-flow (> 50 L/min) systems.
- **Pressure reducing valve:** Normally **open**, sensing port on **outlet**, throttles as downstream pressure rises.
- **Pressure sequence valve:** Normally **closed**, opens when sensed (primary) pressure reaches set value, ensuring ordered actuation.
- **Unloading valve:** Dumps pump flow to tank at near-zero pressure during idle periods; used with **accumulators** to save energy.
- **Counterbalance valve:** Provides controlled back-pressure in vertical load-holding circuits.
- **ISO 1219-1** standardizes all schematic symbols — master them.
- **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $P_c = k x_0 / A_s$ (direct-operated valve)
  - $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ (orifice flow)
  - $\Delta P = P_f - P_c$ (pressure override)
- **Standard safety practice:** Set relief valve at **+10% to +15%** above maximum working pressure.
- **Hydraulic fluid density:** $\rho \approx 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$ for mineral oil (default value in KTU problems unless stated).
- **Pilot-operated vs direct-operated exam tip:** If the problem gives a high flow (> 50 L/min) and asks for a small override, expect a pilot-operated answer with pilot-flow orifice analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compressor Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT722 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Compressor Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 4 Focus):**
> A **compressor** is a mechanical device that converts **mechanical (shaft) energy** into **pneumatic potential energy** by compressing air or gas from a low-pressure inlet condition to a higher-pressure discharge condition. In the context of KTU Module 4 (Pneumatic Power Generation & Air Treatment), compressors form the *primary energy source* of every industrial pneumatic system, feeding the FRL (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) unit and the air receiver.

### 1.1 Working Principle — The "Bicycle Pump" Analogy
Imagine pushing the handle of a **bicycle pump** (reciprocating action) into a cylinder. The air inside is squeezed into a smaller volume, and because molecules are forced closer, the **pressure rises**. A compressor is essentially an industrial, continuous, and far more efficient version of this bicycle pump.

If we instead use a **spinning fan blade inside a curved housing**, the air is hurled outward by centrifugal force — this is the principle of a **centrifugal compressor** (think of a turbocharger in a diesel engine).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Energy Conversion Pathway in a Compressor:**
> **Electrical Energy → Mechanical Energy (Motor Shaft) → Pneumatic Energy (Pressurized Air)**

### 1.2 Classification of Compressors (As per KTU Module 4)
Compressors are broadly classified based on the **mechanism of compression** and **direction of air flow**:

| Classification Basis | Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Positive Displacement** | Reciprocating (Piston) | Single-stage, Two-stage reciprocating |
| **Positive Displacement** | Rotary | Vane, Screw (Oil-injected/Oil-free), Lobe, Scroll |
| **Dynamic (Turbo)** | Centrifugal | Radial-flow impeller type |
| **Dynamic (Turbo)** | Axial | Multi-stage blade type (jet engines) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Positive displacement compressors deliver a *fixed volume per cycle* at a given speed, whereas dynamic compressors deliver a *variable volume* that depends on the discharge back-pressure.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Must Remember for KTU Board Exams)
- **Free Air Delivery (FAD):** Actual volume of air delivered per unit time, measured at **inlet conditions** (typically **1.013 bar, 20°C**). Unit: **m³/min** or **CFM**.
- **Discharge Pressure (P₂):** Final pressure at compressor outlet. Industrial pneumatics typically operates at **6 – 8 bar(g)** or **7 – 10 bar(g)**.
- **Compression Ratio (r):** $r = P_2 / P_1$
- **Swept Volume (Vs):** Geometric displacement per cycle = $\frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times L \times N$ (for reciprocating).
- **Clearance Volume (Vc):** Dead volume remaining in cylinder at end of stroke.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Reciprocating (Piston) Compressors
These are the most common compressors in small-to-medium pneumatic workshops. A piston reciprocates inside a cylinder via a crankshaft.

**Single-Stage Reciprocating Compressor:**
- Air is drawn in through the **intake valve** during the suction stroke.
- Compressed during the **compression stroke**.
- Discharged through the **delivery valve** during the delivery stroke.

**Two-Stage Reciprocating Compressor (with intercooler):**
- Used for higher pressure ratios (typically above 5:1).
- Air is compressed in **Cylinder 1 → Intercooler (cools air back to T₁) → Cylinder 2**.
- The intercooler reduces the work of compression and improves volumetric efficiency.

**Volumetric Efficiency of a Reciprocating Compressor:**
$$\eta_v = \frac{V_a}{V_s} = 1 + C - C \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n}$$

where:
- $V_a$ = actual volume of air drawn
- $V_s$ = swept volume
- $C = V_c / V_s$ = clearance ratio
- $n$ = polytropic index (≈ 1.3 for air, 1.4 for adiabatic)

### 2.2 Rotary Compressors
Positive displacement type with **rotating elements** (no reciprocating motion). Common types:

**a) Rotary Vane Compressor:**
- Eccentric rotor with sliding vanes slots.
- Vanes trap and compress air between rotor and stator.

**b) Rotary Screw Compressor (Twin-Screw):**
- Two intermeshing helical rotors (male and female).
- Widely used in industry — **oil-flooded** or **oil-free** versions.
- Continuous, pulsation-free delivery.

**c) Scroll Compressor:**
- One fixed scroll, one orbiting scroll.
- Air is squeezed between the scrolls toward the center.

### 2.3 Centrifugal (Radial-Flow) Compressor
- A high-speed **impeller** accelerates air radially outward.
- **Diffuser** (volute casing) converts kinetic energy → pressure energy.
- Suited for **high flow, moderate pressure** applications.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compression Ratio | $r = P_2 / P_1$ | dimensionless | $P_1$ = inlet, $P_2$ = discharge |
| 2 | Swept Volume | $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L N$ | m³ | $D$ = bore, $L$ = stroke, $N$ = number of cylinders |
| 3 | Clearance Ratio | $C = V_c / V_s$ | dimensionless | Typically 2–6 % |
| 4 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = 1 + C - C \cdot r^{1/n}$ | dimensionless | Decreases with higher $r$ |
| 5 | Isothermal Work | $W_{iso} = P_1 V_1 \ln(r)$ | J | Ideal cooling (assumed) |
| 6 | Polytropic Work | $W_{poly} = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ r^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$ | J | Real compression |
| 7 | Isothermal Power | $P_{iso} = \frac{W_{iso}}{t} = P_1 \dot{V}_1 \ln(r)$ | W | Frictionless, ideal |
| 8 | Polytropic Power | $P_{poly} = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 \dot{V}_1 \left[ r^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$ | W | Realistic |
| 9 | Indicated Power | $P_i = \frac{P_{poly}}{\eta_{mech}}$ | W | Includes mechanical losses |
| 10 | Free Air Delivery (Power) | $P_{FAD} = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 \dot{V}_{FAD} \left[ r^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$ | W | Using actual FAD volume |
| 11 | Shaft Power | $P_{shaft} = \frac{P_i}{\eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$ | W | Includes motor + drive losses |
| 12 | Specific Work (per kg) | $w = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ r^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$ | J/kg | For unit mass analysis |
| 13 | Capacity | $C_{ap} = \eta_v \cdot V_s \cdot N \cdot k$ | m³/s | $k$ = no. of cylinders |
| 14 | Work Ratio | $W_{ratio} = \frac{W_{iso}}{W_{poly}}$ | dimensionless | Always < 1 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU EXAM RULE — LaTeX Pipe Symbol:**
> In the formulas above, $\ln(\cdot)$ and absolute value bars must be written using `\ln` or `\vert`. **Never** use the keyboard pipe `|` character inside markdown table cells — it breaks the table layout. Use $\vert x \vert$ or $\ln(r)$ syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Automotive paint shops:** Oil-free screw compressors (clean air).
- **Pneumatic tool workshops:** Two-stage reciprocating (high pressure).
- **PET bottle blowing:** High-pressure reciprocating (~30–40 bar).
- **Process industries:** Centrifugal for large flow, low pressure duty.
- **HVAC / Aeration tanks:** Screw or centrifugal.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Polytropic Work of Compression (Per kg of Air)

**Starting thermodynamic relation (1st law for open system, control volume):**
$$dW = -V \, dP$$

**For a polytropic process $PV^n = \text{constant}$:**
$$V = \frac{C}{P^{1/n}}$$

**Differentiating the polytropic relation** $P V^n = k$:
$$V^n \, dP + n P V^{n-1} \, dV = 0$$
$$\Rightarrow V = -\frac{n P \, dV}{dP} \quad \Rightarrow \quad dV = -\frac{V}{n P} dP$$

**Substitute into $dW = -V \, dP$:**
$$dW = -V \, dP$$

**Integrate from state 1 → state 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{poly} &= \int_{1}^{2} V \, dP \quad \text{(taking magnitude of work input)} \\
&= \int_{P_1}^{P_2} \left(\frac{P_1 V_1^n}{P^n}\right) dP \\
&= P_1 V_1^n \int_{P_1}^{P_2} P^{-n} \, dP \\
&= P_1 V_1^n \left[ \frac{P^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{P_1}^{P_2} \\
&= P_1 V_1^n \cdot \frac{P_2^{1-n} - P_1^{1-n}}{1-n} \\
&= \frac{P_1 V_1^n}{n-1} \left[ P_1^{1-n} - P_2^{1-n} \right] \\
&= \frac{P_1 V_1}{n-1} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{n-1} \right] \\
W_{poly} &= \frac{n}{n-1} \, P_1 V_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The final boxed equation is the **most-tested formula** in KTU Module 4. Memorize the structure: $\frac{n}{n-1} \cdot P_1 V_1 \left[ r^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Volumetric Efficiency of a Reciprocating Compressor

Consider an indicator diagram (P-V) for a reciprocating compressor with clearance volume $V_c$:

**Step 1 — At point 3 (end of delivery stroke):** Volume in cylinder = $V_c$, pressure = $P_2$.
**Step 2 — Expansion from 3 → 4 (polytropic):** $P_2 V_c^n = P_1 V_4^n$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_4 &= V_c \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/n} = V_c \cdot r^{1/n}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Fresh air drawn in (4 → 1) at suction pressure $P_1$:**
$$V_a = V_1 - V_4 = (V_c + V_s) - V_c \cdot r^{1/n}$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric efficiency definition:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_v &= \frac{V_a}{V_s} = \frac{V_c + V_s - V_c \, r^{1/n}}{V_s} \\
&= \frac{V_c}{V_s} + 1 - \frac{V_c}{V_s} \cdot r^{1/n} \\
\eta_v &= 1 + C - C \cdot r^{1/n} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C = V_c / V_s$ is the **clearance ratio**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Compressor Type

| Application Parameter | Recommended Compressor | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Low FAD (< 1 m³/min), 7 bar | Single-stage reciprocating | Simple, cheap, sufficient |
| High pressure (> 10 bar) | Two-stage reciprocating with intercooler | Lower discharge temperature, higher $\eta$ |
| Continuous duty, oil-free air | Oil-free screw or scroll | No contamination, low pulsation |
| High FAD (> 20 m³/min), < 4 bar | Centrifugal | High flow, dynamic efficiency |
| Variable demand | VFD-driven screw | Energy savings at part load |
| Portable site work | Diesel-driven reciprocating | Rugged, no electricity needed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Logic Check (Common Question):**
> "Why is a two-stage compressor with intercooler more efficient than a single-stage for the same final pressure?"
> **Answer:** Intercooling brings the air back to T₁ before the second stage, reducing the **specific volume** entering stage 2. Less mass per stroke, but more importantly, less work per unit mass because the temperature rise is split. The total work is less than a single equivalent compression.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pneumatic Compressor System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Atmospheric Air Intake] --> B[Air Filter / Suction Strainer]
    B --> C[Compressor Unit - Reciprocating or Rotary]
    C --> D[Check Valve NRV]
    D --> E[Aftercooler]
    E --> F[Moisture Separator]
    F --> G[Air Receiver Tank]
    G --> H[FRL Unit - Filter Regulator Lubricator]
    H --> I[Pneumatic Distribution Line]
    I --> J[End Use - Actuators and Tools]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style C fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style G fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style H fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,color:#000
    style J fill:#e1e1f5,stroke:#333399,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this:** Air enters at A, gets cleaned at B, pressurized at C, back-flow prevented by the check valve D, cooled at E, dried at F, stored at G, conditioned at H, and finally delivered through distribution to actuators at J. This is the standard ISO 1219 pneumatic supply-line architecture.

### 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram: Reciprocating vs Rotary vs Centrifugal

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compressor Family] --> B[Positive Displacement]
    A --> C[Dynamic Turbo]

    B --> D[Reciprocating Type]
    B --> E[Rotary Type]

    D --> D1[Single Stage - up to 7 bar]
    D --> D2[Two Stage with Intercooler - up to 30 bar]

    E --> E1[Rotary Vane - up to 4 bar]
    E --> E2[Rotary Screw - up to 13 bar]
    E --> E3[Scroll - up to 10 bar]

    C --> F[Centrifugal - High Flow Low Pressure]
    C --> G[Axial - Very High Flow Special]

    style A fill:#fff7c2,stroke:#aa8800,color:#000
    style B fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0044aa,color:#000
    style C fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#aa3300,color:#000
    style D fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    style E fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#006600,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Two-Stage Reciprocating Compressor with Intercooler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AIR[Atmospheric Air] -->|Inlet| LP[Low Pressure Cylinder Stage 1]
    LP -->|Discharge| IC[Intercooler - Heat Exchanger]
    IC -->|Cooled Air| HP[High Pressure Cylinder Stage 2]
    HP -->|Final Discharge| RV[Receiver Tank]
    RV -->|Pressurized Air| OUT[To Pneumatic System]

    style AIR fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    style LP fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000
    style IC fill:#ffe1cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style HP fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc4400,color:#000
    style RV fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    style OUT fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two stages?** Splitting the compression reduces the **temperature rise** ($\Delta T$) at each stage. The work input $W = \int V \, dP$ is lower because the air is denser (cooler) when it enters the second stage, leading to lower total work for the same final pressure. This is a frequently-asked KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a compressor. Differentiate between a positive displacement compressor and a dynamic compressor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A compressor is a machine that raises the pressure of a gas (typically air) by mechanical means, converting shaft work into pneumatic potential energy used to power pneumatic circuits.

| Feature | Positive Displacement | Dynamic (Turbo) |
|---|---|---|
| Compression mechanism | Traps fixed volume and reduces it | Accelerates air via impeller, then diffuses |
| Delivery at fixed speed | Constant volume per cycle | Variable with back-pressure |
| Typical application | High pressure, low-medium flow | High flow, low-medium pressure |
| Example | Reciprocating, Screw, Vane | Centrifugal, Axial |

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for clean tabulated comparison)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the meaning of Free Air Delivery (FAD) and Clearance Ratio. Why is volumetric efficiency of a reciprocating compressor less than 100 %?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **FAD (Free Air Delivery):** The actual volume of air delivered by the compressor per unit time, **measured at the inlet atmospheric conditions** (1.013 bar, 20°C). Unit: m³/min or CFM. (1 mark)
- **Clearance Ratio (C):** The ratio of clearance volume $V_c$ (dead space in cylinder at end of stroke) to the swept volume $V_s$. $C = V_c / V_s$. (1 mark)
- **Why $\eta_v < 100$ %:** Because of the clearance volume — at the end of the delivery stroke, the compressed air trapped in the clearance space **re-expands** on the return stroke, occupying part of the swept volume. Fresh air can only be drawn in for the remaining volume. Therefore, $V_a < V_s$, giving $\eta_v < 1$. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note on KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question is internally optional — you will be given either **Q.11 (a) or (b)** to attempt. The marks split is typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** OR **a single 14-mark analytical problem**. Below, two independent 14-mark alternatives are provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**Q. (a)** A single-stage reciprocating compressor compresses air from **1 bar to 7 bar**. The clearance ratio is **4 %** and the polytropic index is **n = 1.3**. Calculate the **volumetric efficiency** of the compressor. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **two-stage reciprocating compressor with intercooler**. How does intercooling improve efficiency? (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Q. (a) — Volumetric Efficiency

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar}$, $P_2 = 7 \text{ bar}$
- Clearance ratio $C = V_c / V_s = 0.04$
- Polytropic index $n = 1.3$

**Formula:**
$$\eta_v = 1 + C - C \cdot r^{1/n}$$

**Step 1 — Compression ratio:**
$$r = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{7}{1} = 7$$

**Step 2 — Compute $r^{1/n}$:**
$$r^{1/n} = 7^{1/1.3} = 7^{0.7692}$$
$$\ln(7^{0.7692}) = 0.7692 \times \ln 7 = 0.7692 \times 1.9459 = 1.497$$
$$r^{1/n} = e^{1.497} \approx 4.469$$

**Step 3 — Plug into volumetric efficiency formula:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_v &= 1 + 0.04 - 0.04 \times 4.469 \\
&= 1.04 - 0.1788 \\
&= 0.8612 \\
\eta_v &\approx 86.12 \ \% \ \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Answer (7 marks breakdown):**
- Correct formula statement — **1 mark**
- Compression ratio calculation — **1 mark**
- $r^{1/n}$ calculation — **2 marks**
- Final $\eta_v$ numerical value — **2 marks**
- Units / physical interpretation comment — **1 mark**

---

#### Solution to Q. (b) — Two-Stage Compressor Explanation

**Working (4 marks):**
A two-stage reciprocating compressor consists of two cylinders — a **low-pressure (LP) cylinder** and a **high-pressure (HP) cylinder** — connected through an **intercooler**.

1. **Suction stroke (LP cylinder):** Atmospheric air enters LP cylinder through the inlet valve.
2. **Compression in LP cylinder:** Air is compressed from $P_1$ to an intermediate pressure $P_i$ (≈ √(P₁·P₂) for optimal work).
3. **Intercooling:** Hot, high-pressure air from LP cylinder passes through the **intercooler**, where it is cooled back to a temperature close to $T_1$. Cooling is typically done by water jackets or forced air.
4. **Compression in HP cylinder:** Cooled air is further compressed from $P_i$ to final $P_2$.
5. **Delivery:** Final high-pressure air is delivered to the receiver.

**How intercooling improves efficiency (3 marks):**
- Reduces the **specific volume** of air entering the HP cylinder, so less work is required in the second stage.
- Total work of two-stage with intercooling < total work of single-stage compression to same $P_2$.
- Also reduces **discharge temperature**, improving safety and lubrication life.
- Improves **volumetric efficiency** of the second stage.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**Q. (a)** With a labelled schematic, explain the working of a **rotary screw compressor**. List its advantages over a reciprocating compressor. (7 marks)

**Q. (b)** A two-stage air compressor takes in air at **1 bar, 30 °C** and delivers at **15 bar**. The intermediate pressure is **3.5 bar**. Assuming isentropic compression with $n = 1.4$, calculate the **work of compression per kg of air**, and the **power required** if the FAD is **5 m³/min**. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Q. (a) — Rotary Screw Compressor

**Working (4 marks):**
- A rotary screw compressor has **two intermeshing helical rotors** — a **male (lobes) rotor** and a **female (flutes) rotor** — housed in a casing.
- The male rotor drives the female rotor (or both are gear-driven).
- As the rotors turn, air is **drawn in axially** at the suction end.
- The intermeshing rotors **trap and compress** the air progressively along the length of the rotors.
- Compressed air is **discharged radially** at the outlet port.
- In **oil-flooded** versions, oil is injected for cooling, sealing, and lubrication.

**Advantages over reciprocating (3 marks):**
1. **Continuous, pulsation-free delivery** (vs. pulsating in reciprocating).
2. **Higher speed operation** → higher specific output per unit size.
3. **Lower vibration and noise** → no reciprocating inertia.
4. **Lower maintenance** (no valves, pistons, or crankshaft wear parts).
5. **Better volumetric efficiency** at high pressure ratios.

---

#### Solution to Q. (b) — Two-Stage Work and Power Calculation

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $T_1 = 30 \text{ °C} = 303 \text{ K}$
- $P_2 = 15 \text{ bar}$, $P_i = 3.5 \text{ bar}$
- $n = 1.4$ (isentropic)
- FAD $= 5 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} = 5/60 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 0.0833 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- $R = 287 \text{ J/kg·K}$ (for air)

**Step 1 — Mass flow rate:**
$$
\dot{m} = \frac{P_1 \dot{V}_1}{R T_1} = \frac{10^5 \times 0.0833}{287 \times 303}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m} &= \frac{8333.3}{86961} \\
\dot{m} &\approx 0.0958 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Work per stage (isentropic, per kg):**
$$w = \frac{n}{n-1} R T_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$$

For **Stage 1** ($P_1 \rightarrow P_i$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1 &= \frac{1.4}{0.4} \times 287 \times 303 \left[ (3.5)^{0.4/1.4} - 1 \right] \\
&= 3.5 \times 287 \times 303 \left[ 3.5^{0.2857} - 1 \right] \\
&= 304423.5 \left[ 1.4505 - 1 \right] \\
&= 304423.5 \times 0.4505 \\
w_1 &\approx 137{,}143 \text{ J/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **Stage 2** ($P_i \rightarrow P_2$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
w_2 &= 3.5 \times 287 \times 303 \left[ \left(\frac{15}{3.5}\right)^{0.2857} - 1 \right] \\
&= 304423.5 \left[ 4.2857^{0.2857} - 1 \right] \\
&= 304423.5 \left[ 1.5128 - 1 \right] \\
&= 304423.5 \times 0.5128 \\
w_2 &\approx 156{,}108 \text{ J/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total work per kg:**
$$
w_{total} = w_1 + w_2 = 137{,}143 + 156{,}108 = 293{,}251 \text{ J/kg} \approx 293.25 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Power required:**
$$
P = \dot{m} \times w_{total} = 0.0958 \times 293{,}251 \approx 28{,}097 \text{ W} \approx 28.1 \text{ kW}
$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 28.1 \text{ kW}}$$

**Model Answer Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Mass flow rate calculation — **1 mark**
- $w_1$ numerical — **2 marks**
- $w_2$ numerical — **2 marks**
- Total work and power — **2 marks**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert FAD to m³/s** before mass flow calculation. Always write unit conversions explicitly. **(–1 mark)**
> 2. **Using $T_1$ in °C** instead of Kelvin. $T(K) = T(°C) + 273$. **(–1 mark)**
> 3. **Not splitting into two stages** when the problem says "two-stage compressor." A common error is to compute a single-stage work from $P_1 \rightarrow P_2$ directly. **(–2 marks)**
> 4. **Rounding $r^{(n-1)/n}$ too early** — keep at least 4 decimal places until the final answer. **(–0.5 mark)**
> 5. **Confusing isothermal ($n=1$) with isentropic ($n=1.4$)** formulas — they have different structures. **(–2 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Compressor = mechanical energy → pneumatic energy** device (NOT a pump).
- [x] Two main families: **Positive Displacement** and **Dynamic (Turbo)**.
- [x] **Positive Displacement:** Reciprocating, Rotary (Vane / Screw / Scroll / Lobe).
- [x] **Dynamic:** Centrifugal, Axial.
- [x] **FAD** is always measured at **inlet** atmospheric conditions, NOT at the discharge.
- [x] **Clearance volume** is the *only* reason a reciprocating compressor has $\eta_v < 100\%$.
- [x] **$\eta_v$ decreases** as compression ratio $r$ increases (because $r^{1/n}$ grows).
- [x] **Volumetric Efficiency formula:** $\eta_v = 1 + C - C \cdot r^{1/n}$.
- [x] **Polytropic Work formula (MUST MEMORIZE):** $W = \frac{n}{n-1} P_1 V_1 \left[ r^{(n-1)/n} - 1 \right]$.
- [x] **Isothermal Work (lower limit, ideal):** $W = P_1 V_1 \ln(r)$.
- [x] **Adiabatic / Isentropic** with $n = 1.4$ is the KTU default for air.
- [x] **Two-stage with intercooler** is preferred when $r > 5$ — saves work, lowers $\Delta T$.
- [x] **Optimal intermediate pressure** for minimum work: $P_i = \sqrt{P_1 P_2}$.
- [x] **Rotary screw** is the industrial workhorse — continuous, low vibration, oil-flooded or oil-free.
- [x] **Centrifugal** is for **high flow, low pressure** (HVAC, aeration).
- [x] **Always use SI units consistently** in calculations: Pa, m³, K, J.
- [x] **Pipe symbol** in LaTeX tables — use `\ln`, `\vert`, `\mid` — **NEVER** the raw `|` character.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Strategy:** For numerical problems, the examiner follows a fixed marking scheme:
> - **Formula** → 1 mark
> - **Substitution of values (with correct units)** → 2–3 marks
> - **Intermediate calculation** → 1–2 marks
> - **Final numerical answer (with unit)** → 1–2 marks
> - **Interpretation / physical comment** → 1 mark (often forgotten by students — practice this!).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pneumatics and Hydraulics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT722 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pneumatics and Hydraulics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Hydraulics

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydraulics** is the branch of science and engineering that deals with the mechanical properties and use of **liquids** (typically mineral-based hydraulic oils) as the working medium for transmitting and controlling power in industrial systems.

In formal KTU 2024 terminology, hydraulics is defined as:

> **Hydraulics (KTU Definition):** *The technology of using a confined, incompressible fluid (hydraulic oil) under pressure to generate, transmit, and control linear or rotary mechanical power through Pascal's Law.*

**Working Fluid:** Hydraulic oil (viscosity index > **95**, ISO VG 32 / 46 / 68)
**Standard Operating Pressure Range:** **50 – 350 bar** (industrial); up to **700 bar** (heavy presses)

## 1.2 Definition of Pneumatics

> [!NOTE]
> **Pneumatics** is the branch of engineering that uses **compressible gases** (most commonly filtered, lubricated atmospheric air) under pressure as the working medium for the transmission and control of mechanical power.

> **Pneumatics (KTU Definition):** *The technology of using compressed, compressible gases (air/nitrogen) at low to moderate pressures to generate, transmit, and control mechanical motion through pressure-energy conversion.*

**Working Fluid:** Atmospheric air (≈ **78% Nitrogen, 21% Oxygen**)
**Standard Operating Pressure Range:** **4 – 8 bar** (industrial); up to **10 bar** (specialized)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Garden Hose vs Bicycle Pump" Analogy** 🎯

Imagine two scenarios to intuitively grasp the difference:

| Analogy Element | Hydraulics | Pneumatics |
|-----------------|-----------|-----------|
| Everyday object | **Fire-Fighter's Hose** (water under pressure) | **Bicycle Pump** (air under pressure) |
| Fluid behaviour | Water does NOT compress — push = full force | Air SQUEEZES — push = compressed cushion |
| Force produced | Very high (can crush steel) | Moderate (good for movement, not crushing) |
| Speed of action | Slower, controlled | Very fast, snappy |
| Recovery after leak | Drips, stays in tank | Escapes harmlessly into atmosphere |

**Why this analogy works:**
- A water-filled hose gives a *solid kick* the instant you open the valve because **water is incompressible**. This is why hydraulic presses can lift cars.
- A bicycle pump, when pressed, feels *spongy* because the air inside gets **compressed**. This compressibility is why pneumatics excel at fast, repetitive motions like in factory assembly lines.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Students:** *"Hydraulics = Heavy + Slow + Strong"* and *"Pneumatics = Light + Fast + Soft"*

---

## 1.4 Engineering Significance

Both systems are **fluid power systems** — they use a pressurized fluid to do useful work. They form the backbone of:

- **Hydraulics:** Excavators, injection moulding machines, ship steering gear, aircraft landing gear, hydraulic presses (forging/forming), CNC machine tool slides.
- **Pneumatics:** Automotive paint shops, pick-and-place robots, packaging lines, dental drills, brake systems (trucks), semiconductor wafer handling, food & beverage processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The course code **OEMRT722** itself contains "Industrial Hydraulics & Pneumatics" — meaning students must understand **both** systems, their similarities, contrasts, and integration in modern automated factories (Industry 4.0 / IIoT environments).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Physical Principles

Both hydraulics and pneumatics operate on a common set of foundational principles, which differ only by the **compressibility** of the working medium.

### A. Pascal's Law (Common to Both Systems)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pascal's Law (KTU Board Favourite ⭐):** *"Pressure applied to a confined, static fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions, and acts with equal force on equal areas."*

This is the **single most important principle** behind every hydraulic jack, brake, and pneumatic press.

**Implication:** A small force on a small piston can lift a massive load via a larger piston.

### B. Boyle's Law (Pneumatics-Specific)

Since air is compressible, **Boyle's Law** governs its behaviour:

> **Boyle's Law:** At constant temperature, the absolute pressure of a given mass of gas is inversely proportional to its volume.

$$p_1 \cdot V_1 = p_2 \cdot V_2$$

This law is critical when designing **pneumatic accumulators** and calculating **air consumption**.

### C. Charles's Law (Pneumatics-Specific)

> **Charles's Law:** At constant pressure, the volume of a given mass of gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature.

$$\frac{V_1}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2}$$

### D. Combined / Ideal Gas Equation

$$p \cdot V = m \cdot R \cdot T$$

Where:
- $p$ = absolute pressure (Pa)
- $V$ = volume (m³)
- $m$ = mass of gas (kg)
- $R$ = specific gas constant (**287 J/(kg·K)** for air)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

### E. Continuity Equation (Both Systems)

The volumetric flow rate $Q$ through any cross-section is constant (conservation of mass):

$$Q = A \cdot v = \text{constant}$$

Where $A$ is the cross-sectional area and $v$ is the fluid velocity.

## 2.2 Working Fluid Comparison

| Property | Hydraulic Oil | Compressed Air |
|----------|--------------|----------------|
| Density (kg/m³) | **850 – 900** | **≈ 1.225** (at STP) |
| Viscosity | High (thick, sticky) | Negligible (~0.018 mPa·s) |
| Compressibility | **Practically incompressible** ($\beta \approx 1.5 \times 10^9$ Pa) | **Highly compressible** ($\beta \approx 1.42 \times 10^5$ Pa) |
| Bulk Modulus | **1.0 – 1.5 GPa** | **0.142 MPa** |
| Lubrication | Self-lubricating | Requires separate lubrication (FRL unit) |
| Temperature sensitivity | Low (viscosity changes) | High (density & pressure change) |
| Contamination sensitivity | High — particles damage pumps | Low — air can be filtered easily |
| Fire/explosion risk | Oil can ignite at high temp | Non-flammable (air is safe) |
| Cost of fluid | Moderate (₹ 200–500/L) | Essentially free |
| Storage | Reservoir required | Tank/accumulator required |
| Leakage effect | Drips, stains, fire hazard | Harmless to atmosphere |

## 2.3 System Architecture (Common Block)

Both hydraulic and pneumatic systems share the **same six-element backbone**:

1. **Energy Source** — Pump (hydraulic) or Compressor (pneumatic)
2. **Reservoir / Storage** — Oil tank or Air receiver tank
3. **Conditioning Unit** — Filter + Strainer (and FRL for pneumatics)
4. **Control Elements** — Direction control, Pressure control, Flow control valves
5. **Actuators** — Cylinders (linear) or Motors (rotary)
6. **Auxiliary** — Pipes, hoses, fittings, gauges, sensors

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — questions appear in every KTU cycle!**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Applies To |
|---|----------|---------|------------|-----------|
| 1 | Pressure | $p = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pa (N/m²) | Both |
| 2 | Hydraulic force | $F = p \cdot A$ | N | Both |
| 3 | Cylinder volume | $V = A \cdot L$ | m³ | Both |
| 4 | Volumetric flow | $Q = A \cdot v$ | m³/s | Both |
| 5 | Cylinder velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | m/s | Both |
| 6 | Time to stroke | $t = \dfrac{V}{Q} = \dfrac{A \cdot L}{Q}$ | s | Both |
| 7 | Hydraulic power | $P = p \cdot Q$ | W | Both |
| 8 | Pump/compressor power | $P_{input} = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{\eta}$ | W | Both |
| 9 | Pascal's multiplier | $F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | N | Both |
| 10 | Boyle's Law | $p_1 V_1 = p_2 V_2$ | Pa·m³ | **Pneumatic only** |
| 11 | Ideal Gas Law | $pV = mRT$ | J | **Pneumatic only** |
| 12 | Isothermal work | $W = p_1 V_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$ | J | **Pneumatic only** |
| 13 | Adiabatic relation | $p_1 V_1^{\gamma} = p_2 V_2^{\gamma}$ | Pa·m³ | **Pneumatic only** |
| 14 | Free air delivery (FAD) correction | $Q_{FAD} = Q_{actual} \cdot \dfrac{p_{actual}}{p_{std}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{std}}{T_{actual}}$ | m³/min | **Pneumatic only** |
| 15 | Reynolds number (flow regime) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v d}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Both |
| 16 | Pressure drop (Hagen–Poiseuille) | $\Delta p = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi d^{4}}$ | Pa | Both |

Where $\gamma$ = **ratio of specific heats = 1.4** for air, and $\eta$ = overall efficiency (typically **0.85 – 0.95**).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **In Aerospace:** Aircraft flight control surfaces use **hydraulics** (incompressible = instant response). Cabin pressurization uses **pneumatics** (bleed air from engines).
- **In Automotive:** Power steering, brakes, and lift platforms → hydraulics. Air brakes in trucks/buses, air suspension → pneumatics.
- **In Manufacturing:** CNC machine tool slide drives → hydraulics. Pick-and-place grippers, conveyor diverters → pneumatics.
- **In Medicine:** Hydraulic operating tables, pneumatic dental drills, ventilators.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Trick Question:** *"Why is water not preferred over hydraulic oil in industrial hydraulic systems?"*
> **Answer:** Water has poor lubricity (causes pump wear), low viscosity index, promotes rust, and lacks anti-foaming additives. Modern synthetic oils resist **150+ bar** with minimal degradation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pascal's Law and Mechanical Advantage of a Hydraulic Press

**Problem Setup:** A hydraulic press has a small piston of diameter $d_1$ and a large piston of diameter $d_2$. A small force $F_1$ is applied to the small piston. Derive the expression for the load $F_2$ that can be lifted.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply pressure from small piston.**
Pressure created under the small piston (Pascal's Law):

$$p = \frac{F_1}{A_1}$$

Where $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi d_1^{2}}{4}$ is the area of the small piston.

**Step 2 — Transmit pressure equally.**
By Pascal's Law, this same pressure acts on the large piston:

$$p = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

Where $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi d_2^{2}}{4}$ is the area of the large piston.

**Step 3 — Equate the two pressure expressions.**

$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to solve for $F_2$.**

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the area formulas.**

$$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{\pi d_2^{2}/4}{\pi d_1^{2}/4} = \left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Final Result.**

$$\boxed{F_2 = F_1 \cdot \left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^{2}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Doubling the diameter ratio quadruples the output force. This is why hydraulic jacks are so compact yet powerful.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Velocity and Force Relationship in Coupled Cylinders

**Setup:** Two cylinders are connected. A force $F_1$ pushes piston 1 (area $A_1$) at velocity $v_1$. Find the velocity $v_2$ and force $F_2$ on piston 2 (area $A_2$). Assume ideal (lossless) fluid.

**Step 1 — Apply the continuity equation.**
The volumetric flow rate is conserved:

$$Q_1 = Q_2$$

$$A_1 \cdot v_1 = A_2 \cdot v_2$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $v_2$.**

$$v_2 = v_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Pascal's Law for force.**

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

**Step 4 — Power balance check (proof of consistency).**

$$P_1 = F_1 \cdot v_1$$

$$P_2 = F_2 \cdot v_2 = \left(F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right) \cdot \left(v_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right) = F_1 \cdot v_1 = P_1$$

✅ Power in = Power out (conservation of energy confirmed for an ideal system).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Air Consumption of a Single-Acting Pneumatic Cylinder

**Problem:** A single-acting cylinder has bore $D = 50$ mm and stroke $L = 200$ mm. It operates at gauge pressure $p_g = 5$ bar and returns by an internal spring. Atmospheric pressure $p_{atm} = 1.013$ bar. Temperature = 300 K. The cylinder completes **30 strokes/min**. Find the **free air consumption** in m³/min.

**Step 1 — Compute cylinder volume (geometric).**

$$V_{cyl} = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot L = \frac{\pi (0.050)^{2}}{4} \cdot 0.200$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{cyl} &= \frac{\pi \cdot 0.0025}{4} \cdot 0.200 \\
&= 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.200 \\
&= 3.927 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3} \\
&\approx 0.000393 \text{ m}^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to absolute pressure.**

$$p_1 = p_{atm} + p_g = 1.013 + 5 = 6.013 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Boyle's Law to find free air equivalent.**
Free air means air at atmospheric pressure ($p_2 = 1.013$ bar) at the same temperature.

$$p_1 V_1 = p_2 V_{free}$$

$$V_{free,per\ stroke} = V_1 \cdot \frac{p_1}{p_2} = 0.000393 \cdot \frac{6.013}{1.013}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{free,per\ stroke} &= 0.000393 \cdot 5.936 \\
&= 2.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3} \\
&\approx 2.333 \text{ litres per stroke}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Account for cycle rate.**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{free} &= V_{free,per\ stroke} \times N \\
&= 2.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3} \times 30 \text{ strokes/min} \\
&= 0.0700 \text{ m}^{3/min}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{free} \approx 0.07 \text{ m}^{3/min} \text{ (or 70 litres/min)}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Hydraulic Power Delivered to a Cylinder

**Problem:** A hydraulic cylinder with bore $D = 100$ mm receives oil at $p = 200$ bar and flow rate $Q = 60$ L/min. Find the (a) cylinder force, (b) piston velocity, (c) hydraulic power.

**Given:**
- $D = 100$ mm $= 0.100$ m
- $p = 200$ bar $= 200 \times 10^{5}$ Pa $= 2 \times 10^{7}$ Pa
- $Q = 60$ L/min $= 60 \times 10^{-3}$ / $60$ m³/s $= 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s

**Step (a) — Cylinder Force.**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.1)^{2}}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$F = p \cdot A = (2 \times 10^{7}) \cdot (7.854 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$\boxed{F = 1.571 \times 10^{5} \text{ N} \approx 157.1 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step (b) — Piston Velocity.**

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{v = 0.1273 \text{ m/s} \approx 127.3 \text{ mm/s}}$$

**Step (c) — Hydraulic Power.**

$$P = p \cdot Q = (2 \times 10^{7}) \cdot (1 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$\boxed{P = 2 \times 10^{4} \text{ W} = 20 \text{ kW}}$$

**Cross-check:** $F \cdot v = 157{,}100 \cdot 0.1273 = 20{,}000$ W = 20 kW ✅

---

## 3.5 Comparative Performance Matrix

| Performance Criterion | Hydraulics | Pneumatics | Winner |
|----------------------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Force-to-weight ratio | Very High | Low | **Hydraulics** |
| Speed of actuation | Moderate (0.1 – 1 m/s) | Very High (up to 5 m/s) | **Pneumatics** |
| Position accuracy | Excellent (±0.01 mm with servo) | Moderate (±1 mm typical) | **Hydraulics** |
| Cost of power transmission | Moderate (oil + filtration) | Very Low (free air) | **Pneumatics** |
| Cleanliness | Messy (leak-prone) | Very clean | **Pneumatics** |
| Explosion-proof suitability | Limited (oil mist) | Excellent (no flammable fluid) | **Pneumatics** |
| Stiffness (load rejection) | High (incompressible) | Low (compressible = spongy) | **Hydraulics** |
| Heat dissipation | Requires cooler | Self-cooling (expansion cools) | **Pneumatics** |
| Component life | Long (10⁷ cycles) | Very long (10⁸+ cycles) | **Pneumatics** |
| Maintenance cost | Higher (seals, filters) | Lower (simple filtration) | **Pneumatics** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Common Architecture of Fluid Power Systems

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional flow** common to both hydraulic and pneumatic systems. Notice how the structure is identical — only the components' implementation differs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["ENERGY SOURCE"] --> nodeB["PRIMARY CONDITIONING"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["RESERVOIR OR RECEIVER"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["FILTER AND FRL UNIT"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["PRESSURE CONTROL VALVE"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["DIRECTION CONTROL VALVE"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["FLOW CONTROL VALVE"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["ACTUATOR CYLINDER OR MOTOR"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["WORK OUTPUT"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["RETURN LINE TO TANK"]
    nodeJ --> nodeC
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **nodeA** generates the raw pressure energy (pump or compressor).
- **nodeB–nodeD** clean, store, and condition the fluid.
- **nodeE–nodeG** are the **3M Control** (Muscle, Motion, Modulation): Pressure, Direction, Flow.
- **nodeH–nodeI** is where pressure energy becomes **mechanical work**.
- **nodeJ** returns fluid to storage for re-use.

---

## 4.2 Side-by-Side Block Comparison: Hydraulic vs Pneumatic System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph H_SYS ["HYDRAULIC SYSTEM BLOCK"]
        direction TB
        h1["Electric Motor or Engine"] --> h2["Hydraulic Pump"]
        h2 --> h3["Oil Reservoir Tank"]
        h3 --> h4["Suction Strainer"]
        h4 --> h5["Pressure Relief Valve"]
        h5 --> h6["Direction Control Valve DCV"]
        h6 --> h7["Flow Control Valve FCV"]
        h7 --> h8["Hydraulic Cylinder"]
        h8 --> h9["Load Movement"]
        h8 --> h10["Return Filter"]
        h10 --> h3
    end

    subgraph P_SYS ["PNEUMATIC SYSTEM BLOCK"]
        direction TB
        p1["Electric Motor"] --> p2["Air Compressor"]
        p2 --> p3["Air Receiver Tank"]
        p3 --> p4["FRL Unit F R L"]
        p4 --> p5["Pressure Regulator"]
        p5 --> p6["Direction Control Valve 3 by 2 or 5 by 2"]
        p6 --> p7["Flow Control Valve"]
        p7 --> p8["Pneumatic Cylinder"]
        p8 --> p9["Load Movement"]
        p8 --> p10["Silencer and Exhaust"]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Detailed Functional Flow: Pneumatic Cylinder Actuation Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Compressed Air at 6 bar]) --> f1["FILTER removes dust and moisture"]
    f1 --> r1["REGULATOR reduces pressure to working level 5 bar"]
    r1 --> l1["LUBRICATOR atomizes oil mist into air stream"]
    l1 --> dcv["3 BY 2 DCV Normally Closed"]
    dcv -->|PILOT SIGNAL ON| cyl["PNEUMATIC CYLINDER Extends"]
    dcv -->|PILOT SIGNAL OFF| cyl2["CYLINDER Retracts by Spring"]
    cyl --> work["Work Output Linear Force"]
    cyl2 --> exhaust["EXHAUST through silencer"]
    exhaust --> env([Atmosphere])
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The air passes through **F → R → L** in that order. Reversing the order destroys the regulator.
- A 3/2 valve directs air either to extend the cylinder or to vent it.
- Exhaust air is silenced to **< 80 dB** for operator safety.

---

## 4.4 Energy Conversion Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    e1["ELECTRICAL ENERGY"] -->|Motor| e2["MECHANICAL ENERGY"]
    e2 -->|Pump or Compressor| e3["PRESSURE ENERGY in Fluid"]
    e3 -->|Valve Control| e4["DIRECTED FLOW"]
    e4 -->|Cylinder or Motor| e5["MECHANICAL WORK"]
    e5 -->|Output| e6["USEFUL TASK Lifting Clamping Pushing"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Draw this 4-stage energy conversion chain when asked "Explain how a hydraulic system works" — it is worth full marks.

---

## 4.5 Comparison Block: When to Use Which

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q{What is the required FORCE?}
    q -->|More than 25 kN| H[USE HYDRAULICS]
    q -->|Less than 25 kN| s{Is SPEED critical?}
    s -->|Yes High cycles per min| P[USE PNEUMATICS]
    s -->|No| a{Is CLEAN ROOM required?}
    a -->|Yes Semiconductor Food| P
    a -->|No| H
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between hydraulics and pneumatics. List any four differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Hydraulics | Pneumatics |
|---------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| 1 | Working medium | Incompressible oil (e.g., ISO VG 46) | Compressible air (≈ 1.225 kg/m³) |
| 2 | Operating pressure | 50 – 350 bar (industrial) | 4 – 8 bar (industrial) |
| 3 | Force output | Very high (up to several MN in presses) | Low to moderate (typically < 25 kN) |
| 4 | Speed | Slower, easily controlled | Very fast, snappy |
| 5 | Compressibility | Negligible (Bulk Modulus ≈ 1.5 GPa) | High (≈ 0.142 MPa) |
| 6 | Position accuracy | Excellent (±0.01 mm) | Moderate (±1 mm) |
| 7 | Cost of medium | Moderate (oil ₹200–500/L) | Negligible (air free) |
| 8 | Fire hazard | Yes (oil can ignite) | No (air is safe) |

> *(Any four well-explained differences = 3 marks; tabulated form earns full credit.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain Pascal's Law. Give one practical application.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** *"Pressure applied to a confined, static fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions and acts with equal force on equal areas."*

**Explanation:** When pressure $p$ is applied to the small piston of area $A_1$, it creates force $F_1 = p \cdot A_1$. This pressure is transmitted instantaneously through the fluid to the large piston of area $A_2$, producing force $F_2 = p \cdot A_2$. Since $A_2 > A_1$, the output force is amplified by the ratio $\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$.

**Practical Application:** **Hydraulic jack / lift** used to raise vehicles of weight 1–3 tonnes using only a small hand-operated lever.

**Mathematical expression:**

$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2} = p$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional elements of a hydraulic system. Compare each element with its pneumatic equivalent.**

**Model Solution:**

A hydraulic system consists of the following **functional elements**:

1. **Prime Mover / Energy Source**
   - Hydraulic: Electric motor coupled to a hydraulic pump (gear, vane, or piston).
   - Pneumatic: Electric motor coupled to an air compressor (reciprocating or screw type).

2. **Reservoir / Storage**
   - Hydraulic: Oil tank (capacity = 3–5 times pump flow per minute). Includes baffles, breather, level gauge, and drain plug.
   - Pneumatic: Air receiver tank (sized for 1–3 minutes of full-load air consumption).

3. **Filtering & Conditioning**
   - Hydraulic: Suction strainer (100–150 µm) + return line filter (10–25 µm).
   - Pneumatic: **FRL unit** — Filter (5 µm) + Regulator + Lubricator.

4. **Pressure Control**
   - Hydraulic: Pressure relief valve (adjustable, set at 110% of working pressure).
   - Pneumatic: Pressure regulator with gauge.

5. **Direction Control**
   - Hydraulic: 4/3 DCV with manual/solenoid/pilot actuation.
   - Pneumatic: 3/2 or 5/2 DCV, spring-centred or detented.

6. **Flow Control**
   - Hydraulic: Throttle + check valve combinations (meter-in, meter-out).
   - Pneumatic: Needle valve + check valve.

7. **Actuator**
   - Hydraulic: Double-acting cylinder or hydraulic motor.
   - Pneumatic: Single or double-acting cylinder (with spring return for single-acting).

8. **Auxiliary**
   - Hydraulic: Cooler, accumulator, hoses (rated 2× working pressure), quick couplings.
   - Pneumatic: Silencers, air dryer, hoses (rated 1.5× working pressure).

**(Block Diagram — Refer to Section 4.2: H_SYS block.)**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore of 80 mm, a rod diameter of 40 mm, and a stroke of 300 mm. The pump supplies oil at 120 bar. Find:**
1. **The extending force.**
2. **The retracting force.**
3. **The time taken to complete one full stroke if the pump flow is 30 L/min.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Bore $D = 80$ mm $= 0.080$ m
- Rod $d = 40$ mm $= 0.040$ m
- Stroke $L = 300$ mm $= 0.300$ m
- Pressure $p = 120$ bar $= 120 \times 10^{5}$ Pa $= 1.2 \times 10^{7}$ Pa
- Flow $Q = 30$ L/min $= 30 \times 10^{-3} / 60 = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s

**Step 1 — Areas.**

$$A_{bore} = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.080)^{2}}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$A_{rod} = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.040)^{2}}{4} = 1.257 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$A_{extend} = A_{bore} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$A_{retract} = A_{bore} - A_{rod} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} - 1.257 \times 10^{-3} = 3.770 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Extending Force.**

$$F_{ext} = p \cdot A_{extend} = (1.2 \times 10^{7}) \cdot (5.027 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$\boxed{F_{ext} = 60{,}320 \text{ N} \approx 60.32 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3 — Retracting Force.**

$$F_{ret} = p \cdot A_{retract} = (1.2 \times 10^{7}) \cdot (3.770 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$\boxed{F_{ret} = 45{,}240 \text{ N} \approx 45.24 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 4 — Time per Stroke.**

Time for extension (full bore area):

$$t_{ext} = \frac{A_{extend} \cdot L}{Q} = \frac{(5.027 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.300}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.016 \text{ s}$$

Time for retraction (annulus area):

$$t_{ret} = \frac{A_{retract} \cdot L}{Q} = \frac{(3.770 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.300}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.262 \text{ s}$$

Total time per full stroke (extension + retraction):

$$\boxed{T_{cycle} = t_{ext} + t_{ret} = 3.016 + 2.262 = 5.278 \text{ s}}$$

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working principle of a pneumatic system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the FRL unit in detail.**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
A pneumatic system uses **compressed air** as the working medium. The compressor (typically reciprocating, 2-stage) draws in atmospheric air, compresses it (to 7–8 bar), and stores it in a receiver tank. From there, the air passes through an **FRL unit**, gets regulated to the working pressure, and is directed via **DCVs** to the **cylinder** or **motor** to do mechanical work.

**Detailed Role of FRL Unit (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator):**

| Component | Function | Specification |
|-----------|----------|---------------|
| **F — Filter** | Removes dust, dirt, water vapour, oil aerosols | Filtration: 5 µm (standard), 0.01 µm (oil-free); Auto-drain type |
| **R — Regulator** | Reduces and stabilizes downstream pressure | Range: 0.5 – 10 bar; with locking adjustment knob |
| **L — Lubricator** | Injects atomized oil mist into the air stream | Oil flow: 1 drop/min per 100 L/min air |

**Why the order F → R → L matters:**
- Filter **first** cleans the air before it enters the regulator (protects the regulator seat).
- Regulator **second** drops pressure so the lubricator operates at low differential (typically 1 bar) for proper oil atomization.
- Lubricator **last** injects oil near the actuator so it travels with the air to lubricate the cylinder seals.

> **If reversed (L → R → F):** The regulator would clog with dust, the lubricator would be starved of pressure differential, and the filter would capture the oil (defeating its purpose).

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.3 — Detailed Functional Flow.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A pneumatic cylinder of 50 mm bore and 150 mm stroke operates at 6 bar gauge. It completes 40 cycles per minute (extend + retract). Calculate:**
1. **The theoretical thrust force.**
2. **The air consumption per cycle in litres (free air).**
3. **The compressor size required in L/min.**

Take atmospheric pressure = 1.013 bar. Assume isothermal conditions.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m
- $L = 150$ mm $= 0.150$ m
- $p_g = 6$ bar → $p_1 = 6 + 1.013 = 7.013$ bar (absolute)
- $p_{atm} = 1.013$ bar
- $N = 40$ cycles/min

**Step 1 — Cylinder Bore Area.**

$$A = \frac{\pi (0.050)^{2}}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Thrust Force (single-acting, full bore).**

$$F = p_g \cdot A = (6 \times 10^{5}) \cdot (1.963 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$\boxed{F = 1178 \text{ N} \approx 1.18 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3 — Cylinder Volume (geometric).**

$$V_{cyl} = A \cdot L = (1.963 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.150 = 2.945 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Free Air Equivalent per Stroke (Boyle's Law).**

$$p_1 V_1 = p_2 V_{free}$$

$$V_{free,stroke} = V_{cyl} \cdot \frac{p_1}{p_2} = 2.945 \times 10^{-4} \cdot \frac{7.013}{1.013}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{free,stroke} &= 2.945 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 6.923 \\
&= 2.039 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3} \\
&\approx 2.039 \text{ L per stroke}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Air Consumption per Cycle (extend + retract).**

Assuming single-acting cylinder with spring return, only the extend stroke consumes compressed air:

$$V_{cycle} = 2.039 \text{ L per cycle}$$

> *(For double-acting, multiply by 2 since both chambers consume air.)*

**Step 6 — Compressor Capacity Required.**

$$Q_{comp} = V_{cycle} \times N = 2.039 \times 40 = 81.56 \text{ L/min}$$

Add ~20% margin for leakage and pulsation:

$$\boxed{Q_{comp,design} \approx 98 \text{ L/min} \approx 0.1 \text{ m}^{3/min}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** ⚠️
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert gauge to absolute pressure** in pneumatic problems — KTU examiners specifically test this; missing it costs 1 full mark.
> 2. **Mixing up extending vs retracting area** in hydraulic double-acting cylinders. Always remember: **$A_{retract} = A_{bore} - A_{rod}$**, NOT $A_{bore}$ alone.
> 3. **Using "bar" instead of "Pa" in formulas** — 1 bar $= 10^{5}$ Pa. Direct substitution of 120 bar into $F = pA$ will give a wildly wrong answer.
> 4. **Confusing stroke time with cycle time** — A "cycle" = extension + retraction. Many students calculate only the extend time and lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Wrong area unit** — Bore in mm means area in mm², force in N when pressure is in N/mm² (MPa). Be consistent.
> 6. **Ignoring atmospheric pressure in pneumatic free-air calculations** — Air was originally at 1 atm; you must convert compressed volume back to free-air equivalent.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read Before Exam Night**

- ✅ **Hydraulics uses OIL (incompressible) → high force, slow, accurate.**
- ✅ **Pneumatics uses AIR (compressible) → low force, fast, cheap.**
- ✅ **Pascal's Law** applies to **BOTH** — it is the unifying principle.
- ✅ **Standard pressures:** Hydraulics = **50–350 bar**; Pneumatics = **4–8 bar**.
- ✅ **Force equation:** $F = p \cdot A$ (universal).
- ✅ **Cylinder speed:** $v = Q / A$ (universal).
- ✅ **Power:** $P = p \cdot Q$ in SI (Watts when $p$ in Pa, $Q$ in m³/s).
- ✅ **Boyle's Law** is **only for pneumatics:** $p_1 V_1 = p_2 V_2$.
- ✅ **FRL** order in pneumatics is **F → R → L** (NEVER reverse).
- ✅ **Bulk modulus** of oil $\approx$ **1.5 GPa** (≈ 10,000× that of air).
- ✅ **Double-acting cylinder areas:** $A_{ext} = A_{bore}$; $A_{ret} = A_{bore} - A_{rod}$.
- ✅ **Always convert gauge → absolute** when applying Boyle's Law to air.
- ✅ **Pascal's mechanical advantage:** $F_2 = F_1 \cdot (d_2/d_1)^2$ — area scales as diameter².
- ✅ **Hydraulic oil properties:** ISO VG 32/46/68, viscosity index > 95, flash point > 200°C.
- ✅ **Famous applications:**
   - Hydraulics → excavator arm, hydraulic jack, injection moulding, aircraft landing gear.
   - Pneumatics → dentist drill, paint spray gun, conveyor diverter, vehicle air brake.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Course Outcome mapping:** This topic primarily addresses **CO1** (Recall fundamentals of fluid power) and **CO2** (Apply principles to compute system parameters).
- ✅ **Common mistake to avoid:** Saying "air has no pressure energy" — WRONG. Air at 6 bar stores real pressure energy; it's just compressible, so the response is "springier."

> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Pascal's Law is universal. Oil is incompressible; air is compressible. Remember the areas. Always convert units. Mind the gauge vs absolute."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Air
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMRT722 - INDUSTRIAL HYDRAULICS & PNEUMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Properties of Air

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air** is a **mechanical mixture of gases** (predominantly Nitrogen ≈ 78% and Oxygen ≈ 21%, with traces of Argon, CO₂, Neon, Helium, etc.) that serves as the **transmission medium** in all pneumatic systems. In Industrial Hydraulics & Pneumatics, "air" specifically refers to **atmospheric air drawn in, compressed, conditioned, and distributed** to actuate linear and rotary actuators, valves, and pneumatic logic circuits.

For engineering analysis, air is treated as an **Ideal Gas** (assumed perfectly compressible, with negligible viscosity under low-pressure, low-speed flow conditions). Its physical behavior is governed by the **Ideal Gas Equation of State**:

$$P \cdot V = m \cdot R \cdot T$$

where **P** is absolute pressure, **V** is volume, **m** is mass, **R** is the **specific gas constant for air (287 J/kg·K)**, and **T** is absolute temperature in Kelvin.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Note:** In KTU question papers, examiners almost always expect the **standard atmospheric pressure = 1.01325 bar = 101.325 kPa = 14.7 psi** and the **standard temperature = 15 °C (288 K)** or **20 °C (293 K)** depending on the IS/ISO reference used. Memorize both values.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of air like a **spring-loaded crowd of invisible billiard balls** inside a closed room. When you compress the room (reduce volume), the balls push back harder on the walls (pressure rises). When you heat the room (add energy), the balls bounce faster and push harder too. This dual response — *volume* AND *temperature* both change pressure — is the **defining hallmark of a compressible gas**, and it is what makes pneumatics fundamentally different from hydraulics (where fluid is virtually incompressible).

A practical industrial analogy: a **pneumatic cylinder pushing a load** is like a person blowing up a balloon to push a ping-pong ball — the air inside "gives" and compresses, unlike water in a hydraulic cylinder which behaves almost like a solid pushrod.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Must Memorize)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Specific Gas Constant, **R** | **287 J/(kg·K)** |
| Universal Gas Constant, **R̄** | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Molecular Mass of Air, **M** | **28.97 g/mol** (≈ 29 kg/kmol) |
| Ratio of Specific Heats, **γ = Cp/Cv** | **1.4** (at room temperature) |
| Specific Heat at Constant Pressure, **Cp** | **1005 J/(kg·K)** |
| Specific Heat at Constant Volume, **Cv** | **718 J/(kg·K)** |
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure, **Patm** | **1.01325 bar = 101.325 kPa** |
| Density of Air at STP | **1.225 kg/m³** (at 15 °C, 1 atm) |

---

## 1.4 Why Study Properties of Air in Pneumatics?

- **System Design**: Compressor sizing, receiver tank capacity, pipe diameter, and pressure drop calculations all depend on air density.
- **Performance Prediction**: Force output of cylinders, response time of valves, and actuator speeds are temperature- and pressure-dependent.
- **Quality Control**: Moisture, dust, and oil content affect component life and must be filtered — governed by ISO 8573-1 air purity classes.
- **Safety**: Compressed air stores enormous energy; understanding compressibility is essential for safe accumulator design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of Core Air Properties

### 2.1.1 Density (ρ)
Density of air is **not constant** — it varies strongly with pressure and temperature. For an ideal gas:

$$\rho = \frac{P}{R \cdot T}$$

- **At higher altitude:** ρ decreases (lower P).
- **At higher temperature:** ρ decreases (hot air rises — the chimney effect).
- **Why this matters in pneumatics:** A compressor drawing hot ambient air produces less mass of air per unit volume, reducing system efficiency.

---

### 2.1.2 Specific Weight (γ_air)
Weight per unit volume:

$$\gamma_{air} = \rho \cdot g$$

At standard conditions: γ_air ≈ **12.07 N/m³** (negligible compared to hydraulic fluid at ~8820 N/m³, which is why pneumatic systems can never match hydraulic force density).

---

### 2.1.3 Compressibility — The Hallmark of Air

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compressibility** is the single most important property that distinguishes pneumatics from hydraulics. Air can be compressed to a fraction of its volume with modest pressure, whereas hydraulic fluid resists compression by a factor of ~10,000.

Quantified by the **Isothermal Bulk Modulus of Air**:

$$K_{iso} = P \text{ (absolute)}$$

That is, at **1 atm**, the bulk modulus of air is only **101.325 kPa**, while for hydraulic oil it is approximately **1.6 to 2.0 GPa** — roughly **15,000 to 20,000 times stiffer**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Adiabatic Bulk Modulus** (used for fast/sudden compression as in pneumatic cylinders):
> $$K_{adi} = \gamma \cdot P = 1.4 \cdot P$$
> Since γ = 1.4 for air, adiabatic processes give a 40% stiffer gas than isothermal — this is why pneumatic systems heat up during rapid cycling.

---

### 2.1.4 Viscosity of Air
Dynamic viscosity of air: **μ ≈ 1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s** at 20 °C.
Kinematic viscosity: **ν ≈ 15.1 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**.

Unlike liquids, the viscosity of air **increases with temperature** (gas molecules move faster and collide more). In hydraulics, viscosity decreases with temperature.

---

### 2.1.5 Humidity (Moisture Content)
Atmospheric air always contains water vapor. The amount of water vapor air can hold depends on temperature (the **saturation pressure** rises with T).

- **Relative Humidity (RH)** = (Partial pressure of water vapor / Saturation pressure at that T) × 100%
- **Absolute Humidity** = mass of water vapor per unit volume (g/m³)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Compressing air concentrates the moisture. When saturated compressed air cools in the after-cooler or receiver tank, water **condenses out** — this is the primary cause of corrosion and valve malfunction in pneumatic systems.
